Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 8B — Ordinary Meaning Rule



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 8B]

\[CITATION: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) — Ordinary Meaning Rule]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: statutory interpretation — ordinary meaning and purpose]

\[CONCEPTS: ordinary meaning, statutory interpretation, purposive interpretation, legislative context, dictionary meaning, interpretation of statutes, meaning of legislative words]



Statutory interpretation in Tasmania begins with the ordinary meaning rule, as codified in Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 8B. The provision directs courts to interpret statutory language according to the ordinary meaning conveyed by the provision, read in its legislative context and in light of the purpose or object of the Act.



Section 8B(3) states that the ordinary meaning of a provision must be determined by considering the text in the context of the Act as a whole and its legislative purpose. This reflects the modern purposive approach to statutory interpretation, under which meaning is not determined solely by dictionary definitions but by considering the function of the provision within the legislative scheme.



Section 8B(1)(c) permits reference to external sources such as dictionaries. However, such references are not primary tools of interpretation. Instead, dictionary meanings may be used only to confirm the interpretation already suggested by the statutory context and purpose.



This interpretive framework means that courts will generally proceed through the following analytical sequence:



Identify the statutory text in question.



Consider the surrounding statutory context.



Determine the legislative purpose or object of the Act.



Confirm the ordinary meaning using external interpretive aids if necessary.



The rule ensures that statutory interpretation prioritises the legislative intent expressed through the text, while still allowing limited use of interpretive aids to confirm meaning.



Authority — Subordinate Legislation Cannot Interpret Empowering Legislation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Attorney General v Barnes \[2023] TASSC]

\[TOPIC: interpretive limits of subordinate legislation]

\[CONCEPTS: subordinate legislation interpretation, statutory hierarchy, delegated legislation limits, interpretation of enabling statute, statutory construction]



The decision in Attorney General v Barnes \[2023] TASSC confirms the principle that subordinate legislation cannot be used to interpret the meaning of the empowering statute.



The Supreme Court of Tasmania emphasised that subordinate legislation derives its authority from the primary statute and must operate within the limits of that statute. Because of this hierarchical relationship, subordinate legislation cannot be used to determine or expand the meaning of the primary legislation that authorises it.



The interpretive hierarchy operates as follows:



The primary statute establishes the legal framework and defines the scope of delegated powers.



Subordinate legislation implements those powers but cannot alter or reinterpret the statutory meaning.



If a conflict arises, the primary statute prevails.



This principle protects the constitutional structure of delegated legislation and ensures that the meaning of legislation enacted by Parliament is not indirectly altered through regulations or other subordinate instruments.



Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 29 — Reckoning of Time



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 29]

\[CITATION: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) — Calculation of Statutory Time Periods]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: calculation of statutory time periods]

\[CONCEPTS: reckoning of time, exclusion of first day, statutory time limits, calculation of legal periods, commencement of time period]



The calculation of statutory time periods in Tasmania is governed by Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 29, which establishes how time periods expressed in legislation are to be calculated.



Section 29 provides that when a period of time is “dated or reckoned from a given day”, the calculation must exclude that day. This means the first day of the period begins on the following day.



For example:



If legislation requires an action within seven days from 1 January, the period begins on 2 January and ends on 8 January.



This rule reflects a longstanding interpretive principle applied across common law jurisdictions. However, the statutory rule can be displaced where legislation expressly states otherwise.



In criminal procedure and sentencing contexts, courts may also specify commencement wording that alters the calculation. For example:



An order that begins “from” a specified date usually excludes that day.



An order that begins “commencing on” a specified date typically includes that day.



Accordingly, the precise wording of the legislative provision or judicial order determines whether the statutory default rule in s 29 applies.



Authority — Exclusion of First Day in Time Calculation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Prowse v McIntyre \[1961] Tas SR 9]

\[TOPIC: statutory time calculation — exclusion of first day]

\[CONCEPTS: reckoning of time, statutory interpretation of time periods, exclusion of day, calculation of limitation periods, statutory deadlines]



The decision in Prowse v McIntyre \[1961] Tas SR 9 confirms the principle that when legislation provides that a period is to be calculated “from” a specified date, the first day is excluded unless the statute indicates otherwise.



The case applies the interpretive rule reflected in Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 29, holding that statutory time calculations normally exclude the initial day when time is reckoned from a particular date.



This principle promotes consistency in calculating statutory deadlines and limitation periods. It prevents ambiguity about whether the day of the triggering event should be included in the calculation.



The authority is frequently cited in Tasmanian statutory interpretation when determining:



limitation periods



procedural deadlines



statutory waiting periods



commencement of legal rights or obligations



Oaths Act 2001 (Tas) s 13 — Commissioners for Declarations



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Oaths Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 13]

\[CITATION: Oaths Act 2001 (Tas)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: execution of statutory declarations by commissioners]

\[CONCEPTS: commissioner for declarations, statutory declaration signing, authentication of documents, legal practitioner authority, police authority]



Under Oaths Act 2001 (Tas) s 13, a person acting as a commissioner for declarations must formally indicate their authority when witnessing or signing documents in that capacity.



Section 13 requires that when signing a document as a commissioner for declarations, the person must add after their signature:



The words “commissioner for declarations”; and



Their title or qualification where their authority arises from a qualifying professional position under s 12(2).



In Tasmania, persons authorised to act as commissioners for declarations include police officers and legal practitioners, among other specified office holders.



The purpose of the statutory requirement is to ensure that statutory declarations and similar documents clearly indicate the authority under which they were witnessed or verified. This assists courts, government agencies, and other institutions in verifying the authenticity and legal validity of the document.



Failure to correctly indicate the commissioner’s status may raise questions about the validity of the declaration or its admissibility in administrative or legal proceedings.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 55A — Failure to Provide Name and Address



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 55A]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) — Failure to Identify to Police]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: failure to provide identifying details to police]

\[CONCEPTS: identification offence, police questioning powers, name and address requirement, arrest without warrant, refusal to identify]



Under Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 55A, it is an offence for a person to fail to provide their name and address to a police officer when lawfully required to do so.



The power arises where a police officer has reasonable grounds to believe that the person:



Has committed an offence; or



Is currently committing an offence.



If a person refuses or fails to provide the requested identifying information, the legislation authorises police to arrest the person without warrant.



The provision serves several purposes in criminal procedure:



enabling identification of suspected offenders



facilitating the issuing of summons or charges



preventing offenders from evading legal accountability



Because the offence depends on the officer having reasonable grounds for the belief that an offence is involved, the lawfulness of the requirement to provide identifying information may be scrutinised in court if challenged.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301 — Execution of Arrest Warrants



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 301]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) — Arrest Warrant Execution]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: execution of arrest warrants]

\[CONCEPTS: arrest warrant execution, warrant arrest procedure, reasonable belief warrant exists, copy of warrant, notice of process, lawful arrest under warrant, arrest formalities]



Execution of an arrest warrant in Tasmania is governed by Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301, which regulates how a person may be lawfully arrested under warrant. The source material states that it is not necessary for the arresting officer to physically possess the warrant at the exact time of arrest, provided the officer acts on a reasonable belief that the warrant exists and gives the arrested person a copy as soon as practicable.



The rule has two practical components:



There must be lawful authority in the form of an existing warrant.



The arrested person must be informed of the process under which the arrest occurs, with a copy provided as soon as practicable.



Section 301 therefore focuses on the legality of the arresting authority and the communication of that authority to the defendant. The provision is directed to substance rather than rigid form. The arrest is not invalid merely because the document is not physically in hand at the moment of apprehension, so long as the statutory requirements are met shortly thereafter.



This rule is significant in practice because warrant arrests often occur in dynamic circumstances. The law accommodates operational reality while preserving the arrested person’s right to know the legal basis of the detention.



Arrest Without Warrant — Statutory Sources of Power



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: statutory powers of arrest without warrant]

\[CONCEPTS: arrest without warrant, police arrest powers, statutory arrest authority, family violence arrest, police offences arrest, misuse of drugs arrest, criminal code arrest]



Arrest without warrant in Tasmania depends on specific statutory authority, not a general police discretion. The source identifies several enactments that confer such powers in different contexts.



The principal statutory sources listed are:



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 27



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 11



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 55



Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 29



Each provision operates within its own legislative context and may impose a different threshold, such as reasonable belief or another statutory condition. The availability of an arrest power therefore requires close attention to the particular Act being used.



The existence of a statutory power is only one part of lawful arrest. For the arrest to be lawful there must also be an arrest in fact, meaning the suspect is clearly placed under compulsion and is no longer free to leave. In addition, associated duties, such as informing the person of the reason for arrest where practicable, remain relevant.



The source emphasises that analysis of arrest powers must distinguish the legal source of power from the separate requirements governing how an arrest is effected.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301(2) — Duty to Give Reasons for Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 301(2)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) — Duty to Inform on Arrest]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: duty to inform arrested person of cause of arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: reasons for arrest, notice of cause of arrest, arrest explanation, practicable requirement, arrest formalities, lawful arrest procedure, informing suspect]



The duty to give reasons for arrest in Tasmania is imposed by Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301(2). That subsection provides that a person arresting another, whether with or without warrant, must give notice, if practicable, of the process or warrant under which they are acting, or of the cause of the arrest.



The rule has three core features:



It applies to arrests both with and without warrant.



It requires communication of either the warrant or the cause of arrest.



The obligation is qualified by practicability.



The requirement is important because arrest is a serious interference with personal liberty. Informing the person of the basis for arrest helps justify the detention and allows the person to understand why they are being deprived of liberty.



The statutory qualification of practicability means the duty is not absolute in all circumstances. However, the source material indicates that Tasmania applies the exception narrowly. Operational difficulty, tension, or volatility does not easily excuse non-compliance. The officer must have a genuine basis for saying it was not practicable to give the explanation.



This provision is central to determining whether an arrest was lawfully carried out, particularly where subsequent charges depend on police acting in the execution of their duty.



Authority — Impracticability and Reasons for Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Johnson v Reeves \[2004] TASSC 110]

\[TOPIC: when it is not practicable to state reasons for arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: impracticability, reasons for arrest, inability to hear police, arrest communication, lawful arrest exception, execution of duty]



Johnson v Reeves \[2004] TASSC 110 explains the limited circumstances in which it may be not practicable to inform a person of the reason for arrest under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301(2).



The principle confirmed is that if it would be pointless to inform, or attempt to inform, the arrested person of the cause of arrest because they could not meaningfully receive the information, it may not be practicable to do so. The source gives the example that where a person would not be able to hear the police, the statutory duty may be excused.



The authority supports a functional approach to practicability. The question is not whether communication was inconvenient, but whether it was realistically capable of serving its purpose. If the explanation cannot be heard or understood in the circumstances, the law does not demand empty formalism.



At the same time, the case does not remove the duty merely because the arrest is difficult or fast-moving. The exception remains tied to genuine impracticability, not mere operational preference.



Authority — Strictness of Duty to State Reasons for Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Wilkie v Patterson \[2016] TASMC 6]

\[TOPIC: high standard for compliance with duty to state reasons for arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: strict arrest requirements, reasons for arrest, volatile arrest scene, police explanation duty, execution of duty, arrest unlawfulness]



Wilkie v Patterson \[2016] TASMC 6 is authority for the proposition that the duty to state reasons for arrest is applied with a very high standard of compliance in Tasmania.



According to the source, the case indicates that even the threat of violence against police, or the existence of a volatile situation, does not readily justify a failure to explain the reason for arrest. The decision therefore reinforces that the exception in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301(2) is narrow.



The legal principle supported by the case is that police must not treat stressful or difficult arrest conditions as automatically displacing the obligation to inform. The arresting officer must still make the explanation unless it is truly not practicable.



This authority is significant because the lawfulness of an arrest may affect later offences such as resisting or assaulting police in the execution of duty. If the arrest was not lawfully effected, the prosecution may face difficulty establishing that police were acting within lawful authority.



Common Law Duty to Give Reasons for Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: common law requirement to inform person of reasons for arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: common law arrest, reason for arrest, liberty protection, obvious reason exception, arrest justification, explanation to detainee, arrest law]



At common law, a person making an arrest must generally inform the arrested person of the reason for the arrest. The source identifies this doctrine as operating alongside the statutory duty in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301(2).



The doctrine exists because arrest is a substantial interference with liberty. The law requires that interference to be justified and communicated to the person affected. Informing the person of the basis for arrest also gives them an opportunity to provide an explanation that may immediately resolve the matter.



The source identifies one common law exception: where the reason for arrest is obvious, such as where a robber is caught red-handed, it may not be necessary to state the reason expressly. That exception is narrower than a general convenience-based exception and turns on whether the arrested person already knows in substance why they are being arrested.



This common law doctrine remains important because it applies independently of statute and may bear on the lawfulness of arrests, especially in summary matters.



Authority — Principal Common Law Source on Reasons for Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Christie v Leachinsky \[1947] AC 573]

\[TOPIC: common law duty to inform arrested person of reason for arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: Christie v Leachinsky, reason for arrest, liberty justification, obvious reason exception, opportunity to explain, common law arrest]



Christie v Leachinsky \[1947] AC 573 is the principal common law authority explaining the duty to inform a person of the reasons for arrest.



The case identifies two core justifications for the rule:



Arrest is a major interference with personal liberty and must therefore be justified to the person arrested.



Informing the person of the reason for arrest gives them an opportunity to offer an explanation and potentially secure prompt release.



The case also recognises that where the reason for arrest is obvious in the circumstances, an express statement may not always be required. However, that exception is confined to cases where the arrested person would already understand the substance of the reason.



This authority underpins the common law framework for lawful arrest and continues to inform Tasmanian analysis, particularly where statutory provisions do not exhaust the field.



Authority — Common Law Duty Applies to Summary Offences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Gow v Davies \[1992] 1 Tas R 1]

\[TOPIC: application of common law arrest principles to summary offences]

\[CONCEPTS: summary offences, common law arrest duty, reasons for arrest, Tasmanian arrest law, execution of duty, arrest legality]



Gow v Davies \[1992] 1 Tas R 1 supports the proposition that the common law duty to inform a person of the reason for arrest applies in Tasmania to summary offences.



The source links this proposition with Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4(3) and the broader common law framework reflected in Christie v Leachinsky \[1947] AC 573. The case is therefore relevant in distinguishing the statutory regime applicable to indictable offences from common law principles that continue to operate in summary matters.



The authority is important because it confirms that lawful arrest analysis cannot be confined to the Criminal Code alone. In summary offence contexts, common law requirements may remain central to determining whether police were acting lawfully and in the execution of their duty.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) — “Found Offending” and Committing an Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) — Meaning of Found Offending]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: meaning of found offending and committing an offence for arrest powers]

\[CONCEPTS: found offending, committing offence, arrest without warrant, reasonable grounds, flagrante delicto, fresh pursuit, police powers]



The doctrine of “found offending” concerns when police may arrest without warrant a person who is committing an offence or is apprehended in circumstances closely connected to the offence. The source distinguishes between powers tied to a person being “found offending” and powers tied to a person “committing” an offence.



The key point is that these expressions are interpreted by reference to what is known to the arresting officer at the time. The lawfulness of the arrest does not necessarily depend on whether the person later proves to have committed the offence as a matter of ultimate fact.



The source material indicates that where the statutory language uses “committing” an offence, the power may be exercised where the arresting officer believes on reasonable grounds that the person is committing the relevant offence. The doctrine also extends to situations of immediate and fresh pursuit after the offence is detected.



This analysis matters because offences such as resisting or assaulting police in the execution of duty may depend on whether the initial arrest was lawfully carried out under a valid statutory power.



Authority — Reasonable Grounds and “Committing” an Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Kim Hibble v Jody William Phegan; Kim Hibble v Dean Mark Jenkins; David John Brooks v Leigh Andrew Phegan; Kim Hibble v Kim Maree Ogden; David John Brooks v Andrew David Hulse \[1997] TASSC 52]

\[TOPIC: arrest power where police believe on reasonable grounds a person is committing an offence]

\[CONCEPTS: committing offence, reasonable grounds, found offending, fresh pursuit, personal exercise of arrest power, senior officer direction, execution of duty]



The decision in Kim Hibble v Jody William Phegan; Kim Hibble v Dean Mark Jenkins; David John Brooks v Leigh Andrew Phegan; Kim Hibble v Kim Maree Ogden; David John Brooks v Andrew David Hulse \[1997] TASSC 52 explains how arrest powers operate where legislation refers to a person “committing” an offence.



The legal principles drawn from the source are:



The power is exercisable where a police officer believes on reasonable grounds that the person is committing the relevant offence.



It is not necessary that the person later be proved actually to have been offending in law at the time of arrest.



The power extends to a person taken flagrante delicto, a person reasonably believed to be taken flagrante delicto, and a person taken immediately and in fresh pursuit after being found in those circumstances.



The arrest power must be exercised personally by the arresting officer on the facts known to that officer. A mere direction from a senior officer does not itself provide lawful authority.



The case is particularly important because it emphasises that arrest is a serious interference with liberty and must rest on lawful authority actually possessed by the arresting officer.



Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas) s 3 — Meaning of “In Custody”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 3]

\[CITATION: Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas) — Definition of In Custody]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: statutory meaning of in custody]

\[CONCEPTS: in custody, lawful arrest, detention and interrogation, warrant arrest, arrest under section 27, custody protections, custodial status]



The expression “in custody” for the purposes of the Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas) is defined by s 3. The source states that a person is in custody if they are:



Under lawful arrest by warrant; or



Under lawful arrest under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 27 or a provision of any other Act.



This definition is critical because the statutory protections in the Act apply only to persons who satisfy that custodial threshold. The Act does not simply apply whenever a person is dealing with police. It is tied to lawful arrest.



The definition therefore requires two separate inquiries:



Was there an arrest in fact?



Was that arrest lawful under warrant or statute?



If the answer to either question is no, the protections may not apply. The source also notes that practical difficulty can arise in determining a suspect’s precise custodial status, particularly where police control is exerted without formal words of arrest.



This provision is foundational to detention law in Tasmania because it determines the class of persons entitled to the procedural safeguards created by the Act.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 106 — Lawful Custody



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 106]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) — Meaning of Lawful Custody]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: meaning of lawful custody]

\[CONCEPTS: lawful custody, custody definition, criminal code custody, lawful detention, arrest status, custody offence context]



The source identifies Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 106 as defining lawful custody. That definition is relevant in multiple doctrinal contexts, including offences relating to custody and statutory powers that depend on a person already being lawfully detained.



The practical significance of lawful custody in the source material is twofold:



It helps determine whether a person is properly held under legal authority.



It provides a threshold for later police powers, such as search powers that only arise once a person is already in lawful custody.



Where legislation depends on lawful custody, the analysis is not satisfied merely because the person is physically restrained. The restraint must be supported by valid legal authority. If the underlying arrest or detention is unlawful, a power conditioned on lawful custody may fail.



This concept is therefore a gateway issue in criminal procedure and in evaluating the legality of subsequent police conduct.



Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas) — Protections for Arrested Persons



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas) — Protections for Persons in Custody]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: statutory protections for persons in custody]

\[CONCEPTS: right to silence, right to lawyer, right to contact friend or relative, interpreter, reasonable questioning time, custody officer duties, magistrate without delay]



The Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas) provides a statutory framework of protections for persons who are in custody. The source identifies the principal protections as follows:



The arrestee must be taken before a magistrate or justice as soon as practicable after arrest unless released or bailed under s 4(1).



The arrestee must be informed of the right to silence before questioning under s 4(5).



The arrestee must be informed of the right to communicate with a friend or relative and a legal practitioner before questioning under s 6(1).



Police must defer questioning and investigation to permit that communication, subject to statutory exceptions, under s 6(2).



Police must provide reasonable facilities for the communication under s 6(7).



An interpreter must be provided where needed under s 5.



Questioning may only continue for a reasonable time under s 4(2)(a), assessed by the considerations in s 4(4).



The person must be taken before a custody officer without delay and placed in that officer’s custody under s 15(1).



The custody officer must perform statutory duties under s 15(2) and (4) and ensure treatment in accordance with the Act under s 16(1).



These protections safeguard both fairness and the reliability of custodial processes.



Custodial Status — Volunteers, Persons Under Arrest, and Persons in Police Custody Without Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: categories of custodial status for detention law]

\[CONCEPTS: volunteer, under arrest, police custody without arrest, free to leave, informal arrest, custodial categories, detention status]



The source distinguishes three major custodial categories relevant to Tasmanian detention law:



Volunteers assisting police with inquiries



Persons under arrest



Persons in police custody though not formally under arrest



A volunteer is not under arrest and is, in law, not detained. Such a person remains free to leave and was therefore not treated by the legislation as necessarily requiring the protections afforded to persons in custody.



A person under lawful arrest is not free to leave and must submit to lawful authority. For arrest to exist there must be both a power to arrest and an arrest in fact, meaning police make it clear that the person is under compulsion.



The third category is more complex. The source states that Tasmanian case law has recognised that a person may be in the custody of police though not formally under arrest. This creates uncertainty about whether all statutory protections that depend on lawful arrest apply to such a person.



The classification matters because statutory safeguards turn on legal status, not merely on the practical atmosphere of the encounter.

Forensic Procedure Orders — Application, Public Interest, and Custody Powers

The source states that the application is made under s 14 of the Act, and that the court must consider public interest factors under s 17. The source identifies two substantive features of the regime:



Police may apply for a non-consensual order.

Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 26A — Power to Enter Premises to Effect Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 26A]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) — Entry to Premises to Effect Arrest]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: entry to premises to effect arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: entry to premises, arrest entry power, power to enter home, effect arrest, police entry, premises access, arrest enforcement]



The power to enter premises in order to effect an arrest is identified in the source as arising under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 26A.



This power is important because, at common law, entry onto or into private premises is ordinarily a trespass unless justified by consent, warrant, statutory authority, or another recognised legal basis. Section 26A is therefore a statutory source of authority permitting entry where the legal conditions for exercising the arrest power are met.



The source treats the provision as a distinct entry power rather than merely an incident of arrest. That distinction matters because the law separately regulates:



Whether there is a lawful power to arrest; and



Whether there is lawful authority to enter the premises where the person is to be apprehended.



If the statutory conditions are not satisfied, entry may be unlawful even if police otherwise suspect the person of offending. The existence of the provision also interacts with the doctrine of implied licence, which may permit limited approach to premises but does not itself authorise full entry into the home.



Authority — Implied Licence to Approach a Dwelling



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Halliday v Nevill \[1984] HCA 80]

\[TOPIC: implied licence to enter driveway or path for legitimate approach]

\[CONCEPTS: implied licence, driveway access, path to front door, lawful approach, visitors generally, trespass limits, police approach]



Halliday v Nevill \[1984] HCA 80 is authority for the principle that where the path or driveway to a suburban dwelling is left unobstructed, the entrance gate is unlocked, and there is no sign or indication forbidding entry, the law implies a licence permitting members of the public to approach the premises for a legitimate purpose.



The source quotes the High Court headnote to the effect that such a licence extends to going upon the path or driveway for a legitimate purpose that does not interfere with the occupier’s possession or cause injury.



The principle is important in police powers analysis because it explains how officers may lawfully approach a residence to knock, speak to an occupier, or make inquiries without immediately becoming trespassers. However, the licence is limited in scope. It is not a general power to search, enter the home, or remain for an illegitimate purpose.



The doctrine is therefore best understood as a limited permission implied by ordinary social expectations, not a broad law enforcement power.



Authority — Implied Licence Does Not Extend Into the House



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Lee \[2005] TASSC 117]

\[TOPIC: limit of implied licence in police entry cases]

\[CONCEPTS: implied licence limits, driveway not house, entry to home, trespass, police on premises, approach to door, unlawful entry]



Tasmania v Lee \[2005] TASSC 117 clarifies the limits of the implied licence doctrine in Tasmania. The source states that while there may be an implied licence for police to go onto the driveway or path leading to the door of the house, there is no implied licence authorising entry into the house itself.



The principle confirmed is that entry beyond the area ordinarily used for approach, such as crossing the threshold into the home without invitation or other lawful authority, constitutes trespass.



This distinction is important because police powers analysis must separate:



Lawful approach to the premises; and



Lawful entry into the dwelling.



A lawful approach does not itself create authority to enter further. Entry into the house requires consent, warrant, statutory power, or some other recognised justification.



Common Law Seizure of Property From Third Parties



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: seizure of evidence from non-suspects at common law]

\[CONCEPTS: third-party seizure, non-suspect property, evidentiary seizure, mobile phone seizure, refusal unreasonable, preservation of evidence, common law police power]



The source identifies a possible common law power permitting police to seize evidence or potential sources of evidence from third parties who are not themselves suspects. The leading authority cited is Ghani v Jones \[1970] 1 QB 693.



The doctrine, as described in the source, has been invoked in situations such as seizure of a bystander’s mobile phone where the device appears to contain footage of a serious offence. The key limitation is that the third party’s refusal to hand over the property must be quite unreasonable before the seizure is justified.



The source notes both the practical police interest in preserving evidence and the significant inconvenience and privacy impact on the owner. It also indicates that the doctrine is controversial and must be applied carefully.



This rule is therefore not a general licence to take property from bystanders. It is a narrow evidentiary doctrine concerned with serious offences, material evidence, and unreasonable refusal.



Authority — Fivefold Conditions for Seizure From Third Parties



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Ghani v Jones \[1970] 1 QB 693]

\[TOPIC: common law conditions for seizure of property connected with serious offence]

\[CONCEPTS: serious offence, fruit of crime, instrumentality, material evidence, refusal unreasonable, no longer than necessary, judged at time of seizure]



Ghani v Jones \[1970] 1 QB 693 is the leading authority cited in the source for the common law power to seize property from a non-suspect where the property is materially connected to a serious offence.



The source attributes the following fivefold conditions to Lord Denning’s formulation:



Police must have reasonable grounds for believing a serious offence has been committed.



They must have reasonable grounds for believing the article is the fruit of the crime, the instrument by which it was committed, or material evidence of its commission.



They must have reasonable grounds to believe the person in possession committed the crime, was implicated in it, was an accessory, or at least that refusal to hand over the item is quite unreasonable.



Police must keep the item no longer than reasonably necessary to complete investigations or preserve it as evidence.



The lawfulness of the seizure is judged at the time it occurs, not with hindsight.



The source also treats this case as a high-water mark of common law seizure power.



Consent to Search — Silence or Non-Resistance Is Not Consent



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: consent to search must be actual and not inferred from passivity]

\[CONCEPTS: search consent, silence not consent, non-resistance, passive compliance, voluntary agreement, unlawful search, Tasmania v Hodges]



Consent to search requires actual voluntary agreement. The source states that merely not resisting a search, or failing to say no, does not amount to consent.



This principle is important because searches are often defended on the basis that the person did not object. The doctrine rejects that approach. Passive acquiescence, silence, or submission in the face of police authority is not the same as genuine consent.



The rule protects against the dilution of personal liberty and privacy rights by ensuring that consent is not inferred too easily from ambiguous conduct. In practice, where police rely on consent, the circumstances must demonstrate real permission rather than mere compliance.



Authority — Silence and Passive Compliance Do Not Establish Consent



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Hodges \[2022] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022)]

\[TOPIC: absence of resistance does not amount to consent to search]

\[CONCEPTS: no consent, passive acquiescence, unlawful search, private premises, lawful entry precondition, hotel room as premises, search power limits]



Tasmania v Hodges \[2022] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022) supports two principles drawn from the source.



First, the case confirms that a person does not consent to a search merely because they do not resist or do not expressly say no. Consent must be actual and voluntary, not inferred from silence or submission.



Second, the source states that a precondition to search powers based on reasonable belief under the Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) is lawful entry onto private premises. The case is also said to support the proposition that private premises may include a hotel room in certain circumstances.



The authority therefore links consent doctrine with entry doctrine. Even where police believe there are grounds for a drug search, the statutory power does not override the need for lawful entry onto private premises.



Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) s 16 — Search of a Conveyance Without Warrant



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 16]

\[CITATION: Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) — Warrantless Search of Conveyance]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: warrantless search of a conveyance]

\[CONCEPTS: vehicle search, conveyance search, warrantless search, reasonable belief, car search, transport search, statutory vehicle power]



The source states that under Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) s 16, police may search a conveyance without a warrant if the statutory threshold of reasonable belief is satisfied.



The term conveyance includes vehicles such as cars. The power is distinct from a premises search and reflects the practical mobility of vehicles and the risk that evidence may be moved or lost before a warrant can be obtained.



Because the source refers only briefly to the provision, the principal supported point is that the warrantless search depends on a reasonable belief threshold and is directed to conveyances rather than premises generally.



This distinction matters because different statutory standards and procedural safeguards may apply depending on whether police are searching a person, vehicle, or premises.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 58B — Search of Person in Lawful Custody



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 58B]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) — Non-Invasive Search in Custody]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: non-invasive search of person already in lawful custody]

\[CONCEPTS: personal search, lawful custody, non-invasive search, detainee search, police custody search, section 58B, custody threshold]



The source states that Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 58B permits a non-invasive search of a person who is already in lawful custody.



This means the search power is not itself a power to create custody. It arises only after the person has lawfully entered the custodial status required by the legislation. The source links the concept of lawful custody back to Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 106.



The practical implication is that if the underlying arrest or detention is unlawful, a search power conditioned on lawful custody may also fail. The legality of custody is therefore a threshold issue for the legality of the search.



Because the source describes the search as non-invasive, the provision should be understood as authorising a limited form of personal search rather than a more intrusive forensic or body-search regime.



Mobile Phones as “Documents” Under Search and Evidentiary Analysis



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: treatment of mobile phones as documents and practical scope of examination]

\[CONCEPTS: phone as document, digital evidence, evidence act, phone contents, flight mode, remote wiping, search scope, external applications]

\[REVIEW]



The source states that a mobile phone falls within the Evidence Act definition of a “document” and that police may examine the contents of the phone. The source does not specify the full Act title or section. \[REVIEW]



The source also suggests a practical distinction in scope. It indicates that examination of the phone’s contents likely extends only to material that can be accessed as part of the document-like contents of the phone, and may not necessarily extend to all external applications. It further notes that police may take steps to prevent destruction of evidence, such as placing the phone in flight mode to avoid remote wiping.



Because the source expresses uncertainty about some aspects of the practical limit of examination, this chunk records only the points directly supported by the text and marks the matter for review.



Search Warrant Scope — No Fishing Exercise



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: limits on scope of search warrants]

\[CONCEPTS: fishing expedition, warrant scope, particularity, search limits, unrelated items, warrant boundaries, targeted search]



A search warrant authorises a targeted search for items connected to the offence and subject matter identified in the warrant. It does not permit a general exploratory search or “fishing exercise.”



This principle requires the warrant to be read according to its authorised scope. Police must remain within the boundaries set by the warrant and cannot use it as a general opportunity to search for unrelated offending.



The doctrine is especially important where police seek to justify seizure of items not expressly listed in the warrant. In such cases, the validity of the seizure depends on an independent legal basis rather than the warrant alone.



Authority — Search Warrants Cannot Be Used for Fishing Expeditions



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Crowley v Murphy (1981) 34 ALR 496]

\[TOPIC: prohibition on using search warrant as fishing exercise]

\[CONCEPTS: fishing expedition, search warrant scope, particularity, unrelated evidence, warrant abuse, targeted search]

\[REVIEW]



The source cites Crowley v Murphy for the proposition that police cannot use a search warrant to engage in a fishing exercise. The full citation is not supplied in the source. \[REVIEW]



The principle supported is that the warrant must be confined to its lawful object. Police cannot rely on a valid warrant as a roving commission to search generally for anything incriminating.



Because the source gives only the short case name and not the full citation, this chunk is limited to the doctrinal proposition expressly supported by the uploaded text.



Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) s 6 — Seizure of Evidential Material Found During Search



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 6]

\[CITATION: Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) — Seizure of Unanticipated Evidential Material]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: seizure of evidential material not specifically particularised in warrant]

\[CONCEPTS: seizure under warrant, evidential material, another offence, reasonable grounds, serious offence, material evidence, no longer than necessary]



The source states that Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) s 6 substantially embodies the principles associated with Ghani v Jones concerning seizure of material found during the execution of a warrant.



According to the source, the executing officer may seize material found on the premises that the officer believes on reasonable grounds to be:



Evidential material in relation to the offence to which the warrant relates; or



Evidential material in relation to another offence.



The source also identifies additional conditions associated with the broader doctrine:



The matter must relate to a serious offence.



The property must be the fruit of the crime, the means by which it was committed, or material evidence of its commission.



Police must keep the item for no longer than necessary to complete investigations or preserve it as evidence.



The lawfulness of police conduct is judged at the time of seizure, not by later events.



This rule permits targeted seizure beyond the exact wording of the warrant where the statutory conditions are met, but it does not authorise a general exploratory search.



Common Law Right Against Unauthorised Search and Entry



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: no common law search or entry without arrest, warrant, or consent]

\[CONCEPTS: trespass, private property, search by consent, search incident to arrest, warrant requirement, privacy, common law rights]



The source states the general common law position that search and seizure are permitted only when justified by arrest, warrant, or consent. Absent such authority, entry onto private property and the examination or taking of property are unlawful.



This principle reflects the common law’s protection of property and privacy. It rejects the existence of a general police power to search persons or premises without lawful authority. The source also notes that search and seizure powers must be understood as exceptional incursions on individual liberty and property rights.



The doctrine therefore operates as a starting point: unless police can point to a lawful source of authority, the search or seizure is invalid.



Authority — Fundamental Common Law Protection of Private Property



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Coco v R (1994) 179 CLR 427]

\[TOPIC: unauthorised entry on private property is trespass]

\[CONCEPTS: trespass, private property rights, exclusion of others, fundamental common law right, state intrusion, privacy values, search legality]



Coco v R (1994) 179 CLR 427 is cited in the source for the principle that unauthorised entry on private property is a trespass and that the right of a person in possession of premises to exclude others is a fundamental common law right.



The authority is used to emphasise that governmental searches require clear legal justification. It supports a rights-based approach to search law in which privacy and property interests are not displaced by implication.



In the context of criminal procedure, the case is important because it frames search powers as exceptional incursions on fundamental rights. The need for lawful authority must therefore be shown clearly and specifically.



Authority — Search Incident to Arrest at Common Law



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Barnett \& Grant v Campbell (1902) 21 NZLR 484]

\[TOPIC: common law search incident to arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: search on arrest, incident of arrest, evidence of guilt, officer safety, prevent escape, preserve evidence, common law search]



The source cites Barnett \& Grant v Campbell (1902) 21 NZLR 484 for the proposition that, as an incident to lawful arrest, a police officer may search the accused and take possession of property tending to show guilt.



The source also links the doctrine to related protective purposes, including searching for objects with which the person might injure themselves or others, facilitate escape, or destroy or dispose of evidence.



The principle is therefore not a general search power but an incident of lawful arrest justified by evidentiary and safety considerations. The lawfulness of the arrest remains a threshold requirement.



Authority — Lawful Purpose Required for Personal Search



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lindley v Rutter \[1981] QB 128]

\[TOPIC: officer must personally form lawful basis for search]

\[CONCEPTS: lawful purpose, personal search, standing orders insufficient, officer state of mind, dignity and privacy, unlawful search, execution of duty]



Lindley v Rutter \[1981] QB 128 establishes that a police officer conducting a personal search must personally have, and apply their mind to, a lawful purpose for the search.



The source describes a case in which officers believed they were acting under a standing order to search every female prisoner and remove her brassiere. The principle drawn is that a standing order from a senior officer is not itself enough. The officer must personally form the required state of mind and have a lawful basis for the search in the circumstances of the individual case.



The authority also emphasises that personal searches are an affront to dignity and privacy, and that different forms of search involve different degrees of intrusion. The greater the affront, the stronger the justification required.



The case is therefore important both for the requirement of lawful authority and for the subjective element that the officer must actually act for a lawful purpose.



Reasonable Suspicion and Reasonable Belief in Search Law



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: distinction between reasonable suspicion and reasonable belief]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable suspicion, reasonable belief, factual basis, lower threshold, search standards, police threshold, suspicion versus belief]



Search powers often depend on either reasonable suspicion or reasonable belief, and the source warns that the distinction must be handled carefully. Reasonable suspicion is a lower threshold than reasonable belief, but it still requires some factual basis.



The doctrine is important because the validity of a search may depend entirely on whether the correct statutory threshold was met. A suspicion may be enough under one enactment, whereas another may require belief. Confusing the two can render the search unlawful.



The source also links the threshold question with warrant specificity and the description of the evidence sought. The legal basis for the search and the object of the search must align.



Authority — Definition of Reasonable Suspicion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: George v Rockett (1990) 170 CLR 104]

\[TOPIC: meaning of reasonable suspicion and distinction from belief]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable suspicion defined, conjecture or surmise, factual basis, less than belief, objective standard, police suspicion, search threshold]



George v Rockett (1990) 170 CLR 104 is cited in the source for the classic statements of principle concerning reasonable suspicion.



The authority supports these propositions:



Reasonable suspicion is the existence of facts sufficient to induce that state of mind in a reasonable person.



Suspicion is a state of conjecture or surmise where proof is lacking.



The facts that reasonably ground suspicion may be insufficient to ground belief.



Even so, some factual basis must be shown.



The case is central to search and warrant law because it marks the boundary between mere hunch and legally sufficient suspicion. It also highlights that suspicion is a lower threshold than belief, but not a speculative one.



Authority — Unlawful Search and Resistance



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Nguyen v Elliott (unreported, Supreme Court, Victoria, Hedigan J, No 7540 of 1994, 6 February 1995)]

\[TOPIC: no offence of resisting where search was unwarranted and outside duty]

\[CONCEPTS: unlawful search, no execution of duty, resistance, no reasonable basis, drugs search, suspicious behaviour, invalid police search]



The source uses Nguyen v Elliott as an example of unlawful search where police had no reasonable basis for believing the person had drugs and therefore no right to search.



The principle drawn is that where the search is unwarranted and not within lawful police duty, resistance to that search may not amount to resisting an officer in the course of duty. The source also states that subsequent arrest founded on the resistance may likewise be unlawful.



The source notes reluctance in the appellate treatment of the case, but the doctrinal proposition recorded in the uploaded material is that an unwarranted search cannot be retroactively validated by offences said to arise from resisting it.



Authority — Issuing Officer Must Personally Be Satisfied



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: MacLeod (1991) 61 A Crim R 465]

\[TOPIC: subjective satisfaction of issuing officer and particularity of warrant]

\[CONCEPTS: issuing officer satisfaction, oath information, warrant particularity, object of search, offence specificity, not enough informant suspicion, warrant validity]



The source states that MacLeod (1991) 61 A Crim R 465 applied George v Rockett and held that the relevant state of mind is that of the issuing officer.



The principles identified are:



The issuing officer must personally satisfy themselves that the statutory criteria are present.



It is not enough that the informant suspects there are grounds.



The warrant must display particularity in relation to both the object sought and the offence under investigation.



Mere reference to a statutory section is not enough.



This authority reinforces that warrant issue is not a rubber-stamp process. The issuing officer must exercise an independent judgment grounded in sworn information.



Authority — Issuing Authority Need Not Investigate Everything but Must Resolve Concerns



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Beneficial Finance Corp v AFP (1991) 31 FCR 523]

\[TOPIC: extent of issuing authority’s obligation to inquire further]

\[CONCEPTS: issuing authority inquiry, ambiguities, incomplete material, warrant issue, independent judgment, concerns to be resolved, search warrant validity]



Beneficial Finance Corp v AFP (1991) 31 FCR 523 is cited in the source for the proposition that the issuing officer does not have a general obligation to go behind the warrant application and investigate the matter further, even if the material is incomplete.



However, the source states that where the issuing authority has concerns or identifies ambiguities, there is an obligation to clear them up or make further inquiries. The authority therefore strikes a balance between practical warrant administration and the need for genuine independent satisfaction.

Search of Premises — Common Law Protection of the Home



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: common law protection against entry and search of private premises]

\[CONCEPTS: home as protected space, trespass, private premises, search of premises, property rights, threshold of house, common law protection]



At common law, private premises are strongly protected against unauthorised state entry and search. The source treats the home as a particularly protected space, and the legality of entry turns on justification by law.



This doctrine underpins modern search law and explains why warrant, consent, arrest-related authority, or statute are ordinarily required before police may enter and search premises.



Authority — The Home Cannot Be Entered Without Legal Justification



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Entick v Carrington (1765) 2 Wilson KB 275; (1765) 19 St Tr 1029]

\[TOPIC: foundational common law protection against unlawful entry and search]

\[CONCEPTS: Entick v Carrington, home protection, property rights, state trespass, legal justification, privacy, common law liberty]



Entick v Carrington (1765) 2 Wilson KB 275; (1765) 19 St Tr 1029 is the foundational authority cited in the source for the proposition that the state cannot enter private premises without legal justification.



The source quotes the famous statement that even the poorest man may in his cottage defy the forces of the Crown, unless there is justification by law. The case is central to the common law conception of private premises as protected against arbitrary intrusion.



Its significance in criminal procedure lies in its enduring statement that executive convenience cannot substitute for lawful authority.



Authority — Seizure of Material Evidence Found on Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Dillon v O'Brien \& Davies (1887) 16 Cox CC 245]

\[TOPIC: seizure of property found in possession on lawful arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: lawful arrest, material evidence, possession at arrest, felony and misdemeanour, seizure incident to arrest, evidence preservation]



Dillon v O'Brien \& Davies (1887) 16 Cox CC 245 is cited for the proposition that, upon lawful arrest, police may take and detain property found in the person’s possession where the property will form material evidence in the prosecution for the crime.



The source notes that the principle was applied not only to treason and felony but also to misdemeanours. This places the case within the line of authority supporting a limited evidentiary power of seizure incident to lawful arrest.



The doctrine remains connected to the requirement that the arrest itself be lawful and that the seized material bear a real evidentiary connection to the offence.



Authority — Search of Premises After Arrest Must Relate to the Arrested Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Jeffrey v Black \[1977] 3 WLR 895]

\[TOPIC: police may not search arrestee’s premises for unrelated matters without warrant or consent]

\[CONCEPTS: premises search after arrest, unrelated evidence, no warrant no consent, silent not consent, connection with offence, serious crime distinction, search limits]



Jeffrey v Black \[1977] 3 WLR 895 is cited in the source for the principle that police may not search the premises of an arrestee without warrant or consent for evidence unrelated to the offence for which the person was arrested.



The authority supports these propositions:



There must be a connection between the search and the original reason for arrest.



If the search relates to that offence, police may search for and take things connected with it.



They are not authorised to search for evidence unrelated to the initial offence.



Silence is not consent.



The case also supports the proposition that such search powers are associated with serious crime rather than mere misdemeanours.



The source frames the case as protective of the accused’s right to refuse entry.



Authority — Search of Premises Connected to Arrest Warrant



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R (on the application of Rottman) v Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis \[2002] 2 All ER 865; \[2002] 2 WLR 1315]

\[TOPIC: scope of premises search associated with arrest warrant]

\[CONCEPTS: extradition arrest warrant, premises search, arrest on site, search authority, buildings on premises, immediate vicinity, procedural validity]



The source uses R (on the application of Rottman) v Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis \[2002] 2 All ER 865; \[2002] 2 WLR 1315 to support the proposition that where a person is arrested on premises, it may be procedurally sound to search those premises without the need for a separate additional warrant.



The source also suggests the reasoning extends to buildings on the premises even where the person is arrested outside the building structure, thereby extending the logic of earlier authority.



Because the uploaded text provides only a concise doctrinal note, this chunk records the principle as stated in the source without broader elaboration.



Authority — Australian Restriction of Ghani-Type Common Law Search Powers



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Challenge Plastics Pty Ltd v Collector of Customs (1993) 115 ALR 149]

\[TOPIC: Australian limitation on broad common law search powers]

\[CONCEPTS: Australian common law, Ghani rejected, search powers restricted, Dillon v O'Brien, possession at arrest, common law limits, seizure doctrine]



Challenge Plastics Pty Ltd v Collector of Customs (1993) 115 ALR 149 is cited in the source as authority that Ghani v Jones went too far in expanding common law powers of search and seizure.



The principle attributed to the case is that Australian common law search powers are more restricted and are tied to the narrower doctrine in Dillon v O'Brien, namely property in the possession of the arrested person at the time of arrest in connection with felony or misdemeanour.



The case is therefore significant because it marks a departure from broader English formulations and reasserts a more confined Australian position on common law search and seizure.



Authority — Search and Seizure Rights Depend on Possession and Lawful Basis



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Laurens v Willers (2002) 131 A Crim R 281]

\[TOPIC: seizure powers and limits of common law trespass-based rights]

\[CONCEPTS: exclusive possession, caravan park, search warrant seizure, common law basis, stolen goods, Australian limits, possession rights]



Laurens v Willers (2002) 131 A Crim R 281 is cited in the source as discussing the basis of common law search and seizure rights in terms of trespass and exclusive possession.



The source notes two points:



The applicants in that case were in a caravan park in which they had no exclusive possession, which affected the analysis.



The case is part of the line of authority moving away from Ghani v Jones as a general principle in Australian law.



The source also refers to a second common law power to seize stolen goods where this can be done without violence or harm to the goods. The case is therefore used to illustrate the limited and context-specific nature of common law seizure rights in Australia.



Authority — Coerced Submission to House Search Is Not Lawful Consent



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Woodberry \[2012] TASSC 89]

\[TOPIC: police cannot compel person to submit to house search absent lawful basis]

\[CONCEPTS: coerced search, right to refuse entry, no power to compel, exclusion of evidence, unlawful house search, consent or warrant, police overreach]



Tasmania v Woodberry \[2012] TASSC 89 is authority for the proposition that police cannot compel a person to submit to a search of their house where no lawful power authorises that compulsion.



The source describes a situation in which the defendant was taken from her place of business and told she would need to accompany police to the house. She claimed she was compelled to comply with the search. The court held there was no power to compel submission to a house search and that the officers had disregarded her right to refuse entry. The evidence obtained was excluded.



The case supports two key principles:



Police must respect the occupier’s right to refuse entry where there is no warrant, consent, or other lawful authority.



Evidence obtained through compelled compliance with an unlawful search may be excluded.



Authority — Tasmanian Use of Ghani Within Search Warrant Seizure Context



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Spyke \& Alexander, Tas Unreported Serial No 2 of 1981 BC8171022]

\[TOPIC: application of Ghani-type reasoning in Tasmanian search warrant seizure context]

\[CONCEPTS: Tasmanian unreported case, forged cheques, seizure of items from other offences, Ghani application, search warrant acts, evidential seizure]



The source identifies Spyke \& Alexander, Tas Unreported Serial No 2 of 1981 BC8171022 as applying Ghani v Jones in the Tasmanian context and associating that reasoning with the Tasmanian search warrant legislation permitting seizure of items relating to other offences encountered during a warranted search.



The source notes that the reasoning is not extensively explained and contrasts the case with Challenge Plastics, which more carefully considered the law. The authority is therefore relevant as an example of Tasmanian acceptance, at least in some circumstances, of Ghani-type seizure reasoning when police are already lawfully on the premises under warrant.



Search Warrants — Purpose and Common Law Safeguard



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: purpose of search warrants and protection of property rights]

\[CONCEPTS: search warrants, judicial oversight, protection of occupiers, independent judicial officer, property rights, limitation on police powers, privacy protection, control of executive power]



A search warrant at common law operates as a safeguard against arbitrary interference with private property by law enforcement. The fundamental design of the warrant requirement is to interpose an independent judicial officer between the executive branch and citizens before a search of premises occurs.



Historically, the common law treated entry onto private premises as a serious interference with property rights. The right of an occupier to exclude others from their premises was regarded as fundamental. As a result, the common law imposed strict requirements before authorising searches by state officials.



The search warrant mechanism functions as a control device. Before police may lawfully enter private premises to search for evidence, they must present information to an independent issuing authority such as a magistrate or justice of the peace. That judicial officer must assess the information and determine whether the legal threshold for issuing a warrant has been satisfied.



This structure ensures that decisions about intrusions into private property are not made solely by investigators. Instead, a neutral decision-maker evaluates whether the state’s investigative interests justify the proposed search.



The historical background of search warrants also explains why the common law rejected general warrants. General warrants authorised searches without clear limits as to place or items sought and were historically associated with oppressive practices, including those of the English Star Chamber. The common law therefore developed a strong tradition of protecting occupiers’ rights by requiring specificity and judicial scrutiny in the warrant process.



Authority — Reasonable Suspicion Requirement for Search Warrants

Authority — Independent Judicial Mind



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: George v Rockett (1990) 170 CLR 104]

\[TOPIC: reasonable suspicion standard for issuing search warrants]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable suspicion, issuing magistrate, warrant application, judicial independence, evidential threshold, suspicion vs belief, objective basis for suspicion]



The High Court in George v Rockett (1990) 170 CLR 104 confirmed the legal threshold for issuing a search warrant and clarified the role of the issuing judicial officer.



The Court held that a warrant may be issued where the issuing authority is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for suspecting that evidential material will be located at the premises to be searched. This requirement establishes an objective standard. The suspicion must be supported by facts capable of inducing that suspicion in a reasonable person.



The decision emphasised that a suspicion is more than a mere idle wondering. While it does not require proof or even a belief that the suspected facts are true, there must be factual circumstances that reasonably support the suspicion.



A central principle established by the case is that the issuing judicial officer must bring an independent mind to the application. The magistrate or justice of the peace cannot simply rely on the suspicions of the police officer making the application. Instead, the judicial officer must personally assess the information presented and determine whether the statutory criteria are satisfied.



The High Court explained that the effectiveness of the warrant system depends on this independence. If issuing authorities merely endorse police applications without independent consideration, the safeguard against arbitrary intrusion into private property would be undermined.



Authority — Independent Assessment by Issuing Authority



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: MacLeod (1991) 61 A Crim R 465]

\[TOPIC: requirement that issuing authority independently form suspicion or belief]

\[CONCEPTS: issuing authority, reasonable belief, judicial independence, warrant validity, evidentiary sufficiency, assessment of affidavit evidence]



MacLeod (1991) 61 A Crim R 465 applied the principles established in George v Rockett and clarified that the validity of a search warrant depends on the state of mind of the issuing authority rather than that of the applicant.



The case established that the issuing magistrate or justice must independently form the required suspicion or belief based on the evidence placed before them. The judicial officer cannot simply rely on the assertions or conclusions of the police applicant.



Applications for search warrants are typically supported by sworn information or affidavit material. That material must contain sufficient factual detail to enable the issuing authority to determine whether reasonable grounds for suspicion exist.



In MacLeod, the warrant contained no technical defects. However, the court examined whether the issuing justice had been provided with sufficient information to form the required suspicion or belief. The evidence available, including notes recorded by the justice, did not demonstrate that adequate material had been placed before the issuing authority.



As a result, the court could not be satisfied that the justice had properly formed the required suspicion or belief. The case therefore illustrates that the validity of a warrant depends not merely on formal compliance but on whether the issuing authority was actually able to independently assess the factual basis for the warrant.



Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) s 5 — When a Search Warrant May Be Issued



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 5]

\[CITATION: Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) s 5 — Grounds: Reasonable Belief Test]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: statutory grounds for issuing search warrants]

\[CONCEPTS: evidential material, reasonable grounds for suspicion, warrant issuance, premises search, issuing officer, future presence of evidence]



Section 5(1) of the Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) establishes the primary statutory basis for issuing search warrants in Tasmania.



Under this provision, an issuing officer may issue a warrant authorising the search of premises if the officer is satisfied by information provided on oath that there are reasonable grounds for suspecting that evidential material is present at the premises or will be present within the next 72 hours.



The requirement that the issuing officer be satisfied on information given on oath ensures that applications are supported by sworn evidence rather than mere speculation. The threshold of reasonable suspicion reflects the investigative stage of criminal proceedings and does not require proof of guilt.



The concept of evidential material is broad and may include items connected with the commission of an offence, items used to commit an offence, or material that may assist in proving the offence.



Section 4 of the Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas) further provides that the Act does not limit or exclude other statutory powers authorising searches, seizures, arrests, or detention. As a result, the search warrant regime operates alongside other investigative powers granted under Tasmanian and Commonwealth legislation.



Authority — Disclosure Obligations in Search Warrant Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Karina Fisheries v Mitson (1990) 26 FCR 473]

\[TOPIC: duty of full disclosure in warrant applications]

\[CONCEPTS: disclosure obligations, warrant application, material facts, privacy protection, investigative fairness, applicant duty]



Karina Fisheries v Mitson (1990) 26 FCR 473 held that an applicant seeking a search warrant must disclose all material facts that could affect the issuing authority’s decision.



The Federal Court emphasised that the duty of disclosure arises from the common law tradition of protecting the privacy and property rights of individuals against arbitrary search powers. Because warrant applications are typically made without the presence of the affected occupier, the issuing authority relies entirely on the applicant’s presentation of relevant facts.



The Court therefore held that the informant must disclose all information that could influence the decision to issue the warrant, including information that might undermine the application. Failure to disclose such information may invalidate the warrant.



This approach emphasises the protective function of judicial scrutiny in the warrant process. If applicants selectively present only information favourable to the application, the issuing officer’s ability to independently assess the legality of the proposed search is compromised.



Authority — Limited Disclosure Obligation for Warrant Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lego Australia v Paraggio (1994) 52 FCR 542]

\[TOPIC: non-disclosure invalidates warrant only if fraudulent]

\[CONCEPTS: non-disclosure, fraud in warrant application, misrepresentation, warrant validity, applicant good faith]



In Lego Australia v Paraggio (1994) 52 FCR 542, the Federal Court adopted a narrower approach to disclosure obligations in search warrant applications.



The Court held that a search warrant will not automatically be invalidated merely because the applicant failed to disclose potentially relevant information. Instead, a warrant will generally only be set aside where the non-disclosure involved fraud, misrepresentation, or a lack of good faith.



Under this approach, the central question is whether the information presented to the issuing authority was sufficient on its face to satisfy the statutory requirements for issuing the warrant. If the sworn information establishes the required legal threshold, the warrant may remain valid even if other information was not disclosed.



This approach contrasts with the broader disclosure duty recognised in Karina Fisheries v Mitson. As a result, there is some doctrinal tension in the authorities regarding the scope of disclosure obligations.



In practice, many prosecutors and investigators adopt a conservative approach by disclosing all relevant information to the issuing authority to minimise the risk that the warrant will later be challenged.



Authority — Legal Professional Privilege Limits Execution of Search Warrants



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Baker v Campbell (1983) 153 CLR 52]

\[TOPIC: legal professional privilege and search warrants]

\[CONCEPTS: legal professional privilege, privileged documents, search warrant limits, lawyer client confidentiality, evidentiary privilege, protection of legal communications]



The High Court in Baker v Campbell (1983) 153 CLR 52 confirmed that legal professional privilege (LPP) imposes significant limits on the execution of search warrants.



The Court held that legal professional privilege is not merely a rule of evidence confined to court proceedings. Instead, it is a fundamental common law right protecting confidential communications between a lawyer and their client. Because of its fundamental status, the privilege applies beyond litigation contexts and restricts the ability of investigative authorities to seize privileged documents during searches.
