It also records two practical propositions: first, that the offence occurs when the relevant act is committed, not when a later consequence is experienced, illustrated by the example of a call made in breach of an order rather than the later hearing of a voicemail; secondly, that Tasmanian time, rather than interstate time, should be used. The source also cross-refers to Domestic Violence Orders (National Recognition) Act 2016 s 17 for DVO matters.



block\_005



Withdrawing Complaints in Indictable Matters



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Withdrawal of complaints before plea in indictable matters]

\[CONCEPTS: withdrawing complaint, indictable matter, before plea, complaint procedure, criminal process termination, prosecutorial withdrawal]



The source states that withdrawal of complaints is available in indictable matters only where pleas have not yet been entered. The rule extracted is narrow but clear: once plea has been entered, the complaint cannot be withdrawn in the same uncomplicated way.



block\_005



Authority — Hibble v Langmaid \[2012] TASMC 7



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Hibble v Langmaid \[2012] TASMC 7]

\[TOPIC: Withdrawal of complaints in indictable matters before plea]

\[CONCEPTS: complaint withdrawal, indictable proceedings, pre-plea withdrawal, Tasmanian Magistrates Court, procedural termination]



The source cites Hibble v Langmaid \[2012] TASMC 7 immediately under the heading dealing with withdrawal of complaints and the proposition that withdrawal applies in indictable matters only where pleas have not been entered. On the material provided, the case is authority for that procedural limitation. \[REVIEW]



block\_005



Authority — Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55]

\[TOPIC: Deletion of surplus particulars from complaint]

\[CONCEPTS: surplus particulars, delete paragraphs, complaint amendment, single proved act, harassment particulars, complaint drafting, proof of one act]



Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55 is cited in the source for the proposition that where a complaint pleads multiple factual acts, the court may delete unproved paragraphs or acts and still find the charge proved on a single proved example. The source gives as an example a harassment-based breach charge where multiple actions were alleged, but one proved particular was enough after the others were deleted under s 31. The principle is that surplus or unproved particulars may be excised without defeating the complaint if what remains still supports the charge.



block\_005



Counter-Principle — No Late Amendment Causing Incurable Unfairness



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 31]

\[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 31]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Refusal of late amendment creating incurable unfairness]

\[CONCEPTS: incurable unfairness, late amendment, no notice, end of hearing, prejudice, complaint particulars, amendment refusal]



The source states that a court is unlikely to amend particulars under Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 31 where an amendment without notice to the defendant would create incurable unfairness at that stage of the proceedings, particularly at the end of the hearing where the difference is significant. The doctrinal point is that amendment power is not absolute; it is constrained by fairness and may be refused where the case has been run on a materially different footing.



block\_005



Amendments to Complaints — Pith and Substance Doctrine



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: Amendment of complaint where pith and substance remains constant]

\[CONCEPTS: pith and substance, complaint amendment, same actus reus, cognate offence, no prejudice, technical defect, valid law reference]



A complaint may be amended, including in some circumstances outside the limitation period, provided the amendment does not work injustice or prejudice and the pith and substance of the charge remains constant. The source explains that amendment is conditioned on the complaint referencing the wrong but still valid law or regulation, and that justices or magistrates are not required to dismiss complaints for unmeritorious technical reasons. It also states that the complaint can be changed to a different offence so long as the pith and substance remains constant and the complaint is not defective to the point of disclosing no offence or having an unsupported variance from the evidence.



block\_005



Authority — Malahoff v White (Tas SC 37/1991)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Malahoff v White (Tas SC 37/1991)]

\[TOPIC: Amendment allowed where offence remains same in pith and substance]

\[CONCEPTS: pith and substance, same actus reus, different statutory source, no essential change, complaint amendment, disqualification offence]



Malahoff v White (Tas SC 37/1991) is cited in the source for the proposition that amendment is permissible where the offence charged after amendment is of the same pith and substance as the original complaint. The source quotes the reasoning that the same act of driving on the same occasion, coupled with disqualification, remained the same essential conduct even though the disqualification arose under different Acts and the available sanctions may have differed. The authority supports the proposition that not every change in legal reference or sanction changes the essential elements of the charge.



block\_005



Authority — Barnes v Hovington \[2015] TASSC



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Barnes v Hovington \[2015] TASSC]

\[TOPIC: Out-of-time amendment where amended charge is same or cognate in pith and substance]

\[CONCEPTS: out of time amendment, cognate offence, allied in nature, akin in quality, no injustice, stage of proceedings, pith and substance]



Barnes v Hovington \[2015] TASSC is cited in the source for Porter J’s statement that no injustice is done where an amendment results in the defendant facing a charge that is the same in pith and substance as that originally faced, or a cognate one that is allied in nature or akin in quality. The source also records that unfairness may nonetheless arise depending on the stage of proceedings at which amendment is sought. The authority therefore supports both amendment flexibility and a stage-sensitive fairness limitation.



block\_005



Authority — Carlson v Wilkie \[2018] TASSC 1



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Carlson v Wilkie \[2018] TASSC 1]

\[TOPIC: Victim’s name not an element of assault]

\[CONCEPTS: assault elements, victim identity, not an element, complaint particulars, amendment significance, formal defect]



Carlson v Wilkie \[2018] TASSC 1 is cited in the source for the proposition that the victim’s name is not an element of the crime of assault. The practical significance is that an error or change concerning the victim’s name may not alter the legal substance of the charge in the same way as omission of an actual element would.



block\_005



Authority — Constable Julie McCullough v Bronwyn Marie Auksorius \[2025] TASMC 4



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Constable Julie McCullough v Bronwyn Marie Auksorius \[2025] TASMC 4]

\[TOPIC: Complaint referencing repealed law is a bad complaint not capable of curative amendment]

\[CONCEPTS: bad complaint, repealed regulation, law not in force, invalid complaint, amendment limits, good complaint distinction]



Constable Julie McCullough v Bronwyn Marie Auksorius \[2025] TASMC 4 is cited in the source for the proposition that a complaint which references repealed regulations rather than the regulations actually in force may be a “bad complaint” rather than a merely imperfect good complaint. The significance drawn in the source is that where the complaint refers to law that did not exist when the complaint was generated, the defect is more fundamental than the kind of curable technical error discussed in Malahoff v White.



block\_005



Amendments to Complaints at the Close of the Case — Need to Show Prejudice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Amendment at close of case and prejudice requirement]

\[CONCEPTS: close of case amendment, prejudice, minor amendment, recall witnesses, further questions, case run on amendment basis, late amendment]



Where amendment is sought at the close of the case, the key question is prejudice. The source states that late amendment may still be permitted where the defendant cannot demonstrate prejudice, especially where the case has effectively been run on the basis of the amendment sought and the defendant is given procedural opportunities such as asking further questions. The rule is therefore not that late amendment is barred, but that it becomes substantially harder and turns on practical fairness.



block\_005



Authority — Nilsson v McDonald \[2009] TASSC 1



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Nilsson v McDonald \[2009] TASSC 1]

\[TOPIC: Minor amendment at close of case upheld where no prejudice shown]

\[CONCEPTS: no prejudice, minor amendment, close of trial, further questioning, complaint particulars, appeal dismissed, case run on amended basis]



Nilsson v McDonald \[2009] TASSC 1 is cited in the source as authority that amendment at the end of the hearing may be upheld where the defendant cannot demonstrate prejudice. The source records that the amendment was minor, the defence had cross-examined on the relevant issues, the magistrate offered the opportunity for further questions, and there was no identified witness or evidence the defence would otherwise have called. The case supports a functional prejudice analysis rather than a purely formal objection to timing.



block\_005



Authority — Ford v Tasmania \[2012] TASSC 34



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Ford v Tasmania \[2012] TASSC 34]

\[TOPIC: Natural justice requirements where complaint is effectively enlarged by amendment]

\[CONCEPTS: natural justice, enlarged case, recall complainant, further cross-examination, reply submissions, amendment by magistrate, unfair conviction]



Ford v Tasmania \[2012] TASSC 34 is cited in the source for the proposition that where the case effectively becomes enlarged, fairness may require the complaint to be amended, the complainant to be recalled for further cross-examination, and defence counsel to be given a further opportunity to make submissions. The source states that because those steps were not taken, the applicant was not afforded natural justice and conviction should not have followed. The authority therefore identifies procedural mechanisms by which prejudice from amendment can be ameliorated.



block\_005



Authority — Jerrim v Smith \[2020] TASMC 6



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Jerrim v Smith \[2020] TASMC 6]

\[TOPIC: Amendment refused where original complaint omitted an element and disclosed no offence]

\[CONCEPTS: omitted element, no offence known to law, defective complaint, amendment refused, high threshold, hearing stage, bad drafting]



Jerrim v Smith \[2020] TASMC 6 is cited in the source as an example of failure to obtain amendment at hearing stage. The source states that an element of the offence had been omitted from the initial drafting, with the consequence that the complaint alleged no offence known to law, and amendment was refused. The principle supported is that amendment cannot be used to rescue a fundamentally bad complaint that never properly disclosed an offence in the first place.



block\_005



Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 27 and Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) regs 6, 17 — Oral Complaint Followed by Written Complaint



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas); Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 27; reg 6; reg 17]

\[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 27; Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 6; Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 17]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: When a written complaint is “made” after oral information following arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: oral complaint, written complaint, afterhours court, 7 day lodgment, complaint made, first appearance, Form 1, arrested without warrant]



The source sets out an interpretive argument about the interaction between Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 27 and Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) regs 6 and 17. Section 27(2) allows oral information of the substance of the charge to stand in place of a complaint where a person has been arrested without warrant, up to the stage at which a complaint is needed for the person to plead. Regulation 17 requires a complaint to be lodged within 7 days after the complaint is made, while regulation 6 prescribes that a complaint must be in Form 1 and, except for a complaint by a public officer, sworn before a justice. The source’s doctrinal argument is that the written complaint is not yet “made” at the time of the initial oral information; rather, it need only be made when a formal complaint is required for the defendant to plead, at which point it must conform with reg 6. \[REVIEW]



block\_005



Joinder

One Matter of Complaint — Joinder of Defendants in Summary Complaints



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Joinder of different defendants in summary complaint]

\[CONCEPTS: joinder of defendants, summary complaint, no statutory bar, lack of consent not bar, one matter of complaint, procedural joinder]



The source states that there is no statutory bar to different defendants being heard together in summary complaint proceedings merely because they are not accessories, and that lack of consent is not itself a bar. The rule extracted is that joint hearing of different defendants in summary proceedings is a matter for legal argument and fairness, not an automatically prohibited procedural arrangement.



block\_005



Authority — Norman Brinkman and Fishpak Pty Ltd v Martin Stephen Dix and P J A Wright \[1997] TASSC 50; (1997) 6 Tas R 460



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Norman Brinkman and Fishpak Pty Ltd v Martin Stephen Dix and P J A Wright \[1997] TASSC 50; (1997) 6 Tas R 460]

\[TOPIC: No statutory bar to different defendants being heard together]

\[CONCEPTS: joint hearing, different defendants, no statutory bar, summary complaint, consent not determinative, Tasmanian Supreme Court]



Norman Brinkman and Fishpak Pty Ltd v Martin Stephen Dix and P J A Wright \[1997] TASSC 50; (1997) 6 Tas R 460 is cited in the source for the proposition that there is no statutory bar to different defendants being heard together where they are not accessories, and that absence of consent does not itself prevent joinder. The case is therefore used as authority that the question is one of legal and forensic argument rather than categorical prohibition.



block\_005



Rule Against Duplicity — Practical Approach to What Constitutes One Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: Practical application of rule against duplicity]

\[CONCEPTS: duplicity, one offence per count, same transaction, criminal enterprise, connected acts, practical approach, common purpose]



The rule against duplicity requires that only one offence be charged in a count, but the source records Lord Diplock’s explanation that the rule has historically been applied in a practical rather than strictly analytical way. Where acts of a similar nature committed by one or more defendants are connected in time, place, or common purpose so that they can fairly be regarded as forming part of the same transaction or criminal enterprise, they may be charged together. The principle therefore looks to practical connectedness rather than formal segmentation of every act.



block\_005



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 311 — Joinder of Multiple Crimes in One Indictment



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 311]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 311]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Joinder of charges in indictment]

\[CONCEPTS: same facts, closely related facts, series of crimes, same or similar character, indictment joinder, sufficient correlation, multiple counts]



Section 311 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) permits charges of more than one crime to be joined in the same indictment if they arise substantially out of the same facts or closely related facts, or are, or form part of, a series of crimes of the same or similar character. In any other case, the indictment must charge one crime only. The source emphasises that the second limb requires more than legal similarity: there must also be a nexus or sufficient correlation enabling the offences to be described as a series without straining the word beyond its reasonable meaning. Relevant considerations include time, place, and other surrounding circumstances as well as legal character. The source also notes that the provision should not be given an unduly restrictive meaning.



block\_005



Authority — Tasmania v Oakley \[2015] TASSC 12



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Oakley \[2015] TASSC 12]

\[TOPIC: Meaning of “series” and nexus requirement under Criminal Code s 311]

\[CONCEPTS: series of offences, nexus, similarity, correlation, factual setting, not disparate, time place circumstances]



Tasmania v Oakley \[2015] TASSC 12 is cited in the source for Porter J’s explanation that under the second limb of Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 311, it is not enough that charges be of the same or similar character; they must also be, or form part of, a series. To qualify as a series there must be a nexus between the offences, a feature of similarity or connection, and sufficient correlation in time, place, and surrounding circumstances. The source also records the caution that the provision should not be construed unduly restrictively.



block\_005



Joint Trial of Multiple Accused — Prima Facie Rule and Fair Trial Qualification



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 363]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 363]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Joint trial and separate trial discretion]

\[CONCEPTS: joint trial, same indictment, separate trial, prima facie rule, fair trial, positive injustice, severance]



The source states that under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 363, where two or more persons are charged in the same indictment, the judge may direct separate trials. It further records the law as clear that the prima facie rule is that accused persons jointly charged with committing the same offence should be tried jointly. The qualification is the right to a fair trial: the prima facie rule will be departed from where there is a real risk of positive injustice to an accused if the matter proceeds jointly. The doctrine therefore begins from joint trial as the default, but treats severance as available where fairness requires it.



block\_005



Alternative Convictions

Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 72B — Alternative Convictions Where Indictable Code Offence Heard Summarily



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 72B]

\[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 72B]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Alternative conviction in summary hearing of indictable code offence]

\[CONCEPTS: alternative conviction, indictable offence heard summarily, code offence, lesser alternative, summary jurisdiction, facts do not substantiate primary charge]



Section 72B of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas) applies where an indictable Code offence is heard summarily under the Justices Act. The source states that if the Criminal Code provides for an alternative conviction where the facts do not substantiate the primary charge, s 72B makes that alternative conviction available in the summary hearing as well. The rule therefore preserves Code-based alternative conviction mechanisms when indictable offences are dealt with summarily, rather than confining them to indictment procedure.



block\_005



Prosecutorial Role and Ethical Function

Prosecutorial Duty as Minister of Justice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: Prosecutor as minister of justice]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecutorial duty, minister of justice, fairness in criminal trial, role of prosecutor, impartial prosecution, justice not conviction, ethical prosecution, Crown responsibility]



A prosecutor in criminal proceedings acts as a minister of justice rather than a partisan advocate seeking victory. The defining principle of prosecutorial conduct is that the objective of criminal proceedings is the administration of justice, not the securing of a conviction.



Courts have repeatedly emphasised that prosecutors represent the authority of the State. As a result, their conduct carries significant weight and legitimacy. Because of this institutional authority, prosecutors must exercise heightened restraint and fairness in presenting their case.



The prosecutor’s role therefore involves two core obligations:



Present the Crown case with earnestness and vigour, ensuring that all admissible and relevant evidence supporting the prosecution is placed before the court.



Avoid improper methods or unfair tactics that could lead to a wrongful conviction.



The role excludes any notion that the prosecution is a contest to be won or lost. Instead, prosecutors assist the court by presenting credible evidence relevant to the alleged offence while ensuring that the accused receives a fair trial.



This ethical framework recognises the structural imbalance between the State and the accused. Prosecutors possess significant powers, including the ability to determine charges and select evidence. The doctrine that prosecutors are “ministers of justice” functions as a safeguard against misuse of this authority and promotes public confidence in the legitimacy of the criminal justice system.



Prosecutorial Decision to Prosecute

Reasonable Prospect of Conviction Test



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 310(4)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Threshold for instituting or continuing prosecution]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable prospect of conviction, evidentiary sufficiency, prosecutorial discretion, admissible evidence test, criminal prosecution threshold, charging decision, prosecution assessment]



Before instituting or continuing a criminal prosecution, prosecutors must determine whether there is a reasonable prospect of conviction. This evidentiary threshold ensures that prosecutions proceed only where the available evidence is sufficiently strong to justify the use of the criminal process.



Under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 310(4), a prosecution should not be commenced or maintained unless there is admissible, substantial, and reliable evidence indicating that:



A criminal offence known to law has been committed; and



The offence was committed by an identifiable person.



The reasonable prospect of conviction test requires an objective and dispassionate assessment of the available evidence. The purpose of the test is twofold:



To reduce the risk of prosecuting innocent persons; and



To prevent the unnecessary expenditure of public resources on prosecutions that cannot realistically succeed.



When applying this test, prosecutors must consider matters affecting the admissibility and reliability of evidence. Relevant considerations include:



Whether evidence may be excluded under evidentiary rules.



Whether admissions by the accused may be unreliable due to age, intelligence, or understanding.



Whether witnesses appear unreliable, hostile, biased, or influenced.



Whether identification evidence is strong and reliable.



Whether necessary witnesses are competent and available to give evidence under the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas).



Only after concluding that the evidentiary threshold is satisfied should a prosecutor proceed to consider whether prosecution is required in the public interest.



Public Interest Test in Prosecutorial Decision-Making



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Public interest considerations in prosecutorial discretion]

\[CONCEPTS: public interest prosecution test, prosecutorial discretion, seriousness of offence, deterrence, mitigating factors, prosecution policy, criminal justice discretion]



Even where there is a reasonable prospect of conviction, prosecutors must determine whether the public interest requires the prosecution to proceed. The existence of sufficient evidence alone does not mandate prosecution.



The public interest assessment requires consideration of the provable facts and the surrounding circumstances of the case. Prosecutors must recognise that the criminal justice system should not automatically pursue every technically provable offence.



Relevant public interest factors include:



Seriousness of the alleged offence or whether it is trivial or merely technical.



Mitigating or aggravating circumstances associated with the conduct.



The age, health, intelligence, or mental condition of the alleged offender.



The offender’s antecedents and background.



The staleness of the alleged offence or delay in prosecution.



The degree of culpability of the alleged offender.



The availability of alternatives to prosecution.



The need for deterrence, both general and specific.



The likely sentencing outcome, including whether conviction would produce unduly harsh consequences.



The attitude of the victim, including whether forcing testimony may harm the victim.



The cost and length of trial relative to the seriousness of the offence.



The risk to the public if prosecution does not proceed.



Generally, the more serious the offence, the more likely it is that the public interest will favour prosecution. However, in less serious matters prosecutors may properly decide that justice is better served through alternatives to prosecution or discontinuance of proceedings.



Disclosure Obligations

Prosecution Duty of Disclosure



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Crown disclosure obligations in criminal proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: disclosure duty, exculpatory evidence, fairness to accused, prosecution disclosure, material weakening Crown case, criminal procedure fairness]



The prosecution has a duty to disclose material in its possession that may undermine the Crown case or assist the defence. This obligation arises from the overarching requirement that criminal trials be conducted fairly.



Although the prosecution is not required to call every piece of evidence that might weaken its case, fairness requires that such material be disclosed to the defence so that the accused can properly challenge the prosecution case.



The duty of disclosure exists because criminal proceedings are not adversarial contests in which the State seeks victory at any cost. Rather, the prosecution must assist the court in achieving justice by ensuring that all relevant and potentially exculpatory information is available to the defence.



Failure to disclose material capable of undermining the prosecution case has historically contributed to miscarriages of justice. Consequently, courts emphasise that the guiding principle governing disclosure is full and fair disclosure of relevant material.



Authority — R v Brown \[1998] AC 367



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Brown \[1998] AC 367]

\[TOPIC: Crown obligation to disclose material undermining its case]

\[CONCEPTS: disclosure duty, fairness in criminal trials, prosecution disclosure obligations, exculpatory material, reliability of witnesses, defence rights]



The principle governing prosecutorial disclosure was articulated by Lord Hope in R v Brown \[1998] AC 367, a statement later adopted by Kirby J in (2005) 224 CLR 125 as accurately reflecting Australian law.



The case establishes that the prosecution is not obliged to call evidence that may undermine its case, but fairness requires that any material in the prosecution’s possession which may undermine the Crown case be disclosed to the defence.



Similarly, if the prosecution chooses to call particular witnesses, it must disclose material that could cast doubt on the credibility or reliability of those witnesses. The obligation is therefore not limited to exculpatory evidence directly supporting the defence, but also extends to material that could affect the assessment of prosecution witnesses.



This rule reinforces the central objective of criminal procedure: ensuring that the accused receives a fair trial by preventing the suppression of evidence relevant to the truth-finding process.



Authority — R v H \[2004] 1 All ER 1269



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v H \[2004] 1 All ER 1269]

\[TOPIC: Golden rule of prosecution disclosure]

\[CONCEPTS: disclosure obligation, prosecution fairness, material weakening Crown case, miscarriages of justice, criminal disclosure rule]



In R v H \[2004] 1 All ER 1269, Lord Bingham emphasised that fairness in criminal trials requires disclosure of any material held by the prosecution that either weakens the prosecution case or strengthens the defence case.



The judgment highlighted that historical miscarriages of justice frequently resulted from failures by prosecutors to disclose such material. For this reason, the court articulated what has been described as the “golden rule” of disclosure:



All material capable of weakening the Crown case or strengthening the defence case should be disclosed to the defence unless there is a lawful basis for withholding it.



This principle reinforces the fundamental procedural safeguard that the defence must be given a fair opportunity to test the prosecution case and present its own case effectively.



Authority — Robertson v Reardon \[2025] TASSC 21



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Robertson v Reardon \[2025] TASSC 21]

\[TOPIC: Disclosure necessary to present complete narrative]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecution disclosure, narrative evidence, incomplete evidence presentation, alibi defence, fairness in criminal trials]



In Robertson v Reardon \[2025] TASSC 21, the Supreme Court of Tasmania emphasised that the prosecution must disclose evidence necessary to present the full narrative of the events forming the basis of the prosecution case.



The case involved charges of evading police and drug driving in which the identity of the driver and an alibi defence were central issues. Evidence existed that arguably supported aspects of the defendant’s account but had not been disclosed or played in court.



The Court held that the evidence in question was necessary to unfold the narrative and provide a complete account of the relevant events. The failure to disclose or present such material raised concerns about the fairness of the trial process.



The case illustrates that disclosure obligations extend beyond purely exculpatory evidence. Material that helps contextualise events or clarify disputed issues may also need to be disclosed to ensure that the jury receives an accurate and complete understanding of the prosecution case.



Witnesses — Prosecutorial Obligation

Model Rules of Professional Conduct and Practice r 20.7 — Duty to Call Witnesses Necessary to Present the Whole Picture



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Prosecutor’s duty to call necessary witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: call all material witnesses, whole picture, prosecution witnesses, admissible evidence, necessary witness, committal witness, witness statements, fairness to accused, complete narrative, Crown case]



A prosecutor must call as part of the prosecution case all witnesses whose evidence is necessary to present the whole picture, subject to limited exceptions. The source material identifies Model Rules of Professional Conduct and Practice r 20.7 as requiring the prosecutor to call witnesses whose testimony is admissible and necessary to the presentation of the case.



The rule extends to witnesses:



Whose testimony is admissible and necessary for presentation of the whole picture.



Whose testimony could provide admissible evidence relevant to a matter in issue.



Whose testimony or statements were used in committal proceedings.



From whom statements were obtained in preparation or conduct of the prosecution case.



The prosecutor may decline to call such a witness only where:



The opponent consents.



The issue is covered by an admission made on behalf of the accused.



The prosecutor reasonably believes the administration of justice would be harmed by calling further witnesses on a point already adequately established.



The rule also recognises that a prosecutor is not obliged to call a witness whose testimony is plainly unreliable because the witness is “in the camp of the accused”. If a prosecutor decides not to call a witness under an available exception, the prosecutor must inform the opponent of the witness’s identity and the grounds for that decision. If the opponent requests that such a witness be called so as to permit cross-examination, the prosecutor must call the witness.



Tasmanian DPP Guidelines — Duty to Call Witnesses Necessary to Present the Whole Picture



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: DPP guideline on calling material witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: DPP witness guideline, necessary witness, whole picture, admissible and available evidence, inconsistent witness, objective lack of credibility, written reasons, witness available to defence]



The Tasmanian DPP Guidelines state that prosecutors must call all witnesses whose testimony is necessary for the presentation of the whole picture, so far as that picture can be presented by admissible and available evidence. The duty is framed as one of fairness and completeness, not strategic advantage.



The source makes clear that the fact a witness gives an account inconsistent with the prosecution case is not, by itself, a sufficient reason not to call that witness. A witness should only be omitted on credibility grounds where there is clear, objective material showing that all admissible accounts by that witness lack credibility.



Where a prosecutor decides not to call a witness in these circumstances, the reasons should be:



Recorded in writing.



Placed on the file.



Communicated in substance by making the witness available to the defence where possible.



The source identifies additional practical reasons that may justify not calling a witness, including:



The issue is not disputed and has already been proved by other witnesses.



Counsel for the accused consents to the witness not being called.



The witness is unavailable.



The witness’s physical or mental health weighs against calling them.



The guideline reflects the broader principle that the prosecution must not shape the evidentiary picture by suppressing inconvenient witnesses. The focus remains on fairness to the accused and accurate presentation of the events relied on by the Crown.



block\_006



Authority — R v Apostilides (1984) 154 CLR 563



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Apostilides (1984) 154 CLR 563]

\[TOPIC: Prosecutor’s responsibility for deciding whether to call a witness]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecutor decides witnesses, trial judge cannot direct witness call, miscarriage of justice, Crown discretion, comment on missing witness, exceptional judicial calling of witness]



R v Apostilides (1984) 154 CLR 563 establishes the governing Australian principles on the prosecution’s responsibility to decide whether a person will be called as a witness for the Crown. The case does not treat the issue as an unrestricted tactical choice. It identifies the prosecutor’s responsibility as a personal judgment to be exercised consistently with the functions of the office.



The propositions extracted in the source are:



The Crown prosecutor alone bears responsibility for deciding whether a person will be called for the Crown.



The trial judge may ask the prosecutor for reasons, but is not obliged to do so and does not adjudicate the sufficiency of those reasons.



At the close of the Crown case, the judge may invite reconsideration but cannot direct the prosecutor to call a particular witness.



In summing up, the judge may comment on the apparent effect of the failure to call a person.



Save in the most exceptional circumstances, the trial judge should not personally call a witness.



A failure to call a witness only warrants setting aside a conviction where, viewed in the context of the trial as a whole, it results in a miscarriage of justice.



The case therefore preserves prosecutorial autonomy while recognising that unfair non-calling of witnesses may undermine trial fairness and the safety of the conviction.



Authority — Richardson v The Queen (1974) 131 CLR 116



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Richardson v The Queen (1974) 131 CLR 116]

\[TOPIC: Prosecutor’s fairness obligation in presenting the Crown case]

\[CONCEPTS: fairness to accused, Crown case presentation, material witness duty, prosecutor function, witness obligation, prosecution fairness]



Richardson v The Queen (1974) 131 CLR 116 supports the proposition that the prosecutor has the responsibility of ensuring that the Crown case is presented with fairness to the accused. The source material uses the case as authority for the broader doctrine that the prosecution may often be expected to call a witness as part of the case for the prosecution where fairness requires it.



The case is not presented as imposing a rigid statutory duty owed personally to the accused. Rather, it describes the functions of a Crown prosecutor. The prosecutor’s choices about witnesses must be made consistently with those functions, which include placing before the court the evidence necessary for fair determination of the charge.



The principle drawn from the source is that fairness requires the prosecution to avoid manipulating the evidentiary picture by selecting only favourable witnesses where other material witnesses are available. The prosecutor’s obligation arises from the Crown’s public role and from the requirement that criminal proceedings be conducted justly.



Richardson therefore underpins later authorities stating that the prosecution is ordinarily expected to call available material witnesses, including witnesses whose accounts may not align neatly with the prosecution theory, unless there is a proper reason not to do so.



Authority — Weissensteiner v R (1993) 178 CLR 217



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Weissensteiner v R (1993) 178 CLR 217]

\[TOPIC: Failure to call witness and effect on reasonable doubt]

\[CONCEPTS: missing witness, reasonable doubt, fairness in Crown case, expected witness, prosecution obligation, witness not called]



Weissensteiner v R (1993) 178 CLR 217, as cited in the source and approved in Howlett v Budd \[2003] TASSC 49, draws an important distinction between defence non-calling of a witness and prosecution non-calling of a witness.



The principle extracted in the source is that where the issue concerns the prosecution’s failure to call a witness whom it might reasonably have been expected to call, the question is not merely whether factual inferences may be drawn, but whether the circumstances should cause the jury to entertain a reasonable doubt about the guilt of the accused.



The case is used to reinforce two connected ideas:



The prosecutor has responsibility to ensure the Crown case is presented fairly to the accused.



Missing prosecution evidence may matter because it can affect confidence in the completeness and reliability of the Crown case.



The significance of the authority is that prosecutorial omissions are analysed through the lens of fairness and reasonable doubt, rather than only through ordinary evidentiary inference. Where an expected witness is absent without proper explanation, that absence may weaken the prosecution case in a substantive way.



General Rule — Obligation to Call Material Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: Prosecution obligation to call material witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: material witness, unfold the narrative, complete account of events, all relevant witnesses, favourable and unfavourable evidence, Crown duty, whole truth]



The prosecution must ordinarily call all material witnesses necessary to unfold the narrative and provide a complete account of the events on which the prosecution case is based. The source states this doctrine both generally and through multiple authorities.



A material witness is ordinarily one whose evidence is necessary to:



Explain the sequence of events relied on by the prosecution.



Prove an element of the offence.



Provide an eyewitness or otherwise significant account of the conduct in issue.



Present a fair and complete picture, even if aspects of the account are unhelpful to the prosecution.



The doctrine recognises that the prosecution seeks truth, not merely conviction. For that reason, the Crown must place before the court evidence that is favourable and unfavourable to its theory where the evidence is material to the issues in dispute.



The source also makes clear that the duty is not absolute. A witness need not be called where there is a proper and objectively supportable reason, such as demonstrated unreliability, unnecessary repetition, unavailability, privilege, or another recognised justification. However, inconvenience to the prosecution or inconsistency with the case theory is not enough on its own.



This doctrine is central to prosecutorial fairness because it prevents the Crown from constructing an artificially selective narrative and helps ensure that the accused is tried on the real evidentiary picture.



Authority — Whitehorn v R (1983) 152 CLR 657



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Whitehorn v R (1983) 152 CLR 657]

\[TOPIC: Definition of material witness and limits of witness selection]

\[CONCEPTS: material witness, unfold the narrative, complete account, unreliable witness, repetitive evidence, witnesses available to defence]



Whitehorn v R (1983) 152 CLR 657 is used in the source as a leading authority on what constitutes a material witness and when the prosecution may decline to call one.



The principle drawn from Dawson J’s reasons is that all available witnesses should be called whose evidence is necessary to unfold the narrative and give a complete account of the events on which the prosecution is based. This will generally include eyewitnesses to events going to proof of the charged offence, even where their accounts are inconsistent with the Crown case.



The authority also recognises two principal limits:



A prosecutor is not bound to call a witness whose evidence is judged to be unreliable, untrustworthy, or otherwise incapable of belief.



Where the number of witnesses available to prove a matter would make it unnecessarily repetitious to call them all, a selection may be made.



The source further notes that witnesses named on the indictment, presentment, or information should nevertheless be made available by the prosecution so they may be called by the defence and, if practicable, be present at court.



Whitehorn is therefore a central authority for both the breadth of the Crown’s witness-calling obligation and its limited exceptions.



Authority — Dyers v R (2002) 210 CLR 285



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Dyers v R (2002) 210 CLR 285]

\[TOPIC: Duty to present complete narrative through material witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: material witness, complete narrative, Crown witness duty, prosecution fairness, missing witness, reasonable doubt]



Dyers v R (2002) 210 CLR 285 is cited in the source for the proposition that the prosecution must call witnesses necessary to unfold the narrative of events unless there is a proper reason not to do so.



The authority supports the general rule that the Crown case should not be presented as a partial or strategically narrowed version of events where available evidence from material witnesses is necessary to explain what occurred. In that sense, the case reinforces the fairness-based obligation that the prosecution present a complete evidentiary account rather than only the parts most favourable to conviction.



In the source, Dyers sits with Whitehorn and Mahmood as authority for the proposition that the obligation to call material witnesses is fundamental to the proper conduct of a criminal trial. The significance of the authority is that absence of an expected witness may affect the reliability and completeness of the prosecution case and may bear on reasonable doubt.



Authority — Mahmood v Western Australia (2008) 232 CLR 397



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Mahmood v Western Australia (2008) 232 CLR 397]

\[TOPIC: Crown obligation to call material witnesses necessary to the prosecution narrative]

\[CONCEPTS: material witness, missing witness, Crown fairness, prosecution narrative, witness obligation, complete account]



Mahmood v Western Australia (2008) 232 CLR 397 is cited in the source as supporting the doctrine that the prosecution must call witnesses necessary to unfold the narrative of the events relied upon by the Crown.



The authority is used as part of a line of cases confirming that the prosecutor’s role is not confined to proving the minimum elements through selective evidence. Rather, fairness ordinarily requires the calling of material witnesses whose evidence is needed to provide a proper account of the relevant events.



The importance of the case in the source is doctrinal rather than factual. It reinforces that the prosecution’s witness-calling decisions are assessed against the requirement that the Crown case be complete and fair. Failure to call an expected material witness may undermine confidence in the integrity of the prosecution case, particularly where the witness could clarify contested events.



Authority — R v Soma (2003) 212 CLR 299



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Soma (2003) 212 CLR 299]

\[TOPIC: Prosecution must present favourable and unfavourable evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: whole truth, favourable and unfavourable evidence, prosecution fairness, Crown duty, complete evidentiary picture, not partisan]



R v Soma (2003) 212 CLR 299 is cited in the source for the proposition that, because the prosecution seeks the truth, it must call evidence that is both favourable and unfavourable to its case.



The authority is important because it rejects the notion that the Crown may properly shape its case by suppressing admissible material merely because it does not fit the prosecution theory. The prosecution’s role is not to construct the most persuasive partisan narrative. Its role is to present the evidence necessary for fair adjudication of the charge.



The principle supported by the case is that the Crown’s obligation to fairness may require the presentation of evidence that weakens or complicates the prosecution case, provided the evidence is material and admissible. This reflects the prosecutor’s public role and the requirement that criminal proceedings be directed toward justice.



Authority — R v Shaw (1991) 57 A Crim R 425



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Shaw (1991) 57 A Crim R 425]

\[TOPIC: Contradictory statements are not enough to justify non-calling of witness]

\[CONCEPTS: contradictory witness statements, witness inconsistency, not enough to omit witness, prosecution fairness, material witness, Crown obligation]



R v Shaw (1991) 57 A Crim R 425 is cited in the source for the proposition that the mere fact a potential witness has made contradictory statements in the past is not a sufficient reason for the prosecution to decline to call that witness.



The case supports the broader doctrine that inconsistency and unhelpfulness are not the same as objective unreliability. A prosecutor cannot avoid calling a material witness simply because the witness may prove difficult, inconsistent, or less supportive of the Crown case than desired.



The principle matters because many material witnesses in criminal cases may have imperfect recollection or prior inconsistencies. The proper question is whether there are identifiable circumstances clearly establishing that the evidence is unreliable or incapable of belief, not whether the evidence is neat or wholly favourable to the prosecution.



Authority — R v Newland (1997) 98 A Crim R 455



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Newland (1997) 98 A Crim R 455]

\[TOPIC: Identifiable unreliability required before witness may be omitted]

\[CONCEPTS: identifiable unreliability, witness incapable of belief, Crown witness decision, objective basis, not mere suspicion, material witness]



R v Newland (1997) 98 A Crim R 455 is cited in the source as authority that the prosecution may decline to call a witness where the evidence is likely to be unreliable, untrustworthy, or otherwise incapable of belief. However, the source makes clear that mere suspicion is insufficient.



The principle supported is that there must be identifiable circumstances clearly establishing unreliability before the prosecutor is justified in omitting a material witness on that basis. The prosecution cannot rely on a broad or impressionistic concern that the witness might be poor or unhelpful.



This authority therefore helps define the boundary between a proper credibility-based decision and an improper partisan choice to suppress inconvenient evidence.



Authority — R v Ashton, Farmer \& Randall \[2003] TASSC 140



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Ashton, Farmer \& Randall \[2003] TASSC 140]

\[TOPIC: Witness inconsistent with Crown case should still be called unless objectively incredible]

\[CONCEPTS: inconsistent witness, objective lack of credibility, Crown duty, whole picture, Tasmanian authority, material witness]



R v Ashton, Farmer \& Randall \[2003] TASSC 140 is cited in the source for the proposition that the mere fact a witness will give an account inconsistent with the prosecution case is not enough to justify not calling that witness.



The authority supports the Tasmanian DPP guideline that a witness should only be omitted where there is clear, objective material showing that all admissible accounts by that witness lack credibility. The standard is therefore stringent. Inconsistency with the Crown theory is not a substitute for demonstrated unreliability.



The case reinforces the underlying doctrine that the prosecution must present the whole picture fairly and cannot select only witnesses whose testimony aligns with its preferred version of events.



Credibility of Prosecution Witnesses — No General Duty to Investigate Credibility Material



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Limits on prosecution obligation to investigate witness credibility matters]

\[CONCEPTS: credibility investigation, no duty to search world, prosecution limits, disclosure boundary, witness credibility material, Crown investigation duty]



The prosecution is not under a general obligation to go out into the world and investigate matters that might reflect adversely on the credibility of prosecution witnesses. The source states this limit expressly and supports it by Tasmanian authority.



The rule distinguishes between:



Material already in the prosecution’s possession that may undermine the Crown case or assist the defence, which must ordinarily be disclosed; and



Material not known to the prosecution and not already obtained, which the prosecution is not generally required to investigate simply because it might bear on witness credibility.



This distinction prevents disclosure obligations from being converted into an unlimited investigative burden. The prosecution must act fairly with material it has or controls, but it is not ordinarily required to conduct open-ended credibility inquiries about each witness.



Authority — Tasmania v Farhat \[2017] TASSC 42



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Farhat \[2017] TASSC 42]

\[TOPIC: No obligation to investigate external credibility material]

\[CONCEPTS: witness credibility, disclosure limits, no duty to investigate, prosecution obligation, Tasmanian authority, Crown fairness]



Tasmania v Farhat \[2017] TASSC 42 is cited in the source as authority that the prosecution is not obliged to investigate external matters that might reflect upon the credibility of prosecution witnesses and then disclose those matters.



The principle supported is a limiting one. The prosecution’s fairness obligations are substantial, but they do not require the Crown to undertake boundless inquiries into any possible material that might weaken a witness. The duty is focused on disclosure of relevant material already held or obtained, not speculative investigation into all possible credibility attacks.



The authority therefore defines an outer limit to prosecutorial disclosure and preparation obligations.



Authority — Darren John Burston v David John Brook \[1996] TASSC 152



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Darren John Burston v David John Brook \[1996] TASSC 152]

\[TOPIC: Failure to call expected material witnesses may amount to inappropriate prosecution conduct]

\[CONCEPTS: material witnesses not called, no explanation given, summary prosecution, inappropriate conduct, expected witness, prosecution duty]



Darren John Burston v David John Brook \[1996] TASSC 152 is cited in the source as an example where the prosecution failed to call several witnesses who might have been expected to give evidence, including a manager and police officers, without explanation.



The source records that this failure was described as “mystifying” and as suggesting, on its face, quite inappropriate conduct of the prosecution. The case was used to support the proposition that in summary prosecutions the prosecutor has the same duties concerning the calling of witnesses as those applicable to Crown counsel in Richardson and Apostilides.



The principle drawn is that unexplained failure to call apparently material witnesses may amount to a serious departure from prosecutorial obligations, especially where those witnesses are closely connected to the sequence of events giving rise to the charge.



Authority — Martin v Visser \[2000] TASSC 173



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Martin v Visser \[2000] TASSC 173]

\[TOPIC: Limits of obligation to call every possible witness]

\[CONCEPTS: not every bystander must be called, practical limits, witness selection, materiality, summary prosecution, overcalling witnesses]



Martin v Visser \[2000] TASSC 173 is cited in the source as a contrast to cases where the prosecution failed to call genuinely material witnesses. The authority illustrates that the prosecution is not required to call every person who might conceivably have witnessed surrounding events.



The source records judicial comments rejecting the suggestion that “everybody in sight should be called as a witness”. The principle supported is that the prosecution obligation is one of materiality, not exhaustive witness collection. A witness must be called where their evidence is necessary to unfold the narrative or prove significant contested facts, but the duty does not extend to marginal, cumulative, or practically unnecessary witnesses.



The case therefore marks an outer limit on the doctrine requiring the Crown to call material witnesses.



Authority — Hrycyszyn v Bonde \[2003] TASSC 126



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Hrycyszyn v Bonde \[2003] TASSC 126]

\[TOPIC: No obligation to identify and call unknown witness where impractical]

\[CONCEPTS: unknown witness, impractical to find witness, no adverse inference, prosecutor not required to identify everyone, missing witness doctrine]



Hrycyszyn v Bonde \[2003] TASSC 126 is cited in the source for the proposition that the prosecution is under no obligation to call an unknown witness where it is impractical to identify and locate that person.



The source records that counsel conceded no duty rested on the prosecutor to call the unknown person and that no authority supported drawing an adverse inference from the failure to identify and call the person on the scant material available. The court held there was no basis for concluding that the magistrate should have dealt with the issue in reasons.



The authority supports the practical limit that the prosecution’s duty concerns available material witnesses, not hypothetical or unidentified persons whose existence or evidence is uncertain.



Good Reasons for Not Calling a Witness



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: Circumstances justifying decision not to call a witness]

\[CONCEPTS: good reason not to call witness, unavailable witness, privilege, repetitious evidence, unreliable witness, unknown evidence, objective justification]



The prosecution may decline to call a witness where there is a proper and objectively supportable reason. The source identifies several accepted categories.



A witness may properly be omitted where the evidence:



Is likely to be unreliable, untrustworthy, or otherwise incapable of belief, provided this is established by identifiable circumstances rather than mere suspicion.



Would be unnecessarily repetitious because enough witnesses are already available to prove the same matter.



Relates to an issue raised by the defence on which the defence bears the onus of proof.



Is unknown because the police did not obtain a statement and the prosecution genuinely does not know what the witness would say.



Cannot be compelled because the witness is unavailable, outside jurisdiction, deported, or protected by privilege.



Would not be reasonably obtainable in practice.



The doctrine permits sensible limits on the Crown obligation to call witnesses, but it does not authorise selective omission for strategic advantage. A good reason must be anchored in fairness, practicality, or evidentiary principle.



Authority — Gillan v Police (SA) (2004) 149 A Crim R 354



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Gillan v Police (SA) (2004) 149 A Crim R 354]

\[TOPIC: Good reason not to call witness where prosecution does not know what evidence they would give]

\[CONCEPTS: no witness statement, unknown evidence, not called witness, practical justification, prosecution knowledge]



Gillan v Police (SA) (2004) 149 A Crim R 354 is cited in the source as authority that the prosecution may have a good reason not to call a witness where it does not know what evidence the witness would give, for example because police did not obtain a statement.



The principle is practical rather than absolute. It recognises that the prosecution cannot always be expected to call a person whose evidence is genuinely unknown. However, the source also contrasts this position with later authority suggesting that passive reliance on police failure may not always be satisfactory.



Authority — Solis v The Queen \[2018] VSCA 275



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Solis v The Queen \[2018] VSCA 275]

\[TOPIC: Police failure to question relevant person may not justify non-calling]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecutor cannot remain passive, police failure, relevant person not questioned, unsatisfactory reason, witness investigation, Crown responsibility]



Solis v The Queen \[2018] VSCA 275 is referred to in the source as a contrast to decisions treating unknown witness evidence as a sufficient reason for non-calling. The source notes that the case said a failure by police to question a relevant person is not a satisfactory reason for not calling that person.



The principle extracted is that a prosecutor cannot simply remain passive and allow police investigative omissions to dictate who will or will not be called at trial. Where a relevant person is apparent, the prosecution may need to do more than rely on the fact that no statement was taken.



This authority therefore pushes back against an overbroad use of “we do not know what the witness would say” as a justification for non-calling.



Authority — R v Reardon (No 2) (2004) 60 NSWLR 454



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Reardon (No 2) (2004) 60 NSWLR 454]

\[TOPIC: Witness need not be called where not compellable]

\[CONCEPTS: non-compellable witness, inability to compel evidence, good reason not to call, prosecution limit, witness availability]



R v Reardon (No 2) (2004) 60 NSWLR 454 is cited in the source as authority that the prosecution may have a good reason for not calling a witness where the witness could not have been compelled to give evidence.



The principle is straightforward: the obligation to call material witnesses is bounded by practical and legal availability. Where the law does not permit compulsion, non-calling may be justified.



Authority — Fonseka v R (2003) 140 A Crim R 395



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Fonseka v R (2003) 140 A Crim R 395]

\[TOPIC: Witness need not be called where deported and unavailable]

\[CONCEPTS: deported witness, unavailable witness, cannot subpoena, good reason not to call, prosecution practicality]



Fonseka v R (2003) 140 A Crim R 395 is cited in the source as authority that the prosecution may have a good reason not to call a witness where the witness has been deported and cannot be subpoenaed.



The case supports the practical limit that the Crown’s obligation applies to witnesses who are realistically and legally available. Physical or legal impossibility of securing testimony may justify non-calling.



Authority — R v Kessing (2008) 73 NSWLR 22



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Kessing (2008) 73 NSWLR 22]

\[TOPIC: Witness need not be called where witness would claim privilege]

\[CONCEPTS: privilege, witness refusal, Evidence Act privilege, non-calling witness, legal impediment, prosecution justification]



R v Kessing (2008) 73 NSWLR 22 is cited in the source as authority that the prosecution may have a good reason not to call a witness where the witness has indicated that he or she would not give evidence because of a privilege under the evidence legislation.



The principle is that legal privilege may create a sufficient barrier to testimony to justify the prosecutor declining to call the witness.



Evidence Act Cross-Examination Rule and Witness Calling



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Effect of statutory cross-examination powers on witness-calling decisions]

\[CONCEPTS: cross-examine own witness, statutory leave, Evidence Act s 38, unfavourable Crown witness, cannot refuse to call because hard to cross-examine]



The source states that an earlier reason sometimes used to justify not calling a witness was the prosecution’s desire to preserve an ability to cross-examine that person, because the common law imposed restrictions on cross-examining one’s own witness. That reason has been weakened or removed by statutory reform.



Under the evidence legislation referred to in the source, s 38 gives the prosecution a greater opportunity to cross-examine its own witness. As a result, the prosecution can no longer rely as readily on anticipated hostility or inconsistency as a reason for not calling an otherwise material witness.



The doctrinal effect is that the Crown’s obligation to call material evidence is strengthened. Where a witness is material but may be unfavourable, the prosecutor may still call the witness and seek leave to cross-examine if the statutory requirements are met.



Authority — Santo v R \[2009] NSWCCA 269



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Santo v R \[2009] NSWCCA 269]

\[TOPIC: Greater statutory cross-examination powers reduce justification for non-calling witness]

\[CONCEPTS: cross-examine own witness, statutory leave, not reason to omit witness, Crown witness duty, s 38]



Santo v R \[2009] NSWCCA 269 is cited in the source for the proposition that, because statutory evidence law provides greater scope to cross-examine one’s own witness, this is no longer a reasonable basis for refusing to call a material witness.



The authority supports the view that the prosecution should ordinarily call material evidence and then use available statutory mechanisms, if necessary, to address hostility or inconsistency, rather than omitting the witness altogether.



Authority — Kanaan v R \[2006] NSWCCA 109



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Kanaan v R \[2006] NSWCCA 109]

\[TOPIC: Unjust to refuse leave to cross-examine where Crown calls witness due to duty to present material evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: leave to cross-examine, material evidence, Crown witness, s 38, unfavourable witness, prosecution fairness]



Kanaan v R \[2006] NSWCCA 109 is cited in the source as authority that it will usually be unjust to refuse the prosecution leave to cross-examine a witness where the prosecution has called that witness because of its duty to place all material evidence before the jury.



The case therefore supports the practical operation of the Crown’s witness-calling duty under modern evidence legislation.



Authority — Kneebone v R (1999) 47 NSWLR 450



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Kneebone v R (1999) 47 NSWLR 450]

\[TOPIC: Crown should call material witness despite unfavourable evidence and may then seek cross-examination]

\[CONCEPTS: unfavourable witness, cross-examination, material evidence, Crown duty, witness not called, statutory fairness]



Kneebone v R (1999) 47 NSWLR 450 is cited in the source in support of two connected propositions. First, the prosecution should not refuse to call a material witness simply because the evidence may be unfavourable. Second, where the witness is called because of the duty to present all material evidence, the Crown may be entitled to cross-examine that witness under the applicable statutory scheme.



The authority helps explain why anticipated hostility is not, without more, a sufficient basis for withholding a material witness.



Can a Prosecutor Select Witnesses to Suit the Case?



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: Prosecutor cannot selectively call witnesses to suit case theory]

\[CONCEPTS: selective witness calling, case theory manipulation, all material witnesses, fair trial, prosecution objectivity, Crown not partisan]



A prosecutor cannot properly select witnesses merely to suit the prosecution case theory. The source answers this directly: the obligation is to call all material witnesses unless there is a proper reason not to do so.



The reason for the rule is that the object of criminal procedure is a fair trial, not a polished partisan presentation. Because the evidence legislation allows some capacity to deal with unfavourable witnesses, the prosecution cannot justify omission simply because a witness may weaken or complicate the Crown case.



Selective witness-calling becomes improper where it produces an incomplete or distorted narrative of the events in issue. The Crown must therefore distinguish between:



Proper omission based on objective unreliability, repetition, privilege, or unavailability; and



Improper omission based on strategic convenience or fear of evidentiary complexity.



Authority — Police v Kyriacou \[2009] SASC 66



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v Kyriacou \[2009] SASC 66]

\[TOPIC: Limited application of Jones v Dunkel reasoning in criminal proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: Jones v Dunkel, criminal proceedings, missing witness inference, limited application, adverse inference, prosecution witness]



Police v Kyriacou \[2009] SASC 66 is cited in the source as authority that the Jones v Dunkel principle has only limited application in criminal proceedings. The source notes this in the context of arguments about what follows when the prosecution does not call an apparent witness.



The significance of the authority is that failure to call a witness in a criminal trial does not automatically justify the same kind of adverse inference commonly discussed in civil proceedings. The question is more tightly controlled by criminal trial fairness, the burden of proof, and the prosecution’s specific duties regarding material witnesses.



Authority — Randall v R; Farmer v R \[2004] TASSC 42



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Randall v R; Farmer v R \[2004] TASSC 42]

\[TOPIC: General rule against inviting jury to speculate about uncalled witness]

\[CONCEPTS: jury direction, uncalled witness, do not speculate, prosecution duty, missing witness comment, rare exception]



Randall v R; Farmer v R \[2004] TASSC 42 is cited in the source for the proposition that, as a general rule, a trial judge should not direct a jury that the prosecution would have been expected to call persons other than those it did call, nor direct that the jury may infer that absent witnesses would not have assisted the prosecution.



Instead, the general rule is that the jury should be told not to speculate about what an uncalled person might have said. Exceptions are described as rare and arise only where it is shown that the prosecution’s failure to call the person was in breach of the duty to call material witnesses.



The authority therefore places limits on the forensic use of missing witnesses before a jury while preserving a narrow exception for true prosecutorial breach.



Failure to Call a Witness — Effect on Conviction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: When non-calling of witness may affect conviction]

\[CONCEPTS: miscarriage of justice, missing material witness, appeal ground, safety of conviction, fairness of trial, witness not called]



A prosecutor’s failure to call a witness does not automatically invalidate a conviction. The source makes clear that the decisive question is whether, when the trial is viewed as a whole, the failure gave rise to a miscarriage of justice or left the case in a state where reasonable doubt should have been entertained.



This principle combines two strands in the source:



From Apostilides, a conviction is only set aside where the non-calling of the witness, viewed in the full context of the trial, results in a miscarriage of justice.



From Weissensteiner, the issue may be whether the circumstances should have caused the jury to entertain a reasonable doubt about guilt.



The rule therefore focuses not on formal witness-counting, but on the effect of the omission on trial fairness and confidence in the verdict.



Witness-Calling Responsibilities in Criminal Trials



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: prosecutorial responsibility to call witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecution duty, calling witnesses, fairness in criminal trial, adversarial trial structure, prosecution disclosure obligations, unfolding the prosecution case, witness selection, Crown prosecutor role]



A fundamental principle of criminal procedure is that the decision whether to call witnesses in a trial is primarily a matter for the parties rather than the trial judge. The Crown prosecutor’s role is not that of a neutral investigator but rather a participant in the adversarial process whose function is to present the prosecution case fairly while assisting the court in achieving justice.



In Whitehorn v R \[1983] HCA 42; (1983) 152 CLR 657, the High Court explained that a Crown prosecutor must act fairly in the discharge of prosecutorial duties. The prosecutor’s responsibility is to assist the court in attaining justice between the Crown and the accused. However, this obligation does not transform the prosecutor into a detached or disinterested participant.



The adversarial structure of criminal trials means:



The Crown and the accused determine the evidence they will call.



Each party determines what questions are asked in examination-in-chief and cross-examination.



The judge ensures fairness and legality but does not assume the role of either party.



This principle reflects the broader characterisation of criminal proceedings articulated in Ratten v The Queen (1974) 131 CLR 510, which emphasised that criminal proceedings are a “trial, not an inquisition”.



A judge may invite the prosecution to reconsider whether a particular witness should be called if the judge believes that witness is necessary for a fair trial. However, the judge must not assume the role of the prosecution by calling the witness personally or conducting the case on the Crown’s behalf.



Authority — Whitehorn v R



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Whitehorn v R \[1983] HCA 42; (1983) 152 CLR 657]

\[TOPIC: limits on judicial involvement in calling witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: adversarial trial, judicial neutrality, Crown prosecutor duty, trial fairness, calling witnesses, judge role in criminal trial]



The High Court in Whitehorn v R \[1983] HCA 42 confirmed that although a trial judge must ensure fairness in criminal proceedings, the judge cannot assume responsibilities that properly belong to the parties.



The Court held that the decision whether to call a witness is a matter for the prosecution or the defence. If a judge believes that a witness should be called, the appropriate course is to invite the prosecution to reconsider that decision. If the prosecution refuses and the judge remains concerned, the judge may comment on the failure to call the witness.



However, the judge must not usurp the role of the parties by directly conducting the case or calling witnesses independently. Such conduct would undermine the adversarial structure of the criminal justice system.



Failure of the Prosecution to Call Expected Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: consequences of failing to call relevant witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: failure to call witness, prosecution obligation, unfolding the prosecution case, adverse inference, fairness of trial, essential witness evidence]



Where the prosecution fails to call a witness whose evidence appears essential to the presentation of its case, the court may scrutinise that omission when assessing the safety of the verdict.



In Darren John Burston v David John Brooks \[1996] TASSC 152, the Supreme Court of Tasmania considered the implications of a prosecutor failing to call witnesses who would ordinarily be expected to give evidence. The court noted that prosecutors have duties similar to those described in Richardson v R (1974) 131 CLR 116 and R v Apostolides (1984) 154 CLR 563 regarding the calling of witnesses.



These duties include ensuring that the evidence necessary to present the prosecution case fully is placed before the court. If witnesses central to the narrative of the prosecution case are not called and no adequate explanation is provided, the failure may raise concerns about the reliability or completeness of the prosecution case.



The absence of such evidence may also interact with principles similar to the inference discussed in Jones v Dunkel (1959) 101 CLR 298, although the application of that rule in criminal proceedings is constrained because of the special duties imposed on prosecutors.



Ultimately, the critical question is whether the omission contributes to the conclusion that the verdict may be unsafe or unsatisfactory.



Authority — Burston v Brooks



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Darren John Burston v David John Brooks \[1996] TASSC 152]

\[TOPIC: consequences of failing to call expected witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecution witness duty, missing witness inference, unfolding the prosecution case, unsafe verdict, evidentiary omission]



In Burston v Brooks \[1996] TASSC 152, the Supreme Court of Tasmania considered the significance of a prosecutor’s failure to call witnesses whose evidence appeared central to the prosecution narrative.



The Court held that where the prosecution fails to call a witness whose evidence is necessary to properly unfold the case, the court must consider whether the absence of that evidence undermines confidence in the verdict.



This approach reflects principles identified in R v Apostolides (1984) 154 CLR 563, which emphasise that prosecutors should call all material witnesses necessary to present the case fairly unless there are valid reasons not to do so.



Prosecutorial Duty to Call Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: professional rules governing prosecutorial witness selection]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecutorial ethics, witness obligations, prosecution disclosure, calling witnesses rule, fairness in prosecution]



Professional conduct rules impose obligations on prosecutors to call witnesses necessary to present the prosecution case fairly.



Under Model Rules of Professional Conduct and Practice rule 20.7, a prosecutor must call witnesses as part of the prosecution case where their evidence:



Is admissible and necessary to present the full picture of the case.



Provides admissible evidence relevant to matters in issue.



Was relied upon during committal proceedings.



Was obtained during preparation of the prosecution case.



The rule recognises that prosecutors must ensure the court receives a complete and accurate account of the relevant facts.



However, the rules also recognise practical limitations. A prosecutor may decline to call a witness where:



the defence consents to the witness not being called;



the evidence is already admitted through formal admissions; or



calling the witness would harm the administration of justice because the point is adequately established by other evidence.



Where the prosecutor decides not to call such a witness, the defence must be informed of the decision and the reasons for it.



Additionally, if the prosecution refuses to call a witness on the ground that the witness is unreliable or aligned with the accused, the defence may request that the witness still be called for cross-examination.



Legal Professional Privilege — Dominant Purpose Test



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]
