\[TOPIC: legal professional privilege]

\[CONCEPTS: dominant purpose test, confidential communication, legal advice privilege, litigation privilege, lawyer client confidentiality, privileged documents]



Legal professional privilege protects confidential communications made for the dominant purpose of obtaining legal advice or for use in actual or anticipated litigation.



For privilege to apply, two key elements must be established:



The communication must be confidential.



The dominant purpose of the communication must be either obtaining legal advice or preparing for litigation.



Privilege may apply to communications between lawyers and clients as well as to documents created by lawyers, clients, or third parties where the dominant purpose requirement is satisfied.



Australian law recognises two principal categories of privilege:



Legal advice privilege – protecting confidential communications made for the purpose of obtaining legal advice.



Litigation privilege – protecting communications prepared for the dominant purpose of existing or anticipated legal proceedings.



The burden of establishing privilege lies with the party asserting it. If a document would have existed regardless of the intention to obtain legal advice, it will not ordinarily attract privilege.



Privilege can apply to communications even where they do not pass directly between lawyer and client. For example, notes made by a client recording advice given by a lawyer may be privileged.



However, documents created independently and later provided to a lawyer for advice do not become privileged merely because they are supplied to legal counsel.



Waiver of Legal Professional Privilege



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: waiver of privilege]

\[CONCEPTS: waiver of privilege, implied waiver, inconsistent conduct, disclosure of legal advice, confidentiality loss]



Legal professional privilege may be lost through waiver, which occurs when the privilege holder acts inconsistently with the maintenance of confidentiality.



Waiver may occur in two forms:



Express waiver – where the privilege holder intentionally discloses privileged material.



Implied waiver – where conduct is inconsistent with maintaining confidentiality even if waiver was not intended.



Courts assess whether it would be unfair or misleading to permit a party to maintain privilege after acting inconsistently with the confidential nature of the communication.



Examples of conduct that may constitute waiver include:



disclosing privileged advice to third parties;



relying on legal advice to justify conduct in litigation;



voluntarily producing privileged documents in discovery.



However, simply stating that legal advice exists does not necessarily waive privilege.



In some circumstances, inadvertent disclosure will not constitute waiver provided the party who disclosed the material acts promptly to rectify the mistake.



Crime–Fraud Exception to Legal Professional Privilege



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: crime or fraud exception to privilege]

\[CONCEPTS: illegal purpose exception, fraud exception, privilege limitation, lawyer client communications, unlawful conduct]



Legal professional privilege does not protect communications made for the purpose of facilitating illegal or improper conduct.



This exception applies even where the lawyer involved was unaware of the unlawful purpose.



In Aucare Dairy Pty Ltd v Huang \[2017] FCA 746, the Federal Court held that privilege could not be claimed over communications where there was a strong prima facie case that the communications were connected to fraudulent conduct.



The court emphasised that privilege is intended to protect legitimate legal advice rather than shield wrongdoing. Accordingly, where communications are made to further illegal activity, privilege does not apply.



Charge Negotiation (Plea Bargaining)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: prosecution approach to charge negotiation]

\[CONCEPTS: plea bargaining, charge negotiation, prosecution discretion, appropriate sentence basis, evidence sufficiency]



Charge negotiation refers to discussions between prosecution and defence regarding whether the accused may plead guilty to alternative charges.



According to prosecutorial guidelines, a proposal for charge negotiation should only be considered where:



The proposed charges reflect the nature and seriousness of the accused’s conduct.



The charges provide an adequate basis for an appropriate sentence.



There is sufficient evidence to support those charges.



When assessing whether to accept or propose variations to charges, prosecutors may consider factors including:



the accused’s cooperation with investigators;



the likely sentence if charges are varied;



the strength of the prosecution case;



the interests of victims and witnesses;



the desirability of resolving the case promptly;



the accused’s criminal history.



However, the prosecution must not accept a plea to charges if the accused maintains innocence regarding the charges to which they propose to plead guilty.



Apprehended Judicial Bias



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: apprehended bias test]

\[CONCEPTS: judicial bias, reasonable apprehension of bias, fair minded observer test, impartial tribunal, natural justice]



The doctrine of apprehended bias ensures that judicial decisions are made by an impartial tribunal.



The governing test was articulated in Livesey v NSW Bar Association (1983) 151 CLR 288, which states that a judicial officer should not hear a matter if a fair-minded observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial and unprejudiced mind to the determination of the issues.



The test is objective and focuses on the perspective of an informed, reasonable observer.



In Baker v Barratt \[2019] TASSC 28, the Supreme Court of Tasmania emphasised that apprehended bias concerns the appearance of pre-judgment, not merely predisposition.



A decision-maker is considered biased where they are so committed to a particular conclusion that they cannot be persuaded by evidence or argument.



This distinction reflects the explanation given in Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs v Jia Legeng \[2001] HCA 17, which clarified that predisposition or inclination does not necessarily constitute bias.



Apparent Bias as Denial of Natural Justice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: apparent bias and error of law]

\[CONCEPTS: apparent bias, natural justice breach, judicial impartiality, procedural fairness, error of law]



Apparent bias constitutes a denial of natural justice and may amount to an error of law.



In Kirkland v Tippett \[2000] TASSC 94, the Supreme Court of Tasmania held that where a fair-minded observer would reasonably apprehend that a judicial officer might not approach a case with impartiality, the proceedings may be invalid.



The Court noted that the appearance of bias is sufficient; actual bias need not be proven.



Similarly, Schreuder v Australian Securities Commission (1996) 6 TAS R 223 recognised that apparent bias may constitute an error of law reviewable under statutory appeal provisions such as Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 107(4).



Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas) — Prohibition on Recording Conversations



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas) s 5(1)(b)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: recording private conversations]

\[CONCEPTS: covert recording, listening device offence, private conversation recording, unlawful recording, surveillance device law]



Section 5(1)(b) of the Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas) prohibits a person from using a listening device to record a private conversation to which they are a party.



A recording made in breach of this provision may be inadmissible in evidence under Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas) s 14(1).



However, the Act recognises exceptions to the prohibition.



Lawful Interests Exception to Recording Prohibition



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas) s 5(3)(b)(i)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[TOPIC: lawful interests exception to covert recordings]

\[CONCEPTS: lawful interests defence, covert recording exception, protecting interests, self protection recording, family violence evidence]



Section 5(3)(b)(i) of the Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas) provides a defence where the use of a listening device was reasonably necessary for the protection of the lawful interests of the person making the recording.



Courts have interpreted “lawful interests” broadly to include protection from harm, danger or wrongdoing.



In Sepulveda v The Queen \[2006] NSWCCA 379, the court explained that protection of lawful interests may include defensive recording of conversations relating to criminal conduct.



In Moore v Harrison \[2018] TASMC, the court accepted that recording conversations to protect victims from family violence could fall within the lawful interests exception, particularly where recordings provide reliable evidence of otherwise private misconduct.



The determination of whether recording was “reasonably necessary” involves balancing factors such as:



the immediacy of the threat;



the availability of lawful alternatives such as warrants;



the importance of preserving reliable evidence.



Absence of Evidence Not Necessarily Determinative



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: absence of corroborating evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: absence of evidence, police recollection, probative weight, credibility assessment, evidentiary evaluation]



The absence of particular evidence, including the absence of recollection by police officers, does not necessarily undermine a complainant’s account.



In Shepperd v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 12, the Supreme Court of Tasmania held that police officers failing to observe injuries did not necessarily indicate that the injuries did not exist.



The court emphasised that police attending incidents may not always conduct detailed forensic observations, particularly where their immediate role is responding to an incident rather than collecting evidence for trial.



Failure to Record Reasons in Police Notes



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: evidentiary significance of incomplete police notes]

\[CONCEPTS: police notes, contemporaneous records, omissions in statement, evidentiary reliability]



A police officer’s failure to record certain matters in contemporaneous notes or statements does not necessarily invalidate the legality of police conduct.



In Moore v Knowles \[2017] TASMC 12, the court considered a challenge to the legality of a vehicle search where the officer had omitted to record the basis of the belief justifying the search.



The court accepted that officers may omit such details because they are engaged with the suspect and the immediate conduct of the search rather than documenting their reasoning.



Telecommunications Location Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of mobile phone tower data]

\[CONCEPTS: telecommunications evidence, cell tower data, circumstantial evidence, location tracking, probative value]



Telecommunications location evidence may be admissible as circumstantial evidence linking an accused to the vicinity of a crime.



In Wagner (a pseudonym) v The King \[2025] VSCA 56, the Victorian Court of Appeal considered evidence derived from mobile phone tower data indicating that the accused’s phone was located near several burglary and arson sites.



The Court held that the probative value of such evidence was not outweighed by unfair prejudice. Limitations in the precision of location data could be explored through cross-examination and addressed through judicial directions to the jury.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106 — Rebuttal Evidence Affecting Witness Credibility



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 106]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106 — Credibility Rebuttal Evidence]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: rebuttal evidence attacking witness credibility]

\[CONCEPTS: collateral evidence, witness credibility, rebuttal evidence, cross-examination denial, evidence of dishonesty, motive to lie, contradiction evidence, impeachment of witness]



Section 106 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) permits the admission of evidence that would traditionally have been characterised as collateral evidence where that evidence bears upon the credibility of a witness. The provision modifies the common law rule that extrinsic evidence cannot be led merely to contradict a witness on matters going solely to credit.



The key procedural requirement is that the evidence must be introduced as rebuttal evidence. This means the substance of the contradictory allegation must first be put to the witness in cross-examination. If the witness denies the allegation or fails to admit the substance of it, evidence contradicting the witness may then be adduced.



Evidence admissible under s 106 may include evidence tending to prove that a witness knowingly made a false representation while under an obligation to tell the truth. Importantly, the statute does not require the evidence itself to conclusively prove dishonesty. It is sufficient if the evidence “tends to prove” that the witness knowingly made a false statement.



The practical operation of the section therefore involves three stages:



The contradictory matter must be put to the witness in cross-examination.



The witness must deny or fail to accept the substance of the allegation.



Rebuttal evidence may then be led to contradict the witness and undermine credibility.



Section 106 significantly expands the circumstances in which credibility-related evidence may be admitted compared with the strict common law collateral rule.



Authority — Admission of Recorded Prison Calls as Rebuttal Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v Endlay \[2020] TASMC 13]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of recorded communications to contradict witness testimony]

\[CONCEPTS: prison telephone recordings, rebuttal evidence, credibility attack, circumstantial proof of dishonesty, witness lying under oath, s 106 Evidence Act, motive to lie]



In Police v Endlay \[2020] TASMC 13 the Magistrates Court confirmed that recorded prison telephone calls could be admitted under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106 as rebuttal evidence going to the credibility of a witness.



The prosecution relied on recordings of prison telephone calls between the accused and his father. The recordings revealed instructions from the accused to persuade the complainant to withdraw her allegations and provide a statutory declaration denying any assault.



The complainant denied during evidence that she had been influenced by the accused or had fabricated her allegations. After those denials, the prosecution sought to rely on the recorded calls as rebuttal evidence under s 106.



The Court held that the recordings were admissible because they tended to prove that the complainant knowingly gave false evidence. The Court emphasised that the statutory threshold was not proof of dishonesty but evidence that tends to prove a knowingly false representation.



The admissibility decision relied on circumstantial reasoning including:



Frequent telephone contact between the complainant and the accused’s father.



Discussions about the allegations during those calls.



The accused communicating instructions to his father during prison calls.



The complainant subsequently acting in ways consistent with those instructions.



Taken collectively, these circumstances permitted the inference that the complainant had knowingly provided false evidence, justifying admission of the recordings under s 106(2)(e).



Police Body-Worn Cameras — Statutory Authorisation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Powers (Surveillance Devices) Act 2006 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Police Powers (Surveillance Devices) Act 2006 (Tas)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: police use of personal body-worn cameras]

\[CONCEPTS: police body cameras, audio-visual recording, surveillance devices, police evidence recording, protected information, police powers legislation, body-worn video evidence]



The Surveillance Legislation Amendments (Personal Police Cameras) Act 2018 amended both the Listening Devices Act 1991 (Tas) and the Police Powers (Surveillance Devices) Act 2006 (Tas) to expressly authorise the use of personal cameras by police officers.



The amendments allow police officers to record audio and visual material using cameras worn on the body or otherwise carried during the course of policing duties. These amendments removed earlier legislative limitations that had previously constrained the use of such recording devices.



Members of the public may object to being recorded; however, police officers retain discretion to continue the recording where it is lawful and operationally necessary in the circumstances.



Police officers who are in uniform are not legally required to inform members of the public that they are being recorded, although providing such notice is often considered best practice.



The amendments also introduced a regulatory framework governing the handling of information captured through police body-worn cameras. Recordings are classified as “protected information”, and the legislation creates offences relating to the improper release or publication of that information.



Body-worn camera footage remains subject to standard disclosure and Right to Information processes. However, the statutory framework imposes additional safeguards to prevent misuse or unauthorised dissemination of recorded police interactions.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 69 — Admissibility of Emergency Call Recordings as Business Records



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 69]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 69]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: emergency call recordings as business records]

\[CONCEPTS: 000 recordings, emergency dispatch records, business record exception, hearsay exception, recorded representations, ambulance service records, call centre recordings]



Recordings of emergency “000” calls may be admitted in criminal proceedings as business records under s 69 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas).



Investigating officers commonly give evidence explaining how the recording was obtained. This usually involves identifying the call entry within the relevant police or emergency services database, making a formal request for the recording, obtaining access to the file, and preserving it for evidentiary use.



The business record exception to the hearsay rule applies where:



The document forms part of the records belonging to or kept by a business.



The representation recorded in the document was made in the course of, or for the purposes of, that business.



Emergency service organisations, including ambulance and dispatch services, constitute “businesses” for the purposes of the Evidence Act.



A voice recording qualifies as a “document” under the statutory definition, meaning that audio recordings of emergency calls can fall within the scope of the business records exception where they were created and maintained in the course of emergency service operations.



Authority — Emergency Call Recording as Business Record



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Dolega \[2016] TASSC 65]

\[TOPIC: emergency service recordings as business records]

\[CONCEPTS: ambulance service records, recorded representations, hearsay exception, business record evidence, emergency call recording, documentary evidence]



In Tasmania v Dolega \[2016] TASSC 65 the Supreme Court confirmed that a recording of an emergency service call may constitute a business record for the purposes of Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 69.



The Court held that a voice recording is capable of falling within the statutory definition of a “document”. The ambulance service responsible for the recording qualified as a “business” within the meaning of the Act.



Because the recording formed part of the records kept by the ambulance service in the course of its operations, and the representation contained within the recording was made for the purposes of that business, the requirements of s 69(1) were satisfied.



The decision confirms that emergency call recordings can be admitted under the business records exception to the hearsay rule where the foundational requirements concerning business record status are established.



Social Media Evidence — Authentication and Admissibility



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of social media communications]

\[CONCEPTS: Facebook evidence, online messages, digital evidence authentication, social media admissions, identity of account holder, electronic communications evidence, screenshot evidence]



Social media material may be admitted as evidence in criminal proceedings where the usual evidentiary requirements are satisfied. Such material can be relied upon in several ways, including as:



direct evidence of an offence,



admissions by an accused person, or



identification or relationship evidence connecting parties.



Obtaining records directly from social media companies may be difficult where the relevant data is held overseas. For example, Facebook records are generally maintained by the company’s United States entity and cannot readily be obtained through Australian subpoenas.



A common evidentiary approach is to tender screenshots of the relevant messages or posts. These screenshots are usually incorporated into the complainant’s statement, where the complainant identifies the account and confirms that the communication was received from the accused.



Authenticity is often established by evidence identifying features of the account, such as the user’s name, profile image, previous communications, or identifiable personal content.



The admissibility of social media evidence typically requires addressing four evidentiary hurdles:



Relevance to a fact in issue.



Authenticity of the document or communication.



Compliance with hearsay rules or an applicable exception.



Absence of unfair prejudice outweighing probative value.



Where these requirements are satisfied, social media communications may form powerful evidentiary material in criminal proceedings.



Family Violence Management System Records — Evidentiary Use



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: use of FVMS incident details in affidavit evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: FVMS records, family violence records, affidavit evidence, redacted records, incident details, prosecution disclosure, police records, documentary preparation]



Family Violence Management System records are generally used in criminal proceedings by extracting relevant incident details into an affidavit rather than tendering the FVMS report itself. The practical rule is that the evidentiary document should contain the relevant incident information drawn from the Family Violence Management System, while the underlying report is ordinarily retained in reserve rather than automatically produced.



This approach serves two purposes. First, it places before the court the relevant factual material in admissible form. Second, it reduces the risk of over-disclosure of irrelevant or sensitive material contained within the broader FVMS record.



Where there is a real prospect that the defence may call for production of the underlying record, redacted copies should be prepared and available for the hearing. The redaction process should remove material that is irrelevant, privileged, or otherwise not properly disclosable, while preserving any content that may become necessary for fairness or forensic testing.



The evidentiary point is therefore not that FVMS reports are tendered wholesale, but that incident information from the system is translated into admissible affidavit form, with redacted source documents held ready if production is later required. This is a document-management and admissibility practice designed to balance evidentiary utility with controlled disclosure.



block\_008



Social Media Evidence — Authenticity Assessment



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: authenticity of social media records and screenshots]

\[CONCEPTS: authenticity, Facebook screenshots, digital authorship, identity of account holder, electronic records, online messages, who sent the message, document reliability]



Authenticity is a central admissibility issue for social media evidence because the court must be satisfied that the account, message, or post is what the tendering party claims it to be. In criminal proceedings, the usual question is whether the social media communication can reasonably be attributed to the accused.



A screenshot or printout may sometimes assist with authenticity on its face, but social media printouts will often require supporting evidence because the document alone may be too weak to justify an inference about authorship or account ownership. Supporting evidence may include a complainant identifying the account as one used by the accused, evidence of prior communications through that account, profile photographs, personal details, historical posts, or other distinctive account features.



The strongest practical method is to attach or incorporate screenshots into a complainant’s statement and have the complainant explain why the account was recognised as belonging to the defendant. A secondary method is a police statement setting out the investigation undertaken, the account features observed, and the manner in which the screenshots were obtained.



Authenticity therefore turns on whether the court can draw a reasonable inference, from the document itself and the surrounding evidence, that the communication originated from the accused or from an account sufficiently connected to that person.



block\_008



Authority — Authenticity May Be Assessed from the Face of a Document



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Australian Competition and Consumer Commission v Air New Zealand Ltd (No 1) \[2012] FCA 1355]

\[TOPIC: authenticity determined on the face of the document for relevance purposes]

\[CONCEPTS: document authenticity, face-value assessment, relevance, reasonable inference, business records, electronic documents, digital printouts]



Australian Competition and Consumer Commission v Air New Zealand Ltd (No 1) \[2012] FCA 1355 supports the principle that authenticity can, in some cases, be assessed from the document itself for the purpose of determining relevance.



The authority is used for the proposition that a court may examine a document and decide whether it is capable of supporting a reasonable inference as to authenticity without first requiring exhaustive external proof. The reasoning is linked to the court’s role in determining preliminary relevance questions.



Applied to social media evidence, the case supports an argument that a printout or screenshot may sometimes be sufficiently distinctive on its face to justify admission. However, the source material cautions that Facebook printouts will often not be compelling enough by themselves and may need additional supporting evidence identifying the account and its connection to the accused.



The practical effect of the authority is not that all digital printouts are automatically authentic, but that the court may determine authenticity by reasonable inference from the document where the document itself is sufficiently persuasive. Social media material usually becomes stronger when accompanied by identifying evidence from a complainant or investigating officer.



block\_008



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 166–167 — Production for Forensic Examination



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 166–167]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 166–167]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: production of documents or representations for forensic assessment]

\[CONCEPTS: forensic examination, document production, digital evidence testing, defence inspection, authenticity challenge, evidentiary testing, electronic records, forensic assessment]



Sections 166 and 167 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) are relevant where a party, usually the defence, seeks access to a document or representation so that it can be subjected to forensic examination. The rule is important where authenticity, authorship, manipulation, or integrity of a document is in issue.



In practical terms, these provisions may be invoked where a party argues that the original or underlying digital material should be produced for expert review rather than relying only on screenshots, copies, or summaries. This is particularly relevant to electronic communications and social media evidence, where questions may arise about editing, account control, metadata, or fabrication.



The doctrinal significance of the provisions is that documentary evidence may require more than simple tender of a copy if fairness requires that the opposing party have an opportunity to examine the underlying representation in a meaningful way. The source material specifically notes that the defence may rely on s 167, read with s 166, to contend that the representation should be provided so it can be forensically assessed.



The provisions therefore operate as fairness-oriented procedural tools allowing deeper examination of documentary and digital evidence when authenticity or reliability is disputed.



block\_008



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 88 — Admissions Threshold



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 88]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 88]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility threshold for admissions]

\[CONCEPTS: admissions evidence, accused statements, low admissibility threshold, confession evidence, incriminating statements, out-of-court admissions, party statements]



Section 88 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) governs the admissibility of admissions and is treated in the source material as imposing a relatively low threshold for admissibility. The practical significance is that where a statement is properly characterised as an admission by a party, the main admissibility obstacles often shift away from hearsay and toward fairness, prejudice, voluntariness, or exclusionary discretions.



In the context of social media or electronic communications, this means that once authorship and authenticity are sufficiently established, a post, message, or recorded statement by the accused may be tendered as admission evidence without needing to rely on the business records exception.



The doctrinal point is that the classification of material as an admission can simplify the evidentiary path to admissibility. It does not, however, eliminate other possible objections, including arguments based on unfairness, prejudice, impropriety, or exclusion under other provisions of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas).



The practical sequence is therefore:



Prove the statement is attributable to the accused.



Characterise it as an admission.



Address any remaining exclusionary arguments.



That makes s 88 an important gateway provision for digital and oral statements tendered against an accused.



block\_008



Business Record Exception — Documentary Foundation Requirements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: supporting proof required for business records]

\[CONCEPTS: business records, hearsay exception, keeper of records, CCTV evidence, documentary foundation, record-keeping practices, source identification, proof of provenance]



The business records exception requires more than merely producing a document and calling it a business record. The evidentiary foundation must support the proposition that the record was created, kept, and produced as part of the ordinary operations of a business.



The source material uses CCTV as the working example. A CCTV recording should ordinarily be accompanied by evidence from the person responsible for the system or records, identifying the footage as originating from a particular location, at a particular time, showing the relevant event, and explaining how it came into police possession. That evidence establishes provenance, continuity, and connection to the business record system.



The rule is particularly relevant where the document is tendered as an exception to hearsay. If the material is instead direct evidence of an offence, the business record exception may not be necessary. The distinction matters because parties sometimes overcomplicate admissibility by invoking hearsay exceptions where the evidence is admissible for a non-hearsay purpose or as direct proof of conduct.



The core principle is that the business records pathway requires supported proof of business origin, record-keeping context, and connection between the tendered document and the asserted source.



block\_008



DNA Evidence — Chain of Identification and Expert Proof



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: proving DNA evidence through sampling and barcode linkage]

\[CONCEPTS: DNA evidence, chain of custody, barcode reference, forensic sampling, expert witness, DNA report, sample identification, forensic continuity]



DNA evidence is ordinarily proved by establishing a chain linking the sampled item or location to the forensic report through a recorded barcode or reference number. The evidentiary task is not limited to tendering the report; it requires proof that the analysed sample is the same sample taken from the relevant item or place.



The source procedure is:



The sample site is recorded, either by photograph or by seizure of the relevant item.



The item or location is assigned a barcode or sample number.



The sampling officer records the sample details and retains the identifying reference.



The sample is sent to Tasmanian forensic services for analysis.



The resulting DNA report refers to the specific barcode numbers.



At hearing, the sample taker should be called to identify the sample or item and confirm the barcode or reference number assigned. Once that evidentiary link is made, the analyst can be called as an expert witness. The analyst’s qualifications should be established, the report authenticated, and the report then tendered.



The source further notes that DNA analysis alone does not establish how DNA came to be deposited on an item or how long it had been there. That limits the inferential reach of DNA evidence and makes it important not to overstate what the science proves.



block\_008



Fingerprint Evidence — Oral Proof and Reference Linkage



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: proving fingerprint evidence through serial or barcode reference]

\[CONCEPTS: fingerprint evidence, chain of custody, serial number, barcode linkage, oral evidence, forensic identification, fingerprint examiner, sample continuity]



Fingerprint evidence is proved in a similar way to DNA evidence by linking the recovered print or sampled item to the forensic comparison process through an identifying serial or barcode reference. The crucial evidentiary issue is continuity between the scene, the recovered material, and the fingerprint analysis.



The source indicates that fingerprint matters often differ from DNA matters because there may be no formal written report and the evidence may instead be presented through oral testimony and statutory declaration material. For that reason, the witness must orally explain the relevant identifying details and make the connection between the exhibit and the recorded serial or barcode number.



The practical evidentiary sequence is:



Identify the item or location from which the print was obtained.



Prove the assigned serial or barcode reference.



Call the relevant fingerprint witness to explain the examination and result.



Connect the forensic conclusion back to the identified exhibit or sample.



The doctrinal point is that fingerprint evidence still requires ordinary proof of provenance and continuity. It cannot safely rest on conclusion alone. The reference number or exhibit link is the bridge between field collection and the forensic opinion.



block\_008



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 170–172 — Bank Record Affidavits



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 170–172]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 170–172]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admission of bank records by keeper-of-records affidavit]

\[CONCEPTS: bank records, affidavit evidence, keeper of records, business records affidavit, service requirements, documentary proof, financial records, author requirements]



Bank records obtained under warrant or request may be admitted through an affidavit from the bank’s keeper of records under s 170 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas), provided the statutory preconditions are met. The source material notes that police may request copies of bank records by account number or by name, and those records commonly arrive with an affidavit from the bank officer responsible for the records.



The evidentiary shortcut under s 170 depends on compliance with both authorship requirements and procedural service requirements. The material specifically notes that the affidavit pathway requires satisfaction of the “author” requirements and service, not merely disclosure, on the defence under ss 171–172.



The practical rule is that the party seeking to rely on the affidavit must ensure:



The affidavit is from an appropriate records custodian or authorising person.



The affidavit identifies the records in a manner sufficient to connect them to the bank’s record-keeping system.



The defence is formally served in accordance with the statutory provisions.



This makes the bank-records affidavit process a specialised documentary admission mechanism that reduces the need to call live evidence from bank representatives, but only where the statutory procedural safeguards are strictly followed.



block\_008



Identification Evidence — Dock Identification



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: limits on dock identification]

\[CONCEPTS: dock identification, in-court identification, prior out-of-court identification, suggestive identification, accused presented as suspect, witness recognition, identification fairness]



Dock identification is ordinarily not permitted unless it is confirmatory of a prior acceptable out-of-court identification. The legal concern is that the courtroom setting is inherently suggestive because the accused is presented to the witness as the person suspected of the crime.



Where a witness has not become familiar with the accused through prior knowledge or association, and has not seen the accused since the events in question, an in-court identification will normally be excluded if it occurs in circumstances where the accused is specifically presented as the suspected offender.



The rule reflects the danger that a witness may unconsciously identify the person in the dock because of the courtroom setting rather than because of genuine recollection from the time of the events.



The practical principle is:



A prior reliable out-of-court identification is ordinarily required.



Dock identification is usually confined to confirming that earlier identification.



Absent prior familiarity or prior identification, in-court identification is generally too suggestive to be admitted safely.



This is a fairness-based evidentiary restriction designed to reduce the risk of mistaken identification and wrongful conviction.



block\_008



Authority — Dock Identification Usually Impermissible Without Prior Identification



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v Murtagh \[2009] TASMC 05]

\[TOPIC: limitations on dock identification evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: dock identification, suggestive courtroom identification, prior familiarity, confirmatory identification, risk of error, eyewitness evidence]



Police v Murtagh \[2009] TASMC 05 affirms that dock identifications are not usually permitted other than as confirmatory of an acceptable prior out-of-court identification.



The authority states that where a witness has not become familiar with the accused by previous knowledge or association, and has not seen the accused since the event in question, identification evidence will not normally be admitted if the purported identification occurs in circumstances where the accused is specifically presented to the witness as a person suspected of the relevant crime.



The principle is directed to the suggestive nature of courtroom identification. The accused’s position in the dock may itself influence the witness and produce an appearance of recognition that is not based on reliable memory.



The case therefore supports a restrictive approach to in-court identification evidence and reinforces the need for earlier, less suggestive identification procedures where identification is genuinely in issue.



block\_008



Voice Identification Evidence — Ad Hoc Opinion Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of voice recognition evidence as ad hoc opinion]

\[CONCEPTS: voice identification, voice recognition, ad hoc opinion, speaker recognition, auditory identification, lay opinion evidence, recognition by voice]



Voice identification evidence may be admissible as ad hoc opinion evidence where the witness is in a position to recognise the speaker’s voice from prior familiarity or exposure. The source material treats voice recognition not as an inadmissible speculative opinion, but as a form of admissible recognition evidence where the witness can identify a person by auditory perception.



The evidentiary rationale is similar to visual recognition evidence. A witness who is sufficiently familiar with a person’s voice may express an opinion that the voice heard on a recording, telephone call, or other communication was that of the accused.



The admissibility question turns on whether the opinion is grounded in actual familiarity and observation rather than mere guesswork. Where that foundation exists, the evidence may assist the fact-finder in identifying the speaker.



The weight of the evidence will depend on matters such as the witness’s degree of prior familiarity, the quality of the recording, the distinctiveness of the voice, and the possibility of mistake. The court must remain cautious, but the category is recognised as capable of admission.



block\_008



Authority — Voice Recognition as Ad Hoc Opinion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Davey v Tasmania \[2020] TASCCA 12]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of voice recognition opinion evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: voice recognition, ad hoc opinion, speaker identification, auditory familiarity, recognition evidence, opinion admissibility]



Davey v Tasmania \[2020] TASCCA 12 is cited in the source as authority that voice recognition evidence can be admissible as ad hoc opinion evidence.



The legal principle supported by the case is that a witness may, in an appropriate case, identify a speaker by voice and express that identification as opinion evidence where the opinion is based on familiarity sufficient to make the view rationally based on what the witness perceived.



The case supports treating voice recognition as a recognised evidentiary category rather than excluding it merely because it involves an evaluative conclusion. Its admissibility depends on an adequate factual foundation showing genuine familiarity with the voice in question.



block\_008



Recognition Evidence — Need for Special Caution



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: caution in cases depending on recognition evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: recognition evidence, mistaken identification, honest mistake, familiar person identification, eyewitness caution, wrongful conviction risk, visual recognition]



Recognition evidence generally carries greater reliability than identification of a stranger, but it still requires special caution because honest and convincing witnesses can be mistaken even when identifying someone they know.



The source material states that courts should be specially cautious before convicting in a case depending on identification or recognition evidence, even where there is other evidence. Mistakes in identification occur, innocent people have been convicted because of honest mistake, and even close friends or relatives may be wrong in purported recognition.



Recognition evidence is usually stronger than first-time identification because certain distortive effects may be less significant. Even so, the issue remains whether the witness’s recollection is accurate, not whether the witness appears sincere.



The doctrinal rule is therefore not that recognition evidence is weak, but that it remains a category requiring explicit caution. The fact-finder must consider the possibility of honest error and avoid treating familiarity as a guarantee of reliability.



block\_008



Authority — Recognition Evidence Can Still Produce Honest Mistake



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Sundram v Bearman \[2020] TASMC 18]

\[TOPIC: caution in assessing recognition evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: recognition evidence, mistaken witness, honest mistake, familiar face identification, caution before conviction, reliability of recognition]



Sundram v Bearman \[2020] TASMC 18 supports the principle that courts must be specially cautious before convicting on identification or recognition evidence. The case states that mistakes in identification do occur, that innocent people have been convicted because of honest but mistaken identification, and that even close friends and relatives can be wrong.



The authority further recognises that recognition evidence is generally more reliable than identification evidence strictly so called, but it does not eliminate the risk of error. The principle therefore combines two ideas: recognition may be comparatively stronger, yet it still requires caution and careful evaluation.



The case is important because it rejects any simplistic assumption that a witness who knows the accused must necessarily be right. Honest recognition can still be mistaken.



block\_008



Police Identification Evidence — Relevance of Police Observational Experience



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: weight and admissibility of identification evidence from police officers]

\[CONCEPTS: police identification, trained observation, probative value, police witness, visual memory, observational expertise, reliability of identification]



Identification evidence from a police officer may derive added probative value from the officer’s training and experience in observing and remembering the features of persons under observation. The source material treats police status not as automatically determinative, but as relevant to the weight and admissibility of the identification evidence.



The reasoning is that an experienced police officer may be more alert than an ordinary lay witness to the need to observe features carefully and retain them in memory. That does not make the officer infallible, but it is a factor capable of increasing the probative value of the evidence.



The doctrinal caution is that police experience is relevant, not conclusive. The evidence must still be assessed in light of the whole case, including opportunity to observe, lighting, distance, timing, subsequent events, and corroborative circumstances.



This principle therefore allows a court to take police observational training into account when evaluating identification evidence, while avoiding any blanket rule that police identification must automatically be accepted.



block\_008



Authority — Police Experience May Increase Probative Value of Identification Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Young v Lusted \[2011] TASSC 22]

\[TOPIC: relevance of police training and experience to identification evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: police officer witness, trained observation, probative value, police memory, identification weight, witness expertise]



Young v Lusted \[2011] TASSC 22 supports the principle that a magistrate may take into account the fact that an identifying witness is a police officer with substantial observational training and experience.



The authority states that although police officers can make mistakes like anyone else, it is relevant to probative value that an experienced officer may have greater training and experience in perceiving and remembering the features of a person under observation than many lay persons. The officer may also be more aware of the need to observe and retain memory of those observations.



The principle is not a rule of automatic acceptance. Rather, it allows police experience to be treated as a factor relevant to the evaluation and admissibility of the identification evidence.



block\_008



Authority — Later Authority Potentially Qualifies the Strength of the Earlier Police ID Approach



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Baker v Penneyston \[2018] TASSC 8]

\[TOPIC: later authority affecting treatment of police identification evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: police identification, later authority, caution, evidentiary weight, qualification of precedent, witness reliability]



Baker v Penneyston \[2018] TASSC 8 is noted in the source as authority that slightly undermines or qualifies the force of the reasoning in Young v Lusted \[2011] TASSC 22. The source does not set out the detailed ratio, but it clearly records that the later case should be considered when relying on the proposition that police observational experience materially strengthens identification evidence.



The safe doctrinal position from the source is therefore that Young v Lusted remains relevant, but reliance on police experience as a weight-enhancing factor should be approached with caution and checked against the later authority. \[REVIEW]



block\_008



Voir Dire — Nature and Order of Proceedings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: conduct and management of a voir dire]

\[CONCEPTS: voir dire, admissibility hearing, order of evidence, separate proceeding, criminal trial management, preliminary evidence, trial procedure]



A voir dire is a separate admissibility proceeding within a criminal case used to determine whether disputed evidence should be admitted at trial. The source material states that a criminal trial is deemed to begin when the accused is called upon to plead, but questions of admissibility may then be determined on a voir dire as a distinct process.



The court has a discretion as to which party presents evidence first on a voir dire. However, the defence is rarely, if ever, compelled to go first. Prima facie, evidence on a voir dire is to be given orally, although in practice counsel often seek to have admissibility questions determined wholly or partly on the papers.



The procedural characteristics of a voir dire therefore include:



It is a distinct proceeding concerned with admissibility.



The court controls the order of evidence.



Oral evidence is the default position.



Paper-based determination may be adopted where appropriate.



The practical function of the voir dire is to isolate admissibility questions so that contested evidentiary issues can be resolved without exposing the jury to potentially inadmissible material.



block\_008



Authority — Court’s Discretion as to Order of Evidence on Voir Dire



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Turner (No 12) \[2001] TASSC 103]

\[TOPIC: order of evidence on a voir dire]

\[CONCEPTS: voir dire procedure, order of proof, judicial discretion, defence not compelled first, admissibility hearing]



R v Turner (No 12) \[2001] TASSC 103 is cited as authority that the court has a discretion as to which party presents evidence first on the voir dire. The source also notes that, despite that discretion, the defence is rarely if ever compelled to proceed first.



The authority supports a case-management principle rather than a substantive evidentiary rule. It confirms that the trial judge may structure the hearing in a way that is fair and efficient, while recognising the practical sensitivity of requiring the defence to lead first in admissibility disputes.



block\_008



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 142 — Burden and Standard on Voir Dire



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 142]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 142]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: burden and balance of probabilities standard in admissibility determinations]

\[CONCEPTS: voir dire burden, balance of probabilities, admissibility facts, preliminary facts, standard of proof, party asserting fact, evidentiary threshold]



In proceedings on a voir dire, the burden of proving a fact lies on the party asserting that fact, and the standard of proof is proof on the balance of probabilities under s 142 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas).



This rule applies to factual questions necessary to decide admissibility, including facts relevant to exclusionary provisions and discretionary decisions. It means the court does not apply the criminal standard of beyond reasonable doubt when deciding whether evidence was improperly obtained, whether a factual precondition to admissibility exists, or whether a discretionary exclusion should be engaged.



The rule has two parts:



The asserting party bears the burden of the factual proposition.



The judge decides those facts on the balance of probabilities.



The section is especially important in admissibility contests involving s 138 impropriety, challenges to searches, or other preliminary factual disputes. It ensures that admissibility questions are resolved by civil standard findings even though the broader criminal proceeding remains subject to the higher standard of proof.



block\_008



Authority — Facts Relevant to Admissibility Are Determined on the Balance of Probabilities



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Moore v Knowles \[2017] TASMC 12]

\[TOPIC: operation of s 142 in voir dire and admissibility disputes]

\[CONCEPTS: balance of probabilities, admissibility facts, s 142, search impropriety, evidentiary discretion, preliminary fact finding]



Moore v Knowles \[2017] TASMC 12 confirms that s 142 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) requires the court to determine the facts necessary for deciding a question about admissibility, including the exercise of a discretion, on the balance of probabilities.



The source quotes the case in the context of an argument that evidence found on a search should be excluded under s 138. The significance of the authority is that both the factual basis for the alleged impropriety and the facts relevant to the court’s subsequent discretionary assessment fall within the province of s 142.



The case therefore confirms that admissibility fact-finding is governed by the civil standard even where the evidence may have major consequences for the criminal trial.



block\_008



Voir Dire — Fresh Oath and Limits on Cross-Examination



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: oath requirements and permissible scope of questioning on a voir dire]

\[CONCEPTS: fresh oath, separate proceeding, cross-examination limits, voir dire questioning, abuse of process, relevance on voir dire, accused self-incrimination]



A voir dire is treated as a separate proceeding for oath and questioning purposes. If a witness has already begun giving evidence before the jury and is then called to give evidence on the voir dire, the witness must swear a fresh oath or make a fresh affirmation before giving that evidence.



If the accused gives evidence on the voir dire, the accused should not be asked whether he or she committed the offence charged, nor any question tending to incriminate the accused in relation to that offence. The source identifies this protection as arising under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 189(6).



Cross-examination on the voir dire is limited to issues that arise on the voir dire itself. Questions directed to issues for trial, but not relevant to the admissibility dispute, amount to an abuse of process and should be disallowed as irrelevant.



The governing procedural principles are therefore:



Voir dire evidence is given under a fresh oath or affirmation.



The accused cannot be compelled to answer incriminating questions about the charged offence on the voir dire.



Cross-examination must remain confined to admissibility issues.



These limits preserve fairness and prevent the voir dire from being used as an improper substitute for trial cross-examination.



block\_008



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Unfair Prejudice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 137]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — General Rule]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: exclusion of prosecution evidence where probative value is outweighed by unfair prejudice]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, probative value, exclusion discretion, jury misuse of evidence, inflaming jury, evidentiary balancing, prosecution evidence]



Section 137 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) requires exclusion of prosecution evidence where its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant. The provision is commonly litigated on a voir dire.



The source explains that, when assessing probative value under this section, the court must assume that the jury will accept the evidence as credible and will draw every inference favourable to the Crown that is reasonably open. In Tasmania, that approach is said to follow KMJ v Tasmania.



The concept of unfair prejudice refers not merely to evidence that is damaging, but to evidence that may cause the jury to give it more weight than it deserves, inflame emotion, or distract jurors from their proper task.



In practical terms, parties commonly argue:



The prosecution says judicial directions can neutralise the risk.



The defence says the circumstances are such that directions will not sufficiently cure the prejudice.



Section 137 is therefore a balancing rule directed at protecting the fairness of the trial while still allowing probative evidence to be used where the risk of misuse is not disproportionate.



block\_008



Authority — Probative Value Assessed on Assumption Jury Accepts Crown Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: KMJ v Tasmania (2011) 20 Tas R 425]

\[TOPIC: approach to probative value under s 137]

\[CONCEPTS: s 137, probative value, Crown-favourable inferences, jury acceptance assumption, unfair prejudice, admissibility balancing]



KMJ v Tasmania (2011) 20 Tas R 425 is cited as authority that, when assessing probative value for the purposes of Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137, the court must assume that the jury will accept the evidence as credible and will draw every inference favourable to the Crown that is reasonably open.



The case is important because it fixes the Tasmanian approach to the balancing exercise and distinguishes the preliminary admissibility assessment from the jury’s ultimate role in deciding what evidence should be accepted.



block\_008



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 97–101 — Tendency and Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 97–101]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 97–101]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility requirements for tendency and coincidence evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, coincidence evidence, significant probative value, notice requirement, cross-admissibility, multiple complainants, prejudicial effect]



Tendency and coincidence evidence under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 97–101 is admissible only if the statutory requirements are met, and objections to such evidence are commonly determined on a voir dire, especially in sexual cases involving multiple complainants.



The source identifies the main statutory issues as:



Written tendency or coincidence notice must be given.



The evidence must truly be tendency or coincidence evidence.



The evidence must have significant probative value.



In criminal proceedings, the probative value must substantially outweigh any prejudicial effect under s 101(2).



The source also notes that “significant probative value” means more than mere relevance and connotes evidence that is important or of consequence. Striking similarity is not always required for tendency evidence, although closer and more particular similarities will generally strengthen the argument that the evidence has significant probative value.



The practical disputes usually concern whether the evidence is genuinely tendency or coincidence evidence at all, whether complainant evidence is cross-admissible, and whether the prejudicial risk outweighs the probative force of the material.



block\_008



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90 — Unfair Use of Admissions



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 90]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: exclusion of admissions where use would be unfair to the defendant]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair admissions, exclusion of confession, fairness discretion, accused statements, s 90, police interview fairness, admission evidence]



Section 90 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) permits a judge to refuse to admit evidence of an admission if, having regard to the circumstances in which the admission was made, it would be unfair to the defendant to use the evidence.



The source notes that where the defence relies on s 90, it will often also rely on s 137. That reflects the fact that unfairness in the making or use of an admission may overlap with concerns about prejudicial effect.



The focus of s 90 is not simply reliability but fairness. The court examines the circumstances surrounding the making of the admission and asks whether it would be fair for the prosecution to use it.



The section therefore operates as a protection against the use of admissions obtained or presented in circumstances that would make their forensic use unfair, even where no other exclusionary rule strictly applies.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 138 — Improperly or Illegally Obtained Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 138]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 138 — Exclusion Rule: Probative vs Impropriety]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: exclusion of improperly or illegally obtained evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: improperly obtained evidence, illegally obtained evidence, exclusion discretion, desirability balancing, police impropriety, unlawful search, tainted evidence]



Section 138(1) of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides that evidence obtained improperly, in contravention of an Australian law, or in consequence of an impropriety or of such a contravention, is not to be admitted unless the desirability of admitting the evidence outweighs the undesirability of admitting evidence obtained in that way.



The source material states that the defence bears the burden of proving the impropriety or contravention on the balance of probabilities. If that is established, the Crown then bears the burden of proving facts warranting admission of the evidence.



When s 138(1) is engaged, s 138(3) requires the court to take into account eight specified matters in deciding whether admission is desirable despite the impropriety or illegality. The list is not exhaustive.



The section therefore creates a two-stage framework:



Determine whether the evidence was obtained improperly, illegally, or derivatively from such conduct.



If so, decide whether the desirability of admission outweighs the undesirability of admitting evidence obtained in that manner.



This provision is commonly litigated on the voir dire, especially in relation to searches, warrants, arrests, and investigative non-compliance.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 138 — Burden of Proof for Improperly Obtained Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 138]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 138 — Burden of Proof for Exclusion]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: burden of proof where evidence is alleged to be improperly or illegally obtained]

\[CONCEPTS: improperly obtained evidence, exclusion discretion, burden of proof, police impropriety, unlawful search, admissibility challenge, balance of probabilities]



When exclusion of evidence is sought under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 138, the defence bears the initial burden of establishing that the evidence was obtained improperly, illegally, or in consequence of such conduct.



The relevant factual matters are determined according to the admissibility standard in s 142, meaning the court must decide the issue on the balance of probabilities.



If the defence establishes that the evidence was obtained improperly or unlawfully, the evidentiary burden then shifts to the prosecution. At that stage the prosecution must demonstrate that the desirability of admitting the evidence outweighs the undesirability of admitting evidence obtained in the way it was obtained.



The balancing exercise is guided by the factors listed in s 138(3). Those statutory factors include matters such as the probative value of the evidence, the importance of the evidence to the proceeding, the nature and seriousness of the impropriety, whether the impropriety was deliberate or reckless, and the ease with which the evidence could have been obtained lawfully.



The statutory list is not exhaustive, and courts retain discretion to consider additional factors relevant to fairness and the integrity of the justice system.



Authority — Defence Bears Initial Burden to Prove Impropriety



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Seabourne \[2010] TASSC 35]

\[TOPIC: burden of proof for improperly obtained evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: s 138 Evidence Act, burden of proof, improper police conduct, unlawful search, exclusion of evidence]



Tasmania v Seabourne \[2010] TASSC 35 confirms that the defence bears the burden of proving that evidence was obtained improperly or in contravention of an Australian law before the exclusion discretion under s 138 is engaged.



The authority clarifies that the accused must first establish the impropriety on the balance of probabilities. Only once that threshold is met does the court proceed to the statutory balancing exercise concerning whether the evidence should nevertheless be admitted.



This case therefore confirms the two-stage structure of s 138:



Proof of impropriety or illegality.



Judicial balancing of desirability versus undesirability of admission.



Authority — Impropriety Must Be Established Before s 138 Balancing Occurs



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Acheson v Hibble \[2008] TASSC 42]

\[TOPIC: requirement to establish impropriety before exclusion discretion arises]

\[CONCEPTS: s 138 threshold, improper evidence, police conduct, admissibility discretion, unlawful investigation]



Acheson v Hibble \[2008] TASSC 42 reinforces the requirement that the defence must first establish impropriety or illegality before the balancing test under s 138 is considered.



The authority confirms that the exclusionary discretion is not automatically engaged merely because the defence alleges misconduct. The court must first be satisfied, on the balance of probabilities, that the evidence was obtained improperly or in breach of an Australian law.



Only once that threshold is crossed does the court consider whether the desirability of admitting the evidence outweighs the undesirability of admitting evidence obtained in that way.



Freshness of Memory

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66 — First-Hand Hearsay and Freshness of Memory



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 66]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of first-hand hearsay where events were fresh in memory]

\[CONCEPTS: freshness of memory, first-hand hearsay, prior representation, recent complaint evidence, contemporaneous statements, witness availability]



Section 66 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides an exception to the hearsay rule for first-hand hearsay where the person who made the representation is available to give evidence and the representation was made when the occurrence of the asserted fact was fresh in the memory of the person who made it.



The provision often arises in criminal trials involving complaints made soon after an event, particularly in sexual offence cases involving “recent complaint” evidence.



The central evidentiary question is whether the representation was made at a time when the event was still fresh in the memory of the person making it. Freshness is not defined solely by the passage of time. Instead the court considers the surrounding circumstances, including the nature of the event, the age of the witness, and whether there were reasons why the representation may have been delayed.



Where the conditions of s 66 are satisfied, the earlier statement may be admitted as evidence of the truth of the asserted fact, even though it is technically hearsay.



Because the admissibility of such evidence often turns on factual matters relating to timing and memory, the issue is commonly determined on a voir dire.



Voir Dire Strategic and Procedural Issues

Voir Dire — Strategic Decision Whether to Conduct One



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: counsel decision whether to pursue a voir dire]

\[CONCEPTS: admissibility strategy, trial tactics, evidentiary objection, prosecutorial discretion, defence strategy]



Counsel must decide whether it is strategically advantageous to conduct a voir dire to challenge the admissibility of evidence. The decision often depends on the likelihood that the objection will succeed and the potential consequences if the objection fails.



In some circumstances it may be strategically preferable not to challenge admissibility and instead focus on attacking the reliability or weight of the evidence before the jury.



Where an admissibility challenge is likely to succeed, however, a voir dire provides the mechanism for preventing the jury from hearing prejudicial evidence that should not properly form part of the trial.



Voir Dire — Timing of the Admissibility Hearing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: timing and scheduling of voir dire proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: admissibility timing, jury management, trial scheduling, interruption of proceedings, s 361A Criminal Code]



The timing of a voir dire may significantly affect the conduct of the trial. A voir dire may be held before the jury is empanelled, immediately after empanelment, or during the trial if the admissibility issue only arises once evidence is given.



For particularly lengthy admissibility hearings, Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 361A may be used so that the hearing occurs without inconveniencing a jury.



Conducting a voir dire during the trial may interrupt proceedings before the jury and may cause frustration for the court. However, in some circumstances counsel may strategically wait until a witness gives evidence before raising the objection.



Voir Dire — Scope and Basis of the Objection



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: defining the scope of admissibility objections]

\[CONCEPTS: evidentiary objection, defining issues, admissibility grounds, Evidence Act provisions, procedural clarity]



When raising an objection that will be determined on a voir dire, counsel must clearly identify:



The specific evidence objected to.



The legal basis of the objection.



The statutory provisions relied upon.



The objection should be articulated precisely so that the opposing party understands the case that must be met and the court can clearly identify the issues that need to be resolved.



Authorities emphasise that the basis of the objection should be stated in open court and tied to the relevant provisions of the Evidence Act.



Voir Dire — Oral Evidence or Determination on the Papers



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: evidentiary method for determining admissibility]

\[CONCEPTS: oral testimony, documentary evidence, admissibility hearing procedure, paper determination]



Although evidence on a voir dire is prima facie given orally, the court may determine admissibility wholly or partly on written material if both parties agree.



Oral evidence is usually necessary where:



the court must resolve contested facts, or



a witness’s account of events is relevant to the admissibility issue.



Where the relevant facts are uncontested, the parties may invite the court to decide the issue on the basis of written statements or agreed material.



Voir Dire — Possibility of a Second Voir Dire



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: second admissibility hearing where evidence changes]

\[CONCEPTS: renewed admissibility challenge, changed evidence, procedural fairness, trial management]



A second voir dire may be required if evidence given before the jury differs materially from the evidence previously given on the original voir dire.



Although this situation is uncommon, courts recognise that it may arise where a witness changes their account or where new issues affecting admissibility emerge during the trial.



In such cases the court may reconsider the admissibility question to ensure that the jury is not exposed to evidence that should properly be excluded.



Defences — Doli Incapax

Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 18 — Presumption of Doli Incapax



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 18]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 18]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[TOPIC: criminal responsibility of children aged 10–13]

\[CONCEPTS: doli incapax, child criminal responsibility, presumption of incapacity, youth offending, knowledge of wrongness]



The doctrine of doli incapax creates a rebuttable presumption that children aged between 10 and 13 years lack the capacity to understand that their conduct was seriously wrong.



Section 18 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) reflects this principle. The presumption means that the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the child understood that the conduct was seriously wrong according to ordinary standards of reasonable adults, rather than merely naughty or mischievous.



The presumption applies only to children under 14 years of age.



To rebut the presumption, the prosecution must produce evidence demonstrating that the child had the relevant moral understanding at the time of the alleged offence.



Evidence relevant to rebutting the presumption may include:



statements made by the child,



observations of the child’s behaviour and understanding,



evidence from parents, teachers, or carers,



expert psychological evidence.



The key question is whether the child understood the serious moral wrongness of the conduct at the time it occurred.



Authority — Admissions by a Child May Rebut Doli Incapax



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R (a child) v Whitty (1993) 66 A Crim R 462]

\[TOPIC: admissions demonstrating knowledge of wrongdoing]

\[CONCEPTS: child admissions, understanding wrongness, doli incapax rebuttal, youth interview evidence]



R (a child) v Whitty (1993) 66 A Crim R 462 demonstrates that a child’s own statements may be powerful evidence when assessing whether the presumption of doli incapax has been rebutted.



In that case the child, when interviewed by police regarding shoplifting, stated “I stole” the goods. The court considered the use of the word “stole” as evidence suggesting the child understood that the conduct was morally wrong rather than merely naughty.



Statements made by a child during police interview may therefore provide direct insight into the child’s understanding of the seriousness of the conduct.



Authority — Knowledge of Consequences Does Not Equal Knowledge of Moral Wrongness



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: IPH v Chief Constable of South Wales \[1987] Crim LR 42]

\[TOPIC: distinction between knowledge of harm and knowledge of moral wrongness]

\[CONCEPTS: doli incapax, child understanding, moral wrongness, criminal capacity]



IPH v Chief Constable of South Wales \[1987] Crim LR 42 illustrates that evidence showing a child understood the consequences of an action does not necessarily prove that the child understood the conduct was seriously wrong.



In that case an eleven-year-old admitted knowing that pushing a vehicle into a pole would damage it. The court held that the admission demonstrated awareness that damage would occur but did not establish that the child appreciated the conduct was morally wrong.



The case therefore emphasises that the relevant inquiry concerns moral understanding, not merely awareness of consequences.



Authority — Alternative Evidence Sources to Rebut Doli Incapax



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: The King v TT \[2024] NTSC 62]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of alternative sources of evidence relevant to capacity]

\[CONCEPTS: doli incapax evidence, child development evidence, behavioural evidence, moral understanding]



The King v TT \[2024] NTSC 62 illustrates that various sources of evidence may be used to assess whether a child had the capacity to understand that conduct was seriously wrong.



Evidence may include behavioural evidence, statements made by the child, observations from adults who interacted with the child, and expert evidence concerning the child’s development.



The case demonstrates that courts consider a broad range of evidentiary material when determining whether the presumption of doli incapax has been rebutted.
