Authority — Circumstances of Offending Alone Insufficient to Rebut Doli Incapax



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: TPL v The State of Western Australia \[2025] WASCA 195]

\[TOPIC: insufficiency of offence circumstances to prove moral capacity]

\[CONCEPTS: doli incapax, child capacity, secrecy, deception, planning, moral understanding]



TPL v The State of Western Australia \[2025] WASCA 195 confirms that the circumstances of offending alone — such as secrecy, deception, planning, or use of force — do not necessarily establish that a child understood the conduct was seriously wrong.



The court emphasised that such behaviour may demonstrate awareness of rules or fear of punishment but does not necessarily show an appreciation of serious moral wrongdoing.



The case therefore reinforces the principle that the prosecution must prove moral capacity, not merely behavioural sophistication or knowledge of consequences.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 138 — Discretion to Exclude Improperly or Illegally Obtained Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 138]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 138 — Discretionary Exclusion: Overview]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: exclusion of improperly or illegally obtained evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: illegally obtained evidence, improper police conduct, exclusion discretion, probative value vs impropriety, evidentiary balancing test, unlawfully obtained evidence, judicial discretion, fairness of trial]



Section 138 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) establishes a discretionary rule permitting courts to exclude evidence obtained improperly or in contravention of Australian law.



The provision states that evidence obtained:



Improperly or in contravention of an Australian law, or



In consequence of such impropriety or illegality



is not to be admitted unless the desirability of admitting the evidence outweighs the undesirability of admitting evidence obtained in that way.



The section therefore requires a balancing exercise between competing public interests:



the public interest in convicting offenders, and



the public interest in discouraging unlawful investigative conduct.



Section 138(2) identifies circumstances in which evidence of admissions obtained during questioning is deemed to have been improperly obtained, including where the interrogator:



Performs or omits acts likely to impair the suspect’s capacity to respond rationally, knowing or reasonably expected to know this effect; or



Knowingly makes false statements likely to induce the suspect to make an admission.



Section 138(3) provides non-exhaustive factors relevant to the balancing exercise. These include:



The probative value of the evidence.



The importance of the evidence in the proceeding.



The nature of the offence or proceeding.



The gravity of the impropriety or illegality.



Whether the conduct was deliberate or reckless.



Whether the conduct infringed rights recognised by the ICCPR.



Whether other proceedings may be taken in relation to the impropriety.



The difficulty of obtaining the evidence lawfully.



The section codifies and supplements the common law exclusionary discretion developed in cases such as Bunning v Cross \[1978] HCA 22.



Authority — Burden of Proof for Improperly Obtained Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Bott \[2015] TASSC 13]

\[TOPIC: burden of proof in challenges to illegally obtained evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: evidentiary burden, accused onus, improperly obtained evidence, s 138 balancing test, admissibility of evidence, Crown burden, exclusion discretion]



The decision in Tasmania v Bott \[2015] TASSC 13 clarifies the allocation of evidentiary burdens when exclusion of evidence under s 138 is sought.



The Court confirmed that:



The accused bears the initial onus of establishing that evidence was obtained improperly or illegally.



If that onus is discharged, the burden shifts to the prosecution.



The prosecution must then demonstrate that the desirability of admitting the evidence outweighs the undesirability of admitting evidence obtained improperly.



This reflects the balancing approach articulated in earlier authorities including R v Salem (1997) 96 A Crim R 421, which confirms that the court must assess whether the administration of justice is better served by admission or exclusion.



The rule ensures that the accused must first establish a factual basis for the allegation of illegality or impropriety before the Crown is required to justify admission of the evidence.



Authority — Public Policy Discretion to Exclude Unlawfully Obtained Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Bunning v Cross \[1978] HCA 22; (1978) 141 CLR 54]

\[TOPIC: common law discretion to exclude unlawfully obtained evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: Bunning v Cross test, public policy exclusion, illegally obtained evidence, balancing public interests, police illegality, evidentiary discretion, admissibility balancing]



The High Court decision in Bunning v Cross \[1978] HCA 22 established the foundational common law discretion to exclude evidence obtained through unlawful or improper conduct by law enforcement.



The Court held that illegally obtained evidence is not automatically inadmissible. Instead, the trial judge must exercise a discretion based on public policy considerations.



The governing principle is a balancing exercise between two competing public interests:



The public interest in convicting those who commit criminal offences, and



The public interest in preventing unlawful conduct by law enforcement authorities.



The Court drew on the earlier statement of Barwick CJ in Ireland \[1970] HCA 21, which emphasised that convictions obtained by unlawful methods may be secured “at too high a price.”



In applying the discretion, courts consider factors including:



Whether police deliberately disregarded the law.



Whether the illegality affects the reliability or truthfulness of the evidence.



The ease with which police could have complied with legal requirements.



The seriousness of the offence relative to the impropriety.



Whether legislation deliberately restricts police powers.



These factors guide the court in determining whether admitting the evidence would undermine the integrity of the justice system.



Authority — Improper Conduct Under Evidence Exclusion Principles



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Ridgeway v The Queen \[1995] 184 CLR 19]

\[TOPIC: definition of impropriety in police conduct]

\[CONCEPTS: police impropriety, minimum standards policing, unlawful investigation conduct, exclusion discretion, improper investigative conduct, evidentiary fairness]



The High Court decision in Ridgeway v The Queen \[1995] 184 CLR 19 explains the concept of “impropriety” for the purposes of excluding evidence obtained through investigative misconduct.



Improper conduct refers to behaviour that:



May not be criminal, but



Is inconsistent with the minimum standards of acceptable conduct expected of law enforcement officers.



The concept focuses on whether investigative conduct falls clearly below the standards society is entitled to expect from police authorities.



Tasmanian courts have confirmed that:



Impropriety is assessed by reference to common law principles, and



The focus is on minimum standards, not ideal or best practice policing.



Accordingly, demonstrating that police actions fell short of best investigative practice does not establish impropriety. The conduct must instead be clearly inconsistent with acceptable policing standards.



These principles were summarised in Tasmania v Thompson \[2022] TASSC 27, drawing on earlier authorities including Wilkie v Barnaba \[2021] TASSC 21, Robinson v Woolworths Ltd \[2005] NSWCCA 426, and Kadir v The Queen \[2020] HCA 1.



Authority — Entrapment Is Not a Substantive Defence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Ridgeway v R \[1995] HCA 66]

\[TOPIC: entrapment not recognised as a defence]

\[CONCEPTS: entrapment doctrine, inducement by police, criminal responsibility despite inducement, police misconduct, exclusion of evidence]



The High Court decision in Ridgeway v R \[1995] HCA 66 confirms that entrapment does not constitute a substantive defence in Australian criminal law.



The Court held that a person who voluntarily commits all elements of a criminal offence with the necessary intent remains criminally responsible even if induced to do so by law enforcement officers.



Entrapment therefore does not negate guilt.



However, police conduct involving entrapment may still have legal consequences in two ways:



Evidence obtained through such conduct may be excluded where the conduct amounts to unlawful or improper investigative behaviour.



Courts may exercise their discretion to exclude evidence on public policy grounds, particularly where the crime itself was effectively created by the conduct of authorities.



The doctrine reflects the principle that the courts must protect the integrity of the criminal justice system, ensuring that the state does not improperly manufacture offences while enforcing the law.



Authority — No Common Law Right to a Speedy Trial



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Jago v District Court of New South Wales \[1989] HCA 46]

\[TOPIC: absence of common law right to speedy trial]

\[CONCEPTS: fair trial rights, delay in criminal proceedings, abuse of process, trial delay prejudice, criminal procedure fairness]



The High Court decision in Jago v District Court of New South Wales \[1989] HCA 46 establishes that Australian law does not recognise a freestanding common law right to a speedy trial.



Instead, the relevant legal protection is the right to a fair trial.



A lengthy delay between charge and trial does not automatically invalidate proceedings. However, delay may justify intervention where it renders the trial incapable of being conducted fairly.



The question is determined through a balancing exercise, weighing:



The interests of the accused in receiving a fair hearing, and



The public interest in ensuring criminal allegations are determined by courts.



Tasmanian authority Lillico v McKenna (1995) 5 Tas R 147 confirms that dismissing criminal proceedings due to delay is an extreme remedy and will only be granted in exceptional circumstances where a fair trial is no longer possible.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)

Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 16 — Insanity Defence: Mental Disease Alone Is Insufficient



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 16]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 16]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Scott \[2025] TASSC 53]

\[TOPIC: insanity defence requirements]

\[CONCEPTS: insanity defence, mental disease, criminal responsibility, psychiatric illness not sufficient, legal insanity test, mental impairment defence, proof of insanity]



The insanity defence under s 16 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) requires more than proof that the accused suffered from a mental disease. The law requires proof that the mental disease produced one of the legally recognised forms of incapacity that negates criminal responsibility.



In Tasmania v Scott \[2025] TASSC 53, the Supreme Court confirmed that the presence of a diagnosed mental illness does not automatically excuse criminal liability. The court accepted that bipolar disorder constituted a “mental disease” within the meaning of s 16. However, the court emphasised that diagnosis alone does not satisfy the legal test.



The principle derives from the decision in Hitchens v The Queen \[1962] Tas SR 35, where the Court of Criminal Appeal held that:



The accused bears the burden of establishing insanity on the balance of probabilities.



Proof that a mental disease existed is not sufficient by itself.



The accused must demonstrate that the disease produced one of the statutory forms of incapacity affecting criminal responsibility.



The Court explained that an act may still constitute a crime even though the accused suffers from mental disease if the disease does not produce the mental incapacity required by law.



Thus, the legal inquiry focuses not merely on psychiatric diagnosis but on whether the disease affected the accused’s mental functioning in a way that satisfies the statutory criteria for insanity.



Criminal Justice (Mental Impairment) Act — Procedure Following Finding of Not Guilty by Reason of Insanity



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Justice (Mental Impairment) Act]

\[CITATION: Criminal Justice (Mental Impairment) Act]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[CASE: Bonde v Weate \[2017] TASMC 11]

\[TOPIC: post-verdict procedure following insanity acquittal]

\[CONCEPTS: insanity verdict procedure, mental impairment reports, criminal responsibility procedure, forensic mental health assessment, post-verdict mental impairment process]



When a defendant is found not guilty on the grounds of insanity, the court must follow the procedural framework established under the Criminal Justice (Mental Impairment) Act.



In Bonde v Weate \[2017] TASMC 11, the Court confirmed that the statutory process requires the preparation of mental impairment reports before further orders are made.



The procedure generally includes:



The preparation of reports under s 33 and s 34 of the Criminal Justice (Mental Impairment) Act.



Assessment of the accused’s mental condition and risk profile.



Determination by the court of appropriate orders, which may include detention, supervision, or other treatment arrangements.



The purpose of this procedure is to ensure that where a person is not criminally responsible due to mental impairment, the justice system addresses both:



community protection, and



appropriate mental health management of the accused.



Automatism

Automatism — High Threshold and Burden of Proof



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[CASE: Wilson v Judges \[2025] TASSC 10]

\[TOPIC: automatism defence requirements]

\[CONCEPTS: automatism defence, involuntary conduct, concussion defence, voluntary act presumption, criminal responsibility, unconscious action]



Automatism is a defence asserting that the accused’s actions were not voluntary, meaning the physical acts forming the offence were not the product of conscious control.



In Wilson v Judges \[2025] TASSC 10, the Supreme Court confirmed that the defence carries a high evidentiary threshold.



The law begins with a presumption that a person’s actions are voluntary and intentional. This presumption may be displaced where evidence raises a reasonable possibility that the conduct occurred in a state of automatism.



The court emphasised the following principles:



The defence does not need to prove the exact mechanism that caused the automatism.



Credible evidence, including medical opinion, may raise a reasonable possibility that the conduct was involuntary.



Once the possibility is raised, the prosecution must disprove automatism beyond reasonable doubt.



In Wilson, the defence argued that the accused’s actions were attributable to concussion from a head injury. Because the prosecution failed to call contrary medical evidence or disprove the alternative explanation, the court held that the prosecution had not discharged its burden.



The decision illustrates that once credible evidence raises automatism as a possibility, the Crown must eliminate that possibility beyond reasonable doubt.



Authority — Presumption of Voluntariness in Criminal Acts



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Falconer \[1990] HCA 49; (1990) 171 CLR 30]

\[TOPIC: presumption that conscious acts are voluntary]

\[CONCEPTS: voluntariness presumption, willed act doctrine, inference of voluntariness, criminal act requirement, burden of proof]



The High Court decision in R v Falconer \[1990] HCA 49 establishes that criminal law proceeds on the presumption that acts performed by a conscious person are voluntary.



The prosecution ultimately bears the burden of proving that the accused committed the voluntary act forming the offence. However, the prosecution is generally entitled to rely on an inference of voluntariness where a person appears conscious and capable of acting.



The inference may be rejected only where evidence provides reasonable grounds to believe the accused was unable to control their actions.



Thus:



The prosecution bears the ultimate burden of proving voluntariness beyond reasonable doubt.



The prosecution may rely on the presumption of voluntary action unless evidence raises a contrary possibility.



If evidence suggests the accused lacked control over their actions, the inference may be displaced.



This doctrine underpins the operation of automatism defences and similar arguments concerning involuntary conduct.



Authority — Lack of Memory Does Not Establish Automatism



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Bonde v Morrison \[2015] TASMC 9]

\[TOPIC: memory loss insufficient to prove involuntary conduct]

\[CONCEPTS: memory loss defence, automatism threshold, voluntary act presumption, criminal responsibility, absence of recollection]



The decision in Bonde v Morrison \[2015] TASMC 9 confirms that a defendant’s inability to remember events does not establish automatism.



The court held that a claimed lack of recollection does not displace the presumption that actions performed by an apparently conscious person are voluntary and intentional.



Relying on R v Falconer \[1990] HCA 49, the court emphasised that the presumption of voluntariness will only be displaced where credible evidence raises a reasonable possibility that the accused lacked control over their actions.



A defendant’s memory loss after the event does not, by itself, demonstrate that the acts were involuntary at the time they occurred.



Accordingly, the defence must produce evidence capable of raising a genuine possibility that the accused was unable to control their actions at the relevant time.



Legal Doctrines

De Minimis Non Curat Lex — Inapplicability to Criminal Proceedings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Harrison v Moore \[2018] TASSC 53]

\[TOPIC: de minimis doctrine in criminal law]

\[CONCEPTS: trivial matters doctrine, de minimis rule, minor harm principle, criminal law threshold, trivial conduct defence]



The doctrine de minimis non curat lex, meaning “the law is not concerned with trifling matters,” is a principle under which courts may decline to adjudicate disputes involving insignificant or trivial issues.



However, in Harrison v Moore \[2018] TASSC 53, the Tasmanian Supreme Court confirmed that this doctrine does not apply to criminal proceedings.



The Court held that the maxim cannot be invoked to excuse conduct that satisfies the elements of a criminal offence merely because the conduct is considered minor.



Criminal law operates on the principle that once the statutory elements of an offence are established, liability follows unless a recognised defence applies.



The doctrine therefore has limited application in civil matters but cannot be relied upon to defeat criminal liability in Tasmania.



Self-Defence

Self-Defence — Requirement That Defence Be Raised on the Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[CASE: Irons v Moore \[2019] TASSC 22]

\[TOPIC: evidentiary threshold for self-defence]

\[CONCEPTS: self defence threshold, evidentiary burden, reasonable possibility defence, jury direction, criminal burden of proof]



The defence of self-defence must first be raised as a reasonable possibility on the evidence before it becomes an issue that must be considered by the court.



In Irons v Moore \[2019] TASSC 22, the Supreme Court confirmed the principle derived from Viro v The Queen (1978) 141 CLR 88 that self-defence will only be left to a jury where:



The accused raises the issue; and



The evidence discloses a reasonable possibility that the conduct was done in self-defence.



If the defence is properly raised, the prosecution bears the burden of disproving self-defence beyond reasonable doubt.



Where the issue is not raised by the evidence, the court is not required to consider it.



Authority — Self-Defence May Include Pre-Emptive Force



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Wright v Tasmania \[2005] TASSC 113]

\[TOPIC: pre-emptive use of force in self-defence]

\[CONCEPTS: anticipatory self defence, imminent threat, defensive force, reasonable belief of attack, pre-emptive defensive force]



In Wright v Tasmania \[2005] TASSC 113, the Supreme Court recognised that self-defence may justify the pre-emptive use of force in circumstances where a person reasonably believes an attack is imminent.



The Court held that a person does not necessarily have to wait for an attacker to strike the first blow before acting in self-defence.



If a person reasonably believes that they are about to be attacked, circumstances may justify the use of force to prevent the anticipated attack.



This principle reflects earlier authorities including Beckford v R \[1988] AC 130 and R v Lawrie \[1986] 2 Qd R 502, which recognise that defensive action may occur before the physical assault begins where the threat is imminent.



Honest and Reasonable Mistake — Evidential Threshold to Raise Defence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: evidential threshold for honest and reasonable mistake defence]

\[CONCEPTS: mistake of fact defence, evidential burden, threshold evidence, mere assertion insufficient, credibility of defendant statements, untested evidence, admissibility of statements, raising a defence]



The defence of honest and reasonable mistake requires sufficient evidential material before it can be considered by a court. The threshold question is whether the material relied upon by the accused is capable of raising the issue of a mistaken belief that would negate criminal liability.



A defendant cannot raise the defence merely by making unsupported assertions. Statements that are not admissible evidence, are untested by cross-examination, or are unsupported by independent material may be insufficient to discharge the evidential burden. Courts require some factual basis capable of evaluation.



For example, assertions referring vaguely to unidentified witnesses, unexplained circumstances, or unspecified prior events may be insufficient where they cannot be independently verified or tested. The absence of corroboration, detail, or supporting evidence can undermine the credibility of the asserted belief.



The evidential threshold therefore requires more than speculation or possibility. The accused must produce evidence capable of supporting the existence of the alleged mistaken belief and allowing the court to assess its plausibility.



Authority — Evidential Sufficiency for Mistake Defence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Barnes v Fowler \[2016] TASMC 8]

\[TOPIC: evidential threshold for honest and reasonable mistake]

\[CONCEPTS: evidential burden, mistake of fact defence, mere possibility insufficient, objective assessment of evidence, credibility of assertion]



In Barnes v Fowler \[2016] TASMC 8, Acting Chief Magistrate Daly considered the evidential threshold required to raise the defence of honest and reasonable mistake.



The Court held that mere speculative possibilities are insufficient to establish the evidential foundation necessary to raise the defence. Daly CM stated that evidence suggesting that drinks may have been spiked would require “something more than the merest of possibilities of an opportunity for such an occurrence”.



The Court emphasised that where a defendant’s claim rests solely on vague assertions within their own knowledge, without supporting detail or corroborating facts, the court may conclude that the evidential burden has not been discharged. Evidence that remains untestable or unsupported by independent material may amount to little more than a bare assertion.



The decision illustrates that courts require concrete factual material capable of being evaluated objectively before the defence of honest and reasonable mistake can properly arise.



Honest and Reasonable Mistake — Meaning of “Reasonable”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: objective reasonableness of the accused’s belief]

\[CONCEPTS: honest and reasonable mistake, objective reasonableness, subjective circumstances, accused’s belief, rational basis for belief, mixed subjective-objective test]



The concept of “reasonable” in the defence of honest and reasonable mistake refers to the objective reasonableness of the belief actually held by the accused.



The analysis involves a mixed subjective-objective inquiry:



The belief must genuinely be held by the accused (subjective element).



The belief must be objectively reasonable in the circumstances.



The reasonableness is assessed by reference to the circumstances known to the accused.



Personal characteristics or attributes affecting the accused’s perception may be relevant.



The test does not ask what a hypothetical reasonable person would have believed. Instead, it examines whether the accused’s belief, based on the facts as they perceived them, was rational and capable of sustaining belief.



A belief that is irrational, unsupported by observable facts, or inconsistent with available information will not satisfy the requirement of objective reasonableness.



Authority — Interpretation of “Reasonable”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Hindrum v Lane \[2014] TASFC 5]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of reasonable belief in mistake defence]

\[CONCEPTS: objective reasonableness, subjective circumstances, accused’s perception, rational belief, mixed subjective-objective test]



In Hindrum v Lane \[2014] TASFC 5 the Full Court clarified the meaning of “reasonable” within the defence of honest and reasonable mistake.



The Court held that the requirement of reasonableness:



does not involve the hypothetical “ordinary reasonable person” test;



requires the belief to be that of the accused;



requires the belief to be objectively rational or capable of sustaining belief;



requires the belief to be assessed in the circumstances as perceived by the accused.



This formulation recognises that the evaluation of reasonableness involves both subjective and objective aspects. The court must consider the accused’s personal circumstances, knowledge, and available information at the relevant time.



Authority — Meaning of Honest and Reasonable Belief



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Mei Ying Su (authority discussed in Australian criminal law jurisprudence)]

\[TOPIC: doctrinal meaning of honest and reasonable belief]

\[CONCEPTS: mistake of fact defence, subjective belief, objective reasonableness, accused’s perspective, rational grounds for belief]



Authorities discussed in Mei Ying Su explain that the defence of honest and reasonable mistake requires:



An actual belief by the accused in a particular state of affairs.



That belief must be reasonable in the circumstances as perceived by the accused.



The courts have repeatedly emphasised that the focus is on the accused’s belief rather than a hypothetical reasonable person’s belief. However, the belief must still be rational and capable of being sustained by objective facts.



The doctrine therefore combines subjective belief with an objective assessment of its rationality.



Authority — Internet Research Not a Reasonable Basis for Belief



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Smith v Marshall \[2024] TASMC 12]

\[TOPIC: limits on sources supporting reasonable belief]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable belief, reliability of information sources, legal advice, internet forums, mistaken belief defence]



In Smith v Marshall \[2024] TASMC 12 the court considered whether information obtained from internet chat groups could form the basis of a reasonable belief for the purposes of a mistake defence.



The court concluded that reliance on informal internet discussions was not objectively reasonable. Such sources did not constitute advice from a legal practitioner or other authoritative source capable of reasonably informing the accused about legal obligations.



The decision highlights that the credibility and reliability of the information relied upon by the accused may be critical in determining whether a belief is objectively reasonable.



Honest and Reasonable Mistake — Requirement That Act Would Otherwise Be Lawful



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: mistake of fact defence requires act to be otherwise lawful]

\[CONCEPTS: mistake of fact defence, otherwise lawful conduct, alternative offence, legal justification, mistaken belief]



The defence of honest and reasonable mistake is only available where the mistaken belief, if true, would render the accused’s conduct lawful.



If the conduct would remain unlawful even if the accused’s belief were correct, the defence cannot operate. The mistake must relate to a fact that removes criminal liability entirely.



For example, where legislation criminalises conduct under multiple provisions, a mistaken belief that negates one element may not provide a defence if the same conduct still constitutes another offence.



Authority — Mistake Defence Not Available Where Conduct Still Unlawful



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Bell \[2019] TASSC 34]

\[TOPIC: limits of mistake of fact defence]

\[CONCEPTS: mistake of fact defence, supply to minor, alternative offence, otherwise lawful requirement]



In Tasmania v Bell \[2019] TASSC 34 the defendant was charged with supplying a substance to a minor and argued a mistaken belief as to the minor’s age.



Blow CJ held that the defence of mistake of fact was not available because the conduct would still have constituted an offence under another statutory provision. Even if the defendant had been mistaken about the minor’s age, the act of supplying the substance would still have been unlawful.



The case illustrates that the defence of honest and reasonable mistake only applies where the mistaken belief would render the act entirely lawful.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 20 — Duress (Compulsion) Defence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 20]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 20]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[TOPIC: duress or compulsion defence]

\[CONCEPTS: duress defence, compulsion, threats of death, threats of grievous bodily harm, immediate threat, statutory defence]



Section 20 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) provides that compulsion may operate as a defence where an accused commits an offence under the influence of threats of immediate death or grievous bodily harm.



Key elements of the statutory defence include:



A threat of immediate death or grievous bodily harm.



The accused must be subject to the threat.



The person making the threat must be present at the commission of the offence.



The accused must believe the threat will be executed.



The offence must not be an excepted offence (including murder, treason, rape, robbery and arson).



The defence is unavailable where the accused voluntarily joined an association knowing that threats might be used.



The defence reflects the principle that criminal liability may be excused where the accused acts under coercion that effectively removes voluntary choice.



Authority — Elements of Duress



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Hurley and Murray \[1967] VR 129]

\[TOPIC: common law elements of duress]

\[CONCEPTS: threats of death, imminent threat, ordinary firmness test, causal influence of threat]



Hurley and Murray \[1967] VR 129 set out the classical elements of the common law defence of duress.



The defence may arise where:



The accused acted under threat of death or grievous bodily harm.



A person of ordinary firmness would likely have yielded to the threat.



The threat was present, continuing, imminent and impending.



The accused reasonably believed the threat would be carried out.



The threat induced the commission of the offence.



The offence is not one excluded from the doctrine.



The accused did not voluntarily place themselves in the position of duress.



The accused had no safe means of escape from the threat.



These elements emphasise the coercive nature of the threat and its causal influence on the accused’s conduct.



Authority — Express and Implied Threats in Duress



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: The King v Anna Rowan – A Pseudonym \[2024] HCA 9]

\[TOPIC: nature of threats for duress]

\[CONCEPTS: implied threats, operative threat, continuing threat, coercion defence]



In The King v Anna Rowan – A Pseudonym \[2024] HCA 9 the High Court clarified the nature of threats capable of founding a defence of duress.



The Court confirmed that threats may be express or implied. A threat need not be explicitly stated if the circumstances clearly convey that harm will occur if the accused fails to commit the offence.



The Court also clarified that the threat must be operative at the time of the offence. This does not necessarily require the threat to be made immediately before the offence, provided that the threat remained effective and the accused had no reasonable opportunity to escape its influence.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3 — Parties to Crimes Deemed Principals



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 3]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: parties to crimes deemed principals]

\[CONCEPTS: principal offender, aiding, abetting, instigating, criminal participation]



Section 3 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) defines who may be treated as a principal offender when a crime is committed.



The following persons are deemed to be parties to the crime and may be charged as if they committed it:



A person who actually commits the crime.



A person who performs an act or omission enabling or aiding another to commit the crime.



A person who abets another in committing the crime.



A person who instigates another person to commit the crime.



A person who instigates another to commit an act that would constitute a crime if done by the instigator is deemed guilty of the same crime.



The provision reflects the principle that criminal liability extends to those who intentionally assist, encourage, or procure the commission of offences.



Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 73 — Accessories to Simple Offences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 73]

\[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 73]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: accessorial liability for simple offences]

\[CONCEPTS: accessories, aiding simple offence, abetting offence, instigating offence]



Section 73 of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas) applies the principles of accessorial liability to simple offences.



A person may be treated as guilty of the offence where they:



Commit the offence directly.



Aid or enable another to commit it.



Abet another person in committing it.



Instigate another person to commit it.



A complaint charging the person as a principal is not invalid merely because the evidence ultimately proves accessorial liability.



Aiding and Abetting — Legal Distinction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: distinction between aiding and abetting]

\[CONCEPTS: aiding offence, abetting offence, encouragement, participation, accessorial liability]



“Aiding” and “abetting” are distinct forms of participation in criminal conduct.



Aiding involves a conscious act or omission intended to assist another person to commit the offence.



Abetting involves encouraging or supporting the principal offender in committing the offence.



Both require knowledge of the essential facts of the offence and an intention to assist or encourage the criminal conduct.



Authority — Mens Rea for Accessorial Liability



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Giorgianni v R (1985) 156 CLR 47]

\[TOPIC: knowledge requirement for aiding and abetting]

\[CONCEPTS: mens rea, knowledge of essential facts, intention to assist]



In Giorgianni v R (1985) 156 CLR 47 the High Court confirmed that a person alleged to aid, abet or instigate an offence must possess the requisite mens rea.



The accused must:



Know the essential facts that constitute the offence.



Intend that their conduct assist or encourage the commission of the offence.



Mere presence or accidental assistance is insufficient without the necessary knowledge and intention.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Crimes Committed in Prosecution of Common Purpose



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 4]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Common Purpose Liability: General]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: common purpose liability]

\[CONCEPTS: joint criminal enterprise, probable consequence, group liability, shared unlawful purpose]



Section 4 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) provides that where two or more persons form a common intention to pursue an unlawful purpose, each may be criminally liable for crimes committed in the course of that enterprise.



If a crime is committed during the execution of the common purpose and the commission of that crime was a probable consequence of pursuing the original unlawful purpose, each participant may be deemed to have committed the offence.



The test for whether the additional crime was a probable consequence is objective.



Authority — Interaction Between s 3 and s 4



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Clarke v Tasmania \[2013] TASCCA 11]

\[TOPIC: relationship between aiding liability and common purpose]

\[CONCEPTS: s3 aiding liability, s4 common purpose, probable consequence doctrine]



In Clarke v Tasmania \[2013] TASCCA 11 the Court explained the relationship between s 3 and s 4 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas).



Where multiple offenders are involved, liability should first be assessed under s 3 (aiding, abetting or instigating). If liability cannot be established under s 3, the court may consider s 4.



Section 3 requires proof that the accused possessed the full mens rea for the offence. Section 4 imposes liability where the additional crime was a probable consequence of the shared unlawful enterprise.



Duty to Declare Basis of Alleged Criminal Liability



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Crown duty to identify conceptual basis of criminal liability at outset]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecution opening, basis of liability, conceptual case theory, fair notice to accused, trial directions, particulars of prosecution case, criminal responsibility basis, procedural fairness]



The prosecution must identify the conceptual basis of alleged criminal liability early in the proceeding so that the accused and the court know the legal route by which guilt is said to arise. This obligation goes beyond merely outlining the facts expected to be proved.



Where the Crown relies on direct commission, aiding, abetting, instigation, or another form of criminal responsibility, that basis should be stated with sufficient clarity at the opening of the Crown case. The purpose is to remove uncertainty about the nature of the case and to allow defence counsel to respond properly, the trial judge to manage the case accurately, and directions to be framed on the correct legal issues.



If the Crown position changes after opening, that change should be identified with precision before final addresses. Failure to do so may create unfairness, because directions, defence strategy, and the conceptual understanding of the case may already have been formed on a different basis. The rule is therefore one of procedural fairness and proper case definition, especially in matters involving parties, accessories, and alternative bases of liability.



Authority — Crown Must Identify Conceptual Basis of Case



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Tangye (1997) 92 A Crim R 545]

\[TOPIC: obligation to declare basis of alleged criminal liability]

\[CONCEPTS: conceptual basis of Crown case, prosecution opening, fair notice, criminal liability theory, jury directions, particulars of case]



In R v Tangye (1997) 92 A Crim R 545, the Court stated that the obligation of the Crown Prosecutor in opening the case is not confined to outlining the facts proposed to be established. The Crown must also indicate, in conceptual terms, the nature of the Crown case.



The principle is important in cases involving possible alternative modes of liability, because the accused and the court must know whether the allegation is that the defendant acted as a principal offender, aided, abetted, instigated, or was liable on some other basis. If uncertainty about the conceptual basis of the case is not removed at an early stage, unfairness can arise in the conduct of the defence and in the framing of judicial directions.



The authority also recognises that if the nature of the Crown case changes after opening, the alteration should be identified precisely before final addresses. The case supports a procedural rule of clarity: the prosecution must define the legal pathway to liability, not merely narrate the facts.



Authority — Elements of Aiding Under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(b)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 3(1)(b)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(b) — Arnol: Intention Required for Aiding]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Arnol \[1980] Tas R 222]

\[TOPIC: intention required for aiding under s 3(1)(b)]

\[CONCEPTS: aiding rape, purpose of enabling, intention to aid, principal offender, accessorial liability, jury elements, criminal participation]



In Arnol \[1980] Tas R 222, the Court addressed the elements required to prove aiding under s 3(1)(b) of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas). The case emphasises that liability for aiding requires proof of intentional conduct directed toward assisting the principal offender to commit the crime.



The authority indicates that the prosecution must establish:



That the principal crime was in fact committed.



That the accused did the act alleged against them.



That the accused did that act intending that it should aid the principal offender to commit the crime.



The decision makes clear that the words “for the purpose of” in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(b) mean intended or designed to assist. The critical issue is not whether the act actually provided assistance in a practical sense, but whether the accused did the act with the intention that it aid the commission of the offence. The case therefore confirms that aiding liability under s 3(1)(b) turns on intentional facilitation of the principal crime.



Authority — Active Participation Beyond Mere Presence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Truman v Buxton \[2009] TASSC 96]

\[TOPIC: difference between mere presence and active participation in aiding or abetting]

\[CONCEPTS: mere presence, active participation, theft as aider, encouragement, company with offenders, accessorial liability]



In Truman v Buxton \[2009] TASSC 96, the Court considered when presence with offenders crosses the line into active participation sufficient for aiding or abetting. The authority confirms that mere presence at the scene of an offence is not enough. However, presence combined with knowing involvement and conduct supporting the offence may justify a finding of criminal participation.



The case involved the defendant travelling with others to bottle shops, entering the shops with them, remaining nearby while alcohol was stolen, leaving with the offenders, and departing in the same vehicle with the stolen property. On that evidence, the Court held that the magistrate was entitled to find active participation rather than incidental presence.



The principle supported by the case is that accessorial liability may be proved by a course of conduct showing knowing association with and support for the offending enterprise. The prosecution need not prove spoken encouragement if the defendant’s conduct objectively demonstrates intentional participation in the offence.



Authority — Mere Presence Does Not of Itself Establish Aiding and Abetting



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Coney (1882) 8 QBD 534]

\[TOPIC: mere presence versus intentional encouragement]

\[CONCEPTS: passive spectator, active steps, encouragement, presence at crime scene, intentional support, non-interference]



In R v Coney (1882) 8 QBD 534, the Court explained that a person does not become an aider or abettor merely by being present when a crime is committed. Criminal liability requires some active step by word, action, or conduct intended to encourage or support the principal offender.



The authority distinguishes between passive presence and intentional encouragement. A person may unintentionally encourage another by being present, by silence, or by conduct that is misunderstood, but such incidental encouragement is not enough. By contrast, where a person intentionally uses words, gestures, actions, or deliberate presence to signify approval or support, liability as an aider or abettor may arise.



The case also recognises that voluntary and purposeful presence at the commission of an offence, coupled with a failure to oppose it where opposition might reasonably be expected, can in some circumstances provide cogent evidence from which a tribunal of fact may infer wilful encouragement. The core rule, however, remains that passive observation alone is not criminal participation.



Authority — Encouragement Must Actually Encourage



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Shaw v TSCCA 22]

\[TOPIC: abetting requires encouragement that is operative on the principal offender]

\[CONCEPTS: abetting, ineffective encouragement, communication of encouragement, actual encouragement, criminal participation, accessory liability]

\[REVIEW]



In Shaw v TSCCA 22 \[REVIEW], the source states the principle that a person who intentionally offers encouragement to another to commit a crime may nevertheless avoid liability as an abettor if the encouragement did not in fact encourage the principal offender.



The authority supports the proposition that abetting requires more than a willingness to encourage. The encouragement must be communicated in a way that is operative on the person committing the offence. If the principal offender was already fully intent on committing the crime and derived no encouragement from the accused’s conduct, liability under abetting principles may not arise.



This principle narrows the scope of accessorial liability by distinguishing between encouragement that is merely offered and encouragement that actually contributes to the commission of the offence. Because the citation format in the source is incomplete, the chunk is marked \[REVIEW], but the doctrinal point is that abetting requires encouragement that is not merely intended, but effective in the relevant sense.



Tacit Agreement as Evidence of Aiding



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: tacit agreement inferred from continuing participation after knowledge of offending]

\[CONCEPTS: tacit agreement, continuing participation, inferred assistance, knowledge of offending, group offending, aiding by continued involvement]



Tacit agreement may support a finding of aiding where a person, after becoming aware of the principal offender’s conduct, continues to participate in circumstances showing knowing support for the ongoing offending. The doctrine does not require an express agreement in words. Agreement may be inferred from conduct, continued presence, and deliberate association after knowledge of the criminal activity has arisen.



The central question is whether the accused’s continuing involvement after acquiring knowledge of the offending demonstrates intentional assistance or support. A person may be innocent of initial offending if they were unaware of what was proposed, but become liable for subsequent offences once they understand what is occurring and nevertheless continue in a way that assists or facilitates the offending enterprise.



This doctrine is particularly relevant in sequence-based offending where multiple offences occur over time and the accused’s state of knowledge changes during the course of events.



Authority — Knowledge Acquired During Sequence of Offending



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Mundy A58/1966]

\[TOPIC: tacit agreement and aiding after awareness of principal offending]

\[CONCEPTS: sequence of offences, later knowledge, continued participation, inferred aiding, multiple fires, criminal company]



In R v Mundy A58/1966, the source records a case in which one male drove about with another who set multiple fires. The defendant was not found guilty in relation to the first fire because he lacked prior awareness of what the principal offender intended to do. However, after the first offence he became aware of the conduct and was found guilty of aiding in relation to subsequent offences.



The principle illustrated is that liability for aiding may arise once knowledge is acquired and the accused continues to participate in a way that supports the offending. The authority is important because it shows that criminal responsibility may differ across separate offences within the same course of conduct depending on when the accused obtained the necessary knowledge and whether continued participation then amounted to intentional assistance.



Instigation or Incitement — Intention Requirement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: intention required for instigation or incitement]

\[CONCEPTS: instigation, incitement, intention to procure offence, reckless words insufficient, mens rea, urging another to offend]



Instigation or incitement requires more than words that may influence another person. The prosecution must prove an intention that the words or conduct should procure, encourage, or bring about the commission of the offence.



Words spoken recklessly, irresponsibly, or without a genuine intention to cause the offence do not of themselves amount to instigation. The mental element is therefore central. The issue is not merely what the accused said, but whether the accused intended those words or acts to move another person toward the offending conduct.



In practical terms, surrounding circumstances may be critical in proving intention. Meetings with the proposed offender, preparation, payments, arrangements, or coordinated conduct may provide evidence that the accused meant to encourage or procure the offence rather than merely speaking loosely or recklessly. The doctrine therefore requires proof of deliberate urging or procurement, not just provocative or careless language.



Authority — Reckless Words Alone Do Not Amount to Instigation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Buttle v The Queen (Tas R 209)]

\[TOPIC: intention required for instigation or incitement]

\[CONCEPTS: inciting, instigating, reckless words, intention, mens rea, verbal encouragement]



In Buttle v The Queen (Tas R 209), the source states the principle that the use of words alone, without the necessary intention, cannot amount to the crime of instigation or incitement even if those words were spoken recklessly.



The authority supports the rule that instigation requires proof of mens rea directed to bringing about the relevant criminal act. Recklessness in speech is not enough. The legal question is whether the accused intended, by what was said or done, to procure another person to commit the offence.



This principle is significant in cases where the evidence consists primarily of spoken words. The prosecution must identify circumstances supporting an inference that the accused intended those words to operate as a real encouragement or inducement to offend. Absent that intention, criminal liability for instigation does not arise.



Common Purpose — Application to Continuing Offences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: common purpose may apply to continuing offences such as possession]

\[CONCEPTS: continuing offence, possession offence, common purpose, indictable offence, state of affairs, criminal responsibility]



Common purpose liability may apply to continuing offences, including possession offences, where the offence is classified as a crime and the statutory conditions for common purpose are otherwise met. The fact that possession is a continuing state of affairs rather than a single isolated act does not, of itself, prevent the operation of common purpose principles.



The relevant issue is whether the offence is one to which the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) applies as a crime, and whether the accused shared the unlawful purpose in conjunction with another person. If those conditions are met, the continuing nature of the offence does not exclude liability merely because possession can be described as an ongoing state rather than a discrete momentary act.



This doctrine is important because it confirms that joint criminal liability is not confined to offences constituted only by instantaneous acts or omissions. In appropriate cases, possession-based offending can fall within the reach of common purpose analysis.



Authority — Section 4 Can Apply to Possession Offences That Are Crimes



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: common purpose liability and continuing possession offences]

\[CONCEPTS: possession offence, continuing offence, indictable offence, s 4 Criminal Code, common purpose, crime definition]



In Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2, the Court rejected the argument that common purpose under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 could not apply to an offence of possession because possession is a state of affairs rather than an act or omission. The Court held that where the relevant statute makes possession an indictable offence, it is a “crime” for the purposes of the Code, and s 4 may therefore apply.



The authority confirms two points. First, possession can be treated as a crime where the statutory scheme makes it punishable on indictment. Second, the continuing nature of possession does not exclude the application of common purpose liability. The case is therefore significant for rejecting a narrow view that s 4 only applies to momentary acts or omissions.



The same source also notes that s 4 applies only to crimes, not summary offences. That limitation follows from the text of the section and the Code definition of “crime”.



Joint Criminal Enterprise Liability



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: joint criminal enterprise liability]

\[CONCEPTS: joint criminal enterprise, common plan liability, shared criminal purpose, group offending liability, participation in crime, criminal responsibility of accomplices]



Joint criminal enterprise is a common law doctrine under which multiple persons may be held criminally responsible for a crime committed pursuant to a shared criminal understanding. The principle applies where the prosecution proves beyond reasonable doubt that two or more persons reached an agreement or understanding to commit a particular offence and that the offence was carried out in accordance with that understanding.



Where such an understanding exists, each participant may be held responsible for acts carried out by the others in execution of the agreed criminal purpose. Liability does not depend on each participant personally committing every physical element of the offence. Rather, responsibility arises from the shared commitment to the criminal undertaking.



The doctrine historically operated to attribute liability to parties involved in coordinated criminal conduct, particularly where multiple actors performed different roles in the commission of the offence. However, in jurisdictions governed by criminal codes, including Tasmania, statutory provisions governing parties to offences may displace or modify aspects of the common law doctrine.



In Tasmania, issues concerning liability for participation in crime must be considered in light of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas), particularly ss 3 and 4, which define different bases upon which persons may be criminally responsible for offences committed by others. 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(c) — Meaning of “Abets”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(c)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: abetting by encouragement]

\[CONCEPTS: abetting, encouragement of crime, accomplice liability, presence as encouragement, secondary participation, aiding criminal conduct]



Under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(c), a person who “abets” another in committing a crime is criminally responsible for that crime. Judicial interpretation establishes that the term “abets” means “encourages”.



Encouragement may occur through words, conduct, or even mere presence at the scene of the offence where that presence is intended to encourage the offender. The concept therefore captures situations where a person supports or emboldens the commission of the crime without necessarily performing a physical act contributing directly to the offence.



The Tasmanian Court of Criminal Appeal confirmed that encouragement may arise simply from being present during the commission of an offence if two conditions are satisfied:



The person intends their presence to encourage the offender.



The offender is in fact encouraged by that presence.



In such circumstances, the person may incur criminal liability as an abettor even though they perform no direct act contributing to the physical commission of the offence.



This interpretation recognises that encouragement can reinforce criminal conduct and therefore justifies the imposition of criminal responsibility on those who intentionally support offending behaviour. 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(b) — Aiding Another to Commit a Crime



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(b) — Aiding: General Doctrine]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: aiding the commission of a crime]

\[CONCEPTS: aiding, assistance in crime, secondary liability, enabling offence, participation in offence, intentional assistance]



Section 3(1)(b) of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) provides that a person who performs an act or makes an omission for the purpose of enabling or aiding another person to commit a crime is criminally responsible for that crime.



Aiding involves intentional assistance directed toward facilitating the commission of the offence. The assistance may take many forms, including providing tools, transportation, information, or other support that enables the principal offender to carry out the criminal act.



Criminal responsibility arises only where the assistance is provided with the purpose of enabling or aiding the offence. Accidental assistance or conduct lacking the requisite intention will not satisfy the provision.



The section therefore captures persons who play a supportive but significant role in the commission of a crime. Even though they may not perform the acts constituting the offence itself, they are treated as parties to the crime because their deliberate assistance enables its commission. 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Criminal Responsibility for Common Unlawful Purpose



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Common Purpose: Shaw v Tasmania]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: common purpose liability]

\[CONCEPTS: common unlawful purpose, joint criminal responsibility, probable consequence test, group criminal liability, mutual criminal undertaking]



Section 4 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) establishes criminal responsibility arising from participation in a common unlawful purpose. The provision applies where multiple persons embark on a shared criminal undertaking and a crime is committed during the prosecution of that purpose.



Criminal responsibility under s 4 arises where the prosecution proves:



Two or more persons formed a common intention to prosecute an unlawful purpose together.



A crime was committed in the course of carrying out that purpose.



The crime was of such a nature that its commission was a probable consequence of prosecuting that unlawful purpose.



The provision introduces an objective test of probable consequence, meaning liability may arise even where the specific crime committed was not expressly intended by all participants, provided it was objectively foreseeable as a probable outcome of the common purpose.



Importantly, the provision is not limited to unintended crimes. If the crime committed was expressly contemplated by the participants, it will necessarily satisfy the “probable consequence” requirement.



Section 4 therefore defines the outer limits of criminal responsibility for group criminal conduct by linking liability to the objective probability that the offence would arise from the shared unlawful undertaking. 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 50 — Domestic Discipline



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 50]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[TOPIC: parental correction defence]

\[CONCEPTS: domestic discipline, parental correction, reasonable force toward child, lawful chastisement, child discipline defence]



Section 50 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) recognises a limited defence allowing a parent, or a person standing in the place of a parent, to use reasonable force toward a child for the purpose of correction.



The defence applies only where the force used is reasonable in the circumstances. Judicial authority has articulated three key principles governing the scope of the defence:



The punishment must be moderate and reasonable.



The force used must bear a proper relationship to the age, physique, and mentality of the child.



The punishment must be carried out using reasonable means or instruments.



These principles reflect the law’s attempt to balance parental authority in disciplining children with the protection of children from excessive or abusive conduct.



Force that exceeds what is reasonably necessary for correction will not fall within the protection of the defence and may constitute assault or other criminal offences. 



block\_011



Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 34 — Attempt to Commit an Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 34]

\[CITATION: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 34]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: statutory offence of attempt]

\[CONCEPTS: attempt offence, incomplete crime, attempted offence liability, criminal attempt, punishable attempt]



Section 34 of the Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) provides that where an Act creates an offence, any attempt to commit that offence also constitutes an offence and is punishable in the same manner as the completed offence.



This provision establishes a general rule that criminal liability may arise even where the offence is not successfully completed. The focus of attempt liability lies in conduct directed toward committing the offence combined with the necessary criminal intention.



The section ensures that persons who take substantial steps toward committing an offence cannot escape liability merely because the offence was interrupted or unsuccessful. Attempt offences therefore serve a preventative function by criminalising dangerous conduct that demonstrates an intention to commit crime.



Charges for attempt offences typically specify the offence attempted, for example: “Attempting to commit \[specified crime].” 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 299 — Attempts to Commit Crimes



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 299]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 299]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: attempts to commit crimes]

\[CONCEPTS: attempt liability, incomplete crime, criminal attempt, intention to commit offence, preparatory acts]



Section 299 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) provides that any person who attempts to commit a crime is guilty of a crime.



Attempt offences require proof of actual intention to bring about the prohibited consequence. The mental element for attempt is therefore stricter than for many completed offences; recklessness or negligence is generally insufficient.



The requirement of intention reflects the fact that attempts often involve conduct that has not yet caused the harm prohibited by the criminal law. The law therefore requires proof that the accused intended to produce that prohibited result.



Conduct amounting only to mere preparation may not be sufficient to constitute an attempt. The accused must have progressed beyond preparatory steps toward the commission of the offence. However, where acts clearly demonstrate an intention to cause harm and pose a threat to society, criminal liability may arise even if the offence was not completed. 



block\_011



Bail — Primary Purpose and Attendance at Trial



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: purpose of bail]

\[CONCEPTS: bail principles, securing attendance at trial, non-punitive bail, bail risk assessment, court appearance assurance]



The primary purpose of bail is to secure the accused person’s attendance at court while preserving their liberty pending the determination of criminal proceedings. Bail is not intended to punish the accused or restrict their lifestyle before conviction.



The central question in bail determinations is whether there is a reasonable likelihood that the accused will appear at the hearing or trial. Courts therefore assess factors relevant to the accused’s incentive and capacity to attend court.



Relevant considerations commonly include:



The seriousness of the offence.



The strength of the prosecution case.



The likely penalty if convicted.



The accused’s criminal history.



The accused’s history of complying with court orders.



The accused’s personal ties to the jurisdiction.



The risk of further offending.



The risk of interference with witnesses.



These factors are considered collectively to determine whether releasing the accused on bail would undermine the administration of justice or create unacceptable risks to the community or the judicial process. 



block\_011



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 75 — Hearsay in Bail Hearings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 75]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 75]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: hearsay evidence in interlocutory proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: hearsay exception, interlocutory proceedings, bail evidence rules, admissibility of hearsay, evidentiary flexibility]



Section 75 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) creates an exception to the hearsay rule for interlocutory proceedings, including bail hearings.



Under this provision, hearsay evidence is admissible if the party adducing the evidence also adduces evidence of the source of the hearsay statement. Bail hearings therefore operate under more flexible evidentiary rules than criminal trials.



Because bail hearings are preliminary proceedings concerned with risk assessment rather than determination of guilt, the strict evidentiary rules that apply at trial are relaxed. Hearsay evidence may therefore be admitted even though it would ordinarily be excluded in a criminal trial.



However, the weight given to hearsay evidence will depend on the reliability of its source. For example, anonymous information may carry less evidentiary weight than statements from identified witnesses with direct knowledge of the relevant events. 



block\_011



Family Violence Act — Presumption Against Bail



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: presumption against bail in family violence matters]

\[CONCEPTS: family violence bail presumption, reversed onus bail, protection of complainant, safety of affected person, bail refusal presumption]



In family violence matters, legislation establishes a presumption against the grant of bail. The practical effect of this presumption is that the accused bears the burden of demonstrating that bail should be granted.



Courts must be satisfied that releasing the accused would not be adverse to the safety, wellbeing, or interests of the affected person or affected child. This reflects the heightened concern for victim protection in family violence cases.



Despite the presumption against bail, the accused remains entitled to the presumption of innocence in respect of the alleged offences. Courts may nevertheless consider the possibility that the allegations are true when assessing risk to the complainant.



In determining bail applications in family violence matters, courts must evaluate the totality of the circumstances, including the nature of the alleged offending, the accused’s history, the risk of further violence, and the availability of protective conditions. 



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(a) — Principal Liability Does Not Reproduce Joint Criminal Enterprise



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 3(1)(a)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(a)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[CASE: Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: ordinary meaning of “actually commits the crime”]

\[CONCEPTS: principal offender, actually commits the crime, statutory interpretation, joint criminal enterprise excluded, parties to offence, literal meaning, code displacement of common law]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(a) refers to “every person who actually commits the crime”, and that expression must be given its ordinary literal meaning rather than being read as a restatement of common law joint criminal enterprise liability. The Tasmanian Court of Criminal Appeal in Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2 held that s 3(1)(a) does not extend to every person present at the commission of a crime who has earlier reached an understanding that it be committed.



The significance of the decision is that principal liability under s 3(1)(a) is confined to the person who actually commits the offence, not all participants in a criminal arrangement. If another participant is to be held liable, that liability must be identified under another statutory pathway, such as aiding under s 3(1)(b), abetting under s 3(1)(c), or common purpose liability under s 4.



This interpretation rejects any attempt to treat s 3(1)(a) as silently incorporating common law joint criminal enterprise doctrine. In a Code jurisdiction, criminal responsibility must be anchored to the statutory text. The prosecution must therefore prove the accused falls within the specific statutory mode of participation relied upon, rather than assuming that prior agreement alone makes all parties principal offenders. 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Objective “Probable Consequence” Test



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 4]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Objective Probable Consequence Test]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: objective probable consequence test in common purpose liability]

\[CONCEPTS: probable consequence, objective test, common purpose, unlawful purpose, group liability, foreseeable crime, extended criminal responsibility]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 attributes criminal responsibility to participants in a common unlawful purpose where the committed crime was a probable consequence of prosecuting that purpose. The foundation of liability under s 4 is the “mutual embarkation on a crime”, but the extent of responsibility is defined by an objective connection between the shared unlawful purpose and the crime ultimately committed.



The required analysis is:



Two or more persons formed a common intention to prosecute an unlawful purpose together.



A crime was committed in prosecution of that unlawful purpose.



The crime committed was of such a nature that its commission was a probable consequence of prosecuting that purpose.



The provision is not limited to cases where offence B occurs while the parties intended offence A. A crime expressly intended within the common purpose will also satisfy the probable consequence test, because an intended crime will self-evidently be a probable consequence of carrying out that purpose. Section 4 therefore both extends criminal responsibility beyond the exact acts personally done by the accused and also includes intended crimes where the statutory criteria are met.



The doctrine matters because it defines the outer reach of shared criminal responsibility by reference to objective probability, not merely personal contribution under s 3. 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 3 and 4 — Overlap in Attribution of Criminal Responsibility



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 3 and 4]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[CASE: Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: overlap between participation liability and common purpose liability]

\[CONCEPTS: overlapping liability, ss 3 and 4, alternative pathways to liability, prosecutorial discretion, jury directions, complicity, common purpose]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 3 and 4 may overlap, and a participant in a crime may be criminally responsible under both provisions on the same facts. The Tasmanian Court of Criminal Appeal recognised in Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2 that where the crime committed is the very crime intended by the participants, liability will often arise under s 3 as aiding, abetting, or direct participation, and also under s 4 because the intended crime is necessarily a probable consequence of the common unlawful purpose.



This overlap is not treated as a doctrinal problem. The provisions perform separate but related functions. Section 3 identifies liability through forms of participation such as committing, aiding, or abetting. Section 4 defines liability arising from prosecution of a common unlawful purpose where the committed crime bears the required objective connection to that purpose.



The practical concern is not legal inconsistency but forensic complexity. Reliance on both provisions in every case may produce unnecessarily complicated jury directions. The source material indicates that, as a matter of practice, prosecutors should ordinarily resort to s 4 only where there is a realistic need to rely on its extended ambit or where there is clear evidence of a preformed common unlawful purpose additional to inferences drawn from conduct at the scene.



The key principle is that separate statutory bases of complicity may coexist. 



block\_011



Authority — Meaning of “Abets” as Encouragement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: abetting means encouragement under s 3(1)(c)]

\[CONCEPTS: abets means encourages, encouragement by presence, accomplice liability, secondary participation, abetting offence, presence at scene]



Shaw v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 2 supports the principle that, in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 3(1)(c), the word “abets” means “encourages”. The authority is important because it clarifies that abetting is conceptually distinct from physical assistance. A person may be criminally liable not because they helped perform the act, but because they intentionally encouraged another to commit it.



The case also supports the proposition that encouragement may occur through mere presence. Presence alone is not enough. Liability arises only where the person intends by that presence to encourage the offender and the offender is in fact encouraged by it. The authority therefore preserves a meaningful distinction between innocent presence and intentional supportive presence.



The case further confirms that statutory participation provisions must be read according to their own language. It resists collapsing all forms of shared offending into a general common law theory and instead requires attention to the precise statutory pathway relied on. 



block\_011



Authority — Consciousness of Guilt and Particularisation Risks



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Smith-Towns v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 22]

\[TOPIC: risk that over-specific particulars may confine the prosecution case]

\[CONCEPTS: consciousness of guilt, particulars, prosecution confined by particulars, charge framing, forensic limitation, appeal risk, over-particularisation]



Smith-Towns v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 22 is cited in the source as authority relevant to consciousness of guilt and to the forensic risk that over-specific particulars may confine the prosecution case. The practical proposition drawn from the case is that if the Crown pleads particulars too narrowly, the prosecution may later be limited to proving the case in that specific form.



The doctrinal significance is not that particulars are always required to be broad, but that unnecessary specificity can create avoidable forensic constraint. Where the substance of the allegation can lawfully be framed in broader terms, broader particulars may preserve flexibility while still giving fair notice to the defence.
