This is especially important in offences such as assault, where a single incident may arguably amount to threatening force, attempted force, or actual application of force depending on how the evidence is accepted. If the Crown elects one unnecessarily narrow factual pathway, it may later be prevented from relying on another available characterisation of the same conduct.



The source treats the case as Tasmanian support for cautious drafting of particulars so the prosecution is not accidentally boxed into an unduly confined theory of liability. 



block\_011



Authority — Prosecution May Be Confined by Specific Particulars



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Chong \[2012] QCA 265]

\[TOPIC: prosecution confined by specific particulars]

\[CONCEPTS: confined by particulars, broad pleading, prosecution election, notice to defence, charge drafting, forensic flexibility]



R v Chong \[2012] QCA 265 is cited in the source for the principle that the prosecution may be bound by the specific particulars it elects to provide. The practical lesson drawn from the authority is that particulars should be drafted no more narrowly than necessary.



The legal importance of the principle is procedural fairness. Particulars define the factual case the accused must meet. Once the prosecution has specifically identified the factual basis of the allegation, a substantial departure from those particulars may create unfairness and appellate risk. For that reason, prosecutors should avoid pleading a case so specifically that other legally available factual pathways are excluded without need.



The source emphasises the forensic takeaway rather than detailed appellate reasoning: keep particulars broad where lawful, because the defence is then on notice of the available basis or bases upon which guilt may be alleged. Over-particularisation can unnecessarily restrict the Crown’s ability to rely on the full evidentiary picture emerging at trial. 



block\_011



Particularisation — Single Course of Conduct and Alternative Assault Characterisations



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[CASE: R v Chong \[2012] QCA 265]

\[TOPIC: whether one act can be particularised in alternative ways as assault]

\[CONCEPTS: duplicity, particulars, single course of conduct, assault by threat, assault by attempt, alternative characterisation, election by Crown]



Where a single course of conduct may amount to assault in more than one way, the prosecution is not necessarily required to elect a single factual characterisation if the allegation concerns one incident rather than separate criminal acts. The source uses R v Chong \[2012] QCA 265 to illustrate that a single act may arguably constitute a threat to apply force or an attempt to apply force, depending on the evidence accepted.



In the example extracted from the source, the original particulars alleged that the accused “threw, or threatened to throw, a rock or piece of concrete” toward a police officer. The trial judge treated this as duplicitous and required amendment. On appeal, that approach was said to be mistaken because the case concerned one course of conduct, not separate incidents creating latent ambiguity.



The principle is that where one incident is in issue and the evidentiary contest concerns the extent of the conduct, the prosecution may be able to particularise the allegation broadly enough to encompass the available legal characterisations of that single act. The law against duplicity is directed to preventing unfairness where multiple distinct acts are rolled into one count, not to preventing fair pleading of alternative characterisations of one event. 



block\_011



Authority — “Wilfully” in Arson Includes Recklessness



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: B v Visser]

\[TOPIC: meaning of “wilfully” in arson]

\[CONCEPTS: wilfully, recklessness, arson mental element, likely consequence, awareness of risk, deliberate act, reckless indifference]



B v Visser is cited in the source for the principle that “wilfully” in the law of arson includes recklessness. The rule stated is that arson may be committed if the accused deliberately performs a willed act while aware that setting fire to a particular building is a likely consequence, and proceeds regardless of that risk.



The source links this proposition to R v Hodgson \[1985] Tas R 75 and R v Gardenal-Williams \[1989] Tas R 62. The doctrinal point is that the mental element is not confined to a direct intention to burn the building. It is sufficient that the accused deliberately acts with awareness of the likelihood of that result and is recklessly indifferent to it.



This interpretation materially widens the prosecutorial pathway in arson cases. It permits liability where the evidence does not establish a purpose to ignite the building itself, but does establish deliberate conduct accompanied by awareness of a likely fire consequence. The concept of “wilfully” therefore captures both intentional and reckless incendiary conduct of the relevant kind. 



block\_011



Aiding and Abetting — Knowledge of Essential Facts



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Giorgianni v R (1985) 156 CLR 473; B v Visser]

\[TOPIC: knowledge required for aiding and abetting]

\[CONCEPTS: knowledge of essential facts, aiding and abetting, intentional assistance, accomplice mens rea, awareness of criminal facts, secondary liability]



A person cannot be convicted of aiding or abetting unless the prosecution proves that the person knew the essential facts that made the principal offender’s act criminal and intentionally assisted or encouraged that act. The source states this principle by reference to Giorgianni v R (1985) 156 CLR 473 and applies it in the arson context through B v Visser.



In that context, it was necessary to prove not merely that the accused helped with the physical act, such as lighting paper, but that the accused knew the companion contemplated putting burning paper into the letterbox and knew either:



That the companion intended thereby to set the building on fire, or



That the companion was aware of the likelihood of that consequence and was recklessly indifferent to it.



The source further notes that one fact requiring proof was knowledge of the likelihood that putting burning paper in the letterbox could cause the building to catch fire. The doctrine therefore requires informed and intentional participation, not mere association with the principal or accidental assistance. Secondary liability depends on knowledge of the criminally significant facts. 



block\_011



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Alternative Basis to Aiding and Abetting



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 4]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 — Common Intention as Alternative to Aiding]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: B v Visser]

\[TOPIC: common intention under s 4 as alternative to aiding and abetting]

\[CONCEPTS: alternative liability, common intention, s 4 liability, unlawful purpose, joined criminal purpose, secondary participation]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 4 may provide an alternative basis of liability where aiding and abetting is not the only available pathway. The source notes, in the context of B v Visser, that there may be a live issue as to whether the evidence establishes that the accused joined another person in forming a common intention to prosecute an unlawful purpose.



The doctrinal significance is that an accused may be criminally responsible not because they intentionally aided the principal in the narrow s 3 sense, but because they shared a common unlawful purpose and the crime committed was sufficiently connected to that purpose under s 4. Conversely, if the evidence shows only indifference as to whether the companion would pursue the unlawful purpose, rather than shared intention, s 4 may not be engaged.



The distinction matters because s 4 focuses on participation in a shared unlawful enterprise, whereas aiding and abetting requires intentional assistance or encouragement grounded in knowledge of essential facts. In borderline cases, careful analysis is required to identify whether the prosecution case is one of informed assistance, shared common purpose, or both. 



block\_011



Charging as Principal or Accessory



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: charging as principal or accessory]

\[CONCEPTS: principal offender, accessory liability, section 73 Justices Act, section 3 Criminal Code, charge framing, participation in offence]



A participant in offending may be charged either as a principal or through a statutory form of accessorial liability, depending on the nature of the alleged participation and the court process engaged. The source identifies two charging pathways: s 73 of the Justices Act and s 3 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas).



The practical point is that criminal responsibility is not confined to the person who physically performs every element of the offence. Where the evidence supports participation by assistance, encouragement, or other statutorily recognised involvement, the charge may be framed to reflect that mode of liability. The charging decision should align with the evidence and the legal mechanism by which responsibility is said to arise.



This topic is procedurally important because poor alignment between charge form, particulars, and mode of liability can generate avoidable forensic difficulty. The prosecution should identify at the outset whether the case is being advanced as direct commission, aiding, abetting, or some other recognised basis of complicity. 



block\_011



Alternative Convictions for Attempts — Simple Offences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 74]

\[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 74]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: alternative conviction for attempt in simple offences]

\[CONCEPTS: alternative conviction, attempt not completed, simple offence, lesser outcome, prosecution fallback, incomplete proof]



Section 74 of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas) is identified in the source as a provision allowing alternative conviction for an attempt in the context of simple offences where the completed offence is not proved. The principle is that a failure to prove consummation of the offence does not necessarily require full acquittal if the evidence proves an attempt to commit that offence.



This procedural mechanism is important because it preserves a legally available outcome where the evidence establishes intentional conduct directed toward commission of the offence but falls short of proving full completion. It therefore reduces the risk that technically incomplete proof of the completed offence defeats criminal liability altogether.



The source gives the provision as the simple offence counterpart to the indictable attempt provision. In practice, its relevance arises where the factual findings support an attempted form of offending even though one or more elements of the completed offence are not established to the necessary standard. 



block\_011



Alternative Convictions for Attempts — Indictable Crimes



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 342]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 342]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: alternative conviction for attempt in indictable crimes]

\[CONCEPTS: alternative conviction, indictable crime, attempt verdict, incomplete offence proof, jury alternative, fallback conviction]



Section 342 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) is identified in the source as the indictable-crime counterpart to the simple offence mechanism allowing conviction for an attempt where the completed crime is not proven. The principle is that where the evidence establishes intentional acts amounting to an attempt, but not the full offence charged, the tribunal of fact may still return a conviction for the attempted crime if the statutory conditions are met.



The rule recognises that criminal culpability may exist even where the prosecution cannot prove the crime reached completion. It avoids the all-or-nothing consequence that would otherwise arise if the completed offence were the only available verdict.



This provision has practical importance in jury trials and other indictable proceedings because it preserves a lawful intermediate outcome consistent with the evidence. It also underscores the need to distinguish clearly between proof of completed offending and proof of intentional conduct that has gone sufficiently far to amount to criminal attempt. 



block\_011



Bail — Repeated Applications Require Material Change in Circumstances



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[CASE: Trotter v The Queen \[1977] Tas SR 75]

\[TOPIC: repeated bail applications]

\[CONCEPTS: repeat bail application, material change in circumstances, bail already refused, fresh application limits, abuse of process risk, bail finality]



Once bail has been refused on its merits, a second bail application ordinarily ought not be entertained unless there has been a material change in circumstances. The source attributes this principle to Trotter v The Queen \[1977] Tas SR 75 and treats it as a caution against repeated rehearing of substantially the same application.



The principle serves both practical and doctrinal purposes. It promotes finality in interlocutory decisions, prevents repetitive relitigation of the same risk assessment, and ensures that a renewed application is grounded in something genuinely new rather than dissatisfaction with the earlier outcome.



A material change may include new evidence, a significant change in personal circumstances, a deterioration in delay prospects, or some other matter capable of affecting the bail assessment. Absent such change, the court should not ordinarily revisit a refusal simply because a further application is brought.



The rule is not absolute in the sense of jurisdictional prohibition, but it expresses a strong procedural norm governing the proper exercise of discretion on renewed bail applications. 



block\_011



Bail — Public Interest and Risk of Further Offending



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: public interest as a bail consideration]

\[CONCEPTS: public interest, further offending risk, community safety, bail refusal, risk assessment, offending while released]



Public interest may be relevant to a bail decision, particularly where there is a demonstrated basis to contend that the accused is likely to commit further serious offending if released. The source emphasises that public interest is not a free-floating slogan. It must be tied to evidence or a rational basis showing meaningful risk.



The public interest dimension includes community safety and the protection of property, especially where the accused has breached bail before, committed serious offending while subject to bail, or displayed a pattern suggesting that release would create substantial risk of further offending. At the same time, the source notes that this factor should not be given excessive weight merely because it can be invoked broadly.



The doctrinal function of the consideration is to assess whether release would undermine public protection in a way relevant to bail’s purposes. It is not to punish the accused in advance of trial. Public interest arguments are therefore strongest where they are concretely linked to antecedents, the current allegation, prior conduct on bail, or a pattern of escalating or serious offending. 



block\_011



Authority — Offending While on Bail Indicates Disregard for the Law



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Appleby \& McMahon (1966) 1 NSWR 35; R v Gray \[1977] VR 225]

\[TOPIC: significance of offending while on bail]

\[CONCEPTS: offending on bail, contempt for law, disregard of bail, further offending risk, antecedents, public interest]



The authorities cited in the source support the principle that offending while on bail is highly relevant to the assessment of whether further bail should be granted. R v Appleby \& McMahon (1966) 1 NSWR 35 is cited for the relevance of prior offending while on bail, and R v Gray \[1977] VR 225 is cited for the proposition that commission of offences whilst on bail indicates contempt for, or disregard of, the legal system under which bail was granted.



The doctrinal significance is twofold. First, offending on bail is probative of future risk: it suggests the accused may continue offending if released again. Second, it bears on respect for legal obligations and judicial authority, which is relevant to whether the accused is likely to comply with bail conditions and appear as required.



This factor is particularly weighty where the later offending is serious, similar in kind, or committed in circumstances showing escalation. It supports submissions that release would inadequately protect the public or the administration of justice. 



block\_011



Bail — Prior Convictions as Indicators of Propensity and Disregard of Law



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: relevance of prior convictions in bail decisions]

\[CONCEPTS: prior convictions, propensity, disregard for law, criminal history, bail risk, antecedents]



Prior convictions are relevant to bail in at least two distinct ways. First, prior convictions for similar offending may indicate a propensity to commit offences of the kind presently charged. Second, prior convictions for different offending may still be relevant because they may demonstrate a broader lack of respect for the law and legal authority.



The source also notes that subsequent offending committed after the principal offence but before sentencing for it remains relevant in a criminal justice context because it may negate the appearance that the charged conduct was an isolated aberration. In the bail setting, this broader history assists the court in evaluating risk, compliance, and the adequacy of proposed conditions.



The legal value of criminal history therefore lies not simply in counting prior offences, but in analysing what that history shows about future offending risk, willingness to obey court orders, and respect for legal obligations. Similar prior offending and repeated breach-type conduct will usually carry the greatest weight. 



block\_011



Bail — Strength of Prosecution Case, Seriousness, and Likely Penalty



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: seriousness of charge and incentive not to appear]

\[CONCEPTS: strength of case, seriousness of offence, likely penalty, incentive to abscond, likelihood of conviction, bail refusal risk]



The strength of the prosecution case, the seriousness of the charge, and the severity of the likely penalty are central bail considerations because they bear directly on the accused’s incentive to fail to appear. Where the case appears strong and a significant sentence is realistically in prospect, the accused may have an increased motive to avoid further engagement with the criminal justice system.



The source frames this consideration in practical terms: if the accused is likely to go to jail or suffer serious hardship upon conviction, what incentive do they have to appear at the next occasion? This is not punishment by anticipation. It is a rational assessment of flight risk based on the likely consequences of the prosecution succeeding.



An escalating course of conduct involving multiple similar offences may also strengthen the argument against bail, because it combines seriousness, persistence, and exposure to heavier sentencing outcomes. The factor therefore connects evidentiary strength to behavioural prediction: the stronger and more serious the case, the greater the possible incentive to abscond. 



block\_011



Bail — Risk of Tampering With Prosecution Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: witness interference as a bail consideration]

\[CONCEPTS: tampering with witnesses, interference risk, emotional control, complainant pressure, withdrawal statements, bail opposition]



The risk that an accused will interfere with prosecution witnesses is a recognised and important bail consideration. The concern is not limited to overt threats. In family or intimate relationships, interference may occur through emotional pressure, manipulation, or attempts to induce withdrawal or retraction.



The source correctly suggests that a bare assertion of possible interference is not enough. There should ordinarily be something tangible supporting the contention, such as previous threats, evidence of coercive control, emotional abuse allegations, prior attempts to contact the complainant, or patterns such as withdrawal statements from victims.



The reason this factor matters is that bail is partly directed to preserving the integrity of the judicial process. If release creates a real risk that witnesses may be intimidated, influenced, or dissuaded from giving evidence, that risk may justify refusal of bail or the imposition of stringent conditions aimed at non-contact and separation. 



block\_011



Bail — Personal Circumstances and Ties to the Jurisdiction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: personal circumstances relevant to likelihood of appearance]

\[CONCEPTS: ties to state, accommodation, employment, family support, flight risk, local connections, monitoring address]



An accused’s personal circumstances are relevant to bail because they inform the likelihood that the person will remain within the jurisdiction and appear when required. The source identifies common indicators of local attachment: residence, employment, family location, availability of suitable accommodation, and the existence of an address where police can monitor compliance.



The general principle is that the fewer ties the accused has to the area, the less likely the court may be to conclude that they will appear. Social ties, financial position, family support, and stable accommodation may all reduce perceived flight risk. Conversely, unstable living arrangements, weak local ties, or absence of monitoring capacity may increase it.



This factor does not operate mechanically. Strong ties do not guarantee compliance, and weak ties do not compel refusal. The court must assess whether the specific personal circumstances meaningfully support or undermine the conclusion that the accused will engage with the criminal process. 



block\_011



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 4 — Evidence Act Applies to Bail Hearings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 4]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 4 — Application to Bail Hearings]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: application of the Evidence Act to bail hearings]

\[CONCEPTS: Evidence Act applies, bail hearing evidence, interlocutory procedure, evidentiary framework, statutory evidence rules, admissibility]



The source states that the Evidence Act applies to bail hearings by reason of s 4 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas). The doctrinal point is that bail hearings are not evidentiary free zones. They remain governed by statutory evidence law, subject to the specific exceptions and modifications that apply to interlocutory proceedings.



This matters because practitioners must identify not only the general applicability of the Act, but also the particular provisions that relax or modify ordinary trial rules in the bail context, such as the hearsay exception for interlocutory proceedings. The result is a structured but flexible evidentiary environment rather than a wholly informal process.



The source also notes that only the Magistrates Court can deal with breaches, including breaches of Supreme Court bail orders, citing Richardson, Roger v Chatwin, Damien John \[1998] TASSC 118. That authority sits alongside the broader procedural framework governing bail administration and enforcement. 



block\_011



Bail — Burden of Proof and Reduced Evidentiary Rigour



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[CASE: Levy]

\[TOPIC: bail hearings are not formal trials]

\[CONCEPTS: reduced evidentiary standard, bail burden of proof, interlocutory flexibility, preliminary risk assessment, non-trial process, weight not strict proof]



A bail application is not a formal trial, and the evidentiary and burden-of-proof rules are therefore not applied with the same rigour as at final hearing. The source attributes this proposition to Levy and uses it to explain why bail proceedings focus on practical risk assessment rather than definitive adjudication of guilt.



The point is not that there is no burden or no need for reliable material. Rather, the proceeding operates at a lower level of procedural strictness because the court is making an interim decision about liberty, attendance, community protection, and witness integrity. It is not determining criminal guilt.



This doctrinal setting explains why hearsay may be admissible, why evidentiary objections often go more to weight than admissibility, and why the court may act on a broader range of material than would be permissible at trial. Even so, the quality, source, and reliability of the material remain important to the weight the court should give it. 



block\_011



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 94 — Tendency Rules Do Not Apply to Bail Hearings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 94]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 94 — Tendency Rules Inapplicable to Bail]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: tendency evidence rules inapplicable to bail]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, bail hearing, section 94, not applicable, interlocutory evidence, prior conduct material]



The tendency rules do not apply to bail hearings, by reason of s 94 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas), as noted in the source. The importance of this rule is that material which may suggest a tendency to act in a particular way is not excluded at the bail stage merely because it would engage specialised tendency rules at trial.



That does not mean all such material should be accepted uncritically. The court must still consider relevance, source, and weight. However, the doctrinal barrier created by formal tendency evidence rules does not operate in the same way in the bail context.



This reflects the character of bail as an interlocutory risk assessment. Prior conduct, patterns of behaviour, or antecedent material may be relevant to whether the accused is likely to reoffend, interfere with witnesses, or breach conditions, even though the admissibility framework differs from trial practice. 



block\_011



Bail Conditions — Conditions Must Be Necessary and Case-Specific



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[CASE: Levy v Strickland]

\[TOPIC: limits on imposing bail conditions]

\[CONCEPTS: necessary conditions, case-specific conditions, no standard conditions, judicial discretion, linked to bail considerations, conditional bail]



Bail conditions must be justified by the circumstances of the particular case and linked to one of the primary considerations relevant to the grant or refusal of bail. The source, citing Levy v Strickland, states that the onus is on the Crown to affirmatively persuade the court to exercise its discretion to impose conditions and that a judge cannot simply impose general or standard conditions by virtue of the type of offence charged.



The controlling principle is necessity. Conditions should only be imposed where they are needed to address a recognised bail concern such as risk of absconding, further offending, interference with witnesses, or danger to a person. They are not to be used as informal punishment or as a routine administrative add-on.



This means the court must actively consider the individual facts of the case. A condition appropriate in one matter may be unjustified in another, even for the same nominal offence category. Conditions must be reasoned, proportionate, and tied to the purpose they are meant to serve. 



block\_011



Authority — Bail Conditions May Address Absconding, Safety, Further Offending, and Witness Influence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Wooders \[2022] TASSC 15]

\[TOPIC: permissible purposes of bail conditions]

\[CONCEPTS: bail conditions, interests of justice, witness influence, further offending, community safety, risk management]



Tasmania v Wooders \[2022] TASSC 15 supports the principle that bail conditions may be imposed not only to reduce the risk of absconding, but also to address other matters relevant to the bail decision, including risk of further offending, risk to persons, and influence over potential witnesses.



The source quotes the effect of s 7(4) as permitting such other conditions as the judicial officer thinks desirable in the interests of justice. The authority is therefore significant because it rejects an unduly narrow view of conditional bail. Conditions are a risk-management tool directed at the purposes properly engaged by the application.



The principle still operates within the broader requirement that conditions be grounded in the circumstances of the case. The authority does not license arbitrary or generic restrictions. Rather, it explains the legitimate range of objectives that bail conditions may serve once the court is satisfied those objectives arise on the facts. 



block\_011



Bail Conditions — Residence, Separation, and Proximity to the Complainant



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: residential conditions and proximity to complainant]

\[CONCEPTS: residence condition, proximity risk, separation from complainant, monitoring difficulty, surety supervision, family violence bail]



Residence-based bail conditions must be assessed by reference to whether the proposed accommodation genuinely reduces risk. The source highlights that a residence close to the complainant may do little to reduce danger or to ease the complainant’s distress, particularly where the accused’s movements would be difficult to monitor.



The source also notes that a proposed alternative residence may still be inadequate if history shows that the location, or the presence of a family member there, has not prevented prior breaches. The practical question is not simply whether an address exists, but whether that address meaningfully addresses the bail risk identified.



Residential conditions are therefore strongest where they create real physical separation, permit effective supervision, and reduce the prospect of opportunistic contact or breach. They are weaker where proximity remains high, monitoring is poor, or prior conduct shows that nominal separation has not worked in practice. 



block\_011



Bail Conditions — Driving and Alcohol Restrictions Must Be Justified



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[CASE: Brown \& Lowe (1984) Tas R 103]

\[TOPIC: restrictions on driving and alcohol as bail conditions]

\[CONCEPTS: driving restriction, alcohol prohibition, justified conditions, no usual condition, judicial discretion, case-specific bail terms]



Restrictions on driving, alcohol consumption, and related testing should only be sought where the facts justify them, such as where there are prior or multiple prior offences of that kind. The source, citing Brown \& Lowe (1984) Tas R 103, indicates that there are no express textual limitations on the magistrate’s discretion, but necessary limits are implied by the requirement to exercise judicial discretion in relation to the particular application before the court.



The doctrinal point is that the court cannot impose “the usual condition” merely because the offence category makes such a condition seem routine. Conditions must respond to the actual risks demonstrated by the accused’s case. If a driving or alcohol condition is imposed, it should be because it serves a concrete protective or compliance purpose.



This preserves proportionality and guards against conditions becoming formulaic. Bail conditions are discretionary judicial tools, not standardised penalties attached automatically to certain charges. 



block\_011



Family Violence Act s 12 — Presumption Against Bail and Reversed Onus



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act]

\[SECTION: 12]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act s 12]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Re S \[2005] TASSC 89; Olsen v State of Tasmania \[2005] TASSC 40]

\[TOPIC: presumption against bail in family violence matters]

\[CONCEPTS: presumption against bail, reversed onus, family violence, safety of affected person, wellbeing and interests, respondent must displace presumption]



Section 12 of the Family Violence Act creates a presumption against bail in family violence matters. The source states that family violence “creates a presumption against bail” and that the onus is on the respondent to displace that presumption, citing Re S \[2005] TASSC 89 and Olsen v State of Tasmania \[2005] TASSC 40.



The effect of the provision is that the court must refuse bail unless satisfied that release would not be adverse to the safety, wellbeing, and interests of the affected person or affected child. This is a materially different framework from ordinary bail analysis because it reverses the practical burden. The accused must positively satisfy the court on the statutory question.



This does not abolish ordinary fairness or rational assessment. The court must still examine the actual evidence and circumstances. But the statutory starting point is protective rather than neutral. In practical advocacy, this means the prosecution should focus tightly on specific facts showing risk, while the respondent must identify concrete reasons why release will not adversely affect the protected person or child. 



block\_011



Authority — Presumption of Innocence Remains Relevant Under Family Violence Act s 12



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Director of Public Prosecutions (Acting) v J C N \[2015] TASFC 13]

\[TOPIC: presumption of innocence alongside risk assessment under s 12]

\[CONCEPTS: presumption of innocence, chance allegation is true, risk assessment, family violence bail, complainant safety, interim liberty]



Director of Public Prosecutions (Acting) v J C N \[2015] TASFC 13 supports the principle that an accused remains entitled to the presumption of innocence even in a Family Violence Act s 12 bail application. However, in assessing the safety of the complainant if the respondent is released, the court may take into account the chance that the respondent is guilty of the offences alleged.



The authority is important because it explains how two ideas coexist. The court does not treat the accused as convicted, but neither does it ignore the possibility that the allegations are true. Bail under s 12 is therefore a protective risk assessment informed by the allegations, not a final adjudication upon them.



The practical implication is that the prosecution may submit that if the accused is guilty of the conduct alleged, that conduct demonstrates continuing risk to the complainant. This remains consistent with the presumption of innocence because the court is not making a final finding of guilt, but evaluating safety on an interim basis. 



block\_011



Authority — Each Family Violence Act s 12 Bail Application Must Be Considered Anew



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Sowerby v State of Tasmania \[2024] TASFC 7]

\[TOPIC: fresh consideration required on renewed s 12 bail question]

\[CONCEPTS: fresh bail consideration, not confined to latest breach, reconsider all allegations, family violence bail, prior grant of bail irrelevant, renewed application]



Sowerby v State of Tasmania \[2024] TASFC 7 supports the principle that where s 12 of the Family Violence Act arises again, the court must consider the bail question afresh on the material before it. The source emphasises that it is not to the point that the accused had previously been granted bail. The relevant question is whether, on the material presented at the later hearing, bail should now be granted.



The practical significance is that the inquiry is not confined to only the newest allegation or breach. The court is entitled, and required, to consider afresh all pending family violence allegations relevant to the statutory safety assessment. This displaces arguments that the renewed inquiry should focus narrowly on only the immediate breach event.



The source also notes that the Full Court granted bail on the facts of Sowerby, but that outcome was influenced by the absence of further family violence offending or breaches of protective orders. The authority therefore does not dilute s 12. It clarifies the method of fresh assessment and the breadth of material legitimately considered. 



block\_011



Authority — Wishes of the Complainant Are Not Determinative



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: DPP v JCN \[2015] TASFC 13]

\[TOPIC: complainant’s wishes not determinative of bail outcome]

\[CONCEPTS: complainant supports defendant, wishes irrelevant, court protective duty, family violence dynamics, affected person protection, bail decision]



DPP v JCN \[2015] TASFC 13 supports the principle that the wishes of the complainant are not determinative in family violence bail decisions. The source quotes the effect of para 20: there may be many reasons why a complainant acts in support of a defendant, and the court has a duty to consider the interests of persons who may be affected by family violence and, if necessary, protect them even contrary to the complainant’s wishes.



The authority is important because it recognises the complex relational dynamics often present in family violence matters. Support expressed by the complainant may reflect fear, dependence, reconciliation pressure, emotional attachment, or other factors that do not reliably demonstrate absence of risk.



Accordingly, while the complainant’s position may be part of the material before the court, it cannot displace the court’s statutory protective function. The judicial task remains an independent assessment of safety, wellbeing, and interests under the governing legislation. 



block\_011



Family Violence Act s 12(2)(b) — Demeanour as a Legislative Consideration



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act]

\[SECTION: 12(2)(b)]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act s 12(2)(b)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: demeanour as relevant to bail risk]

\[CONCEPTS: demeanour, outward behaviour, arrest conduct, threats in custody, fleeing police, aggressive presentation, family violence bail]



Section 12(2)(b) of the Family Violence Act identifies demeanour as a relevant consideration in the bail assessment. In this context, demeanour refers to the defendant’s outward behaviour or bearing, particularly conduct bearing on risk, self-control, appreciation of the seriousness of the allegations, and willingness to obey legal restraints.



The source gives practical examples: resisting arrest, yelling, fleeing, making threats, contacting the victim while avoiding police, or otherwise displaying conduct showing poor inhibition, aggression, or lack of insight. Threats made even while in custody may be especially powerful, because they may demonstrate continuing volatility despite immediate legal restraint.



The source also notes that drug or alcohol use may be relevant under this heading where it suggests lowered inhibition, erratic behaviour, or reduced capacity to appreciate court orders. The core point is that demeanour is not a vague moral judgment. It is a statutory risk indicator directed to whether release would adversely affect safety and wellbeing. 



block\_011



Authority — Presence of Children Is an Aggravating Feature of Family Violence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Allen v Kerr \[2009] TASSC 10, 19 Tas R 132]

\[TOPIC: children witnessing family violence as aggravation]

\[CONCEPTS: children present, aggravating factor, domestic violence harm, distress to child, desensitisation to violence, family violence sentencing and bail relevance]



Allen v Kerr \[2009] TASSC 10, 19 Tas R 132 supports the principle that the presence of children during family violence is an aggravating feature of significant importance. The source quotes Porter AJ’s explanation that violence witnessed by children in the domestic environment is distressing and may also desensitise impressionable minds to violence and normalise resort to it.



The authority matters because it gives principled content to why children at the scene are relevant. Their presence is not merely an incidental factual detail. It increases the seriousness of the conduct because of both immediate emotional harm and broader developmental harm.



In the source material, this issue is linked both to aggravation under s 13 and to practical advocacy in bail hearings. Where children were present, heard the offending, or suffered its effects, that fact strengthens the prosecution position on seriousness and protective risk. 



block\_011



Family Violence Act s 12(d) — Suitable Accommodation Is a Mandatory Consideration



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act]

\[SECTION: 12(d)]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act s 12(d)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: availability of suitable accommodation]

\[CONCEPTS: suitable accommodation, protected person, affected child, residential safety, failed residential conditions, proximity risk]



Section 12(d) of the Family Violence Act requires the court to consider the availability of suitable accommodation for the person and any affected person or affected child. The source identifies this as a mandatory consideration, not a merely discretionary background factor.



The inquiry is practical. The court should consider whether proposed accommodation has previously failed as a protective mechanism, whether the address is so close to the protected person that its utility is doubtful, and whether residence there would genuinely alleviate risk or mental distress. A nominal address will not satisfy the statutory concern if it does little to reduce opportunity for contact or breach.



This factor often overlaps with conditions of bail, but it remains analytically distinct because the statute specifically directs attention to accommodation as part of the safety assessment. The court must therefore ask whether the living arrangement realistically supports protection of the affected person or child. 



block\_011



Surety — Positive Duty to Take Reasonable Steps to Secure Compliance



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Wooders \[2022] TASSC 15; Mokbel v DPP \[2006] VSC 487; Re Condon \[1973] VR 427]

\[TOPIC: duties of a surety]

\[CONCEPTS: surety obligations, positive duty, reasonable steps, compliance monitoring, recognizance, forfeiture risk]



A surety has a positive duty to take reasonable steps to ensure that the accused complies with bail conditions and attends as required. The source, quoting Tasmania v Wooders \[2022] TASSC 15 and referring to Mokbel v DPP \[2006] VSC 487 and Re Condon \[1973] VR 427, makes clear that it is not enough for a surety to do nothing and later say they believed the accused would comply.



The surety must actively attempt to secure compliance. Evidence that reasonable steps were taken may be relevant, though not determinative, in any later forfeiture application. The doctrine therefore treats a surety as undertaking a real supervisory responsibility, not merely providing a symbolic assurance.



This principle is important both when assessing the suitability of a proposed surety and when evaluating whether bail conditions relying on surety supervision are likely to be effective. A surety without means, commitment, or capacity to supervise may offer little practical protection. 



block\_011



After-Hours Sureties — Practical Requirements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: after-hours surety procedure]

\[CONCEPTS: after-hours surety, recognizance, bench justices, paid surety, police station payment, release process]



After-hours surety arrangements require practical compliance with recognizance and payment procedures before release can occur. The source states that Bench Justices may make orders for recognizance, but the surety must be present to agree to the order, accept the conditions, and sign the relevant paperwork.



The source also notes that Bench Justices may order a paid surety, and that an arrangement exists with Tasmania Police to facilitate payment at the police station. Once the deposit is made and the bail document is signed, Tasmania Police may release the defendant. A similar arrangement exists for payments made after hours in respect of orders made during business hours, with the prison notified once payment is completed.



The doctrinal value of this material is procedural rather than substantive. It explains that the effectiveness of surety-based bail conditions depends not only on legal authority to impose them, but also on practical compliance with formal requirements necessary to activate the release. 



Bail Act 1994 (Tas)

Bail Act 1994 (Tas) — Role and Supervision Function of Bail Sureties



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Bail Act 1994 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Bail Act 1994 (Tas)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: role of bail surety in supervising compliance with bail]

\[CONCEPTS: bail surety, recognizance obligations, supervision of accused, bail compliance monitoring, financial guarantee for bail, bail deterrence mechanism, ensuring court attendance, responsibility of guarantor]



A surety who enters a recognizance in bail proceedings undertakes a substantial legal and practical responsibility to supervise the conduct of the accused and ensure compliance with bail conditions.



The function of a surety is not limited to providing financial security. The recognizance mechanism is designed to operate as both:



A supervisory structure enabling a responsible person to monitor the behaviour of the accused while on bail; and



A deterrent mechanism discouraging breaches by creating financial and moral pressure.



The supervisory role is significant because a surety may be in a practical position to observe the accused’s behaviour and intervene if the accused appears likely to breach bail conditions.



In determining whether a proposed surety is appropriate, courts commonly consider factors such as:



The relationship between the surety and the accused.



The surety’s ability to supervise or monitor the accused.



The surety’s willingness to cooperate with police or authorities if a breach occurs.



The character, reliability, and antecedents of the proposed surety.



Courts may treat family members as less suitable sureties if the evidence suggests they would be reluctant to enforce compliance or report breaches due to personal loyalties.



The recognizance system therefore operates not merely as a financial guarantee but as a behavioural control mechanism designed to encourage compliance with bail obligations.



block\_012



Authority — Obligations and Function of a Bail Surety



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Wooders \[2022] TASSC 15]

\[TOPIC: legal obligations and purpose of recognizance by surety]

\[CONCEPTS: recognizance obligations, bail surety supervision, financial forfeiture for breach, deterrence of bail breach, bail system integrity, moral pressure on accused]



The decision in Tasmania v Wooders \[2022] TASSC 15 confirms the significant role undertaken by a surety who provides a recognizance for a person released on bail.



The Supreme Court emphasised that a surety’s obligation is substantial and must not be entered into lightly. A surety is often better placed than the court to monitor the conduct of the accused and ensure compliance with bail conditions. The recognizance system therefore operates as a mechanism of supervision and accountability.



The court explained that a surety undertakes a real obligation to ensure that the accused:



Appears at court when required; and



Complies with the conditions of bail.



A key purpose of recognizance is deterrence. An accused person may be discouraged from breaching bail conditions by the knowledge that a family member or friend who has agreed to act as surety will suffer financial loss if a breach occurs.



The court also stressed that the integrity of the bail system depends upon meaningful enforcement of recognizance obligations. If courts routinely declined to enforce forfeiture, the deterrent effect of recognizances would be significantly undermined.



block\_012



Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 20 — Estreatment of Surety Recognizances



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Bail Act 1994 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 20]

\[CITATION: Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 20]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: forfeiture of recognizance provided by bail surety]

\[CONCEPTS: estreatment of recognizance, bail surety forfeiture, financial penalty for breach, enforcement of bail security, surety liability, forfeiture proceedings]



Section 20 of the Bail Act 1994 (Tas) governs applications for estreatment (forfeiture) of recognizances provided by sureties.



Where a defendant breaches bail conditions, the court may order forfeiture of the recognizance provided by a surety. This process is distinct from forfeiture under s 19 of the Act, which applies where the accused deposits money personally as security for compliance with bail conditions.



Under s 19, the legislation requires the court to order forfeiture of the full sum unless the convicted person demonstrates excessive hardship or establishes that it would otherwise be just to order only partial forfeiture.



In contrast, s 20 confers a broader judicial discretion when determining the amount to be forfeited from a surety’s recognizance. The court may order forfeiture of:



the entire amount; or



any portion of the amount that the court considers appropriate.



The discretion reflects the different nature of surety recognizances, which are designed to encourage supervision and deterrence rather than merely provide security.



In exercising the discretion, courts may consider factors such as:



The seriousness of the breach.



The extent of supervision exercised by the surety.



Whether the surety took steps to prevent or report the breach.



The broader objective of maintaining the integrity of the bail system.



block\_012



Authority — Principles Governing Forfeiture of Surety Recognizances



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Crown Court at Maidstone; ex parte Lever \[1995] 2 All ER 35]

\[TOPIC: inherent unfairness in surety forfeiture and deterrence function]

\[CONCEPTS: surety hardship, moral pressure on accused, forfeiture deterrence, fairness in bail system, recognizance enforcement]



In R v Crown Court at Maidstone; ex parte Lever \[1995] 2 All ER 35, the court recognised that the surety system may produce outcomes that appear unfair to innocent third parties.



A surety may suffer financial loss even where they personally did nothing wrong and attempted to ensure compliance with bail conditions. However, the court emphasised that this consequence is inherent in the recognizance system.



The system relies on the moral pressure created by the prospect that a trusted family member or friend may suffer financial loss if the accused breaches bail conditions. This pressure encourages compliance with court orders.



If courts routinely accepted arguments that sureties should not suffer loss because they were personally blameless, the deterrent effect of recognizances would disappear. As a result, courts must generally be willing to enforce forfeiture even where the surety acted in good faith.



The principle therefore recognises that some degree of hardship to sureties is unavoidable if the recognizance system is to function effectively as a mechanism for deterring breaches of bail.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 — The Tendency Rule



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 97]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 — Tendency Rule: General]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of tendency evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, character evidence, propensity evidence, significant probative value test, notice requirement, similar conduct evidence, proof of state of mind]



Section 97 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) establishes the statutory tendency rule governing the admissibility of evidence concerning a person’s character, reputation, conduct, or behavioural tendencies.



The rule provides that tendency evidence is not admissible to prove that a person has a tendency to act in a particular way or possess a particular state of mind unless two requirements are satisfied.



The admissibility test requires:



Written Notice Requirement

The party seeking to adduce the evidence must give reasonable written notice to every other party of their intention to rely on the tendency evidence.



Significant Probative Value Requirement

The court must be satisfied that the evidence will have significant probative value either by itself or in combination with other evidence.



The rule is designed to prevent unfair prejudice arising from the admission of character or propensity evidence while allowing genuinely probative evidence to be admitted where it materially assists in proving an issue in dispute.



Subsection (2) provides exceptions to the notice requirement where the evidence is adduced in accordance with directions made under s 100 or where the evidence is introduced to explain or contradict tendency evidence already adduced by another party.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 — The Coincidence Rule



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 98]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 — Coincidence Rule: General]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of coincidence evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, similar fact reasoning, improbability of coincidence, pattern evidence, significant probative value test, multiple events similarity]



Section 98 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) establishes the coincidence rule governing the admissibility of evidence that two or more events occurred.



The rule prohibits reliance on multiple similar events to prove that a person committed a particular act or had a particular state of mind on the basis that the events are unlikely to have occurred coincidentally unless certain conditions are satisfied.



For coincidence evidence to be admitted:



The party seeking to rely on the evidence must provide reasonable written notice to each other party of their intention to adduce the evidence; and



The court must be satisfied that the evidence will have significant probative value, either by itself or in combination with other evidence.



The rule addresses the use of patterns of behaviour as proof of a particular act. Evidence of multiple events may sometimes make coincidence unlikely and therefore support an inference that the accused committed the alleged conduct.



However, because such reasoning carries a risk of unfair prejudice, the statutory safeguards ensure that the evidence is admitted only where its probative value justifies the potential prejudicial impact.



block\_012



Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas) s 17 — Police Power to Take Person into Protective Custody



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 17]

\[CITATION: Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas) s 17]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: protective custody for mental health assessment]

\[CONCEPTS: protective custody mental health, police mental health powers, assessment order, mental illness suspicion, mental health crisis intervention]



Section 17 of the Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas) authorises a police officer or medical officer to take a person into protective custody for the purpose of mental health assessment.



The power arises where the officer reasonably believes that:



The person has a mental illness; and



The person should be examined to determine whether they satisfy the statutory assessment criteria or treatment criteria.



Protective custody under this provision is designed as a protective and therapeutic intervention rather than a criminal process. The purpose is to ensure that a person who may require urgent psychiatric assessment can be safely transported to an assessment centre.



Once a person is taken into protective custody:



they must generally be assessed within approximately 24 hours of arrival at the assessment centre; and



following assessment, they may be detained for up to 72 hours under an assessment order.



If the mental health tribunal subsequently determines that treatment is necessary, a treatment order may be made which can authorise compulsory treatment for up to six months, subject to periodic review.



block\_012



Authority — Order of Calling Witnesses in Criminal Trials



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: RPS v The Queen (2000) 199 CLR 620]

\[TOPIC: accused not required to give evidence before other defence witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: order of witnesses, defence evidence sequence, accused right not to testify, trial procedure, calling defence witnesses]



The High Court in RPS v The Queen (2000) 199 CLR 620 confirmed that there is no legal rule requiring an accused person to give evidence before other witnesses called by the defence.



Although there is sometimes a practical convention that the accused testifies before other defence witnesses, this is not a requirement of law. A trial judge who instructs counsel that the accused must give evidence before calling other witnesses is mistaken in law.



The case illustrates that the order in which defence witnesses are called is ultimately a matter for the defence. The accused retains the right to decide whether to give evidence and cannot be compelled to testify before other witnesses are called.



The decision therefore reinforces two important procedural principles:



The defence controls the order of its witnesses; and



The accused’s right not to give evidence must be respected.



Bail Act 1994 (Tas)

Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 25 — Revocation of Bail by the Court on Its Own Motion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Bail Act 1994 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 25]

\[CITATION: Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 25]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: court power to revoke bail on its own motion]

\[CONCEPTS: revoke bail, court own motion, bail cancellation, defendant present in court, post-guilt bail revocation, probable guilt, bail status review]



Section 25 of the Bail Act 1994 (Tas) provides a power for the court to revoke bail on its own motion in specified circumstances.



The source material indicates that this power arises where a defendant is before the court and there is probable guilt or a finding of guilt. The text also notes an arguable position that the power may be available whenever a defendant appears on an adjournment order under s 25, although that proposition is not stated as settled law and should be treated cautiously.



A critical procedural point identified in the source is that the wording of s 25 requires the defendant to be present before the order revoking bail is made. On that reading, the court’s power is tied to the accused being physically before the court when the revocation decision is made.



The practical significance of the provision is that the court does not need to wait for a separate application by a party if the statutory preconditions are met. The court may itself reconsider whether the accused should remain at liberty on existing bail.



Because the source raises uncertainty about the outer scope of the provision, particularly in relation to adjournment appearances, any reliance on that broader interpretation should be marked for review.



\[REVIEW]



Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 24 — Applications to Revoke Bail Must Be Made in the Prescribed Manner



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Bail Act 1994 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 24]

\[CITATION: Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 24]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: formal application procedure to revoke bail]

\[CONCEPTS: revoke bail application, prescribed form, affidavit requirement, bail regulations, formal bail application, procedural compliance, application to cancel bail]



Section 24 of the Bail Act 1994 (Tas) governs applications by a party to revoke bail where the court is not acting on its own motion under s 25.



The rule explained in the source is that an application under s 24 must be made in the prescribed manner. The source further states that the Bail Regulations, including regulation 10, require the application to be made in a specific form and to be supported by an affidavit.



The procedural effect of this requirement is that a revocation application is not merely an informal oral request unless the circumstances otherwise lawfully permit it. A party seeking revocation must comply with the prescribed formality requirements so that the court has a proper procedural basis for hearing the application.



The affidavit requirement is significant because it provides the evidentiary foundation for the application. It gives notice of the factual matters relied upon to justify revocation and allows the court to assess whether the existing grant of bail should be revisited.



The source also identifies a practical difficulty where the defendant does not answer the application. In that event, there is said to be no express power under the Bail Act 1994 (Tas) to issue a warrant solely because the defendant fails to answer notice of the revocation application, prompting reliance on the Justices Rules as a possible procedural solution.



block\_012



Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 23 — Application to Vary Bail Conditions



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Bail Act 1994 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 23]

\[CITATION: Bail Act 1994 (Tas) s 23]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: application to vary bail conditions]

\[CONCEPTS: vary bail, bail variation, application to change bail conditions, prescribed form, supporting affidavit, Magistrates Court bail procedure]



Section 23 of the Bail Act 1994 (Tas) permits a person charged with an offence, breach of duty, or subject to an application for a restraint order to apply for bail or to vary existing bail conditions during the proceedings.



The source explains that where the application is not made orally at a mention or similar appearance, the application must be supported by affidavit material. This requirement is drawn from Magistrates Court Practice Direction 6 of 2020 and operates alongside the prescribed form requirements under the Bail Regulations 2014.



For an application to vary bail, the supporting affidavit or statutory declaration should:



Detail the reasons for seeking the variation.



Identify the precise variations sought.



Provide notice to the other parties of the factual basis for the application.



The procedural rationale for requiring supporting material is to ensure fairness and adequate notice. A variation application may affect risk management, compliance expectations, and the position of the prosecution or police, so the reasons for the proposed change must be clearly set out.



The rule therefore treats formal bail variation applications as evidence-based applications requiring proper written notice rather than informal requests unsupported by sworn material.



block\_012



Practice Direction 6 of 2020 — Supporting Documentation for Bail and Bail Variation Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: affidavit requirement for bail and bail variation applications]

\[CONCEPTS: Practice Direction 6 of 2020, supporting affidavit, statutory declaration, bail application documents, vary bail paperwork, notice to parties, Magistrates Court practice]



Practice Direction 6 of 2020 requires that an application for bail or to vary bail lodged in the Magistrates Court be supported by an affidavit or statutory declaration explaining the basis of the application.



The source states that this requirement applies upon lodgement of the application and is intended to ensure that all parties are provided with notice of the reasons why bail is sought or why existing bail conditions should be changed.



In the specific case of an application to vary bail, the supporting material should not merely state that variation is sought. It should set out the actual changes proposed and the reasons those changes are said to be justified.



The requirement performs several procedural functions:



It gives the court an evidentiary basis for determining the application.



It provides procedural fairness to the responding party.



It narrows the issues by identifying the exact variation sought.



It reduces the risk of surprise or informal, unsupported applications.



The source links this practice requirement to the prescribed form regime under the Bail Regulations 2014. Accordingly, the supporting affidavit or statutory declaration is an important procedural component of a properly made bail or bail variation application.



block\_012



Justices Rules 2003 / Justices Act 1959 (Tas)

Justices Rules 2003 r 62 and r 34 — Procedural Response Where Defendant Does Not Answer Bail Revocation Application



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Rules 2003]

\[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 rr 34, 62]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: use of Justices Rules to deal with non-appearance on bail revocation application]

\[CONCEPTS: non-appearance on application, summons read as application, Justices Rules r 62, dismissal for non-appearance, procedural adaptation, bail revocation process]



The source identifies a procedural issue where a defendant does not answer notice of an application to revoke bail. It notes that the Bail Act 1994 (Tas) does not expressly confer a specific power to issue a warrant merely because the respondent defendant fails to appear in answer to the application.



The proposed solution recorded in the source is reliance on rule 62 of the Justices Rules 2003, with the argument that a necessary modification of Part 4 allows the word “summons” to be read interchangeably with “application” in this context.



The reasoning given in support of that approach is that rule 34(3) appears to contemplate a magistrate having power to dismiss an application if nobody appears. The source treats that feature as supporting the view that the Justices Rules can be adapted procedurally to deal with the absence of a respondent on a bail revocation application.



This is not presented in the source as a settled appellate determination, but as a practical procedural solution. The proposition should therefore be treated as arguable rather than definitive unless further authority is identified.



\[REVIEW]



block\_012



Authority — Failure to Answer Court Call May Constitute Failure to Appear



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Gandini v Tasmania Police \[2021] TASSC 17]

\[TOPIC: failure to answer court call and issue of warrant]

\[CONCEPTS: failure to appear, refusing to identify self in court, court call over, warrant for arrest, sovereign citizen conduct, non-response in court]



The authority of Gandini v Tasmania Police \[2021] TASSC 17 is cited in the source for the proposition that where a person is called in court and refuses to accept or acknowledge their name, the court may treat that conduct as a failure to appear and issue a warrant.



The practical context described in the source is the situation sometimes encountered with defendants who refuse to recognise the court process or decline to identify themselves when the matter is called. The principle extracted is that a physical presence in the courtroom is not necessarily enough if the defendant refuses to respond in a way that permits the court to proceed.



The rule supported by the case is that failure to answer the court call can amount to failure to appear for procedural purposes. In that event, the court may issue a warrant even though the defendant is physically present but is refusing to participate or acknowledge the proceeding.



The principle is important because it prevents a defendant from defeating the court process by deliberate non-engagement. Attendance requires more than bodily presence; it requires sufficient participation to permit the court to identify the defendant and deal with the matter.



block\_012



Justices Rules 2003 r 31 — Ex Parte Hearing Where Defendant Does Not Appear



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Rules 2003]

\[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 r 31]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: ex parte hearing in defendant’s absence]

\[CONCEPTS: ex parte hearing, defendant absent, complaint heard without defendant, magistrate discretion, summary procedure, proceeding in absence]



Rule 31 of the Justices Rules 2003 provides one option where a defendant does not turn up for the hearing of a complaint. The court may proceed to hear the matter ex parte.



The source presents ex parte hearing as one of several possible procedural responses to non-appearance. It is not described as automatic. Rather, whether the matter proceeds in the absence of the defendant remains a matter for judicial discretion.



An ex parte hearing permits the court to receive and determine the complaint without the defendant being present. The use of the procedure depends upon the statutory and procedural setting, the nature of the offence, and whether the court considers it fair and appropriate to proceed.



The source also warns that ex parte hearing is “not always ok to do,” indicating that the power must be exercised cautiously. That caution is reinforced by the separate authority of Woodward v Moore \[2017] TASSC 63, which is specifically identified as important on the limits of proceeding ex parte.



The rule should therefore be understood as a discretionary procedural mechanism rather than a default consequence of every failure to appear.



block\_012



Authority — Caution in Proceeding Ex Parte



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Woodward v Moore \[2017] TASSC 63]

\[TOPIC: limits on propriety of ex parte hearing]

\[CONCEPTS: ex parte caution, fairness in absence of defendant, non-appearance procedure, hearing without accused, procedural fairness, magistrate discretion]



The authority of Woodward v Moore \[2017] TASSC 63 is cited in the source as an important reminder that proceeding ex parte is not always permissible or appropriate.



The legal principle supported by the citation is that a court must exercise caution before determining a matter in the defendant’s absence. The existence of procedural power to proceed ex parte does not eliminate the need to consider fairness, adequacy of service, the nature of the matter, and whether the circumstances justify hearing the case without the defendant.



The source’s instruction to “READ this case!!!” reflects its significance as authority against treating ex parte procedure as routine. The principle is that non-appearance does not automatically justify final determination. The court must consider whether the exercise of the power would produce procedural unfairness or an unjust outcome.



This authority is therefore relevant whenever the prosecution invites the court to proceed in the absence of a defendant under the Justices Rules 2003.



block\_012



Justices Rules 2003 r 32 — Personal Service Order or Warrant Following Non-Appearance



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Rules 2003]

\[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 r 32]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: orders for personal service or arrest warrant after non-appearance]

\[CONCEPTS: personal service order, arrest warrant, previous service, defendant absent, compulsory attendance, magistrates procedure]



Rule 32 of the Justices Rules 2003 provides a further procedural option where a defendant does not appear. Instead of proceeding immediately ex parte, a party may seek either an order for personal service or a warrant of arrest, subject to the service history of the matter.



The source identifies this as an alternative to an ex parte hearing under rule 31. The distinction is important. Rule 31 is concerned with determining the matter despite absence, whereas rule 32 allows the court to compel or better secure the defendant’s attendance before the matter is finally heard.



The practical choice between those options will depend on matters such as:



Whether service has already been properly effected.



The seriousness of the allegation.



Whether fairness requires another opportunity to attend.



Whether the court considers immediate ex parte determination inappropriate.



The rule therefore supports a staged procedural response to non-appearance. Rather than defaulting to final adjudication in absence, the court may require personal service or issue a warrant to ensure the defendant is brought before the court.



block\_012



Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 72D — Ex Parte Determination of Prescribed Complaint and Summons Matters



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 72D]

\[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 72D]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: complaint and summons as prima facie evidence in prescribed matters]

\[CONCEPTS: s 72D, prescribed offences, complaint and summons evidence, prima facie evidence, ex parte conviction, defendant absent, summary complaint procedure]



Section 72D of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas) provides a specific ex parte procedure for prescribed matters where a defendant fails to appear in answer to a summons.



The source explains that the section applies only where the charge falls within offences prescribed under Schedule 2 to the Justices Rules 2003. In those circumstances, the matter may be dealt with so that the complaint and summons are received as prima facie evidence.



The provision depends upon rule-making power under s 144(4)(ca), which authorises rules about circumstances in which a defendant need not appear in answer to a summons. Rule 27 of the Justices Rules 2003 refers to Schedule 2, which identifies the prescribed offence categories.



The source emphasises two important limits:



It remains within the discretion of the justices whether to hear the complaint ex parte.



The section cannot be used to prove facts not alleged in the complaint itself.



The practical consequence is that in prescribed low-level matters, if the defendant does not attend, the court may rely on the complaint and summons as sufficient evidence unless the material is rebutted or shown to be legally inadequate.



block\_012



Justices Rules 2003 Schedule 2 — Prescribed Offences for s 72D Procedure



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Rules 2003]

\[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 Sch 2]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: categories of offences prescribed for ex parte determination]

\[CONCEPTS: Schedule 2 offences, prescribed summary matters, ex parte offence list, non-appearance offences, complaint and summons matters, no imprisonment caveat]



Schedule 2 to the Justices Rules 2003 identifies the categories of offences that may be dealt with under the special ex parte procedure in s 72D of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas).



The source lists common examples, including offences under:



Sections 5(4) and 9 of the Bail Act 1994 (Tas).



Numerous provisions of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas), including public annoyance, public decency, dispersal, dangerous articles, obstruction of public officers, and certain property-related offences.



Section 7 of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas).



Offences under the Road Rules 2009.



Sections 41(1) and 51(3) of the Traffic Act 1925 (Tas).



Offences under the Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas), subject to specified exceptions.



The source also records an important caveat: the offence must not carry imprisonment or community corrections as a penalty. That limitation reflects the summary, documentary nature of the ex parte procedure.



The list of prescribed offences matters because s 72D is not a general power for all summary matters. It only operates where the offence has been specifically prescribed through the rules.



block\_012



Authority — Complaint and Summons Under s 72D Cannot Prove Matters Not Alleged



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Howie v Gordon (1986) 60 LGRA 36]

\[TOPIC: evidentiary limits of complaint and summons as proof]

\[CONCEPTS: complaint allegations, summons evidence limit, facts must be pleaded, ex parte proof boundaries, complaint form validity]



The authority of Howie v Gordon (1986) 60 LGRA 36 is cited in the source for the proposition that complaint and summons material received under the ex parte procedure cannot be used as proof of something not alleged in the complaint.



The legal principle is that even where a statute treats the complaint and summons as prima facie or sufficient evidence, the evidentiary effect of those documents is confined to the facts properly set out in the complaint. The prosecution cannot rely on the mechanism to fill gaps in the pleaded factual basis of the offence.



This rule is important because the ex parte procedure is exceptional. It permits proof by documentary material in the absence of a defendant, but only within the boundaries established by the complaint itself. If a relevant fact is not alleged, the statutory evidentiary shortcut does not create it.



The case therefore supports a strict approach to the content and drafting of summary complaints where the prosecution intends to rely on ex parte proof procedures.



block\_012



Authority — Proper Form of Complaint Is Required Before Evidentiary Consequences Arise



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Hush; Ex parte Devanny (1932) 48 CLR 487]

\[TOPIC: complaint must be in proper form before statutory proof consequences operate]

\[CONCEPTS: proper form complaint, formal validity, statutory evidentiary consequence, summary complaint drafting, legal sufficiency of complaint]
