The authority of R v Hush; Ex parte Devanny (1932) 48 CLR 487 is cited in the source for the principle that a statutory provision giving evidentiary effect to a complaint presupposes that the complaint is in proper form.



The principle extracted is that the legislation does not validate a defective complaint. Rather, it states the legal consequences that follow if there is already a complaint in proper form. The document must therefore satisfy the formal and substantive requirements of a valid complaint before the statutory evidentiary shortcut can operate.



In practical terms, this means that the prosecution cannot rely on documentary proof provisions to cure defects in the initiating process. If the complaint is materially deficient, the statutory rule treating its contents as prima facie evidence does not assist.



The principle is especially important in ex parte proceedings, because the court may be invited to act on the complaint without oral evidence. In that context, the validity and sufficiency of the complaint itself becomes central.



block\_012



Authority — Complaint Allegations Must Constitute the Offence Charged



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Ex parte Ryan; Re Johnson (1943) 44 SR (NSW) 12]

\[TOPIC: complaint that does not allege offence cannot sustain conviction]

\[CONCEPTS: allegations insufficient, offence not made out on complaint, summary conviction limit, defective complaint, elements not pleaded]



The authority of Ex parte Ryan; Re Johnson (1943) 44 SR (NSW) 12 is cited in the source for the proposition that if the factual allegations contained in the complaint do not constitute the offence charged, the statutory ex parte procedure does not enable the justices to convict.



The legal principle is straightforward. A complaint must allege facts that, if accepted, amount to the legal elements of the offence. If the complaint fails at that level, a statutory rule giving evidentiary effect to the complaint cannot convert an invalid or legally insufficient allegation into a valid conviction.



This principle protects against convictions based solely on defective pleadings. It ensures that the prosecution still bears the burden of properly alleging the essential ingredients of the offence, even in documentary or ex parte proceedings.



The authority therefore supports close scrutiny of the complaint itself before any reliance is placed on statutory provisions treating that document as prima facie evidence.



block\_012



Legal Doctrine — Meaning of “Prima Facie Evidence” in Complaint and Summons Procedure



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: meaning of prima facie evidence in summary proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: prima facie evidence, sufficient evidence unless rebutted, complaint and summons proof, evidentiary threshold, rebuttable proof, documentary evidence]



The term “prima facie evidence” in the context of complaint and summons procedure refers to evidence that is sufficient to prove the matter unless and until it is rebutted.



The source records a line of authorities stating that the phrase probably means “sufficient evidence” until rebutted. The practical effect is that where a statute provides that a complaint or summons is prima facie evidence of certain matters, the court may act upon that material in the absence of contrary evidence, provided the complaint is otherwise valid and properly alleges the offence.



The doctrine does not mean conclusive proof. Rather, it creates a rebuttable evidentiary position. If the defendant appears and adduces evidence or otherwise undermines the asserted facts, the prima facie status may be displaced.



The concept is important in ex parte and summary procedures because it explains why documentary material can be enough to support a finding in straightforward prescribed matters, while still preserving the possibility of rebuttal or later setting aside.



Authorities cited in the source for this meaning include Galvanized Iron Co v Westoby (1852) 8 Ex 17, Hewitt v Taylor \[1896] 1 QB 287, Garbutt v Durham Joint Committee \[1906] AC 291, and Barraclough v Greenhough \[1867] LR 2 QB 612.



block\_012



Justices Rules 2003 r 38 — Setting Aside Ex Parte Convictions and Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Rules 2003]

\[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 r 38]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: power to set aside ex parte convictions and orders]

\[CONCEPTS: set aside ex parte conviction, reopen summary order, relief from absence judgment, defendant absent remedy, rule 38]



Rule 38 of the Justices Rules 2003 provides for the setting aside of ex parte convictions and orders.



The source refers to this provision to explain why cautionary statements from older interstate authority about ex parte hearings may have more limited force in Tasmania. Because Tasmania provides a formal mechanism for setting aside ex parte outcomes, the procedural system contains a corrective safeguard where a matter has been determined in the absence of the defendant.



The existence of this rule does not remove the need for caution before proceeding ex parte. However, it does affect the overall fairness analysis by recognising that a defendant is not necessarily left without remedy if an ex parte order has been made.



The rule is therefore a significant part of the broader procedural framework governing non-appearance in the Magistrates Court. It balances efficiency in dealing with unattended matters against the need to avoid injustice where the absence should not have resulted in final determination.



block\_012



Authority — Cautionary Approach to Ex Parte Hearings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Walker v Eves (1976) 13 SASR 249]

\[TOPIC: caution in hearing complaints ex parte]

\[CONCEPTS: ex parte caution, hearing without defendant, judicial restraint, fairness in summary procedure, absent defendant]



The source cites Walker v Eves (1976) 13 SASR 249 for cautionary dicta concerning the hearing of complaints ex parte.



The legal significance of the citation is that ex parte procedure should not be treated casually. Determining a complaint without the defendant present creates obvious risks to procedural fairness and accuracy. Courts should therefore remain cautious in deciding whether the circumstances justify proceeding in absence.



The source also notes that the force of that caution is somewhat limited in Tasmania because of the existence of rule 38 of the Justices Rules 2003, which allows ex parte convictions and orders to be set aside. Even so, the authority remains relevant as a reminder that judicial discretion to proceed ex parte should be exercised carefully.



The principle supported by the case is not that ex parte procedure is unlawful, but that it must be used with restraint and with close attention to the fairness of the particular circumstances.



block\_012



Sentencing Principles / Family Violence / Bail Context

Authority — Court Must Protect Persons Affected by Family Violence Despite Complainant Support for Defendant



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: DPP v JCN \[2015] TASFC 13]

\[TOPIC: complainant support does not remove court’s duty to protect in family violence context]

\[CONCEPTS: family violence risk, complainant supports accused, victim protection, court protective duty, bail risk, disregard of court orders]



The Full Court in DPP v JCN \[2015] TASFC 13 is cited in the source for the principle that a court must consider the interests of persons who may be affected by family violence and, if necessary, protect them even where the complainant appears to support the defendant.



The source quotes Pearce J as observing that there are many possible reasons why a complainant may act in support of a defendant. That reality means the court cannot simply treat the complainant’s wishes as determinative of the risk question.



The principle supported by the case is that in family violence matters the court has an independent protective duty. It may therefore act contrary to the wishes of the affected person if that is required to protect safety and wellbeing.



The same source material also links the case to concerns about the defendant having shown disregard for the law and contempt for the force of court orders. In that context, the complainant’s continued association with the defendant does not itself neutralise the objective risk presented by repeated breaches or violent conduct.



The authority is therefore important in bail and protective decision-making where the complainant’s subjective position may not align with the court’s duty to manage risk.



block\_012



Authority — Continuing to Offend While on Bail Is an Aggravating Factor



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Wisniewski v Tasmania \[2007] TASSC 25]

\[TOPIC: aggravating significance of offending while on bail]

\[CONCEPTS: offending on bail, aggravating factor, contempt for law, repeat offending, persistence of conduct, sentencing seriousness]



The authority of Wisniewski v Tasmania \[2007] TASSC 25 is cited in the source for the principle that continuation of criminal conduct after being charged and granted bail is frequently regarded as an aggravating factor on sentence.



The quoted passage explains why. Reoffending while on bail demonstrates contempt for the law and authority. It shows that the offender has been entrusted to remain in the community subject to conditions, yet has continued to commit offences despite that trust.



The case also supports the related proposition that repeated offending after charge may justify increased weight being given to retribution, deterrence, and protection of society. Persistence of conduct while subject to bail conditions increases the gravity of the offending beyond the underlying offences themselves.



The source uses the authority in connection with repeated family violence breaches, dishonesty offending, and failures to appear. It therefore supports a broad sentencing principle: post-charge offending is relevant not merely because it adds more offences, but because it evidences a continuing attitude of disobedience to legal restraint.



block\_012



Authority — Repeated Breaches of Orders and Ongoing Offending Reflect Contempt for Court Restraints



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Maingay v Seabourne \[2009] TASSC 67]

\[TOPIC: repeated breaches of family violence and bail orders aggravate sentence]

\[CONCEPTS: repeated breaches, police family violence order, contempt for orders, relationship continuation irrelevant, sentencing aggravation, ongoing dishonesty offending]



The source cites Maingay v Seabourne \[2009] TASSC 67 as an authority illustrating how repeated offending while on bail and repeated breaches of police family violence orders aggravate sentence.



The case material reproduced in the source identifies several connected principles:



Offending while on bail demonstrates contempt for legal authority.



Breach of a family violence order remains serious even if the protected person may have approached the defendant.



Continued relationship with the complainant does not reduce culpability for assaults, threats, or order breaches.



Repeated dishonesty offences while on bail reinforce the inference that the offender treated both bail and protective orders with contempt.



Failure to appear for sentence can further aggravate the overall position.



The principle emerging from the cited passages is that repeated disobedience of court or police-imposed restraints is highly relevant to sentencing because it shows persistence, disregard for authority, and failure to be deterred by earlier intervention.



The authority therefore supports both aggravation analysis and risk-based assessments in later bail-related proceedings.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) — Definition of “Criminal Proceeding”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) — Definition of Criminal Proceeding]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: meaning of criminal proceeding under the Evidence Act]

\[CONCEPTS: criminal proceeding definition, bail proceeding included, committal included, sentence proceeding, Evidence Act scope, offence prosecution]



The Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) defines “criminal proceeding” broadly to include more than a prosecution at trial.



The source states that a criminal proceeding means a prosecution for an offence and includes:



A proceeding for the committal of a person for trial or sentence for an offence.



A proceeding relating to bail.



This definition matters because later provisions in the Act, including the rules governing tendency and coincidence evidence, operate by reference to whether a matter is a criminal proceeding. The inclusion of bail proceedings is especially significant because it confirms that bail matters fall within the Act’s framework unless a provision expressly excludes them.



The definition therefore establishes that evidentiary rules in the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) may potentially apply to interlocutory and pre-trial processes, not only to the final hearing of criminal charges. Whether a particular Part of the Act actually applies to bail or sentencing then depends on the specific exclusion or application provisions within that Part.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 4 — Proceedings to Which the Act Applies



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 4]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 4 — Scope of Proceedings]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: scope of proceedings covered by the Evidence Act]

\[CONCEPTS: Act applies to bail, interlocutory proceedings, chambers proceedings, sentencing proceedings, Tasmanian court proceedings, Evidence Act scope]



Section 4 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) sets out the proceedings to which the Act applies.



The source records that the Act applies to all proceedings in a Tasmanian court, including proceedings that:



Relate to bail.



Are interlocutory proceedings or proceedings of a similar kind.



Are heard in chambers.



Subject to s 4(2), relate to sentencing.



This is an important interpretive provision because it establishes a broad starting point for the Act’s operation. Rather than being confined to contested trials, the evidentiary regime applies across a wide range of court processes.



The breadth of s 4 also helps explain why later provisions need to carve out exceptions. For example, Part 6 on tendency and coincidence specifically excludes bail and sentencing matters in s 94. Without that exclusion, the general application provision in s 4 would indicate that those proceedings are within the Act’s scope.



Section 4 therefore provides the general rule of application, while later Parts may narrow that operation in relation to particular types of evidence or proceedings.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 94 — Part 6 Does Not Apply to Bail or Sentencing Proceedings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 94]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 94 — Part 6 Non-Application to Bail and Sentencing]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: non-application of tendency and coincidence rules to bail and sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: Part 6 application, bail exclusion, sentencing exclusion, tendency rule not apply, coincidence rule not apply, credibility only evidence]



Section 94 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) defines the application of Part 6, which contains the tendency and coincidence rules.



The source records three limits on the Part’s operation:



Part 6 does not apply to evidence that relates only to the credibility of a witness.



Part 6 does not apply so far as a proceeding relates to bail or sentencing.



Part 6 does not apply to evidence of a person’s character, reputation, conduct, or tendency where that matter itself is a fact in issue.



The second of those limits is especially important in criminal practice. Although bail proceedings are included within the broader definition of criminal proceeding and the Act generally applies to bail matters, the special restrictions in Part 6 on tendency and coincidence evidence do not apply in bail or sentencing contexts.



The practical effect is that a party in bail or sentencing proceedings is not required to satisfy the statutory tendency or coincidence admissibility tests in order to rely on prior conduct or pattern evidence, though ordinary relevance, fairness, and any other applicable rules still matter.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 95 — Evidence Inadmissible for One Purpose Cannot Be Used for That Prohibited Purpose



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 95]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 95]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: prohibited use of tendency or coincidence evidence for barred purpose]

\[CONCEPTS: use for other purpose, inadmissible purpose, tendency evidence misuse, coincidence evidence misuse, evidentiary purpose limitation, cannot backdoor use]



Section 95 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) prevents a party from using evidence to prove a matter for which the evidence is inadmissible under Part 6, even if the evidence is relevant for another purpose.



The source reproduces both limbs of the section:



Evidence that is not admissible under Part 6 to prove a particular matter must not be used to prove that matter even if it is relevant for another purpose.



Evidence that cannot be used against a party to prove a particular matter must not be used against that party to prove that matter even if it is relevant for another purpose.



The section therefore guards against backdoor reasoning. A party cannot circumvent the tendency or coincidence rules by tendering the evidence under a different label and then inviting the court to use it for the prohibited inferential purpose.



The provision is significant because it enforces the substantive limits imposed by Part 6. Without s 95, the notice and probative value requirements in ss 97 and 98 could be undermined by repurposing the evidence after admission.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 96 — Failure to Act Is Included Within Reference to Doing an Act



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 96]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 96]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: failure to act included in Part 6 references to doing an act]

\[CONCEPTS: omission included, failure to act, tendency by omission, coincidence by omission, interpretive extension]



Section 96 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides that a reference in Part 6 to doing an act includes a reference to failing to do that act.



This is a short but important interpretive rule. It clarifies that the tendency and coincidence provisions are not confined to positive conduct. A tendency to omit action, or a pattern of failures to act, may fall within the scope of the Part in the same way as repeated positive acts.



The provision matters because relevant conduct in criminal litigation may involve omissions as much as acts. For example, a repeated failure to report, intervene, comply, or respond may be said to show a behavioural pattern or state of mind. Section 96 ensures that the statutory language is read broadly enough to capture those situations.



The section therefore prevents an artificial distinction between acts and omissions when applying the Part 6 evidentiary framework.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 99 — Notice Requirements for Tendency and Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 99]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 99]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: form of notices under ss 97 and 98]

\[CONCEPTS: notice requirements, tendency notice, coincidence notice, regulations or rules of court, procedural compliance, written notice form]



Section 99 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides that notices required under s 97 or s 98 are to be given in accordance with any regulations or rules of court made for that purpose.



The section does not itself prescribe the detailed content of the notice. Instead, it directs practitioners to the applicable subordinate legislation or court rules governing how notice must be given.



The procedural significance of the section is that compliance with the tendency and coincidence notice requirements is not satisfied merely by informal communication. The notice must conform to the prescribed procedural regime. Failure to do so may prevent the evidence from being admitted unless the court dispenses with the requirement under s 100.



Section 99 therefore links the substantive admissibility rules in ss 97 and 98 with the court’s procedural machinery, ensuring that parties receive proper notice in a standardised and legally recognised form.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 100 — Court May Dispense With Notice Requirements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 100]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 100]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: judicial power to direct that notice requirements do not apply]

\[CONCEPTS: dispense with notice, late notice, tendency evidence leave, coincidence evidence leave, conditions on direction, judicial discretion]



Section 100 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) empowers the court, on application by a party, to direct that the tendency rule or coincidence rule is not to apply to particular evidence despite the party’s failure to give notice under s 97 or s 98.



The source records the structure of the power:



The application may be made before or after the time by which notice would otherwise have been required.



In civil proceedings, the application may be made without notice to another party.



Any direction may be given at or before the hearing.



The direction may be subject to conditions the court thinks fit.



The listed conditions may include requiring notice to specified parties, limiting the notice obligation to specified tendency evidence, or limiting it to specified coincidence evidence.



The provision gives the court practical flexibility where strict compliance with notice rules has not occurred but fairness and efficient case management still permit the evidence to be used. It does not abolish the notice requirement generally. Rather, it creates a discretionary safety valve allowing controlled departure from procedural default.



block\_012



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101 — Additional Restriction on Prosecution Tendency and Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 101]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101 — Prosecution: Substantially Outweigh Test]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: prosecution evidence must substantially outweigh prejudicial effect]

\[CONCEPTS: s 101 balance, prosecution tendency evidence, prosecution coincidence evidence, substantially outweighs prejudice, defendant protection, prejudicial effect]



Section 101 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) imposes an additional safeguard where the prosecution seeks to adduce tendency evidence or coincidence evidence about a defendant in a criminal proceeding.



The section applies in addition to ss 97 and 98. It therefore assumes that the threshold admissibility requirements of notice and significant probative value have already been addressed.



Under s 101(2), tendency evidence or coincidence evidence adduced by the prosecution cannot be used against the defendant unless the probative value of the evidence substantially outweighs any prejudicial effect it may have on the defendant.



This is a stricter balancing exercise than an ordinary relevance assessment. The section recognises the particular risk that propensity-style evidence may lead to unfair reasoning against an accused.



The source also records two exceptions. Section 101 does not apply where the prosecution adduces tendency evidence to explain or contradict tendency evidence adduced by the defendant, or where the prosecution adduces coincidence evidence to explain or contradict coincidence evidence adduced by the defendant.



The provision therefore acts as an important protective filter limiting the prosecution’s use of highly prejudicial pattern or propensity evidence.



block\_012



Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas)

Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas) Sch 2 — Powers of Custodians and Escorts



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas) Sch 2]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: powers used to take patient into protective custody or under escort]

\[CONCEPTS: custodian powers, escort powers, reasonable force, entry without warrant, safeguard medication, transfer physical control, mental health transport powers]



Schedule 2 of the Mental Health Act 2013 (Tas) sets out the operational powers of custodians and escorts when taking a patient into protective custody or under escort.



The source defines a custodian or escort as a police officer, mental health officer, or authorised person empowered under the Act to take the patient into protective custody or under escort.



The Schedule authorises a range of powers necessary to perform that task, including:



Enlisting assistance from any person, including police if necessary.



Using reasonable force against the patient if the patient resists.



Using reasonable force against others who try to prevent the patient being taken.



Entering premises without warrant, doing as little damage as possible, where the custodian reasonably believes the patient may be found there.



Taking possession of medication, physical aids, prescriptions, or other things relevant to the patient’s health, safety, welfare, examination, assessment, treatment, or care.



Transferring physical control of the patient to another authorised custodian or escort.



Remaining not in close physical proximity during examination or assessment if circumstances permit.



These are significant coercive powers, but they are directed to protective mental health intervention rather than criminal punishment.



block\_012



Contempt of Court

Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 25 — Contempt in the Face of the Court May Include Conduct Heard but Not Seen



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 25]

\[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 25]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: contempt in the face of the court can include conduct heard but not seen]

\[CONCEPTS: contempt in face of court, heard but not seen, courtroom disorder, judicial officer awareness, Justices Act contempt]



Section 25 of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas) is identified in the source in connection with contempt of court, specifically contempt in the face of the court.



The source states that there is clear authority for the proposition that something heard by a judicial officer, but not seen, can still amount to contempt in the face of the court. The legal concept is therefore not confined to visual misconduct occurring directly before the bench. Audible conduct may be enough if it constitutes contemptuous behaviour occurring in the immediate presence or awareness of the court.



This matters because courtroom contempt can arise through spoken abuse, disruptive noises, threats, or other conduct that interferes with the administration of justice even if the judicial officer does not visually observe the act itself.



The doctrinal point drawn from the source is that contempt in facie is concerned with contemptuous interference occurring in the court’s immediate domain, not merely with what is physically seen by the presiding officer.



block\_012



Authority — Contempt in the Face of the Court May Be Established by Conduct Heard by the Judicial Officer



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Mansell v Hughes \[2024] TASSC 28]

\[TOPIC: conduct heard but not seen can constitute contempt in facie]

\[CONCEPTS: Mansell v Hughes, contempt in facie, audible contempt, courtroom disruption, judicial hearing of misconduct]



The authority of Mansell v Hughes \[2024] TASSC 28 is cited in the source for the proposition that contempt in the face of the court may be constituted by conduct heard but not seen by the judicial officer.



The case is used as current Tasmanian authority for the proposition that visual observation is not a necessary element of contempt in facie. If the court directly perceives contemptuous conduct through hearing, and the conduct occurs within the relevant courtroom context, that may be sufficient.



The source uses the case alongside older authorities from New South Wales, indicating that the principle is well established. The legal significance is practical: courtroom contempt is not avoided merely because the act took place outside the line of sight of the magistrate or judge.



The principle supported by the case is that what matters is the immediate affront or interference with the court’s authority and functioning, not whether the conduct was seen as opposed to heard.



block\_012



Authority — Conduct Heard but Not Seen May Amount to Contempt in Facie



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Registrar, Court of Appeal v Collins \[1982] 1 NSWLR 682; Fraser v The Queen \[1984] 3 NSWLR 212]

\[TOPIC: supporting authorities for contempt based on conduct heard by court]

\[CONCEPTS: contempt authorities, heard not seen, courtroom affront, judicial awareness, NSW contempt cases]



The source cites Registrar, Court of Appeal v Collins \[1982] 1 NSWLR 682 and Fraser v The Queen \[1984] 3 NSWLR 212 as authorities supporting the principle that conduct heard but not seen may constitute contempt in the face of the court.



These authorities support the broader doctrinal proposition that contempt in facie is not limited to visible behaviour. The judicial officer’s awareness of the contemptuous conduct through hearing may be sufficient where the conduct directly interferes with court authority, order, or process.



The cases are used in the source as clear authority for the proposition later applied in Mansell v Hughes \[2024] TASSC 28. Their value lies in demonstrating that the principle has an established doctrinal foundation rather than being a novel extension.



The authorities therefore reinforce the practical understanding that verbal abuse, disruption, or other audible conduct within the court’s immediate sphere may attract contempt consequences even when not seen.

Liquor Licensing

Liquor Licensing Act — Extended Meaning of “Sell”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: extended statutory meaning of sell in liquor licensing context]

\[CONCEPTS: sell liquor, extended definition, liquor transaction meaning, licensing offence definition, sale of alcohol]



The source records that the term “sell” has an extended definition under the Liquor Licensing Act.



The doctrinal significance of an extended statutory definition is that the concept of sale may include conduct broader than an ordinary common law or everyday understanding of buying and selling. In licensing prosecutions, that kind of definition often matters because liability may turn on whether liquor was sold, offered, supplied for value, or otherwise dealt with in a way captured by the statutory language.



The source does not reproduce the full legislative text of the definition, so the precise boundaries of the extended meaning are not set out in this chunk. What can safely be stated from the source is that prosecutors should not assume that “sell” bears only its ordinary narrow meaning when dealing with liquor licensing offences.



Because the precise terms of the statutory definition are not reproduced in the source extract, the exact content of the extended meaning should be verified against the Act before relying on it in a contested proceeding.



\[REVIEW]



block\_012



Liquor Licensing Act — Evidentiary Provisions Relating to Licence Status and Evidence of Sale



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Liquor Licensing Act]

\[CITATION: Liquor Licensing Act — Averments and Evidence of Sale]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: averments and physical items as evidence in liquor licensing matters]

\[CONCEPTS: averment in complaint, evidence of no licence, bottles as evidence of sale, liquor licensing proof, presumptive proof, sale inference]



The source notes that the Liquor Licensing Act contains evidentiary provisions under which an averment in a complaint may operate as evidence that there was no relevant licence.



The source also notes that the presence of bottles and similar physical items may be evidence of sale. The doctrinal significance is that the Act appears to facilitate proof of key licensing facts by allowing evidentiary shortcuts or presumptive inferences in summary proceedings.



Two distinct ideas appear in the source:



A pleading averment may provide evidence of absence of licence.



The physical presence of bottles or similar items may support an inference that a sale occurred.



The source does not specify the precise statutory section numbers for these rules. Accordingly, the existence of the evidentiary principles is supported by the source, but the exact legislative footing should be checked before citation in formal submissions or contested argument.



Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas) s 18 — Restricted Driver Licences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 18]

\[CITATION: Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas) s 18 — Restricted Driver Licence]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: restricted driver licences after disqualification]

\[CONCEPTS: restricted licence, hardship licence, licence disqualification relief, driving disqualification mitigation, conditional licence, limited driving permit, Tasmania restricted licence]



A restricted driver licence under Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas) s 18 is a discretionary order allowing a disqualified driver to drive subject to strict conditions where the statutory criteria are satisfied.



The court may grant a restricted licence when imposing punishment for certain driving offences if two central requirements are met:



The applicant must demonstrate severe hardship caused by the disqualification.



The court must be satisfied that granting the licence is not contrary to the public interest.



The hardship must arise either to the applicant personally or to dependants. Common examples include:



loss of employment because driving is required for work



inability to attend essential medical treatment



inability to transport dependants to school or medical appointments



lack of viable alternative transport where employment or essential travel requires driving.



The application must also comply with procedural requirements under the Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas), including notification requirements such as service of the application on the Registrar of Motor Vehicles.



Restricted licences may also be available to holders of interstate licences and to provisional licence holders. However, a number of statutory exclusions apply, including circumstances where the offender has declined a good behaviour undertaking relating to demerit points.



The granting of such licences remains an exceptional remedy designed to mitigate severe hardship rather than to restore normal driving privileges. Even where hardship is established, the court retains discretion to refuse the application if public interest considerations require that the disqualification operate fully.



Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas) s 18 — Severe Hardship Requirement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 18]

\[CITATION: Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas) s 18 — Severe Hardship Threshold]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: severe hardship threshold for restricted licence]

\[CONCEPTS: severe hardship, exceptional hardship, employment loss, transport necessity, inability to access services, hardship to dependants, restricted licence test]



The severe hardship requirement under Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas) s 18 requires the applicant to demonstrate that the disqualification will cause hardship of a serious or exceptional nature.



The hardship must be more than mere inconvenience. Courts typically require evidence demonstrating:



The nature of the hardship, such as employment loss or inability to access essential services.



The absence of reasonable alternatives, including public transport or assistance from colleagues or family members.



The impact on dependants, such as children requiring transport to school or medical treatment.



Evidence often examined by courts includes:



the applicant’s qualifications and employment prospects



efforts to secure alternative employment



availability of alternative transport options



distance between the applicant’s residence and place of employment



capacity to utilise leave entitlements.



Assertions alone are insufficient. The court generally expects objective evidence demonstrating that alternative employment or transport options have been explored.



The hardship must be severe and unusual, meaning that ordinary inconvenience arising from disqualification will not satisfy the statutory threshold.



Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 19 — Public Interest Limitation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 19]

\[CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 19]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: public interest limitation on restricted licences]

\[CONCEPTS: public interest test, driving disqualification policy, road safety protection, repeat offender risk, alcohol dependency, deterrence]



Section 19 of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) imposes a public interest limitation on the granting of restricted driver licences.



Even where severe hardship is established under Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 (Tas) s 18, the court must refuse the application if granting the licence would be contrary to the public interest.



Relevant considerations include:



Seriousness of the underlying offence, particularly offences involving high blood alcohol levels or dangerous driving.



Likelihood of reoffending, especially where the applicant has prior driving offences.



The need for general and specific deterrence, ensuring that disqualification orders retain their deterrent effect.



Risk to public safety if the applicant were permitted to drive.



Alcohol dependency, which may render the grant of a restricted licence contrary to the public interest.



The statute specifically requires the court to be satisfied that the applicant is not alcohol-dependent, and medical evidence may be required to rebut a presumption of dependency.



The public interest analysis emphasises that disqualification is a key mechanism for protecting the public from unsafe drivers.



Authority — Davies v Deverell (1992) 1 Tas R 214



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Davies v Deverell (1992) 1 Tas R 214]

\[TOPIC: evidentiary scrutiny of restricted licence applications]

\[CONCEPTS: restricted licence evidence, cross-examination requirement, hardship claims, public interest, driving disqualification]



The decision in Davies v Deverell (1992) 1 Tas R 214 confirms that evidence supporting an application for a restricted licence should ordinarily be tested through cross-examination.



The court held that, except in the clearest cases, the public interest requires that claims of hardship and other relevant matters be properly scrutinised through adversarial testing.



The case also emphasises that an applicant bears a positive onus to satisfy the court that granting the restricted licence would not be contrary to the public interest.



Particular weight may be given to:



the applicant’s record of prior convictions



the circumstances of the offending



the applicant’s driving history.



This authority reinforces the evidentiary rigor required when courts consider applications to mitigate driving disqualification orders.



Authority — Lane v Black \[2013] TASMC 3



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lane v Black \[2013] TASMC 3]

\[TOPIC: evidence required to establish employment hardship]

\[CONCEPTS: hardship evidence, employment loss claims, labour market evidence, restricted licence applications, evidentiary burden]



In Lane v Black \[2013] TASMC 3, the Magistrates Court held that assertions about the inability to obtain employment without a licence require evidentiary support.



The applicant claimed that loss of a licence would prevent alternative employment but had provided no evidence demonstrating:



consultation with employment agencies



investigation of the labour market for persons with similar skills



enquiries into alternative work.



The court found that the absence of such evidence undermined the claim of severe hardship.



The decision demonstrates that courts expect concrete evidence rather than unsupported assertions when assessing hardship claims in restricted licence applications.



Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 29 — Search Powers Based on Prescribed Belief



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 29]

\[CITATION: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 29]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: warrantless search powers for drug offences]

\[CONCEPTS: prescribed belief, drug search power, warrantless search, police powers, controlled substances, detention for search]



Section 29 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) authorises police officers to search a person, vehicle or animal without a warrant where the officer holds a prescribed belief.



A prescribed belief exists where an officer reasonably believes that a controlled substance or related evidence of a drug offence is:



in the possession of a person;



on or in a conveyance; or



on an animal.



If such belief exists, police may detain the relevant person or conveyance and conduct a search.



However, the statute imposes limits on entry to premises. Police cannot enter private premises for the purpose of exercising the power unless they have already lawfully entered and then formed the prescribed belief.



The provision recognises the operational reality that drug offences may require rapid investigative action where obtaining a warrant is impractical.



Authority — Tasmania v Hodges \[2022] TASSC 45



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Hodges \[2022] TASSC 45]

\[TOPIC: meaning of prescribed belief for drug search powers]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable belief standard, drug search threshold, suspicion vs belief, objective grounds, police observations]



In Tasmania v Hodges \[2022] TASSC 45, the Supreme Court clarified the meaning of “prescribed belief” for the purposes of Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 29.



The Court held that belief is:



more than mere suspicion,



but less than proof or knowledge.



The belief must be held on reasonable grounds, meaning the circumstances must objectively justify the belief.



The Court accepted that the officer’s belief could arise from the incremental accumulation of information, including:



prior intelligence regarding the suspect



behavioural indicators such as agitation or unusual demeanour



physical symptoms consistent with drug use.



The case confirms that the reasonableness of the belief must be assessed in light of the operational circumstances in which police decisions are made.



Authority — George v Rockett \[1990] HCA 26



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: George v Rockett \[1990] HCA 26]

\[TOPIC: definition of belief in statutory search powers]

\[CONCEPTS: belief threshold, suspicion vs belief, evidentiary standard, reasonable grounds, police search powers]



In George v Rockett \[1990] HCA 26, the High Court explained that belief requires objective circumstances that point more clearly toward the relevant fact than mere suspicion.



The Court stated that belief does not require proof on the balance of probabilities. Rather, belief represents an inclination of the mind to accept a proposition based on evidence that is stronger than suspicion but weaker than proof.



This authority is commonly applied when interpreting statutory powers that depend on the existence of reasonable belief.



Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) — Possession Quantity Requirement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) — Minimum Quantity for Possession]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: minimum quantity requirement for possession offences]

\[CONCEPTS: drug possession threshold, detectable quantity rule, trace amounts, microscopic residue, drug evidence]



For a possession offence under the Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas), the substance must exist in a quantity capable of being detected by ordinary observation.



Extremely small quantities that can only be identified through microscopic analysis are insufficient to establish possession.



Authority — Williams v R \[1978] HCA 49



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Williams v R \[1978] HCA 49]

\[TOPIC: minimum detectable quantity for drug possession]

\[CONCEPTS: microscopic drug residue, possession threshold, detectable quantity, drug possession evidence]



In Williams v R \[1978] HCA 49, the High Court held that possession cannot be established where the substance exists only in a minute quantity detectable solely through microscopic analysis.



At minimum, the substance must be observable with the naked eye.



Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Criminal Responsibility Thresholds



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Age Thresholds for Criminal Responsibility]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: age thresholds for criminal responsibility]

\[CONCEPTS: criminal responsibility age, doli incapax, youth offending, juvenile responsibility]



Tasmanian law establishes two key thresholds for criminal responsibility in youth offenders.



Under 10 years of age — a child cannot be held criminally responsible for offences.



Between 10 and under 14 years — there is a rebuttable presumption that the child lacks the capacity to understand that their conduct was morally wrong.



This presumption is commonly referred to as doli incapax.



To rebut the presumption, the prosecution must prove that the child understood that the conduct was seriously wrong according to societal standards.



Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) ss 27–28 — Limitations on Joint Charges



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 27–28]

\[CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) ss 27–28]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: joint charges involving youth and adults]

\[CONCEPTS: youth joint charge prohibition, youth adult co-accused, procedural restrictions]



Sections 27 and 28 of the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) regulate circumstances in which a youth may be jointly charged with an adult.



Key rules include:



A youth under 15 years cannot be jointly charged with an adult.



Where a youth aged 15 or older is jointly charged with an adult and both plead not guilty, the court is generally not permitted to determine the charge against the youth.



Exceptions apply where either the youth or the adult pleads guilty. In such circumstances, the court may hear the matter unless the prosecutor objects.



If the prosecutor objects, the matter may be transferred to the Supreme Court under Part VII of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas).



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 170B — Strangulation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 170B]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 170B]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: strangulation offence in family violence context]

\[CONCEPTS: strangulation offence, choking offence, suffocation offence, intentional choking, family violence crime]



The offence of strangulation under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 170B criminalises intentionally choking, suffocating or strangling another person.



The prosecution must prove:



The accused intentionally performed the act of choking, suffocation or strangulation.



The act was unlawful.



The act was committed against another person.



The offence requires specific intent, meaning the accused must have intended the physical act of choking or suffocation.



The terms “choking”, “suffocation” and “strangulation” are not defined in the statute and therefore take their ordinary meaning, which may evolve according to community understanding.



Evidence of physical injury is often absent in strangulation offences, and the absence of injury does not prevent proof of the offence.



Examples of conduct capable of constituting suffocation include:



pressure applied to the chest preventing breathing



compression of the neck using hands or objects



any external pressure restricting airflow.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 177A — Analysis Certificates in Drug Proceedings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 177A]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 177A]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of drug analysis certificates]

\[CONCEPTS: analysis certificate, prima facie evidence, drug testing certificate, service requirement, 14 days notice, evidentiary shortcut, proof of drug analysis, admissible certificate]



Section 177A of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides an evidentiary shortcut for proving the results of drug analysis by certificate.



An analysis certificate under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 177A is admissible as prima facie evidence of the matters stated in it, meaning the prosecution may rely on the certificate without calling the analyst unless the certificate is challenged. The source material states that this procedure is subject to a service requirement: the certificate must be served on the defence at least 14 days before the hearing.



The rule is significant because it streamlines proof of forensic drug analysis in prosecutions involving controlled substances. However, the source also notes an important statutory limitation: this certificate mechanism does not extend to proceedings under the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) because that Act specifically excludes its operation through s 29A.



The practical effect is that prosecutors must check both the evidentiary statute and the substantive offence statute before relying on certificate evidence. Where Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 177A applies, the certificate may establish the analysis result on a prima facie basis. Where the offence is under the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas), the prosecution cannot assume that the same certificate procedure is available and must proceed according to that Act’s own evidentiary framework.



Poisons Act 1971 (Tas) s 86C — Definition of “Ice Pipe”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Poisons Act 1971 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 86C]

\[CITATION: Poisons Act 1971 (Tas) s 86C]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: statutory definition of ice pipe]

\[CONCEPTS: ice pipe, drug paraphernalia, smoking device, controlled drug inhalation, vapour inhalation, crystal drug device, meth pipe, device definition]



The definition of “ice pipe” in Poisons Act 1971 (Tas) s 86C identifies the kinds of devices captured by offences relating to equipment used for smoking crystalline or powdered controlled drugs.



Under Poisons Act 1971 (Tas) s 86C, an ice pipe means a device that:



is capable of being used, or intended to be used, to administer a controlled drug into the body by drawing or inhaling smoke, vapour or fumes resulting from heating or burning the drug in crystal or powder form;



is intended to be used as such a device but requires adjustment, modification or addition before being so used; or



is of a prescribed class, type or description.



The definition is directed to function and intended use rather than brand name or appearance alone. It captures both devices already capable of use and devices intended for that use but not yet fully configured.



This statutory definition matters because it separates specialised drug-smoking devices from ordinary household items. The focus is on whether the device is capable of, intended for, or prescribed as a device for inhaling the products of heating or burning a controlled drug in crystal or powder form. That functional emphasis helps determine whether a seized item falls within the specific statutory category of an ice pipe rather than being treated simply as a neutral object.



Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) — Detection Times for Oral Fluid Drug Testing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) — Drug Detection Times in Oral Fluid]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: general detection times for drugs in oral fluid]

\[CONCEPTS: oral fluid detection, drug detection window, saliva testing, roadside drug test timing, cannabis detection, methylamphetamine detection, recent use indicator, oral sample]



General detection time guidance for oral fluid testing indicates that drugs are typically detectable in saliva for shorter periods than in blood and therefore oral fluid testing is treated as more reflective of recent use.



The source material records the following general detection windows for common drugs of abuse in oral fluid:



Cannabis — about 24 hours



Methylamphetamine — about 3 days



Amphetamine — about 72 hours



MDMA — about 3 days



Morphine — about 7 days



Heroin — about 2 days



The source expressly describes these periods as general guidelines and notes that there may be exceptions. Accordingly, these figures should not be treated as rigid legal thresholds or as conclusive proof of when a substance was ingested. Their value is contextual: they assist in understanding the likely significance of a positive oral fluid result and why oral fluid testing is often framed as evidence of comparatively recent consumption.



This material is better understood as practical evidentiary context than as a fixed statutory rule. In litigation, a court would still need to consider the actual statutory elements, the test result relied on, and any admissible expert or forensic evidence relevant to the timing and significance of detection.



\[REVIEW]



Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 23 — Meaning of “Designed to Be Used”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 23]

\[CITATION: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 23]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: meaning of designed to be used in drug administration offences]

\[CONCEPTS: designed to be used, drug administration device, cannabis smoking device, intended purpose, manufactured for use, paraphernalia proof, actual use versus design]



The phrase “designed to be used” in Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 23 requires more than proof that an item can be used, or is often used, for smoking cannabis.



The source material indicates that s 23 draws a distinction between:



items actually used in connection with the smoking of cannabis; and



items designed for such use, in the sense that they were manufactured, made, or are of a design for that purpose.



That distinction matters because many ordinary objects can be adapted for drug use without having been designed for it. The interpretive point is that habitual or possible use is not enough, by itself, to justify an inference that the item was designed or manufactured for the prohibited purpose.



There must be some evidentiary basis permitting the court to infer that the relevant drug-related purpose was among the purposes of the item’s design or manufacture. The prohibited purpose need not be the sole or dominant purpose, but it must be a genuine design purpose rather than a merely incidental or opportunistic use.



This interpretive principle limits overreach in paraphernalia prosecutions by requiring attention to the nature, design and evidentiary context of the object itself, rather than relying only on its capability or common misuse.



Authority — Thomas v Frankcombe, Turton, Medwin and Pears \[2010] TASMC 6



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Thomas v Frankcombe, Turton, Medwin and Pears \[2010] TASMC 6]

\[TOPIC: proof required that an item was designed for cannabis use]

\[CONCEPTS: bong evidence, police observation evidence, drug device proof, designed for use, actual use versus manufacture, paraphernalia inference, cannabis smoking device]



Thomas v Frankcombe, Turton, Medwin and Pears \[2010] TASMC 6 confirms that evidence from police officers about how items are commonly used by cannabis users may be admissible as evidence of observed practice, but that evidence alone may be insufficient to prove that an item was designed for that purpose.



The case accepts that an officer may give direct evidence from experience that certain smoking devices are regularly used to smoke cannabis. That kind of evidence may be admitted as factual evidence based on observation and experience rather than impermissible opinion. However, the decision draws an important further distinction: proof that an item can be used for smoking cannabis, or is often used that way, does not automatically justify an inference that the item was designed or manufactured for cannabis use.



The authority states that, for Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 23, there must be some evidence permitting an inference that the prohibited purpose was among the purposes of the design or manufacture of the item. Incidental features, such as cannabis-themed motifs, do not necessarily prove design purpose. The case therefore limits prosecutions based solely on generic capability and requires a firmer evidentiary basis when alleging that an item was designed for drug administration.



Authority — Orr v Johnston \[2014] TASMC 5



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Orr v Johnston \[2014] TASMC 5]

\[TOPIC: factual basis capable of supporting prescribed belief]

\[CONCEPTS: prescribed belief, smell of drugs, defendant appearance, prior knowledge, reasonable grounds, search power threshold, factual basis for belief]



Orr v Johnston \[2014] TASMC 5 is authority that a prescribed belief for the purpose of Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) search powers may be founded on a combination of sensory observations, the defendant’s presentation, and prior knowledge of the defendant.



The source material summarises the basis of belief in that case as including:



smell



the appearance of the defendant



the officer’s past knowledge of the defendant.



The significance of the case is that it supports a practical, fact-sensitive assessment of whether police had a sufficient basis to form the required belief. It shows that the threshold is not confined to a single decisive fact. Rather, belief may arise from a combination of observations that, taken together, provide reasonable grounds.



This authority fits with the broader line of cases distinguishing belief from mere suspicion. It illustrates that courts may accept operational police assessments when those assessments are grounded in identifiable facts such as odour, presentation and prior knowledge, provided the combined circumstances objectively support the belief relied on.



Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) s 3 — Responsible Adult



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 3]

\[CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) s 3]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: definition of responsible adult]

\[CONCEPTS: responsible adult, nominated adult, youth support person, youth custody support, approved adult, close association, youth justice definition]



Section 3 of the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) defines “responsible adult” by reference to who may properly support or represent a youth in the statutory setting.



A responsible adult, in relation to a youth, means:



a nominated adult in relation to the youth; or



if there is no nominated adult, a justice of the peace or another person, or class of persons, approved by the Secretary.



A nominated adult is an adult who:



has had a close association with the youth, or has been counselling, advising or aiding the youth;



has not been charged with the offence for which the youth has been taken into custody and is not suspected by police on reasonable grounds of being directly or indirectly involved in that offence; and



has been nominated by the youth.



The definition ensures that the adult involved is both sufficiently connected to the youth and sufficiently independent from the alleged offending. The statutory exclusion of persons charged with, or reasonably suspected of involvement in, the relevant offence protects the integrity of the process and reduces the risk that the youth’s interests will be compromised by conflicted involvement.



Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Pre-Court Diversion Framework



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Pre-Court Diversion Options]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: pre-court diversion options for youth offenders]

\[CONCEPTS: youth diversion, informal caution, formal caution, community conference, restorative justice, pre-court resolution, youth consent, diversion from court]



The Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) establishes a diversion-focused framework that prefers pre-court resolution for youth offending where that is appropriate.



The source material identifies three principal pre-court diversion pathways:



Informal caution — described as a Youth Caution Action Notice, and noted as not available for assaults.



Formal caution — administered by a police officer.



Community conference — convened through Communities Tasmania with an administrator, police officer, the youth and carer, and with victims able to elect to participate.



The source also explains that restorative justice underpins this framework. Restorative justice is described as a facilitated process in which those harmed by offending can discuss the impact, while the offender accepts responsibility and works to repair harm, with an emphasis on healing and prevention rather than punishment alone.



Diversion is not automatic. The source identifies two bars to diversion:



refusal by the youth to accept guilt



the nature or recurrence of the offending making diversion inappropriate.



The source also states that a youth must consent to a formal caution or a conference. This makes participation and accountability central features of the diversion model rather than imposed substitutes for ordinary criminal process.



Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Diversion Patterns and Program Structure



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Diversion Patterns in Practice]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: diversion patterns and structure of youth justice responses]

\[CONCEPTS: youth offence outcomes, diversion rates, informal caution, formal caution, community conference, court referral, youth justice pathway, offence processing]



The youth justice material records that diversion is not merely theoretical under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas), but forms a substantial part of how youth offending is processed in practice.



The source states that about 50% of youth offences historically are met with diversion rather than court, with a stated historical range of about 45–55%. It also records 2024 offence outcomes in the following categories:



Informal caution — 109 matters, 28%



Formal caution — 65 matters, 17%



Community conference — 36 matters, 9%



Court — 167 matters, 44%



Not proceeded with — 4 matters, 1%



These figures indicate a structured pathway in which matters may be resolved by police caution, by formalised diversionary intervention, by conference-based restorative processes, or by ordinary court proceedings depending on seriousness, recurrence, suitability and youth participation.



The practical structure reflected in the source is therefore a graduated response model. Less serious or suitable matters may be resolved through cautioning or conferencing, while more serious, repeated or otherwise unsuitable matters proceed to court. The figures also support the broader doctrinal point that diversion remains a legislatively preferred and operationally significant feature of Tasmanian youth justice.



Justices Rules — Rule 5 Procedure Where Rules Are Silent



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Justices Rules]

\[SECTION: r 5]

\[CITATION: Justices Rules r 5]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: procedural gap-filling rule]

\[CONCEPTS: procedural gaps, Supreme Court practice adoption, magistrates court procedure, criminal procedural rules, default procedural framework, rules of court, procedural authority]



Rule 5 of the Justices Rules establishes the procedural framework for situations where the rules governing summary criminal proceedings do not provide explicit guidance. The rule provides that where a matter is not otherwise provided for by the Justices Rules, the practice of the Supreme Court of Tasmania is to be followed.



This provision operates as a procedural gap-filling mechanism. Its function is to ensure that the absence of express procedural rules in summary jurisdiction does not prevent the orderly administration of justice. Instead, the procedural practices applicable in the Supreme Court may be adopted by analogy.



In practice, the rule frequently becomes relevant when applications arise in the Magistrates Court that are governed by legislation primarily designed for superior courts or where procedural steps are not specifically addressed in the summary rules. For example, applications made under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) may rely upon Supreme Court procedural practices where the Justices Rules do not prescribe the exact method of proceeding.



The effect of rule 5 is not to import the entirety of Supreme Court procedural rules automatically, but rather to allow the Magistrates Court to adopt the relevant practices necessary to resolve the procedural issue before it. The rule therefore preserves flexibility while maintaining procedural consistency across Tasmanian courts.



block\_014



Criminal Rules 2006 — Rule 41A Applications under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Rules 2006]

\[SECTION: r 41A]

\[CITATION: Criminal Rules 2006 (Tas) r 41A]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: preliminary hearings for special witness applications]

\[CONCEPTS: preliminary hearing procedure, special witness applications, judicial discretion evidence, prosecutor assurances, evidentiary flexibility, interlocutory hearings]



Rule 41A of the Criminal Rules 2006 (Tas) governs applications made under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) at a preliminary hearing.



The rule provides that, for the purposes of a preliminary hearing under s 9(2) of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas), a judge may inform himself or herself in any manner the judge considers appropriate. The rule further authorises the court to accept statements or assurances from the prosecutor without requiring formal evidence from the child or person who is expected to give evidence.



This procedural flexibility reflects the protective purpose of the legislation governing vulnerable witnesses. Requiring the proposed witness to give evidence at the preliminary stage would undermine the very protections the legislation seeks to provide.



The rule therefore permits the court to rely on materials such as:



Affidavits or written statements.



Professional reports, including psychological or medical assessments.



Prosecutorial assurances about the circumstances of the witness.



Other relevant documentary evidence.



The rule recognises that applications concerning vulnerable witnesses are interlocutory in nature and that the court should be able to assess the appropriateness of protective measures without unnecessarily exposing the witness to the stress of court proceedings.



block\_014



Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 8 Test for Special Witness Status



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001]

\[SECTION: s 8]

\[CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) s 8]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: statutory test for special witness orders]

\[CONCEPTS: special witness criteria, vulnerable witness test, trauma risk evidence, intimidation evidence, disability witness protection, remote testimony eligibility]



Section 8 of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) establishes the statutory test that must be satisfied before a court may grant special witness protections.



The prosecution must establish, on the balance of probabilities, one of two alternative criteria:



Disability ground

The witness is, or is likely to be, unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner because of an intellectual, mental or physical disability.



Trauma or intimidation ground

Because of factors such as age, cultural background, relationship to a party, the nature of the evidence, or other relevant considerations, the witness is likely:



to suffer severe emotional trauma; or



to become so intimidated or distressed that they cannot give evidence, or cannot give evidence satisfactorily.



The statutory formulation deliberately uses the language of likelihood rather than certainty. Consequently, the evidentiary burden is satisfied where the court is persuaded that the relevant risk is more probable than not.



Evidence commonly relied upon to satisfy the statutory test may include:



psychological or psychiatric reports



police statements describing distress



evidence of intimidation or fear of the accused



medical or therapeutic records



behavioural indicators of trauma.



These protections allow courts to modify the manner in which evidence is given, such as permitting video-link testimony or other protective arrangements designed to minimise harm to vulnerable witnesses.



block\_014



Authority — Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55]

\[TOPIC: mental element of harassment under Family Violence Act]

\[CONCEPTS: harassment definition, intent requirement, PFVO breach elements, family violence orders, mens rea harassment, statutory definition harassment]



The decision in Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55 clarifies the mental element required for conduct constituting “harassing” under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas).



The Supreme Court held that the statutory definition of “harassing” in s 4 of the Act is exhaustive and focuses on the conduct of the defendant rather than the effect on the protected person. As a result, it is not necessary to prove that the defendant intended to cause fear, distress or apprehension in the protected person.



Instead, the required mental element is limited to intent to perform the act which the statute defines as harassment. Once that intentional conduct is established, the legislation deems the conduct to constitute harassment for the purposes of the Act.



This interpretation reflects the protective purpose of the family violence legislative scheme. The Act is designed to provide effective protective mechanisms for persons at risk of family violence, and requiring proof of subjective intent to cause fear would undermine enforcement.



Consequently:



The prosecution must prove the defendant intended the relevant act (for example, following or contacting the protected person).



The prosecution does not need to prove that the defendant intended to cause fear or emotional harm.



The statute itself determines whether the act constitutes harassment.



This construction strengthens the enforceability of family violence orders and provides clarity regarding the conduct prohibited by such orders.



block\_014



Criminal Code Act 1924 — Section 301(2) Duty to Inform Person of Reason for Arrest



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924]

\[SECTION: s 301(2)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 301(2)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: duty to inform arrested person of cause of arrest]

\[CONCEPTS: arrest procedure, informing suspect of reason, lawful arrest requirements, practicability test, notice of arrest, police arrest powers]



Section 301(2) of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) imposes a duty on a person making an arrest to inform the arrested individual of the legal basis for the arrest.



The provision states that it is the duty of a person arresting another, whether with or without a warrant, to give notice, if practicable, of either:



the process or warrant under which the arrest is being made; or



the cause of the arrest.



The requirement reflects a fundamental safeguard against arbitrary detention. An individual whose liberty is being restricted must ordinarily be told why they are being arrested.



However, the statutory obligation is qualified by the phrase “if practicable.” The meaning of this qualification was considered in Johnson v Reeves \[2004] TASSC 110.



Where circumstances make it pointless or impossible to communicate the reason for arrest — for example, if the arrested person cannot hear or understand the explanation — the requirement may not apply because it would not be practicable to provide the explanation.



The provision therefore balances two competing considerations:



the protection of individual liberty through procedural fairness; and



the practical realities of police arrest situations.



Failure to comply with the duty where it is practicable may render the arrest unlawful.



Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 4 Support Persons for Child Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001]

\[SECTION: s 4]

\[CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) s 4]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: support person for child witness]

\[CONCEPTS: support person in court, vulnerable witness assistance, child witness protections, courtroom support, witness comfort measures, trauma reduction]



Section 4 of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) allows a child witness to have an approved support person present while giving evidence in court proceedings.



The provision applies in any proceeding concerning a child witness, not limited to particular categories of offences. Its purpose is to reduce distress and facilitate the ability of children to provide evidence effectively.



The court may approve a person to sit near the child while evidence is being given. The support person is intended to provide reassurance and emotional support during the court process.



However, the legislation imposes an important restriction:



The support person must not be another witness in the proceeding.



This restriction ensures that the presence of the support person does not create evidentiary complications or undermine the fairness of the trial.



The section reflects the broader protective purpose of the Act, which recognises that traditional courtroom processes may be intimidating or distressing for children. By permitting the presence of a support person, the court seeks to mitigate those effects while maintaining the integrity of the proceedings.



block\_014



Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 5 Admission of Prior Statements of Affected Children or Prescribed Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001]

\[SECTION: s 5]

\[CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) s 5]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admission of prior statement as evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: prior statement evidence, recorded child statement, evidence in chief substitution, vulnerable witness evidence, disclosure requirements, cross examination rights]



Section 5 of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) allows prior statements of certain vulnerable witnesses to be admitted as evidence in prescribed or specified proceedings.



The provision applies to the prior written or recorded statements of:



an affected child, or



a prescribed witness.



Where the statutory conditions are met, the statement may be admitted as part of the evidence provided by that witness.



Two key safeguards apply:



Disclosure requirement — the statement must have been disclosed to the defence.



Availability for cross-examination — the witness must still be available to be cross-examined.



The purpose of the provision is to minimise the stress associated with repeatedly recounting traumatic events. Statements made closer in time to the alleged events are often more accurate and reliable because they are recorded while the events remain fresh in the witness’s memory.



The rule therefore balances two interests:



protecting vulnerable witnesses from unnecessary distress; and



preserving the accused person’s right to challenge the evidence through cross-examination.



block\_014



Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 5A Audio-Visual Statements as Examination-in-Chief



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001]

\[SECTION: s 5A]

\[CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) s 5A]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: audiovisual recorded statement as examination in chief]
