\[CONCEPTS: video recorded statement, child evidence procedure, police interview evidence, recorded examination in chief, vulnerable witness testimony, audiovisual evidence]



Section 5A of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) permits an audio-visual statement taken by police from certain witnesses to be tendered as part or the entirety of that witness’s examination-in-chief.



The provision applies where the witness is:



a child,



a special witness,



a victim of an offence under s 35(3) of the Police Offences Act 1935, or



a victim of family violence.



An audio-visual statement recorded by police may therefore be played in court instead of requiring the witness to repeat the same account orally.



The legislative purpose is to reduce the trauma of repeated recounting of distressing events while preserving evidentiary reliability. Video statements are typically recorded shortly after the alleged incident and therefore capture the witness’s recollection while it is fresh.



Additionally, audio-visual statements preserve important aspects of testimony that written statements cannot capture, including:



tone and emotional state



gestures or demonstrations



spontaneous responses during questioning.



The witness remains available for cross-examination, ensuring the accused retains the opportunity to challenge the evidence.



block\_014



Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 6B Remote Evidence for Affected Children and Prescribed Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001]

\[SECTION: s 6B]

\[CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) s 6B]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: remote audiovisual evidence for vulnerable witnesses]

\[CONCEPTS: remote testimony, video link evidence, vulnerable witness procedure, child witness protections, courtroom technology evidence, testimony outside courtroom]



Section 6B of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) provides that certain vulnerable witnesses are to give evidence via remote audio-visual link where no contrary order is made under s 7.



The provision applies to:



affected children, and



prescribed witnesses



in either:



prescribed proceedings, or



specified proceedings.



Unless the court orders otherwise, these witnesses are to give evidence from a location separate from the courtroom using remote audiovisual technology.



The purpose of this arrangement is to reduce the intimidation and distress associated with confronting the accused directly in the courtroom environment. The remote setting may also assist the witness to give clearer and more coherent evidence.



Despite the physical separation from the courtroom, the evidence remains part of the formal trial process and is transmitted live so that the court, the accused and the legal representatives can observe the witness while evidence is given.



The section forms part of the broader statutory framework designed to facilitate participation in the justice system by vulnerable witnesses.



block\_014



Authority — A v Filz \[2003] TASSC 32



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: A v Filz \[2003] TASSC 32]

\[TOPIC: purpose of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act]

\[CONCEPTS: vulnerable witness legislation, protective evidence procedures, confrontation rights balance, witness protection law, statutory purpose interpretation]



The decision in A v Filz \[2003] TASSC 32 discusses the purpose and operation of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas).



The Supreme Court recognised that the Act constitutes beneficial legislation designed to protect vulnerable witnesses while maintaining the fairness of criminal proceedings.



Historically, criminal trials emphasised the accused person’s right to confront their accuser in open court. The Act modifies this traditional principle by introducing procedures that reduce the trauma experienced by vulnerable witnesses when giving evidence.



The Court emphasised that the legislation does not abolish the rights of the accused but instead seeks to balance competing interests:



protecting vulnerable witnesses from distress or intimidation; and



ensuring that the accused retains the ability to test the evidence through cross-examination.



Protective procedures such as video-link evidence, admission of prior recorded statements and special witness arrangements must therefore be understood within the statutory purpose of enabling vulnerable witnesses to give reliable evidence without unnecessary trauma.



block\_014



Authority — Barnes v Crossin \[2017] TASSC 61



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Barnes v Crossin \[2017] TASSC 61]

\[TOPIC: police arrest powers for family violence]

\[CONCEPTS: family violence arrest power, suspicion threshold, preventative arrest, safety of victims, family violence act interpretation]



The decision in Barnes v Crossin \[2017] TASSC 61 clarifies the scope of police arrest powers under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas).



The Court confirmed that the statutory power of arrest is triggered where a police officer suspects that a person has committed family violence as defined by the Act.



Importantly, the Court emphasised that the definition of “family violence” is broader than the concept of a criminal offence. The definition includes a range of behaviours such as intimidation, emotional abuse and property damage.



Consequently, a police officer may lawfully arrest a person even where:



the officer does not suspect a specific criminal offence has been committed; and



the officer does not intend to charge the person immediately.



The purpose of the arrest power is preventative and protective. Arrest may be used to remove a person from an environment where there is a risk of further family violence and to facilitate further investigative or protective measures.



These measures may include:



conducting safety assessments



undertaking risk screening



issuing protective orders



investigating potential offences.



The decision highlights the strong protective focus of family violence legislation and the priority given to the safety of affected persons.



block\_014



Authority — Kerr v Brown \[2013] TASMC 30



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Kerr v Brown \[2013] TASMC 30]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of the term approach in protection orders]

\[CONCEPTS: approach definition, restraining order interpretation, intent requirement contact prohibition, proximity meaning, protection order breach]



The decision in Kerr v Brown \[2013] TASMC 30 considers the meaning of the term “approach” in a protective court order.



The Court held that the term must be interpreted according to its ordinary meaning, taking into account the context of the order and the position of the defendant who must comply with it.



The word “approach” was interpreted as involving:



deliberate conduct, and



intentional initiation of contact or proximity.



Accordingly, the term does not extend to situations where a person passively or accidentally finds themselves near the protected person.



Instead, the concept of approaching requires that the defendant take some action with the intention of bringing themselves into the presence of the protected person or initiating communication with them.



The Court emphasised that protection orders should be interpreted in a manner that is clear and understandable to ordinary persons who must comply with them. Expanding the meaning beyond its ordinary sense could create unfair uncertainty and expose individuals to criminal liability for unintended conduct.



Significant Relationship — Indicia and Threshold



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: determining existence of a significant relationship]

\[CONCEPTS: significant relationship threshold, relationship indicia, cohabitation factors, shared finances, mutual support, relationship recognition, domestic partnership indicators]



A “significant relationship” in Tasmanian law is determined by examining objective indicators of the relationship between two individuals rather than relying on a single defining factor. Courts assess a combination of relational characteristics to determine whether the parties’ connection meets the statutory threshold.



Relevant indicia typically include:



Duration of the relationship, including periods of separation.



Living arrangements, particularly whether the parties cohabit or regularly stay together.



Financial arrangements, including shared spending, joint assets, or financial dependence.



Mutual domestic support, such as cooking, cleaning, or caring responsibilities.



Sexual relationship, although this is not determinative.



Public representation of the relationship, including how the parties refer to each other.



Recognition by family and social circles as being in a relationship.



Joint activities, including holidays and shared social life.



No single factor is determinative. Courts assess the totality of the relationship to determine whether the statutory concept of a significant relationship is satisfied. Distinct financial arrangements or the maintenance of separate residences may weigh against the finding but will not necessarily prevent it if other indicators strongly support relational interdependence. 



block\_015



Authority — Significant Relationship Indicia



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Wiggins v Public Trustee \[2020] TASSC 3]

\[TOPIC: indicia relevant to determining a significant relationship]

\[CONCEPTS: relationship indicia, cohabitation factors, domestic partnership, shared life evidence, mutual financial support]



The Supreme Court in Wiggins v Public Trustee \[2020] TASSC 3 considered whether a claimant qualified as the deceased’s next of kin by virtue of a significant relationship.



The Court evaluated several relational indicators:



The parties had been together for approximately eight years, though including a two-year break.



They maintained separate residences, but regularly stayed at each other’s homes and kept personal belongings there.



The relationship involved sexual intimacy.



They maintained separate bank accounts, but spent money on each other and funded capital improvements for shared benefit.



The parties holidayed together, presented themselves publicly as a couple, and were regarded by family members as being in a relationship.



One partner performed domestic tasks such as cooking and cleaning.



Despite these factors, the Court concluded the relationship did not satisfy the statutory threshold because financial independence and lack of true cohabitation remained decisive considerations.



The case demonstrates that relational indicia must collectively demonstrate a shared domestic life rather than merely a close personal relationship. 



block\_015



Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 68T — Duration of Interim Variations



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)]

\[CITATION: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 68T]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: duration of interim variations to family law orders]

\[CONCEPTS: interim family violence orders, variation of parenting orders, jurisdiction overlap, child safety protection, family law order suspension]



Section 68T of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) governs the duration of interim variations to parenting or contact orders when a state or territory court issues a family violence order.



Previously, where a court revived, varied, or suspended a family law order while issuing an interim family violence order, the variation ceased:



When the interim order ended; or



After 21 days, whichever occurred first.



Legislative amendments removed the fixed 21-day limitation. The revised provision now provides that the revival, variation, or suspension ends at whichever occurs first:



The time specified in the interim order;



When the interim order ceases; or



When a later court order alters the family law arrangement.



This reform gives judicial officers greater flexibility to ensure consistency between parenting arrangements and protective orders. Courts can tailor the duration of changes to the circumstances of the case, particularly where there are risks of family violence affecting a child or protected person. 



block\_015



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 9 — Course of Conduct Requirement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 9]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: element of offence]

\[TOPIC: course of conduct in emotional abuse offences]

\[CONCEPTS: course of conduct, repeated behaviour, emotional abuse element, sustained intimidation, family violence offence]



Section 9 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) requires proof that the accused engaged in a course of conduct constituting emotional abuse.



The concept of a course of conduct generally requires more than a single isolated act. Conduct must be either:



Sustained over time, or



Occur on multiple occasions.



This interpretation aligns with general criminal law principles relating to repeated conduct offences and with the statutory definition of stalking within the Criminal Code.



However, the statutory wording also recognises that certain acts — particularly threats or intimidation that restrict the victim’s freedom of movement — may in some circumstances amount to a course of conduct even if occurring during a single incident.



When drafting charges under s 9, it is not necessary to specify precisely which form of emotional harm occurred. Allegations may be framed in the alternative, describing various forms of controlling or abusive behaviour, provided the complaint gives the defendant reasonable notice of the nature of the allegation. 



block\_015



Authority — Course of Conduct



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Howe v S \[2013] TASMC 33]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of “course of conduct” in family violence offences]

\[CONCEPTS: repeated conduct requirement, sustained behaviour, stalking analogy, emotional abuse threshold, intimidation]



In Howe v S \[2013] TASMC 33, the Magistrates Court considered whether a single conversation could constitute a breach of Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 9.



The Court accepted the general proposition that a course of conduct normally requires more than one act. Magistrate reasoning indicated that the concept usually refers to behaviour that is:



Protracted, or



Repeated on separate occasions.



The Court referred to the definition of stalking in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 192, which defines a course of conduct as behaviour that is sustained or occurs on more than one occasion.



However, the judgment noted that s 9 itself includes conduct such as threats or intimidation restricting freedom of movement, and suggested that in certain circumstances even a single act could satisfy the statutory description if it effectively restricts the victim’s liberty.



The case therefore confirms that while repetition is the general rule, the statutory language may permit exceptions depending on the nature and impact of the conduct.



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 10 — Police Powers in Family Violence Circumstances



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 10]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: police investigative powers in family violence situations]

\[CONCEPTS: police entry powers, seizure authority, reasonable suspicion threshold, family violence investigation powers, emergency intervention, evidence preservation, investigative authority]



Section 10 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) provides police officers with extensive powers to investigate suspected family violence offences and protect affected persons.



The statutory threshold for exercising these powers is generally that a police officer reasonably suspects that family violence has occurred or is occurring. This standard allows police to intervene rapidly in volatile domestic circumstances where immediate evidence gathering and protection of victims may be required.



The powers under this section include authority to:



Enter premises where the officer reasonably suspects family violence has occurred or may occur.



Search premises and persons for evidence relevant to the investigation.



Seize items reasonably believed to be connected with the alleged offence or necessary to prevent further harm.



Take other investigative steps reasonably necessary to respond to the suspected family violence situation.



The purpose of these powers is preventative as well as investigative. The Act recognises that family violence frequently occurs in private settings and may escalate quickly. The reasonable suspicion threshold enables early intervention without requiring the higher evidentiary standard necessary for criminal charges.



These powers operate alongside other statutory police authorities and are intended to allow swift protective responses in circumstances where the safety of victims or children may be at risk.



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 10(b) — Forensic Examination Powers



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 10(b)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: police power to conduct forensic examinations in family violence investigations]

\[CONCEPTS: forensic examination powers, investigative photography, injury documentation, evidence collection, non-invasive forensic procedure, victim evidence recording]



Section 10(b) of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) appears to authorise police officers to subject persons present at a family violence scene to forensic examination for investigative purposes.



Although the Act itself does not define the term “forensic examination”, interpretive guidance may be drawn from the Forensic Procedures Act, which defines a forensic procedure to include non-invasive procedures. Non-invasive procedures commonly include:



Photographs of injuries



Visual inspection of physical condition



Recording visible marks or damage



These procedures are often used to document injuries or other physical evidence relevant to alleged family violence.



Section 5 of the Forensic Procedures Act indicates that the procedural restrictions in that Act generally do not apply to victims. Accordingly, where a person may later become a defendant due to cross-allegations, police may still photograph injuries where the purpose of the examination is investigative and relates to their status as a victim of alleged family violence.



This power assists police in preserving evidence in circumstances where injuries may fade or where later disputes arise about the cause or existence of injuries.



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) ss 11–12 — Arrest and Detention Framework



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) ss 11–12]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: arrest and bail procedures for family violence offences]

\[CONCEPTS: arrest powers, police bail restrictions, PFVO breach procedure, court appearance requirement, detention rules]



Sections 11 and 12 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) establish the arrest and detention regime applicable to family violence offences.



A key distinction exists between breach offences and new family violence allegations.



Arrest for Breach of an Order



Where a person is arrested for breaching a Police Family Violence Order (PFVO) or Family Violence Order (FVO):



Section 12 provides that section 34 of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas) does not apply.



The defendant must be brought before a court as soon as practicable.



Police cannot grant police bail for such breaches.



This reflects the legislature’s view that breach of a protective order constitutes serious conduct requiring judicial supervision.



Arrest for New Family Violence Offences



Where a person is arrested for a new family violence offence that does not involve a breach of an existing order, police retain discretion to:



Release the person unconditionally, or



Grant police bail.



Importantly, the Act does not require police to immediately lay charges following arrest. Police may release a suspect while the investigation continues and later determine whether charges should be filed.



This framework recognises the practical realities of family violence investigations while ensuring breaches of protective orders receive stricter procedural treatment.



Authority — Intent in Breach of Family Violence Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Sundram v Bearman \[2020] TASMC 18]

\[TOPIC: intent requirement for breach offences under the Family Violence Act]

\[CONCEPTS: breach mental element, knowledge of order facts, intentional conduct, harassment by following, absence of strict liability]



In Sundram v Bearman \[2020] TASMC 18, the Magistrates Court confirmed that offences under Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35 are not strict liability offences.



The prosecution must prove that the defendant knew the facts that made their conduct criminal. This includes awareness of the circumstances that brought their behaviour within the terms of the relevant order.



The Court also held that certain breach allegations require proof of intentional conduct. For example, where the alleged breach involves harassment by following a protected person, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant:



Deliberately followed the complainant, and



Knew the complainant’s identity or presence when doing so.



The prosecution must also exclude any reasonable innocent explanation for the defendant’s movements. The defendant does not bear the burden of explaining their conduct; rather, the prosecution must demonstrate that the behaviour was not accidental or coincidental.



The case therefore establishes that breach offences under s 35 require proof of knowledge and intentional conduct, rather than merely proving the physical act.



Authority — Collateral Challenge Rule



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: McKenna v Smith \[2014] TASMC 11]

\[TOPIC: inability to challenge validity of underlying order in breach proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: collateral challenge rule, breach proceedings, validity of orders, procedural fairness, administrative review requirement]



In McKenna v Smith \[2014] TASMC 11, the Magistrates Court confirmed that defendants charged with breach of a family violence order cannot challenge the validity of the underlying order during the criminal proceedings.



The Court held that proceedings for breach of an order are not the appropriate forum to contest whether the order should have been made. Any challenge to the validity of the order must instead be pursued through the relevant administrative or appellate review mechanisms.



Allowing defendants to contest the legitimacy of the order during breach proceedings would undermine the protective purpose of the legislation and frustrate enforcement of the order.



The rule therefore ensures that breach proceedings focus solely on whether:



The order existed, and



The defendant breached its terms.



The legality or correctness of the original order must be addressed separately through the proper legal channels.



Authority — Each Contact as Separate Breach



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Acting Director of Public Prosecutions v JCN \[2015] TASFC 13]

\[TOPIC: separate communications constituting individual breaches of orders]

\[CONCEPTS: repeated breach offences, multiple communications, separate criminal acts, order compliance obligations]



In Acting Director of Public Prosecutions v JCN \[2015] TASFC 13, the Full Court confirmed that each individual act of prohibited contact may constitute a separate breach of a family violence order.



The Court held that where an order prohibits communication with the protected person, each individual communication — such as a phone call or message — may amount to a distinct offence.



This interpretation reflects the protective purpose of family violence orders. Treating repeated communications as separate breaches:



Reinforces the seriousness of the obligation to comply with the order.



Prevents defendants from avoiding liability by characterising repeated conduct as a single course of behaviour.



The decision emphasises that compliance with family violence orders requires strict adherence to each individual condition imposed by the court.



Authority — Contact Exceptions and “Matters Arising Out of the Relationship”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of contact exceptions in family violence orders]

\[CONCEPTS: relationship communication exception, parenting logistics communication, indirect contact, order interpretation]



In Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018] TASSC 55, the Court considered the scope of exceptions allowing parties subject to a family violence order to communicate for the purpose of discussing matters arising out of the relationship, particularly issues relating to children.



The case addressed situations where communication occurs indirectly through children or other intermediaries. The Court recognised that practical parenting arrangements may require limited communication regarding matters such as:



Child handover arrangements



Changes to agreed meeting locations



Immediate logistical decisions affecting the child



Where such communication occurs solely for the purpose of facilitating parenting arrangements and falls within the scope of the relevant exception, it may not constitute a breach of the order.



However, the exception must be interpreted narrowly, and communications that go beyond the permitted purpose may still amount to prohibited contact.



Authority — Interpretation of Family Violence Order Conditions



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Kerr v Brown \[2013] TASMC 30]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of conditions within family violence orders]

\[CONCEPTS: ordinary meaning interpretation, order construction principles, defendant comprehension standard, criminal sanction fairness]



In Kerr v Brown \[2013] TASMC 30, the Magistrates Court outlined the proper approach to interpreting the terms of a family violence order.



The Court held that conditions imposed by such orders must be interpreted according to their ordinary and common meaning, having regard to:



The context of the condition within the order, and



The purpose of the legislation.



Because breach of a family violence order constitutes a criminal offence, it is important that the obligations imposed on the defendant be interpreted in a manner that reflects how the language would be understood by ordinary persons without legal training.



The Court emphasised that the words used in an order should not be given an artificially extended meaning. Defendants must be able to understand their obligations in practical terms so that compliance is realistically achievable.



This interpretive principle promotes fairness in criminal proceedings while preserving the protective function of family violence orders.



Authority — Meaning of “Threat”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Meek (1981) 1 NZLR 499]

\[TOPIC: legal meaning of a threat in criminal law]

\[CONCEPTS: threat definition, hostile declaration, subjective recklessness, fear inducement, intention to be taken seriously]



In R v Meek (1981) 1 NZLR 499, the court considered the legal meaning of a threat within criminal law.



The decision held that the concept of a threat involves words or conduct intended to be taken seriously by the recipient. A threat may consist of:



A declaration of hostile intent, or



Words that reasonably create apprehension that harm may be carried out.



Importantly, the offence may be established even where there is no positive intention to cause fear, provided the accused was at least recklessly indifferent as to whether fear would be aroused.



This approach recognises that threatening language may have significant psychological impact even if the accused claims they did not genuinely intend to carry out the threat.



Authority — Interpretation of Apologies vs Discussions



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v H \[2021] TASMC 13]

\[TOPIC: distinction between apology and discussion under contact exceptions]

\[CONCEPTS: one-way communication, apology message, relationship discussion requirement, order breach analysis]



In Police v H \[2021] TASMC 13, the Court considered whether an apology message fell within an exception allowing communication to discuss matters arising from a relationship.



The Court held that a simple apology did not constitute a “discussion”. A discussion ordinarily involves:



A two-way exchange of ideas or opinions;



Communication intended to elicit a response; or



Dialogue aimed at resolving an issue.



An apology message that merely expresses regret without seeking engagement or response is a one-way communication. Such communication is not properly characterised as discussion of matters arising out of the relationship.



Accordingly, unless the message falls within another authorised exception, it may still constitute a breach of a contact prohibition.



Authority — Seriousness of Family Violence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Brown v Jones \[2021] TASSC 58]

\[TOPIC: seriousness of family violence offending in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: domestic violence seriousness, denunciation, general deterrence, sentencing principles]



In Brown v Jones \[2021] TASSC 58, the Supreme Court emphasised that family violence must always be regarded as serious criminal conduct.



The Court noted that domestic violence is often hidden from public view and may occur in private environments where victims are particularly vulnerable. As a result, sentencing courts must emphasise:



General deterrence, to discourage similar conduct within the community.



Denunciation, signalling society’s condemnation of such behaviour.



Recognition that family violence is a crime against the community, not merely a private dispute between individuals.



The Court cited earlier authorities affirming that sentences imposed for domestic violence offences must communicate strong censure and reinforce community expectations that such conduct will not be tolerated.



Authority — Psychological Harm in Domestic Violence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Cooper v Goodwin \[2024] TASSC 78]

\[TOPIC: psychological harm considerations in family violence sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: psychological harm, emotional trauma, non-physical violence, victim impact, domestic violence consequences]



In Cooper v Goodwin \[2024] TASSC 78, the Supreme Court emphasised that domestic violence is not confined to physical injury.



The Court rejected submissions focusing exclusively on the absence of long-term physical harm and held that psychological and emotional harm must also be considered when assessing the seriousness of offending.



Domestic violence frequently produces consequences such as:



Fear and anxiety



Loss of personal security



Ongoing psychological trauma



The decision reinforces that the criminal law recognises non-physical forms of harm as significant factors when determining the seriousness of family violence offences and the appropriate sentence.



Family Violence Sentencing and Orders

Presence of Children as an Aggravating Sentencing Factor



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[CASE: Allen v Kerr \[2009] TASSC 10]

\[TOPIC: children witnessing family violence as aggravating factor]

\[CONCEPTS: aggravating factor, family violence sentencing, children witnessing violence, psychological harm to children, domestic violence impact on children, sentencing seriousness, exposure to violence]



Violence occurring in the presence of children is recognised in Tasmanian criminal law as a significant aggravating factor in sentencing for family violence offences. Courts treat the presence or involvement of children as increasing the seriousness of offending because of the broader harm inflicted beyond the direct victim.



In Allen v Kerr \[2009] TASSC 10, Porter AJ explained that violence witnessed by children in a domestic setting is particularly damaging. Children who observe violence between family members experience distress and emotional harm, particularly where the victim is a parent or a person occupying a parental role.



The aggravating significance arises from several factors:



Exposure to violence can cause immediate emotional trauma for children.



Witnessing violence may disrupt children’s sense of security within the home.



Repeated exposure risks normalising violent behaviour as an acceptable means of resolving conflict.



The experience may produce long-term psychological consequences and influence future behavioural patterns.



For these reasons, Tasmanian courts consistently recognise that family violence occurring in the presence of children increases the objective seriousness of the offending conduct. Sentencing courts may therefore impose heavier penalties to reflect the expanded harm caused and to reinforce denunciation of domestic violence occurring in environments involving children.



Authority — Rehabilitation and Imprisonment in Family Violence Assault



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Miller v McCullogh \[2024] TASSC 61]

\[TOPIC: sentencing weight of rehabilitation in family violence offences]

\[CONCEPTS: rehabilitation efforts, sentencing mitigation, family violence assaults, guilty plea credit, remorse, domestic violence sentencing, imprisonment appeal]



Miller v McCullogh \[2024] TASSC 61 confirms that rehabilitation efforts undertaken by an offender may mitigate sentence but will not necessarily outweigh the seriousness of violent offending in a family violence context.



In that case the offender committed multiple assaults against a partner, including holding the victim by the neck and engaging in emotional abuse. The offender had prior convictions for assault and breaches of orders. After being charged, the offender sought counselling to address alcohol abuse and mental health issues and later entered guilty pleas.



The Supreme Court accepted that the offender had taken genuine steps toward rehabilitation prior to sentencing. Evidence showed participation in counselling and developing insight into the offending behaviour. However, the court held that those efforts did not substantially reduce the seriousness of the offences given:



The violence occurred in a family violence context.



The offender had relevant prior convictions.



The conduct involved physical violence against a vulnerable partner.



The guilty plea occurred late in the proceedings.



The court concluded that although rehabilitation and remorse were relevant mitigating factors, they carried limited weight given the gravity of the violence and the need for deterrence and denunciation in family violence offending. The appeal against the custodial sentence was therefore rejected.



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)

Family Violence Orders — Statutory Test for Making an FVO



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 16]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 16]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: statutory test for making family violence order]

\[CONCEPTS: family violence order test, balance of probabilities, likelihood of future violence, protective order, domestic violence prevention, court satisfaction requirement]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 16 establishes the statutory conditions under which a court may make a Family Violence Order (FVO).



Under s 16(1), a court may make an FVO if satisfied on the balance of probabilities that:



A person has committed family violence; and



That person may again commit family violence.



The statutory threshold is civil rather than criminal, requiring proof on the balance of probabilities rather than beyond reasonable doubt. The court must assess both past conduct and the likelihood of future violence.



Section 16(2) authorises the court to include any conditions considered necessary or desirable to prevent further family violence or to protect affected persons. The provision gives the court broad discretion to impose protective restrictions tailored to the circumstances.



Examples of conditions permitted by s 16(3) include:



Requiring the respondent to vacate or avoid certain premises, including premises in which they have a legal interest.



Prohibiting possession of firearms.



Requiring surrender or disposal of firearms.



The purpose of the provision is protective rather than punitive. The overarching objective of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) is to ensure the safety, psychological wellbeing and interests of persons affected by family violence.



Interim Family Violence Orders — Power to Issue During Proceedings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 23]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 23]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: interim family violence orders during proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: interim protection order, IFVO powers, urgent protective orders, court interim jurisdiction, domestic violence interim orders]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 23 grants courts power to issue an Interim Family Violence Order (IFVO) at any stage of proceedings involving an application for a Family Violence Order.



The section allows the court to make temporary protective orders before the final determination of the application. The objective is to ensure immediate protection for affected persons where risk exists.



Key procedural features include:



The court may issue an IFVO at any stage of the proceedings.



The order may be made without requiring the full evidentiary test that applies to final FVO determinations under s 16.



Leave is not required to vary or extend an IFVO.



An IFVO may be made even if the respondent is not present in court.



These features allow the court to respond quickly to potential risks associated with family violence. The interim order operates as a protective measure pending final resolution of the application.



Revocation or Variation of FVO — Substantial Change Requirement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 20]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 20]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: leave requirement for variation or revocation of FVO]

\[CONCEPTS: substantial change test, leave requirement, revocation of FVO, variation application, changed circumstances, domestic violence order review]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 20 governs applications seeking to vary or revoke an existing Family Violence Order.



Before an application may proceed, the court must grant leave to hear the application. Leave may only be granted if the court is satisfied that there has been a substantial change in circumstances since the order was made or last varied.



The statutory requirement prevents repeated litigation of matters already determined. The court must assume that all relevant facts existing at the time the original order was made were considered during that earlier determination.



Accordingly, arguments that certain facts were overlooked at the time of the original order are generally irrelevant unless they demonstrate a genuine change in circumstances occurring after the order.



The term “substantial change” is not defined in the Act. Courts interpret it to mean a significant or obvious change affecting the circumstances of the parties or the necessity of the order.



This threshold serves two primary purposes:



Protecting the stability and effectiveness of protective orders.



Preventing misuse of the variation process to relitigate prior decisions.



Authority — Meaning of “Substantial Change”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: McLean v Kemp \[2005] TASSC 100]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of substantial change in FVO proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: substantial change meaning, variation threshold, leave requirement, domestic violence order review, changed circumstances test]



In McLean v Kemp \[2005] TASSC 100, the Supreme Court considered the meaning of “substantial change in circumstances” when determining whether leave should be granted to vary or revoke a Family Violence Order.



The Court explained that the phrase should be interpreted according to its ordinary meaning. The requirement implies that the court must be satisfied that there has been a significant or obvious change affecting the relevant circumstances of the parties since the order was made.



The decision also emphasised an important procedural distinction:



Granting leave to hear an application is not equivalent to deciding the application itself.



The threshold for leave is therefore limited to determining whether sufficient evidence exists to justify reconsideration of the order. It does not involve deciding whether the order should ultimately be varied or revoked.



Ex Parte Final Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 31(7)]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 31(7)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: making final family violence orders in absence of respondent]

\[CONCEPTS: ex parte family violence order, respondent absence, service attempts, arrest warrant power, domestic violence procedure]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 31(7) permits a court to make a final Family Violence Order even if the respondent is not present at the hearing.



The court may do so if satisfied that:



A sealed copy of the application has been served on the respondent; or



Reasonable attempts have been made to serve the sealed application.



Where these requirements are satisfied, the court may proceed to determine the application and make a final order in the respondent’s absence. The court may also issue a warrant for the arrest of the respondent if necessary.



This provision ensures that proceedings cannot be indefinitely delayed by respondents avoiding service or failing to attend court.



Authority — Requirement for Actual Service Attempts



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lacroix v Lacroix \[2015] TASSC 42]

\[TOPIC: service requirement before ex parte family violence order]

\[CONCEPTS: service attempt requirement, ex parte order safeguards, domestic violence procedure, respondent notice, court service obligations]



In Lacroix v Lacroix \[2015] TASSC 42, the Supreme Court emphasised that courts must ensure genuine attempts at service before making final orders in the absence of a respondent.



The decision confirmed that the statutory requirement in Family Violence Act proceedings requires actual attempts to serve the respondent. The court must be satisfied that reasonable steps have been taken to notify the respondent of the proceedings.



This requirement protects procedural fairness while still enabling the court to proceed where respondents attempt to avoid service.



Service of Family Violence Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 25]

\[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 25]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: personal service and commencement of FVO]

\[CONCEPTS: personal service requirement, domestic violence order service, effective date of order, service in court, enforcement commencement]



Under Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 25, a person is taken to have been served with a Family Violence Order if:



The person was present in court when the order was made; or



The order is personally served on the person.



Where personal service occurs, the order takes effect immediately upon service.



The requirement of personal service distinguishes Family Violence Act procedures from the general service rules contained in the Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas). The latter statute allows service of documents in a variety of ways, including postal service, unless an Act specifies otherwise.



Because the Family Violence Act explicitly requires personal service, the broader service mechanisms under the Acts Interpretation Act 1931 do not apply.



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)

FVO by Consent



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 22] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 22] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: family violence order by consent] \[CONCEPTS: consent order, FVO by consent, no admissions, agreed protective order, domestic violence consent order, protective order without contested hearing]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 22 creates a mechanism by which a court may make a Family Violence Order in terms consented to by the parties. The provision allows the court to formalise agreed protective terms without requiring a contested hearing on the allegations.



Section 22 contains two key features:



Under s 22(1), the court may make an FVO in terms consented to by the parties.



Under s 22(2), the court may record that the person against whom the order is made does not admit any of the matters alleged in the application.



The provision is procedurally important because it permits protective orders to be made even where the respondent does not formally admit the underlying allegations. That reflects the protective purpose of the legislation and allows orders to be made without the delay and risk of a contested evidentiary hearing.



The text supplied raises an interpretive question about whether s 22 is a self-contained source of power or whether it depends upon the court first being satisfied of the matters in Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 16. That interpretive issue is addressed separately because it is a distinct doctrinal question from the bare statutory mechanics of a consent order.



Whether Consent Requires Satisfaction of s 16



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 16, 22] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) ss 16, 22] \[TYPE: interpretive principle] \[TOPIC: whether consent FVO is independent of s 16 belief requirement] \[CONCEPTS: independent power, consent order construction, requisite belief, family violence may occur again, balance of probabilities, protective jurisdiction, statutory interpretation]



The interpretive issue raised by Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) ss 16 and 22 is whether a court can make an FVO by consent without first being satisfied on the balance of probabilities that family violence has occurred and may occur again.



Section 16 provides the ordinary merits-based pathway for making an FVO. It requires the court to be satisfied that:



A person has committed family violence.



That person may again commit family violence.



Section 22 separately provides that a court may make an FVO in terms consented to by the parties and may record that the respondent does not admit the alleged matters.



The source text argues that s 22 should be read as an independent power. The reasoning is that once parties consent to the terms of an order, the court’s protective jurisdiction can be exercised without the need to determine the factual allegations to the s 16 standard. On that view, s 22 exists precisely to permit an agreed order without admissions and without a judicial finding that family violence has been proved.



The competing construction is that s 22 does not displace s 16, but instead provides a procedural mechanism for making an order only where the court still reaches the requisite state of satisfaction.



Because the source text presents this as an unresolved interpretive argument rather than settled authority, this chunk is marked for review. \[REVIEW]



Authority — Analogous Construction of a Consent Power



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Marriage v Tasmania Police \[2020] TASMC 4] \[TOPIC: whether consent provision is independent or conditioned by head power] \[CONCEPTS: analogous authority, consent provision, head power, statutory construction, independent source of power, protective order interpretation, no admission order]



Marriage v Tasmania Police \[2020] TASMC 4 is identified in the source as an analogous authority on the construction of a statutory consent power. Although the case concerned the Monetary Penalties Enforcement Service Act rather than the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas), it is said to consider whether a consent provision operates as an independent power or remains dependent on satisfaction of a separate “head” provision.



The relevance of the authority is interpretive rather than direct. It supports examining whether a statutory power to make orders “by consent” should be read as:



A distinct source of power capable of operating without proof of the ordinary substantive preconditions; or



A procedural pathway that remains anchored to a primary substantive threshold.



The source does not supply the detailed holding or reasoning beyond directing attention to paragraph 3 onwards. Accordingly, the case should be treated cautiously and used only as contextual authority for the interpretive debate about Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 22. It does not, on the supplied material alone, conclusively resolve that question.



Because the doctrinal principle drawn from the case is only partially stated in the source, this chunk is marked for review. \[REVIEW]



IFVO Is Not Automatic on Application



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Franklin v Burke \[2022] TASSC 59] \[TOPIC: court not obliged to make interim family violence order merely because application is filed] \[CONCEPTS: judicial discretion, no rubber stamp, interim order discretion, protective application, court not automatic, IFVO threshold, family violence procedure]



Franklin v Burke \[2022] TASSC 59 supports the principle that a court is not obliged to make an Interim Family Violence Order merely because an application has been filed. The existence of an application does not require a court to rubber-stamp interim relief.



The source uses the decision for a narrow procedural proposition: the court retains a judicial discretion and must independently determine whether an interim order should be made. That reflects the broader principle that even in protective jurisdictions, the exercise of statutory power remains a judicial act rather than an administrative formality.



The practical effect of the principle is that submissions for an interim order should address why the court ought to exercise its power, not merely point to the filing of an application. Matters such as risk, urgency, protective need, and the statutory objects of the Act remain relevant to the exercise of discretion.



This authority complements the statutory interim order power under Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 23 by reinforcing that the power is available but not compulsory in every case.



Jurisdiction Over Family Violence Order Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: lower court jurisdiction over family violence order applications] \[CONCEPTS: lower court jurisdiction, magistrates court, who can hear FVO application, jurisdictional limit, family violence proceedings, court definition]



Applications for Family Violence Orders under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) must be made and heard in the lower court. The source states that the Act defines “court” in a way that confines the ordinary FVO application jurisdiction to the lower court, so references in the Act to a “court” are to be read as references to the magistrates court.



The source further states that the Supreme Court does not exercise the general application jurisdiction for Family Violence Orders. Its role is limited to specific powers conferred by the Act, particularly orders under s 36.



The practical jurisdictional consequence is:



Applications for FVOs are to be commenced and determined in the lower court.



Only a lower court can ordinarily make an FVO or deal with the application process.



The Supreme Court’s role is not to hear FVO applications generally, but to exercise only those distinct powers expressly conferred by the statute.



Because the exact definitional provision for “court” is not quoted in the source extract, this chunk is confined to the jurisdictional proposition expressly stated in the supplied material.



Jurisdiction of Justices



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 54T] \[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54T] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: limited family violence jurisdiction of justices] \[CONCEPTS: justice of the peace powers, limited jurisdiction, family violence procedure, interim order powers, revocation only, magistrate versus justice]



Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54T limits the jurisdiction that may be exercised when the lower court is constituted by one or more justices, one of whom is not a magistrate. In that situation, the court may exercise only specified powers under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas).



Those powers are limited to:



Granting or refusing bail under s 12.



Making, varying, extending, or revoking an interim FVO under s 23.



Revoking, but not making, varying, or extending, an FVO under s 20.



The rule therefore draws a clear distinction between the broader powers of a magistrate and the restricted powers of a bench that includes a justice who is not a magistrate. It prevents such a bench from exercising the full range of substantive family violence order powers.



This matters in practice because the composition of the court affects what orders can lawfully be made. A court constituted in the restricted manner cannot make a final FVO or vary or extend an existing FVO, though it may revoke one.



Police Standing in Family Violence Proceedings



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 31(2A)] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 31(2A)] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[CASE: McLean v Kemp \[2005] TASSC 100] \[TOPIC: police standing following legislative amendment] \[CONCEPTS: standing, party to proceedings, police participation, family violence applications, statutory amendment, commissioner of police, procedural status]



The source states that police were not originally treated as a party to family violence proceedings, and that Parliament later amended the legislation to change that position by inserting Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 31(2A).



The stated authority for the earlier position is McLean v Kemp \[2005] TASSC 100. The legislative response is described as a remedial amendment intended to confer standing or party status where the earlier statute had not done so.



The doctrinal point is that police participation in family violence proceedings depends upon statutory conferral, not merely practical involvement in the matter. Once s 31(2A) was introduced, the Act expressly addressed that gap.



This issue is significant because standing determines whether police may appear, oppose, support, or otherwise participate in the proceeding as a party rather than merely as a witness or information provider.



Standing Ambiguity in Fresh Applications After PFVO



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 14(8), 15, 31(2A)] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) ss 14(8), 15, 31(2A)] \[TYPE: interpretive principle] \[CASE: Lacroix v Lacroix \[2015] TASSC 42; Lacroix v Lacroix \[2018] TASFC 8] \[TOPIC: whether police remain parties in fresh family violence applications after PFVO revocation] \[CONCEPTS: standing ambiguity, fresh application, PFVO revoked, police as party, statutory construction, Part 3 application, protective continuity]



The source identifies an unresolved ambiguity about whether police remain a party when a fresh Family Violence Order application is filed in circumstances where a Police Family Violence Order has been revoked by operation of Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(8).



The argument advanced in the source is that where a PFVO is automatically revoked on the making of an IFVO or FVO, an application under s 15 in those circumstances may need to be construed as also being made under Part 3. If that construction is correct, s 31(2A) may engage and confer party status on police.



The practical concern is that without such a construction, a PFVO could be significantly weakened by the making of a more limited FVO without further input from the Commissioner for Police, even though police had earlier formed the view that stronger protection was necessary.



Because the source frames this as an interpretive problem and does not provide a settled authority resolving it, this chunk is marked for review. \[REVIEW]



Family Court Orders and Inconsistency



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: legal doctrine] \[TOPIC: interaction between family violence orders and family court parenting orders] \[CONCEPTS: family court order, parenting order inconsistency, Family Law Act Part 7, section 68R, suspension of parenting orders, children orders, federal family law interaction]



In the Family Violence Act context, a “family court order” is said in the source to mean an order made under Part 7 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) concerning children. The source cautions that not all Family Court orders fall within that definition, because orders may be made under other Parts of the federal Act.



This distinction matters because the federal inconsistency provisions relied upon in family violence practice are confined to parenting orders under Part 7. The source specifically notes Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 68R, which permits a state court making or varying a family violence order to revive, vary, discharge, or suspend a parenting order to the extent of inconsistency.



The doctrinal point is therefore narrow but important:



Practitioners should confirm whether the relevant federal order is in fact a Part 7 parenting order.



Only then do the inconsistency powers, including s 68R, become available in the usual way.



Orders made under other Parts of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) may not engage those powers.



Approved Forms for Orders and Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 30] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 30] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: approved forms for FVO, PFVO and applications] \[CONCEPTS: approved form, prescribed form, Chief Magistrate forms, initiating process, no oral application, family violence forms, filing requirements]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 30 requires that an FVO, a PFVO, or an application under the Act be in a form approved by the Chief Magistrate. The provision makes the approved form a procedural requirement for the commencement or formal making of orders and applications under the Act.



The source links this requirement with a practice direction issued under Magistrates Court Act 1987 (Tas) s 15AA approving forms including:



Family Violence Order



Interim Family Violence Order



Application for Family Violence Order



Application for variation of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order



Application for extension of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order



Application for revocation of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order



The supplied note stresses that these are written forms, not oral applications. The practical consequence suggested in the source is that an oral request for interim protection is better understood not as a formal application under s 30, but as an invitation for the magistrate to exercise the power to make an interim order under Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 23.



Application Requirements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 54L] \[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54L] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: formal requirements for family violence applications] \[CONCEPTS: application contents, statutory declaration, affidavit requirement, filing with clerk, order sought, police application, private application]



Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54L sets out the general formal requirements for applications in Family Violence Act proceedings.



An application must:



Specify the order sought.



Include supporting sworn material.



Be filed with the clerk.



The form of sworn material depends on the applicant:



If the application is made by a police officer, it must include a statutory declaration by the officer that, to the best of the officer’s knowledge and belief, the information in the application is true.



If the application is made by another applicant, it must include an affidavit by that person to the same effect.



The rule distinguishes police and non-police applicants while preserving the core requirement that the application be supported by sworn evidence. The requirement serves both evidentiary and procedural functions by ensuring that the court is asked to act upon material carrying formal legal responsibility for its truthfulness.



Service of Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 54N] \[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54N] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: personal service of family violence applications] \[CONCEPTS: service of application, sealed copy, personal service, commissioner of police, memorandum of service, family violence procedure, proof of service]



Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54N governs service of applications under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas). The rule requires personal service of a sealed copy of the application as soon as practicable after filing.



The service obligations depend on the nature of the application:



For an application for an FVO, or an application to vary, extend, or revoke an FVO, all other parties must be personally served.



For an application to vary, extend, or revoke a PFVO, all other parties and the Commissioner of Police must be personally served.



Where a police officer applies to revoke an FVO or PFVO, service of a sealed copy is not required.



The person effecting service must also:



Endorse a copy of the application with a memorandum stating the day, place, and mode of service.



Sign the memorandum.



Lodge the endorsed copy with the clerk as soon as practicable.



The rule imposes a structured proof-of-service regime to ensure the court can verify that procedural fairness requirements have been met before the matter proceeds.



Dispensing With Part 9B Requirements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 54S] \[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54S] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: power to dispense with family violence procedural requirements] \[CONCEPTS: dispense with compliance, procedural flexibility, justices discretion, Part 9B, family violence procedure, terms and conditions]



Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54S gives justices a discretionary power to dispense with compliance with provisions of Part 9B of the Rules in proceedings under Part 4 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas).



The power may be exercised:



Upon such terms and conditions as the justices think necessary.



In relation to any provision of Part 9B.



In proceedings under Part 4 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas).



This rule creates procedural flexibility. It allows the court to relieve parties from strict compliance where the interests of justice, practicality, or urgency require it. However, the rule does not itself displace substantive statutory requirements. It is a procedural dispensing power, not a source of authority to ignore the Act.



The existence of this rule may become relevant where strict adherence to application, service, or hearing procedures would frustrate the protective purpose of the legislation.



Hearing of Applications and Affidavit Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 54P] \[CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54P] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: affidavit evidence and cross-examination in FVO hearings] \[CONCEPTS: affidavit evidence, hearing procedure, cross-examination notice, family violence hearing, written evidence, attendance requirement, affidavit use]



Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) reg 54P governs the hearing of applications under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) and permits evidence to be given by affidavit.



The rule provides that:



Evidence may be given by affidavit.



A person who makes an affidavit is not required to attend unless a party or the justice requires attendance.



A party may require the deponent to attend for cross-examination by written notice.



The notice must be given not less than 10 clear working days before the proceedings.



If the deponent fails to attend after being required for cross-examination, the court may:



Refuse to allow the affidavit to be used.



Allow the affidavit to be used on conditions the court thinks fit.



Adjourn the proceedings until the person attends for cross-examination.



The rule balances procedural efficiency with fairness. It allows written evidence to be used in a protective jurisdiction while preserving the right to test that evidence where necessary.



Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas)

Form of Affidavit



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 55] \[CITATION: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) reg 55] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: formal requirements for affidavits] \[CONCEPTS: affidavit form, first person, numbered paragraphs, annexures, signature requirements, sworn document, irregularity in form]



Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) reg 55 prescribes the formal requirements for affidavits used in proceedings.



An affidavit must:



Specify the title of the action.



Be drawn in the first person.



State the deponent’s name, address, and occupation, or otherwise describe the person.



Be divided into consecutively numbered paragraphs relating, as far as practicable, to separate portions of the subject matter.



State sums of money or numbers in figures only.



Contain a signed statement by the person before whom it was sworn specifying the full name of the deponent and the day and place of swearing.



Be signed on each page by the deponent and the person before whom it was sworn.



Any annexure or exhibit filed with the affidavit must be identified by the title of the action and signed by the person before whom the affidavit was sworn.



Regulation 55(3) also provides that, unless the court orders otherwise, an affidavit may still be received despite irregularity in form. That preserves flexibility and prevents minor formal defects from automatically defeating admissibility.



Contents of Affidavit



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 56] \[CITATION: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) reg 56] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: permissible contents of affidavits] \[CONCEPTS: own knowledge, hearsay in interlocutory matters, source of information, grounds of belief, affidavit evidence, admissible content, personal knowledge]



Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) reg 56 regulates what may be included in an affidavit.



The general rule in reg 56(1) is that an affidavit must be confined to facts to which the deponent can speak from their own knowledge. This reflects the ordinary evidentiary expectation that affidavit material is based on firsthand knowledge rather than hearsay.



An exception exists for interlocutory applications. Under reg 56(2), an affidavit filed for an interlocutory application may include statements based on information received and believed to be true, provided the affidavit also states:



The sources of the information; and



The grounds for believing the information is true.



The rule therefore preserves the distinction between final evidentiary proof and the more flexible approach often taken on interlocutory matters. In family violence proceedings, that distinction may matter where an interim or procedural application is being determined on affidavit material.



Hearing an Interlocutory Application



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas)] \[SECTION: reg 74] \[CITATION: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) reg 74] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: interlocutory application hearing rules] \[CONCEPTS: open court, telephone appearance, video link, adjournment limits, fact proved by affidavit, interlocutory hearing, procedural management]



Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) reg 74 sets out procedural rules for hearing interlocutory applications.



The rule provides that:



Unless the court orders otherwise, an interlocutory application is to be heard in open court.



With leave, a person may appear by telephone or video link.



The hearing may be adjourned on no more than two occasions and only to a fixed date.



A fact may be proved by affidavit at the hearing.



These rules are procedural management provisions directed toward efficient handling of interlocutory matters. They allow flexibility in mode of appearance while placing limits on adjournments and confirming that affidavit evidence may be used to prove facts.



Oaths Act Definition of “Affidavit”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Oaths Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 3] \[CITATION: Oaths Act 2001 (Tas) s 3] \[TYPE: interpretive principle] \[TOPIC: whether Oaths Act definition expands meaning of affidavit in family violence procedure] \[CONCEPTS: affidavit definition, statutory declaration, affirmation, Oaths Act interpretation, procedural uncertainty, sworn material, evidentiary form]



Oaths Act 2001 (Tas) s 3 contains a definition of “affidavit” that includes affirmations, statutory declarations and other declarations for the purposes of that Act. The source questions whether that definition should be treated as affecting the meaning of “affidavit” in Family Violence Act or Magistrates Court procedure.



The concern raised in the source is that the definition expressly operates “in this Act”, which may mean it is not a generally applicable definition for all other Tasmanian statutes and rules. That creates uncertainty about whether a statutory declaration can stand in place of an affidavit unless another provision expressly permits it.



The text also notes that even if a statutory declaration is accepted as a species of sworn material, it may still need to comply with whatever form is prescribed by the relevant procedural rules or the Oaths Act itself.



Because the source expressly states uncertainty on this point and does not provide authority resolving it, this chunk is marked for review. \[REVIEW]



Police Family Violence Orders

Varying, Extending and Revoking a PFVO



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 14(7), 14(8)] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) ss 14(7), 14(8)] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: procedural routes for varying, extending and revoking police family violence orders] \[CONCEPTS: PFVO variation, PFVO extension, PFVO revocation, leave not required, inspector consent, automatic revocation, police protection order]



The source identifies several procedural features governing Police Family Violence Orders under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas).



First, leave of the court is not required to make an application to vary, extend, or revoke a PFVO in court. The source contrasts that with the later position once the matter becomes an FVO, where a second attempt may require leave and proof of substantial change.



Secondly, the source states that an application to vary a PFVO may be made to an inspector, but only with the consent of both parties, referring to Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(7).



Thirdly, Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(8) is said to provide that a PFVO is automatically revoked when an IFVO or FVO is made in relation to both parties.



These rules matter because they show that PFVOs operate as an immediate police-issued protective mechanism, but they may later be superseded, adjusted, or revoked through statutory pathways that differ from those applying to final court-made FVOs.



Authority to Issue a PFVO and Evidentiary Presumption



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 35(3)] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35(3)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: rebuttable presumption of validity of police family violence order] \[CONCEPTS: rebuttable presumption, PFVO validity, proving order was in force, issuing authority, evidentiary shortcut, contravention prosecution, validly made order]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35(3) creates an evidentiary presumption concerning a Police Family Violence Order. The source describes the section as establishing that an allegation that the order was made is evidence that it was made and in force at the time of the alleged contravention.



The practical effect is that the prosecution is not required, in the first instance, to prove every administrative step involved in the making of the PFVO. The section operates as a rebuttable presumption that the order was validly made and remained operative.



That presumption is important in contravention proceedings because it reduces the evidentiary burden of proving the formal validity of the order. However, because the presumption is rebuttable, it may still be displaced by evidence showing that the order was not lawfully made or was not in force.



The source further indicates that mere absence of evidence that the order was validly made is insufficient to rebut the presumption. Positive evidence is required to support a contention that the issuing officer lacked the relevant authority or rank.



Authority — Proof of PFVO Validity and Acting Rank



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: McKenna v Smith \[2014] TASMC 11] \[TOPIC: presumption of PFVO validity and proof of issuing officer’s authority] \[CONCEPTS: acting sergeant, evidentiary presumption, validly made order, police rank authority, prosecution proof, rebuttable presumption, PFVO challenge]



McKenna v Smith \[2014] TASMC 11 confirms that the evidentiary presumption in Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35(3) extends to the validity of the making of a Police Family Violence Order, including the authority of the issuing officer.



The case supports the proposition that where it is alleged that a PFVO was made and in force, that allegation constitutes evidence that it was made pursuant to the statutory power and by an officer of the requisite rank. The court accepted that, absent specific evidence to the contrary, it was not necessary for the prosecution to prove in detail that the officer held the acting rank required to issue the order.



The principle taken from the case is:



Section 35(3) creates a rebuttable presumption that the order was made and validly made.



That presumption includes the issuing officer’s authority unless displaced.



A defendant cannot rebut the presumption merely by pointing to gaps in the prosecution proof; there must be affirmative evidence supporting invalidity.



This authority is particularly important where the issuing officer was acting in a higher rank rather than permanently holding that rank.



Acting Rank and Delegated Authority to Issue PFVO



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: legal doctrine] \[TOPIC: acting rank appointments and delegated police powers relevant to PFVO issuance] \[CONCEPTS: acting sergeant, Police Service Act, delegated authority, Commissioner of Police, temporary higher rank, evidentiary certificate, police powers]



The source explains that a PFVO may only be issued by an officer of the rank of sergeant or above, or by an officer otherwise authorised by the Commissioner of Police. Several statutory provisions are said to support the authority of acting officers and delegated decision-makers.



The source identifies:



Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) s 34 as conferring power on the Commissioner to appoint an officer temporarily to act in a higher role or rank, with the appointee thereby vested with the powers of that office or rank.



Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) s 8 as allowing the Commissioner to delegate powers.



Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) s 95 as providing for evidentiary certification of such delegation.



Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas) s 23AA as supporting the view that a statutory reference to a rank need not identify a named person.



The source also notes the existence of a delegation document to inspectors or higher. Taken together, these provisions support a doctrinal argument that a PFVO issued by an acting sergeant or an authorised delegate can be validly made without proving permanent appointment to the rank.



Because the precise text of the relevant Police Service Act provisions is not reproduced in the extract, this chunk is confined to the doctrinal proposition as stated in the source.



Permitted Conditions of a PFVO



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 14(3)] \[CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(3)] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: conditions that may be imposed in a police family violence order] \[CONCEPTS: PFVO conditions, vacate premises, surrender firearms, no contact, approach restrictions, harassment prohibition, protective terms]



Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(3) limits the conditions that may be included in a Police Family Violence Order. A PFVO must remain within the statutory list of authorised protective restrictions.



The source reproduces the following categories of permitted conditions:



Requiring the person to vacate premises, whether or not they have a legal or equitable interest in them.



Prohibiting entry to premises or permitting entry only on specified conditions.



Requiring surrender of any firearm or other weapon.
