\[CASE: Halliday v Nevill \[1984] HCA 80]

\[TOPIC: implied licence to approach residential property]

\[CONCEPTS: implied licence doctrine, police entry driveway, lawful approach to door, trespass limits, investigation access, private property access]



The High Court in Halliday v Nevill \[1984] HCA 80 recognised that members of the public, including police officers, possess an implied licence to approach the front door of a residence via ordinary access points such as driveways or pathways. This licence allows entry for ordinary purposes such as knocking on the door, making enquiries, or communicating with the occupier.



The implied licence exists because property owners ordinarily permit members of the public to approach their residence for legitimate purposes. Police may therefore walk up a driveway or path to speak with occupants without committing trespass, provided they do not exceed the scope of the licence.



However, the licence is limited in scope. It does not authorise entry into areas not ordinarily accessible to visitors, such as enclosed yards or internal parts of a dwelling, unless separate legal authority exists. The licence may also be revoked expressly by the occupier or impliedly by clear signage or barriers.



In drink- and drug-driving investigations, this doctrine often provides the legal basis for officers to approach a residence where a suspect driver has entered the driveway or property shortly after driving.



block\_019



Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) — Presumption of Drugs Within 6 Hours



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 23A]

\[CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 23A]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: presumption relating to presence of drugs]

\[CONCEPTS: statutory presumption, drug driving proof, six hour rule, evidentiary burden, oral fluid analysis, proof of drug presence]



Section 23A of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) creates a statutory presumption that a driver had a prohibited drug in their system at the relevant time of driving if testing reveals the presence of the drug within six hours of that time.



The provision addresses the evidentiary difficulty inherent in proving the precise drug concentration in a driver’s system at the exact moment of driving. Instead of requiring the prosecution to prove the presence of drugs at that earlier moment, the statute allows a court to infer that drugs were present if they are detected within the prescribed six-hour period following the relevant driving event.



The presumption operates unless displaced by contrary evidence. In practice, it shifts the evidentiary burden onto the defendant to produce evidence capable of rebutting the presumption.



This mechanism reflects a legislative policy objective to facilitate the enforcement of drug-driving laws and reduce the forensic complexity of proving intoxication at the exact time of driving.



block\_019



Authority — Statutory Presumption of Alcohol Concentration



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Ausburn v Tasmania Police \[2009] TASSC 21]

\[TOPIC: operation of statutory BAC presumption]

\[CONCEPTS: BAC presumption, burden on defendant, alcohol concentration proof, drink driving evidence, statutory deeming rule, rebuttal of presumption]



In Ausburn v Tasmania Police \[2009] TASSC 21 the Supreme Court of Tasmania confirmed the operation of the presumption contained in s 23(4) of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas). That provision deems the alcohol concentration recorded in a breath or blood test to represent the driver’s alcohol concentration at the time of the relevant act of driving.



The Court held that the presumption imposes a persuasive burden on the defendant. It is not merely an evidential burden. A defendant must prove on the balance of probabilities that their alcohol concentration at the time of driving did not exceed the prescribed limit.



The purpose of the provision is to simplify the prosecution of drink-driving offences. Without such a presumption, courts would often need to determine alcohol levels retrospectively by examining drinking patterns, absorption rates, and expert evidence.



By deeming the measured concentration to apply at the time of driving unless disproved, the provision significantly reduces the evidentiary complexity of these prosecutions.



block\_019



Traffic Act 1925 (Tas) — Facilitation of Proof by Averment



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Traffic Act 1925 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 51]

\[CITATION: Traffic Act 1925 (Tas) s 51]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: proof of traffic matters by averment]

\[CONCEPTS: averment evidence rule, speeding prosecutions, evidentiary shortcut, proof of traffic sign, age evidence, complaint averment]



Section 51 of the Traffic Act 1925 (Tas) provides evidentiary shortcuts in prosecutions under that Act by allowing certain matters to be proved by averment in a complaint.



Under the provision, an allegation in the complaint that a specified traffic sign applied to a particular stretch of road is itself evidence of that fact unless rebutted. Similarly, averments regarding a person’s age are treated as evidence of the matter stated.



The legislative purpose of the section is to streamline proof of routine factual matters in traffic prosecutions. Without such provisions, the prosecution might otherwise be required to call additional witnesses or produce formal documentation to establish basic facts such as the existence or applicability of traffic signs.



However, the rule does not make the averment conclusive. The defendant retains the right to challenge the asserted fact by adducing contrary evidence.



Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 7B — Liability to Submit to Oral Fluid Testing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 7B]

\[CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 7B]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: liability to roadside oral fluid testing]

\[CONCEPTS: roadside drug testing, oral fluid test, drug driving investigation, police testing powers, driver liability to test, roadside OFT procedure]



Section 7B of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) establishes the circumstances in which a person becomes liable to submit to a roadside oral fluid test (OFT). The provision permits police to require a person who has driven or is suspected of having driven a motor vehicle to undergo a roadside test for the presence of prescribed drugs.



The testing must occur “at or near” the location where the driving occurred. The expression “at or near” is interpreted flexibly in light of the circumstances of the investigation, including the need to maintain road safety and conduct the test within a practical timeframe following driving.



Once a roadside oral fluid test has been conducted and produces a positive indication of drugs, s 7B(5) provides that the person becomes liable to undergo further analysis, typically through a confirmatory oral fluid analysis procedure conducted by an approved operator.



The purpose of the section is to provide police with a structured process for detecting drug-impaired driving, beginning with a roadside screening procedure and progressing to evidentiary analysis where required.



block\_019



Authority — Meaning of “At or Near” for Drug Testing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v Bentley \[2008] TASMC 9]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of “at or near” in roadside drug testing]

\[CONCEPTS: proximity requirement, roadside drug test location, police testing powers, interpretation of statutory phrase, accident investigation testing]



In Police v Bentley \[2008] TASMC 9 the Magistrates Court considered the meaning of the phrase “at or near” in the context of roadside drug testing powers under the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas).



The Court accepted that a roadside oral fluid test conducted approximately 100 metres from the location of the relevant driving incident satisfied the statutory requirement that the test occur “at or near” the relevant place. The decision recognised that the phrase should be interpreted pragmatically rather than strictly.



The Court emphasised that the purpose of the legislation is the promotion of road safety and the effective enforcement of drug-driving laws. A narrow interpretation of the phrase would undermine the practical ability of police to administer roadside tests in circumstances where the exact location of driving cannot safely accommodate testing.



Accordingly, minor displacement from the exact location of driving will not invalidate the test if the connection between the location and the driving event remains clear.



block\_019



Authority — Distance Permitted for “At or Near”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Bennett v Richards \[2011] TASMC 9]

\[TOPIC: permissible distance for roadside drug testing]

\[CONCEPTS: statutory interpretation, roadside drug testing location, proximity requirement, accident investigation, enforcement practicality]



In Bennett v Richards \[2011] TASMC 9 the Magistrates Court further considered the statutory phrase “at or near” within the roadside drug testing regime of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas).



The Court held that a test conducted approximately 1.2 kilometres from the relevant driving location could still satisfy the statutory requirement where the distance was justified by the circumstances of the investigation, including the need to relocate to a safer or more practical location following an accident.



The reasoning reinforced that the phrase must be interpreted in light of the legislative objective of promoting road safety and facilitating effective enforcement. The Court recognised that police may need to move a suspect driver to a different location before administering a test due to safety concerns or investigative requirements.



This authority demonstrates that the statutory language allows a degree of operational flexibility, provided that the connection between the test and the relevant driving remains sufficiently proximate in time and circumstances.



block\_019



Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 8A — Liability Arising from Reasonable Suspicion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 8A]

\[CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 8A]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: liability to testing based on reasonable suspicion]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable suspicion, drug driving investigation, testing liability, police investigative powers, suspicion threshold, roadside testing]



Section 8A of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) provides an additional pathway through which a driver may become liable to drug or alcohol testing. Under s 8A(1), a person found in circumstances that give rise to a reasonable suspicion that they have driven a motor vehicle while under the influence of alcohol or drugs becomes liable to testing.



The threshold of “reasonable suspicion” is lower than proof of intoxication and allows police to act on observable indicators such as behaviour, physical appearance, driving conduct, or other investigative information suggesting impairment.



Once this suspicion arises, the person may be required to submit to a roadside screening test or further evidentiary analysis depending on the circumstances. The section therefore expands police investigative powers beyond purely random testing regimes.



The provision supports proactive enforcement of drug and drink driving laws by allowing police to intervene where objective indicators suggest impairment even if a roadside testing regime has not yet been initiated.



block\_019



Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 10 — Power to Direct Suspect to Attend for Analysis



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 10]

\[CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 10]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: police power to direct attendance for analysis]

\[CONCEPTS: breath analysis direction, evidentiary testing, police authority, compulsory attendance, testing procedure]



Section 10 of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) authorises police to require a person who has become liable to testing under earlier provisions of the Act to proceed to a specified place for evidentiary analysis.



This direction commonly arises following a positive roadside screening test. The police officer may direct the person to attend a police station, mobile testing unit, or other authorised location where a formal evidentiary breath or oral fluid analysis can be conducted by an approved operator.



Once the direction is given, the person must comply with the requirement and cooperate with the testing procedure until the analysis has been completed. Failure to comply with a lawful direction under the Act may constitute a separate offence.



The section ensures that roadside screening tests can be followed by evidentiary testing capable of producing admissible proof in court proceedings.



block\_019



Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 13C — Requirements for Taking Oral Fluid Samples



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 13C]

\[CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 13C]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: procedural requirements for oral fluid sample collection]

\[CONCEPTS: drug testing procedure, evidentiary sample handling, forensic analysis requirements, sample collection rules]



Section 13C of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) prescribes the procedural requirements governing the collection and handling of oral fluid samples used in evidentiary drug analysis.



The section requires that samples be taken in accordance with prescribed requirements contained in the relevant regulations. These requirements ensure the integrity and reliability of the evidentiary sample by establishing consistent procedures for collection, handling, labelling, and analysis.



Following the completion of laboratory analysis, the forensic testing authority must provide Tasmania Police with a report within 21 days after the report has been completed. Police must then provide the defendant with a copy of that report. While the Act requires that the report be provided, the section does not impose a specific time limit on police for serving the report on the defendant.



The purpose of the provision is to preserve evidentiary integrity and ensure transparency in the testing process.



block\_019



Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 29 — Service of Evidentiary Certificates



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 29]

\[CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 29]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: service requirement for evidentiary certificates]

\[CONCEPTS: certificate evidence, service requirement, evidentiary admissibility, analyst certificate, proof of service]



Section 29 of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) governs the service and evidentiary use of certificates relating to alcohol and drug testing procedures.



The section requires that certificates relied upon by the prosecution be served on the defendant at least 14 days prior to the hearing. Proper service of the certificate is a precondition to relying upon it as admissible evidence without requiring the author of the certificate to attend court.



A certificate of service may itself be relied upon as proof that service occurred. The Act adopts the meaning of “service” contained in the Acts Interpretation Act, meaning that service by post may satisfy the requirement.



Where the certificate has been properly served, it may be tendered as evidence of the matters stated within it without calling the person who prepared the certificate. However, the defendant retains the right to require the attendance of the relevant analyst or operator if they wish to challenge the evidence.



Public Nuisance — Definition



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: public nuisance definition]

\[CONCEPTS: public nuisance, class of public affected, interference with public comfort, obstruction of highway, community inconvenience, disturbance of public rights, nuisance offence]



Public nuisance in Tasmanian criminal law refers to conduct that materially interferes with the reasonable comfort or convenience of a class of the public. The concept arises in offences relating to nuisance under the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas).



The defining feature of public nuisance is that the conduct must affect the public or a class of the public, rather than merely an individual. The interference must be sufficiently substantial to impact public rights, such as the use of public spaces or the reasonable enjoyment of communal amenities.



Typical examples include:



Obstruction of public highways.



Activities that materially inconvenience members of the public.



Conduct interfering with public health, safety, or comfort.



The determination of whether conduct amounts to public nuisance involves considering:



The extent of interference with public rights.



The duration of the interference.



Whether the interference is reasonable or unreasonable in the circumstances.



Notably, it is not necessary to prove that specific individuals experienced annoyance or discomfort in order to establish the offence. The prosecution need only show that the conduct was capable of materially affecting the reasonable comfort and convenience of the public.



Authority — Public Nuisance Affecting a Class of the Public



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lusted v Van der Meer \[2012] TASMC 8]

\[TOPIC: definition of public nuisance]

\[CONCEPTS: public nuisance definition, class of public affected, obstruction of highway nuisance, material interference with public rights, nuisance offence standard]



The decision in Lusted v Van der Meer \[2012] TASMC 8 confirms that conduct constitutes a public nuisance only where it materially affects “Her Majesty’s subjects generally or at least a class of Her Majesty’s subjects.”



The case affirms that public nuisance requires interference with public rights or public convenience, rather than private inconvenience.



The Court explained that:



A public nuisance may arise from acts or omissions.



Conduct must materially affect the reasonable comfort and convenience of the life of a class of the public.



Obstruction of a public highway can constitute a nuisance depending on the degree, duration, and reasonableness of the obstruction.



The Court also confirmed that proof of actual annoyance experienced by particular individuals is not required for conviction.



block\_020



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) — Dangerous Article Definition



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 3]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: dangerous article definition]

\[CONCEPTS: dangerous article, weapon definition, modified weapon, knife possession, weapon capable of harm, intended weapon use, police weapons law]



The Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 3 provides an inclusive definition of a “dangerous article.”



A dangerous article includes:



Any weapon other than a firearm.



Any article adapted or modified so that it is capable of being used as a weapon.



Any article intended to be used as a weapon.



A knife.



Because the definition is inclusive rather than exhaustive, an item may be classified as a dangerous article even if it does not strictly fall within one of the listed categories, provided it presents a danger consistent with the statutory concept.



The definition therefore extends to a wide range of objects, particularly where their design, modification, or intended use indicates that they could cause harm or be used as a weapon.



Dangerous Articles — Meaning of Possession



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[CASE: Momcilovic v The Queen \[2011] HCA 34]

\[TOPIC: meaning of possession in criminal law]

\[CONCEPTS: possession definition, custody or control, physical possession, constructive possession, control over object, possession legal meaning]



Possession in criminal law generally refers to physical custody or control of an item.



Where legislation does not define possession, courts adopt the ordinary meaning of the term, encompassing both:



Actual possession (physical holding or custody of the object), and



Constructive possession (control over the object even if not physically holding it).



The High Court in Momcilovic v The Queen \[2011] HCA 34 recognised that possession commonly involves physical custody or control, and this meaning is applied when interpreting statutory offences involving possession unless the statute provides a specialised definition.



Authority — Dual-Purpose Objects and Dangerous Articles



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Craig v Lowrey \[2012] TASMC 41]

\[TOPIC: dangerous article and dual-purpose objects]

\[CONCEPTS: dual purpose weapon, machete weapon classification, dangerous article test, intended weapon use, weapon capable of harm]



The decision in Craig v Lowrey \[2012] TASMC 41 addresses whether objects with legitimate uses can still qualify as dangerous articles.



The Court observed that objects such as a machete may have dual purposes, including legitimate uses such as farming or recreation. However, the relevant inquiry is whether the object:



Is a weapon, or



Is capable of being used as a weapon and therefore qualifies as a dangerous article.



The Court noted dictionary definitions indicating that a weapon is “a thing designed for use for inflicting bodily harm or physical damage.”



Even where an object has legitimate uses, the Court may still classify it as a dangerous article if its characteristics indicate a significant potential for causing harm.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37B — Motor Vehicle Stealing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37B]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: motor vehicle stealing offence]

\[CONCEPTS: joyriding offence, unauthorised vehicle use, driving without consent, vehicle theft summary offence, motor vehicle misuse]



The offence of motor vehicle stealing under Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37B criminalises the unauthorised driving or use of a motor vehicle without the consent of the owner or a person lawfully in charge of the vehicle.



Unlike theft offences under the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas), the offence does not require an intention to permanently deprive the owner of the vehicle.



The offence was historically introduced to address conduct commonly described as “joyriding.”



Elements of the offence



To prove the offence, the prosecution must establish:



The defendant drove or used a motor vehicle.



The vehicle was used without the consent of:



the owner; or



a person lawfully in charge of the vehicle who had authority to give consent.



At the time of driving or using the vehicle, the defendant knew that consent had not been given.



Authority — Elements of Motor Vehicle Stealing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Willox v Warren \[1987] TASSC 74]

\[TOPIC: elements and mental element of motor vehicle stealing]

\[CONCEPTS: knowledge of lack of consent, joyriding offence, motor vehicle use offence, mental element vehicle stealing]



In Willox v Warren \[1987] TASSC 74 the Supreme Court clarified the elements of the offence under Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37B.



The Court held that the essential external elements are:



The defendant drove or used a motor vehicle.



The defendant did so without the consent of the owner or person lawfully in charge of the vehicle.



In addition to those external elements, the prosecution must prove a mental element: that the defendant knew the owner or lawful controller had not given consent.



The Court emphasised that the offence differs fundamentally from theft offences, which require dishonesty and an intention permanently to deprive the owner of property.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 226 — Definition of Stealing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 226]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 226]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: statutory definition of stealing]

\[CONCEPTS: theft definition, dishonest taking, conversion of property, intent to permanently deprive, larceny definition]



Section 226 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) defines the offence of stealing.



A person steals if they dishonestly take or convert property belonging to another without consent and with the intention of permanently depriving the owner of that property.



The section provides that a person steals if they:



Take property belonging to another person; or



Convert property to their own use when they are lawfully in possession of it (for example as a bailee or servant).



In both cases the conduct must be:



Dishonest, and



Accompanied by an intent permanently to deprive the owner.



The definition of “takes” includes obtaining possession:



by trick,



by intimidation,



through mistake by the owner where the taker knows of the mistake, or



by finding property while believing that the owner can be discovered by reasonable means.



The term “owner” includes any person who has possession, control, or a proprietary interest in the property.



Dishonesty in Stealing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: dishonesty element in theft]

\[CONCEPTS: dishonesty test, objective community standards, theft mens rea, Feely test dishonesty, ordinary decent people standard]



Dishonesty is a core mental element of the offence of stealing under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 226.



The modern approach to dishonesty involves a two-stage analysis:



The jury must determine the knowledge, belief, or intention of the accused relevant to the alleged dishonesty.



The jury must decide whether the conduct was dishonest according to the standards of ordinary and decent people.



The High Court accepted this approach in Peters (1998) 192 CLR 493 and reaffirmed it in Macleod (2003). The approach reflects the principles in Feely, without adopting the earlier Ghosh requirement that the accused must recognise that the conduct was dishonest.



There is therefore no requirement that the accused realised their conduct was dishonest, provided that ordinary people would consider it dishonest in light of the accused’s state of knowledge or belief.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37N — Power to Clamp or Confiscate Vehicles



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 37N]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37N]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: vehicle clamping and confiscation powers]

\[CONCEPTS: vehicle confiscation power, hooning enforcement, vehicle clamping authority, prescribed vehicle offences, police seizure of vehicle, post-offence confiscation]



Section 37N of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) provides police with the power to clamp or confiscate a motor vehicle where a prescribed vehicle offence has been committed.



The provision operates as an enforcement mechanism aimed primarily at dangerous driving or hooning-related offences. Where a prescribed offence is established, police may immobilise or seize the vehicle involved in the offending.



A key feature of the power is that it may be exercised immediately or within a limited period following the offending conduct. The statute allows police to clamp or confiscate the vehicle up to 14 days after the commission of the offence.



The duration for which a vehicle may be clamped or confiscated is determined by the statutory scheme and varies depending on:



The nature of the prescribed offence.



Whether the offender has previous relevant offences.



The relevant confiscation periods are set out in subdivisions of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) dealing with vehicle sanctions for prescribed driving offences.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37MA — Mandatory Special Penalty for Clamped or Confiscated Vehicles



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 37MA]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37MA]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: mandatory special penalty for vehicle confiscation offences]

\[CONCEPTS: mandatory vehicle penalty, hooning penalty regime, statutory mandatory fine, vehicle confiscation penalty, sentencing override provision]



Section 37MA of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) establishes a mandatory special penalty that applies where a motor vehicle is clamped or confiscated in connection with prescribed offences.



The special penalty applies in addition to any other penalty imposed for the underlying offence. The legislation specifies that the penalty must be imposed, meaning that courts have no discretion to waive or reduce the penalty.



Importantly, the special penalty operates outside the usual sentencing discretion provided under the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas). The court is therefore required to impose the statutory penalty even if other sentencing options would normally be available.



The amount of the special penalty is not fixed in the Act itself but is prescribed by regulation.



Police Offences Regulations 2014 (Tas) reg 7 — Amount of Special Penalty



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Regulations 2014 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Regulations 2014 (Tas) reg 7]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: special penalty amount for vehicle confiscation offences]

\[CONCEPTS: penalty units hooning offences, vehicle confiscation fine amount, regulatory penalty amount, hooning penalty calculation, fixed vehicle offence fine]



Regulation 7 of the Police Offences Regulations 2014 (Tas) prescribes the amount of the mandatory special penalty imposed under Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37MA for vehicle clamping or confiscation offences.



The applicable amount depends on the date of offending, because regulatory amendments have altered the prescribed penalty.



The regulatory framework provides:



Offending prior to 12 December 2024 — the special penalty amount is $600.



Offending on or after 12 December 2024 — the special penalty is four penalty units.



The change reflects amendments introduced through updated regulations governing vehicle-related offences under the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas).



When determining the appropriate penalty, courts must apply the regulatory amount in force at the time the offence occurred.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37Y — Motor Vehicle Forfeiture Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 37Y]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37Y]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: motor vehicle forfeiture applications]

\[CONCEPTS: vehicle forfeiture application, repeat hooning offences, court forfeiture order, third vehicle offence forfeiture, confiscation proceedings]



Section 37Y of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) provides a mechanism for court-ordered forfeiture of a motor vehicle involved in repeated prescribed offences.



An application for forfeiture may be made where an offender has committed a third prescribed offence involving the vehicle. In such circumstances, the prosecution may apply to the court for an order that the vehicle be forfeited.



The forfeiture application is determined by the court after considering the relevant circumstances, including:



The offender’s prior history of prescribed vehicle offences.



The circumstances of the current offence.



The ownership status of the vehicle.



The purpose of the forfeiture regime is to deter repeated vehicle-related offending and to remove vehicles used persistently in unlawful driving conduct.



Authority — Considerations for Vehicle Forfeiture



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Perkins v Douglas \[2010] TASMC 8]

\[TOPIC: considerations in vehicle forfeiture applications]

\[CONCEPTS: vehicle forfeiture discretion, repeat vehicle offending, confiscation proceedings, court factors vehicle forfeiture, hooning vehicle confiscation]



The decision in Perkins v Douglas \[2010] TASMC 8 identifies factors relevant to determining whether a motor vehicle should be forfeited following repeated prescribed offences.



When preparing a forfeiture application, the prosecution may rely on evidence demonstrating the offender’s prior offending history and the circumstances surrounding the confiscation of the vehicle.



Relevant considerations include:



Evidence of previous convictions for prescribed vehicle offences.



Whether the vehicle has previously been involved in unlawful driving conduct.



The offender’s driving history.



In proceedings involving forfeiture, it is common to obtain certificates of conviction for relevant offences and records such as driving histories in order to establish the offender’s pattern of conduct.



The Court may also consider the circumstances of the offence that resulted in the confiscation, although the primary focus remains the offender’s pattern of vehicle-related offending.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37J — Excessive Noise, Smoke or Emissions from Vehicles



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 37J]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37J]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: excessive noise or smoke from motor vehicles]

\[CONCEPTS: excessive vehicle noise offence, hooning behaviour, smoke emissions vehicle offence, vehicle nuisance offence, engine revving offence]



Section 37J of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) creates an offence relating to excessive noise, smoke, or emissions produced by motor vehicles.



The offence targets conduct commonly associated with hooning or reckless vehicle use, including:



Deliberate generation of excessive engine noise.



Causing excessive smoke or tyre emissions.



Operating a vehicle in a manner that produces unreasonable disturbance.



The offence focuses on conduct that produces unreasonable or excessive disturbance, particularly where such disturbance affects public amenity or safety.



Authority — Excessive Vehicle Noise Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lane v Kelly \[2010] TASMC 20]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of excessive vehicle noise offences]

\[CONCEPTS: hooning case authority, excessive vehicle noise interpretation, vehicle disturbance offence, vehicle smoke offence interpretation]



The case of Lane v Kelly \[2010] TASMC 20 provides judicial consideration of offences involving excessive noise or smoke from vehicles under the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas).



The decision confirms that courts assess the offence by considering whether the conduct produced noise, smoke, or emissions that were excessive in the circumstances.



Relevant factors may include:



The nature of the vehicle operation.



The degree of disturbance created.



The surrounding circumstances in which the vehicle was used.



The case demonstrates that offences involving vehicle noise or smoke are evaluated by reference to the practical impact of the conduct, rather than merely the mechanical features of the vehicle.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37GA — Driver Demand Notices



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: 37GA]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37GA]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: police driver demand notice powers]

\[CONCEPTS: driver demand notice, identify driver requirement, vehicle offence investigation, statutory duty to provide driver details, police notice demand]



Section 37GA of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) authorises police officers to serve a notice of demand requiring the owner or registered operator of a motor vehicle to provide information identifying the driver or occupants of the vehicle.



A notice of demand may be served where police have a basis to believe that the occupant or driver of a vehicle committed an offence.



The notice may be served on:



The owner of the vehicle; or



The registered operator of the vehicle.



The statutory definition of “owner” in the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) includes a person whom a police officer reasonably believes was the driver of the vehicle, meaning that strict proof of ownership is not required at the time of issuing the notice.



The purpose of the provision is to assist police in identifying drivers involved in vehicle-related offences when the offender’s identity is not immediately known.



Authority — Driver Demand Notices



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Baker v Smith \[2025] TASSC 43]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of driver demand notice provisions]

\[CONCEPTS: driver demand case authority, allegation threshold notice, self-incrimination statutory abrogation, driver identification requirement]



The Supreme Court decision in Baker v Smith \[2025] TASSC 43 clarifies the interpretation of the driver demand provisions under the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas).



The Court held that:



The statutory precondition for issuing a notice under the relevant provisions requires only an allegation that an offence occurred, rather than proof that the offence was committed.



The offences created by s 37G and s 37GA are distinct, with different elements and penalties.



Proof that a person is the registered owner of the vehicle may be established through admissions or conduct, and does not necessarily require documentary proof such as a registration certificate.



The provisions expressly abrogate the privilege against self-incrimination, meaning that requiring the owner to identify the driver does not unlawfully infringe the right to silence.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 39 — Possession of Property Reasonably Believed to Be Stolen



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 39]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 39]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: possession of property reasonably believed stolen]

\[CONCEPTS: stolen property possession, police reasonable belief, unlawfully obtained property, police suspicion property offence, evidentiary threshold belief, stolen goods offence]



Section 39(1) of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) creates an offence where a person has possession or control of property that is reasonably believed by a police officer to have been stolen or unlawfully obtained.



The offence operates on the basis of a police officer’s reasonable belief rather than proof that the property was in fact stolen. The statutory requirement acts as a safeguard against arbitrary enforcement by requiring a belief grounded in objectively assessable facts.



Judicial discussion indicates that the requirement of reasonable belief serves to prevent random or capricious exercises of police power, ensuring that officers do not charge individuals merely on speculative suspicion.



The belief must arise from identifiable circumstances capable of inducing the relevant belief in a reasonable person. The officer must therefore possess factual information supporting the conclusion that the property was likely stolen or unlawfully obtained.



This offence differs from theft offences under the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) in that it focuses on the possession of suspicious property, rather than requiring proof that the accused themselves stole the item.



Authority — Meaning of “Reasonably Believed”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lane v Jones \[2012] TASMC 11]

\[TOPIC: definition of reasonable belief]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable belief test, acceptance of proposition, exclusion of alternatives, police belief standard, subjective belief threshold]



In Lane v Jones \[2012] TASMC 11, the Tasmanian Magistrates Court clarified the meaning of the phrase “reasonably believed” within the context of statutory powers and offences.



The Court held that a belief must amount to acceptance of a proposition to the exclusion of other propositions. This means the officer must actually accept that the relevant state of affairs exists, rather than merely suspecting it.



The test contains both subjective and objective elements:



The officer must genuinely hold the belief (subjective element).



There must be reasonable grounds for holding that belief (objective element).



The decision emphasises that the mental state required is more than mere suspicion. Suspicion involves doubt or uncertainty, whereas belief involves a state of mind inclined toward acceptance of a particular conclusion.



This interpretation ensures that police powers dependent on reasonable belief are exercised only where factual circumstances justify a rational inference supporting the belief.



block\_021



Authority — Reasonable Grounds for Belief



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: George v Rockett \[1990] HCA 26]

\[TOPIC: reasonable grounds for suspicion or belief]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds test, objective basis for belief, police powers standard, factual foundation suspicion, statutory belief threshold]



In George v Rockett \[1990] HCA 26, the High Court explained the legal meaning of statutory requirements that an officer hold a belief or suspicion on “reasonable grounds.”



The Court held that where legislation requires reasonable grounds for a state of mind such as belief or suspicion, there must exist facts sufficient to induce that state of mind in a reasonable person.



The objective circumstances do not need to prove the suspected fact on the balance of probabilities. Rather, they must provide a rational basis capable of supporting the relevant state of mind.



The Court described belief as:



an inclination of the mind towards accepting a proposition,



based on evidence that may fall short of proof.



Thus, reasonable belief may arise from evidence that leaves some room for conjecture, provided that the facts reasonably support the inference drawn.



This principle is widely applied in assessing statutory powers exercised by police officers.



Trespass and Police Entry onto Private Property



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: police entry and trespass]

\[CONCEPTS: trespass police entry, implied licence knock door, police powers property entry, consent to enter property, revocation of licence, police trespass]



At common law, any person who enters private premises without the consent of the owner or occupier is a trespasser, even if the purpose of entry is lawful.



This principle applies equally to police officers. Police do not possess a general right to enter private property unless authorised by statute, warrant, or valid consent.



However, the law recognises an implied licence permitting members of the public to approach a residential dwelling for legitimate purposes such as walking to the front door and knocking.



This implied licence allows a person to:



Approach the front entrance using the ordinary access path.



Knock or attempt to communicate with occupants.



Leave if permission to remain is not granted.



Importantly, the licence may be revoked at any time by the occupier. Once revoked, remaining on the premises constitutes trespass.



Police officers relying on this implied licence must comply with its limits. If the licence is revoked or if their conduct exceeds its scope, their continued presence may become unlawful.



Authority — Implied Licence to Approach a Dwelling



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Roy v O’Neill \[2020] HCA 45]

\[TOPIC: implied licence for police to approach residence]

\[CONCEPTS: implied licence doctrine, knock and talk police, trespass police entry, revocation of licence, residential entry powers]



In Roy v O’Neill \[2020] HCA 45, the High Court confirmed that the law implies a limited licence allowing individuals, including police officers, to approach a residential dwelling and knock on the door.



The licence is confined to conduct consistent with ordinary social expectations. It permits entry only for the purpose of seeking communication with the occupant.



Critically, the licence may be revoked by the occupier, either expressly or implicitly. Once revoked, the person must leave the property promptly.



The decision reinforces that police officers have no special common law privilege to enter private property, and must rely on the same implied licence available to members of the public unless statutory authority exists.



Authority — No Implied Licence to Enter Accommodation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Hodges \[2022] TASSC 45]

\[TOPIC: police entry into hotel or apartment premises]

\[CONCEPTS: hotel room privacy, police consent entry, property licence expiry, police authority to enter room, accommodation privacy rights]



In Tasmania v Hodges \[2022] TASSC 45, the Supreme Court of Tasmania considered whether police could enter a hotel-style apartment after the occupant’s licence to occupy had expired.



The Court held that even if the accused no longer had a contractual right to occupy the room, that fact did not automatically authorise police entry.



Police authority to enter depended on consent from the property owner or authorised management, unless another lawful basis for entry existed.



The decision emphasises that the termination of a person’s contractual licence to occupy premises does not create an implied licence allowing police to enter the premises without consent.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 273 — Unlawful Injury to Property



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 273]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 273]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: unlawful destruction or injury to property]

\[CONCEPTS: property damage offence, injuring property crime, destroying property offence, unlawful damage, criminal damage Tasmania]



Section 273 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) creates the indictable offence of unlawfully destroying or injuring property.



The offence is expressed broadly. Any person who unlawfully destroys or injures any property commits a crime.



The charge is typically described as “unlawfully injuring property.”



The offence must be read alongside the general principles in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 267, which define when injury to property becomes criminal.



These provisions collectively establish that:



Property damage must be unlawful.



The act must be done wilfully.



The conduct must occur without claim of right.



If these conditions are satisfied, criminal liability may arise even where the accused has some ownership interest in the property.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 267 — General Principles Governing Injury to Property



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 267]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 267]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: principles governing property damage offences]

\[CONCEPTS: injury to property principles, intent to defraud property damage, wilful property damage requirement, claim of right defence property, ownership not justification]



Section 267 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) establishes foundational rules governing property damage offences.



Key principles include:



Intent to defraud — Injury to property caused with intent to defraud is unlawful even if committed by the owner of the property.



Partial ownership irrelevant — A person is not justified in damaging property merely because they have a partial interest in it.



Mental element requirement — Property damage does not constitute a crime unless it is done wilfully and without claim of right.



These principles ensure that criminal liability may arise even where the accused has some proprietary interest in the property.



Authority — Meaning of “Wilfully”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Hodgson \[1985] Tas R 75]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of wilfully in property offences]

\[CONCEPTS: wilful conduct definition, intent requirement property damage, subjective recklessness property offence, mental element wilfully]



In Hodgson \[1985] Tas R 75, Cox J considered the meaning of the word “wilfully” in the context of property damage offences.



The Court noted that the term is one of uncertain or variable meaning, but in criminal law it generally refers to:



Specific intent to cause the relevant result, or



Subjective recklessness, where the accused foresees the likelihood of the prohibited result occurring.



Thus, a person may act wilfully either by intending to cause damage or by consciously disregarding the risk that damage will occur.



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37 — Destroying or Injuring Property



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 37]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: summary offence of damaging property]

\[CONCEPTS: summary property damage offence, destroy or injure property summary charge, police offences property damage, unlawful damage summary offence]



Section 37 of the Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) creates a summary offence where a person unlawfully destroys or injures property.



Key provisions include:



Destroying or injuring property unlawfully.



Unlawfully killing, maiming, or wounding another person’s animal.



Removing or interfering with boundary marks, survey marks, signposts, or notices set by a public authority.



The offence carries a maximum penalty of 10 penalty units or imprisonment for up to 12 months.



Although contained in a summary offences statute, the offence is considered a “like offence” to the criminal damage offence in the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas). As a result, the Code principles governing property damage, including the rule that partial ownership does not justify damage, apply to this offence.



Authority — Joint Ownership Not a Defence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Howell v Dakin (1964) TSR 142]

\[TOPIC: joint ownership and property damage liability]

\[CONCEPTS: jointly owned property damage, criminal damage joint ownership, co-owner property destruction, property offence ownership defence]



In Howell v Dakin (1964) TSR 142, the Court held that joint ownership of property does not provide a defence to a charge of unlawfully destroying or injuring property.



The statutory prohibition refers broadly to the destruction or injury of “any property.” The Court emphasised that this language encompasses property jointly owned by the accused and another person.



Consequently, a person may commit the offence even where they possess a proprietary interest in the property damaged.



Assault — Forms of the Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: forms of assault]

\[CONCEPTS: assault definition Tasmania, applying force assault, attempted force assault, threat assault gesture, deprivation of liberty assault]



An assault in Tasmanian criminal law may occur in several distinct ways.



An assault may consist of:



Intentionally applying force to another person, either directly or indirectly.



Attempting to apply force to another person.



Threatening by gesture to apply force where the accused has the present ability to carry out the threat or causes the victim reasonably to believe that such ability exists.



Depriving another person of liberty, such as through unlawful detention.



These categories reflect both the traditional common law concept of battery and broader statutory definitions under the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas).



Assault — Defences Negating Unlawfulness



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[TOPIC: defences to assault]

\[CONCEPTS: self defence assault, lawful arrest force, parental correction defence, justification assault, excuse assault]



Several statutory defences may negate the unlawfulness of conduct that would otherwise constitute assault.



Examples include:



Self-defence — Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 46.



Parental correction — Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 50, permitting reasonable force for discipline of a child.



Use of reasonable force in executing a lawful arrest — Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 26.



Where these defences apply, the application of force may be legally justified and therefore not constitute criminal assault.



Authority — Victim Refuses to Give Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Irons v Moore \[2019] TASSC 22]

\[TOPIC: evidentiary implications of non-cooperating complainant]

\[CONCEPTS: hostile witness assault case, victim refuses evidence, recent complaint evidence, circumstantial assault proof]



In Irons v Moore \[2019] TASSC 22, the Court considered a situation where the complainant declined to give meaningful evidence and claimed an inability to remember events.



Despite the absence of direct testimony from the victim, the prosecution relied on:



physical injuries observed by witnesses, and



recent complaint evidence given by the complainant to others.



The case illustrates that an assault charge may proceed based on circumstantial and corroborative evidence, even where the victim refuses to cooperate.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — When Consent Does Not Negate Assault



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 182(4)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Ineffective Consent: General Doctrine]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: ineffective consent in assault]

\[CONCEPTS: consent ineffective assault, unlawful assault despite consent, breach of peace assault, injurious to public assault, bodily harm consent]



Section 182(4) of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) provides that an assault may remain unlawful even where the victim consents.



Consent will not excuse liability where all four statutory conditions are satisfied:



The act is otherwise unlawful.



The act is injurious to the public.



The act is injurious to the person assaulted.



The act involves a breach of the peace.



The prosecution bears the burden of proving each of these conditions beyond reasonable doubt.



Authority — Breach of the Peace Definition



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Howell v R (1981) 73 Crim App R 31]

\[TOPIC: definition of breach of the peace]

\[CONCEPTS: breach of peace definition, violence public order, fear of violence legal test, assault public disturbance]



In Howell v R (1981) 73 Crim App R 31, the Court defined a breach of the peace as occurring whenever:



harm is actually done, or



harm is likely to be done,



to a person or to property in their presence, or



a person is placed in fear of such harm through violence or disturbance.



The concept includes violence arising from assault, affray, riot, unlawful assembly, or similar disturbances.



This definition has been widely adopted in Australian common law.



Authority — Consent and Bodily Harm



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Holmes \[1993] TASSC 5]

\[TOPIC: bodily harm and consent in assault]

\[CONCEPTS: bodily harm definition assault, consent limits assault, physical injury threshold, violence consent rule]



In R v Holmes \[1993] TASSC 5, the Supreme Court held that where the prosecution proves that the accused inflicted violence likely to cause bodily harm, consent becomes irrelevant.



The Court explained that bodily harm means any hurt or injury that interferes with health or comfort. The injury:



need not be permanent, but



must be more than merely transient or trifling.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(3) — Ordinary Social Contact



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 182(3)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(3)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: ordinary incidents of social intercourse]

\[CONCEPTS: social contact defence assault, everyday contact exclusion assault, non hostile touching, social interaction law]



Section 182(3) of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) excludes from assault ordinary incidents of social intercourse.



Examples include:



patting someone on the shoulder to attract attention,



gently pushing past others in a crowded environment.



Such acts are not considered assault if performed inoffensively and without hostility.



Authority — Hostility Converting Social Contact into Assault



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Turner v Horan \[2021] TASSC 16]

\[TOPIC: hostility converting innocuous conduct into assault]

\[CONCEPTS: hostile intent assault, social contact assault, intent to apply force, hostile touching]



In Turner v Horan \[2021] TASSC 16, the Supreme Court explained that hostility or hostile intent may convert conduct that would otherwise fall within ordinary social interaction into assault.



Even where force is minor, if applied in circumstances demonstrating hostility, the conduct may constitute an assault.



Types of Assault Offences in Tasmania



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: categories of assault offences]

\[CONCEPTS: assault offence categories, aggravated assault, indecent assault, assault police, assault aggravation]



Tasmanian law recognises several categories of assault offences, including:



Assault — Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 35(1).



Assault under aggregating circumstances — Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 35(2).



Assault in circumstances of aggravation — Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 35(1B).



Aggravated assault — Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 183.



Indecent assault — Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 35(3) and Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 127.



Assaulting police officers — specific statutory offences.



Authority — Objective and Subjective Components of Reasonable Belief



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v Lea \[2008] TASMC 1]

\[TOPIC: reasonable belief under Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 39]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable belief, objective grounds, subjective belief, possession of stolen property, police charging threshold, more than suspicion, factual foundation for belief]



In Police v Lea \[2008] TASMC 1, the Tasmanian Magistrates Court explained that the requirement in Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 39 that property be “reasonably believed” by a police officer to have been stolen or unlawfully obtained contains both a subjective and an objective component. The officer must in fact hold the relevant belief, and there must also be facts sufficient to induce that belief in a reasonable person.



The decision adopts the approach stated in George v Rockett \[1990] HCA 26, namely that where legislation requires reasonable grounds for a state of mind such as belief or suspicion, there must be objective facts capable of inducing that state of mind in a reasonable person. Belief does not require proof on the balance of probabilities, but it does require more than a mere possibility or unparticularised concern.



The case also distinguishes belief from suspicion. Belief is an inclination of the mind toward accepting a proposition, whereas suspicion retains a stronger element of doubt and mistrust. For a charge under Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 39, the requisite state of mind is belief, not mere suspicion. The officer must therefore possess enough factual information to rationally accept, rather than merely wonder, that the property was stolen or unlawfully obtained.



Authority — Suspicion Arising from Abnormality or Incongruity



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Winwood v Burk \[2002] TASSC 54]

\[TOPIC: circumstances capable of attracting suspicion in relation to property]

\[CONCEPTS: suspicion, suspicious property, abnormality of possession, incongruity, surrounding circumstances, defendant behaviour, stolen goods indicators]



In Winwood v Burk \[2002] TASSC 54, the Supreme Court of Tasmania discussed the types of circumstances that may reasonably attract suspicion to property. Drawing on earlier authority, the Court explained that suspicion may arise from the nature of the property, the circumstances in which it is found, and the conduct of the defendant in relation to it.



The decision recognises that it is impossible to exhaustively define every circumstance capable of attracting suspicion. However, one recurring indicator is the impression of abnormality or incongruity in the property being in the possession of the person who has it, or in the way that person is dealing with it. Suspicion is therefore not limited to direct evidence of theft. It may arise from the combination of unusual possession, questionable surrounding circumstances, and behaviour inconsistent with innocent ownership.



This authority is important where legislation depends on a police state of mind grounded in surrounding facts. It illustrates that suspicion may be reasonably induced by contextual features even when direct proof of theft is unavailable. At the same time, the case does not collapse suspicion into belief. Suspicion may be reasonably held on facts that do not yet justify the stronger acceptance required for belief.



Authority — Distinction Between Suspicion and Belief



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Burnett v Brown (1929) 24 Tas LR 23]

\[TOPIC: meaning of suspicion and suspect]

\[CONCEPTS: suspicion, doubt and mistrust, inclination to believe, degrees of suspicion, weaker than belief, police suspicion threshold]



In Burnett v Brown (1929) 24 Tas LR 23, Clark J described the words “suspicion” and “suspect” as conveying the idea of doubt and mistrust as much as belief. His Honour said that suspicion involves a doubting and mistrusting inclination to believe, and that the strength of that inclination may vary according to the cogency of the evidence, but the element of doubt always remains.



This explanation is doctrinally significant because it marks a clear difference between suspicion and belief. Suspicion permits uncertainty and unresolved doubt. Belief, by contrast, requires a greater degree of mental assent to the relevant proposition. Where legislation requires a police officer to hold a belief, it is not enough that the officer merely suspects the relevant fact.



The case is therefore useful in interpreting statutory formulas that differentiate between powers or offences based on suspicion and those based on belief. It confirms that suspicion is a real but weaker state of mind, and that the law may demand something more where liberty or criminal liability turns on the officer’s conclusion.



Authority — Refusal to Consent and Bare Allegations Are Not Enough



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Rolls v Richardson \[2014] TASMC 12]

\[TOPIC: limits on reasonable belief for police arrest and property-related suspicion]

\[CONCEPTS: refusal to consent search, bare allegation, radio room information, independent police assessment, innocence consistent conduct, objectively reasonable belief]



In Rolls v Richardson \[2014] TASMC 12, the Court held that conduct such as refusing to consent to a search or walking away from police does not, without more, provide reasonable grounds for the requisite belief needed to justify police action. The exercise of legal rights is as consistent with innocence as with guilt and cannot alone support the formation of the necessary belief.



The decision also addresses police reliance on information supplied by others. A police officer cannot lawfully form the required belief by uncritically accepting a bare statement or conclusion from a radio operator or another officer where no underlying circumstances are disclosed. The officer must personally assess facts capable of supporting the belief. A bald allegation, unsupported by explanatory detail, does not provide an objectively reasonable foundation.



The case does not require exhaustive police investigation before acting. Belief on reasonable grounds is less than proof, and urgent circumstances may justify prompt action. However, there must still be enough information to allow a reasonable person in the officer’s position to exclude obvious innocent explanations and incline toward acceptance of the alleged offending. Without that factual basis, the belief is not objectively reasonable.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Otherwise Unlawful



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 182(4)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Meaning of Otherwise Unlawful]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: meaning of otherwise unlawful in consensual assault]

\[CONCEPTS: otherwise unlawful, consensual assault, additional unlawfulness, separate offence, assault and consent, Tasmania unique condition]



The phrase “otherwise unlawful” in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) means that, before consent is rendered ineffective, the consensual application of force must also be unlawful on some basis beyond the mere existence of consented-to force. The source material suggests that this condition is generally understood to require that the conduct amount to an assault plus some other offence or independent source of unlawfulness.



Examples given in the source include conduct that may also constitute offences such as affray, duelling, or being armed in public. On this view, the condition supplies an additional element of wrongfulness that would otherwise be absent where the putative victim has freely consented.



This interpretation is important because s 182(4) does not abolish consent across all consensual violence. Rather, it sets out four cumulative conditions that must all be proved by the Crown. The “otherwise unlawful” condition therefore narrows the class of consensual conduct that remains criminal. It indicates that not every harmful consensual act is automatically unlawful under Tasmanian law merely because force was applied. The Crown must identify a separate basis on which the act was already unlawful before consent can be displaced under the section.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Injurious to the Public



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 182(4)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Meaning of Injurious to the Public]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: meaning of injurious to the public in consensual assault]

\[CONCEPTS: injurious to the public, public interest, consensual violence, fist fights, prize fights, sport exception, sadomasochistic conduct]



The phrase “injurious to the public” in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) refers to conduct that is not in the public interest. The source material links this idea to common law authorities treating certain forms of consensual violence as harmful to the public because they lack sufficient social utility or occur in circumstances contrary to public order and welfare.



The distinction is commonly drawn between activities that the law accepts as serving some recognised social good and those that do not. Sporting contests, lawful correction of children, reasonable surgical procedures, dangerous exhibitions, and some forms of well-intentioned horseplay have historically been treated differently from prize fights, street fights, or consensual violence inflicted for no good reason. Activities falling outside established exceptions may be regarded as injurious to the public.



The concept is policy-laden and evaluative. It does not depend solely on whether the complainant agreed. Instead, it asks whether the conduct belongs to a category that the law is prepared to tolerate as socially legitimate. Under s 182(4), the Crown must prove this condition in addition to the other three statutory requirements before consent becomes ineffective.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Injurious to the Person Assaulted



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 182(4)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Harm Threshold: Injurious to Person]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: harm threshold for injurious to the person assaulted]

\[CONCEPTS: injury threshold, bodily harm, health or comfort, more than trifling, harm to complainant, assault injury requirement]



The condition that the assault be “injurious to the person assaulted” in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) requires proof that the complainant suffered actual harm. The source material indicates that the threshold is not especially high, but it does require more than conduct that is merely trivial, fleeting, or harmless in any practical sense.



The discussion links the concept to the ordinary understanding of bodily harm, namely hurt or injury calculated to interfere with health or comfort. The harm need not be permanent. However, it must be something real and adverse to the complainant, rather than a purely technical or abstract interference. This aligns with the statement in R v Holmes \[1993] TASSC 5 that bodily harm includes any hurt or injury interfering with health or comfort, provided it is more than merely transient or trifling.



Accordingly, in a consensual assault case, the Crown cannot rely on s 182(4) unless it proves that some actual injury or harm was inflicted on the complainant. The requirement is separate from the public interest inquiry and from breach of the peace. Each condition must be independently established.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Breach of the Peace



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 182(4)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Breach of Peace Negating Consent]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: breach of the peace as a condition negating consent]

\[CONCEPTS: breach of the peace, violence requirement, fear of harm, assault disturbance, public violence, consent no defence]



Under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4), consent will not excuse an assault unless the Crown fails to prove that the act involved a breach of the peace. The source material explains that this phrase bears its common law meaning and is centrally concerned with actual or threatened violence.



A breach of the peace occurs where harm is done or is likely to be done to a person, or in that person’s presence to their property, or where a person is placed in fear of such harm through an assault, affray, riot, unlawful assembly, or similar disturbance. The concept is wider than public fist fights in the street, but violence or the likelihood of violence remains its essence.



The source material notes that this threshold is often relatively easy for the prosecution to satisfy in assault matters, especially where bodily harm was caused or likely to be caused. Where consensual violence crosses into harmful, disorderly, or violent conduct, the breach of the peace requirement is likely to be met. It is nevertheless a separate statutory element, and the Crown must still prove it as one of the four cumulative conditions in s 182(4).



Authority — Crown Must Prove All Four Conditions in s 182(4)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Woolmington v DPP \[1935] AC 462]

\[TOPIC: onus of proof for conditions negating consent]

\[CONCEPTS: burden of proof, Crown onus, all elements required, beyond reasonable doubt, consensual assault, statutory conditions]



The source material relies on Woolmington v DPP \[1935] AC 462 for the proposition that the Crown bears the onus of proof in relation to all four conditions in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4). It is not enough for the prosecution to prove one or more of the listed matters. It must prove all four beyond reasonable doubt if consent is to be rendered ineffective.



This is significant because s 182(4) is cumulative in operation. The prosecution must establish that the act was otherwise unlawful, injurious to the public, injurious to the person assaulted, and involved a breach of the peace. Failure to prove any one of those conditions means the statutory route to displacing consent is not made out.



The authority is therefore not about defining assault itself, but about the allocation of the burden and standard of proof where the prosecution seeks to negate consent by invoking a statutory exception. It reinforces the ordinary criminal law principle that the prosecution must prove every matter necessary to establish criminal liability.



Authority — Violence Is Essential to Breach of the Peace



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Nilsson v McDonald]

\[TOPIC: violence requirement within breach of the peace]

\[CONCEPTS: breach of the peace, violence or likelihood of violence, common law meaning, assault disorder, fear of violence]

\[REVIEW]



In Nilsson v McDonald, as summarised in the source material, Blow J stated that the phrase “breach of the peace” in the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) was intended to refer to the common law concept of a breach of the peace. His Honour said that there cannot be a breach of the peace unless there is violence or a likelihood of violence.



The doctrinal significance of the case is that it anchors the statutory phrase to its established common law meaning rather than treating it as an undefined or purely local expression. That narrows the inquiry to conduct involving actual harm, threatened harm, or a real prospect of violence.



This authority supports the interpretation that consensual conduct will not satisfy the breach of the peace condition in s 182(4) unless the surrounding circumstances involve violence or the realistic prospect of it. The case citation is incomplete in the source material, so this chunk is marked \[REVIEW] for citation confirmation before append to master.



Authority — Necessity as a Defence in Assault Matters



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Bonde v Ackroyd \[2011] TASMC 7]

\[TOPIC: necessity defence in assault proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: necessity defence, assault defence, justification by emergency, excuse for force, criminal responsibility exception]



Bonde v Ackroyd \[2011] TASMC 7 is identified in the source material as authority relevant to the defence of necessity in assault matters. On the material provided, the doctrinal point is that necessity may operate as a defence capable of precluding criminal responsibility where the use of force occurred under circumstances of pressing necessity.



Because the source extract provides only the citation and doctrinal label, but no further reasoning or articulated test, the case can only safely be recorded as authority that the defence is relevant in this field. It should not be expanded beyond that without the full text or a fuller source extract.



Accordingly, this authority should be treated as supporting the proposition that necessity may arise as a defence to assault in appropriate circumstances, but the precise elements, threshold, and scope of the defence require verification from the full judgment or a fuller source passage before broader doctrinal use.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 51 and 127 — Express Statutory Limits on Consent



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 51, 127]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 51, 127]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: statutory situations where consent is expressly immaterial]

\[CONCEPTS: consent no defence, consent to death invalid, likely to cause death, child consent indecent assault, express statutory exclusion]



The source material identifies two situations in which consent is expressly made immaterial by the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas). First, under s 51, no consent may be given to the infliction of death on oneself or to the infliction of an injury likely to cause death. Second, under s 127, the consent of a victim under the age of 17 years is no defence to a charge of indecent assault.



These provisions differ from s 182(4). Section 182(4) sets out a conditional pathway by which consent may be displaced if the Crown proves four cumulative matters. Sections 51 and 127 instead identify contexts in which consent is legislatively excluded altogether.



The doctrinal importance of these sections is that they demonstrate that Tasmanian criminal law does not treat consent as a universal answer to offences involving force, injury, or sexual touching. In some areas, Parliament has expressly removed consent from consideration because of the gravity of the harm or the need to protect vulnerable persons.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 54–55 — Married Persons and Property Offences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 54–55]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 54–55]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: defence]

\[TOPIC: marital liability and exceptions in property offending]

\[CONCEPTS: spouse property offences, cohabitation immunity, deserting spouse, intent to defraud, married person criminal responsibility, property of spouse]



Sections 54–55 of the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) regulate criminal responsibility of married persons for offences committed in relation to the other spouse’s property. Section 54(1) provides that, except as otherwise stated, a married person is not to be convicted of an offence committed during cohabitation with respect to the property of the other spouse.



However, s 54(2) creates important exceptions. Criminal responsibility remains where the offence is committed while leaving, deserting, or about to leave or desert the other spouse, and also where the offence is committed with intent to defraud a person other than the spouse.
