Section 55 then provides the general rule that, except as expressly otherwise stated, a married person bears the same criminal responsibility for acts and omissions as if unmarried. The combined effect is that any spousal immunity is limited and specific. It does not amount to a general exemption from criminal liability between spouses, and it must be read carefully alongside offences involving jointly owned or spousal property.



Indecent Assault — Meaning of “Indecent”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: meaning of indecent conduct in sexual assault offences]

\[CONCEPTS: indecent conduct, sexual connotation, community standards of decency, affront to modesty, offensive behaviour, sexual touching, moral propriety, context of conduct]



The concept of “indecency” in assault offences refers to conduct that carries a sexual connotation and is regarded as offensive to community standards of propriety. The term is not defined within the Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) and therefore takes its ordinary meaning in the context of the alleged conduct.



An act may be regarded as indecent where:



The act carries a sexual connotation, either because of:



the part of the body targeted, or



the body part used by the offender in the act.



The behaviour is considered by ordinary standards to be:



unbecoming or offensive to common propriety



an affront to modesty



contrary to community standards of decency



offensive to the ordinary modesty of the average person.



The determination of indecency is context-dependent. Factors such as the circumstances, the relationship between the parties, and the surrounding conduct may influence whether an act is regarded as indecent.



Authority — Meaning of indecent conduct



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v FRS \[2020] TASMC 9]

\[TOPIC: definition of indecent conduct in assault offences]

\[CONCEPTS: sexual connotation, modesty, community standards, indecent assault definition, offensive conduct, context evaluation]



Police v FRS \[2020] TASMC 9 confirmed that indecent conduct must possess a sexual connotation and offend community standards of modesty. The court explained that an act may be indecent where it is unbecoming or offensive to common propriety or contrary to the standards of right-minded persons. Context plays an important role in determining whether conduct carries the necessary sexual character.



Indecent Assault — Elements of the Offence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 182 and 127]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 182, 127]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: element of offence]

\[TOPIC: elements of indecent assault]

\[CONCEPTS: assault elements, application of force, lack of consent, intentional act, indecent circumstances, criminal assault]



The offence of indecent assault requires proof of both the elements of assault and the additional requirement that the assault occurred in indecent circumstances.



To prove the offence, the prosecution must establish beyond reasonable doubt that:



The defendant



Applied force to the complainant



The application of force was voluntary and intentional



The act occurred without the consent of the complainant



The act was unlawful



The conduct constituted an assault



The assault occurred in indecent circumstances, judged according to the standards of ordinary and decent members of the community.



The indecency element introduces a moral and social evaluation, determined by reference to community standards rather than a statutory definition.



Authority — Indecent intent requirement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Collins v Coleman \[2013] TASMC 19]

\[TOPIC: proof of indecent intent in assault offences]

\[CONCEPTS: indecent intent, sexual gratification, intent element, sexual purpose, criminal intention]



Collins v Coleman \[2013] TASMC 19 explains that where an offence requires “indecent intent”, the prosecution must prove that the physical acts constituting the assault were performed with the specific intention to arouse or gratify sexual desire.



Importantly, the court clarified that the prosecution does not need to prove that the acts themselves are inherently indecent, provided the accompanying intention was sexual.



Assault — Definition



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 182]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: statutory definition of assault]

\[CONCEPTS: threatening gesture, attempt to apply force, present ability, apprehension of violence, assault definition]



Assault under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182 includes conduct where a person attempts or threatens by gesture to apply force to another person.



The section provides that assault occurs where a person:



Attempts to apply force, or



Threatens by gesture to apply force



to another person if the person making the threat:



has the present ability to carry out the threat, or



causes the victim to reasonably believe that such ability exists.



The offence focuses on the creation of apprehension of violence rather than actual physical contact.



Authority — Assault by threatening gesture



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Thornton v The Queen (1984) 14 Tas R 113]

\[TOPIC: elements of assault by threatening gesture]

\[CONCEPTS: threatening gesture, intention to induce fear, assault mental element, apprehension of violence, threatening conduct]



Thornton v The Queen (1984) 14 Tas R 113 confirmed that assault by threatening gesture requires that the gesture be performed with the intention of inducing apprehension in the victim that force is about to be applied.



The Court held that:



a threatening gesture must convey a message of impending force, and



the accused must act with the intention that the victim apprehend violence.



However, the offence does not require an intention to actually apply force.



Assault — Mental Element



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: mental element for assault offences]

\[CONCEPTS: subjective recklessness, intention to apply force, foresight of consequence, criminal state of mind, recklessness vs negligence]



Assault offences require proof of a mental element concerning the application or threat of force.



The prosecution must establish that the accused either:



Intended to apply force, or



Subjectively foresaw the likelihood that force might be applied and proceeded regardless.



This form of recklessness is commonly referred to as subjective recklessness.



Negligence alone is insufficient to establish criminal liability for assault because negligence does not involve the necessary awareness of risk.



Authority — Subjective recklessness



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Beechey v McDonald \[2010] TASSC 47]

\[TOPIC: subjective recklessness as mental element for assault]

\[CONCEPTS: recklessness, foresight of harm, assault mens rea, criminal intent, awareness of risk]



Beechey v McDonald \[2010] TASSC 47 confirmed that the mental element for assault may be satisfied either by actual intent to apply force or by subjective foresight of the likelihood of force being applied, followed by the decision to proceed regardless.



Assault — No Requirement of Immediate Threat



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: temporal element of threatening gesture assault]

\[CONCEPTS: immediacy of threat, present ability, assault threat timing, future violence threats, apprehension of force]



Assault by threatening gesture does not require that the threatened application of force be immediate.



The essential requirement is that the victim believes the accused has the present ability to carry out the threat at the time it is made.



A threat of violence at a future time may still constitute assault if the victim reasonably believes that the accused presently has the ability to carry out the threat.



Authority — No immediacy requirement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lees v Visser \[2000] TASSC 3]

\[TOPIC: absence of immediacy requirement for threatening gesture assault]

\[CONCEPTS: present ability, threat timing, assault by gesture, victim belief, temporal element]



Lees v Visser \[2000] TASSC 3 confirmed that Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182 does not require that the threatened force be immediate. The offence is satisfied where the victim believes the accused presently has the ability to carry out the threat, even if the threatened violence is to occur at a later time.



Assault Police Officer — Elements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 34B]

\[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 34B]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: element of offence]

\[TOPIC: assaulting a police officer in execution of duty]

\[CONCEPTS: police assault offence, execution of duty, assault police officer, lawful duty, officer protection]



The offence of assaulting a police officer requires proof that the accused assaulted a person who was a police officer acting in the execution of their duty.



The prosecution must prove:



The accused committed an assault.



The victim was a police officer.



At the time of the assault the officer was acting in the execution of their duty.



If the officer was not acting in execution of duty, the offence is not established even if an assault occurred.



Authority — Execution of duty



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Jones v Krushka \[2021] TASMC 14]

\[TOPIC: meaning of execution of duty for police]

\[CONCEPTS: police powers, lawful task, execution of duty, policing functions, officer authority]



Jones v Krushka \[2021] TASMC 14 explains that a police officer acts in the execution of duty when performing a lawful task connected with policing functions, such as preventing crime, apprehending offenders, keeping the peace, or protecting life and property.



The inquiry focuses on the particular duty being executed, rather than whether the officer is simply on duty.



Assault Police Officer — Knowledge of Police Status



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: knowledge of police status not required for offence]

\[CONCEPTS: mistake of fact, police officer assault, knowledge requirement, reasonable belief defence, criminal liability]



For offences involving assaulting a police officer, the prosecution is not required to prove that the defendant knew the victim was a police officer.



However, a defendant may rely on a reasonable mistake of fact defence if:



the defendant reasonably believed the officer was acting unlawfully, and



if the facts were as believed, the defendant’s actions would have been lawful.



Authority — Knowledge not required



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Jones v Bacic \[2013] TASMC 14]

\[TOPIC: prosecution need not prove knowledge of police status]

\[CONCEPTS: assault police officer, knowledge element, mistake of fact defence, police identity, criminal responsibility]



Jones v Bacic \[2013] TASMC 14 applied the High Court decision in R v Reynhoudt \[1962] HCA 23 and confirmed that proof of knowledge that the victim was a police officer is not an element of the offence.



Asportation — Distinction Between Kidnapping and False Imprisonment



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: asportation element distinguishing kidnapping]

\[CONCEPTS: kidnapping, false imprisonment, carrying away, movement of victim, deprivation of liberty]



The key distinction between kidnapping and false imprisonment is the element of asportation, meaning the carrying away of the victim from one place to another.



False imprisonment involves unlawful detention without movement, whereas kidnapping requires both:



Deprivation of liberty, and



Movement of the victim from the place where they wish to remain.



Authority — Asportation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Davis v R \[2006] NSWCCA 392]

\[TOPIC: asportation requirement in kidnapping]

\[CONCEPTS: movement element, kidnapping definition, carrying away, confinement vs removal, criminal detention]



Davis v R \[2006] NSWCCA 392 explains that the movement required for asportation need not be substantial, provided the victim is carried away from the place they wish to remain.



Firearms — Extended Definition of “Use”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: extended definition of firearm use]

\[CONCEPTS: firearm use definition, threatening with firearm, causing belief weapon will fire, firearm offences]



In firearms legislation, the term “use” of a firearm includes conduct that creates a reasonable belief that the firearm will be fired.



The definition includes:



Actually firing the firearm, or



Holding the firearm in a way that causes a reasonable belief it will be fired, even if the firearm is incapable of being discharged.



This definition extends criminal liability beyond actual discharge of a firearm.



Authority — Gel blaster classification



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Milligan (No 2) \[2021] TASSC 60]

\[TOPIC: classification of gel blasters as firearms]

\[CONCEPTS: gel blaster, air rifle classification, firearm licensing, firearm definition]



Tasmania v Milligan (No 2) \[2021] TASSC 60 confirmed that gel blasters may fall within the statutory definition of a firearm, often being classified as an air rifle for licensing purposes.



Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 88 — Seizure of Firearms Where Storage Requirements Not Met



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 88]

\[CITATION: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 88]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Police seizure powers for improperly stored firearms]

\[CONCEPTS: firearm seizure, unsafe storage, police powers, ammunition seizure, ordnance seizure, firearm compliance, firearm storage law, police authority]



Section 88 of the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) authorises police to seize firearms and related items where storage requirements are not complied with. The provision allows intervention to prevent risks arising from insecure firearm storage.



A police officer may seize any of the following items if they have reasonable grounds to believe that the item is not being stored in accordance with the storage requirements under the Act:



A firearm



A firearm part



Ammunition



Ordnance



The threshold for seizure is reasonable grounds for belief that the storage obligations imposed by the Firearms Act have not been met. The power is preventative in nature and is designed to ensure that firearms are not kept in a manner that creates a risk to public safety.



The provision does not require proof of an offence before seizure occurs. Instead, the seizure power operates as an administrative enforcement mechanism enabling police to secure weapons that are improperly stored while compliance issues are addressed.



Items seized under this provision may subsequently become the subject of disposal or forfeiture proceedings under Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 149, depending on the circumstances of the seizure and any related criminal proceedings.



Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 149 — Disposal of Surrendered or Seized Firearms



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 149]

\[CITATION: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 149]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Magistrate powers regarding seized or surrendered firearms]

\[CONCEPTS: firearm forfeiture, firearm return, firearm disposal order, magistrate powers, seized firearms, surrendered weapons, court orders, firearm regulation]



Section 149 of the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) establishes the mechanism by which a magistrate determines the ultimate disposition of firearms or ammunition that have been seized or surrendered under the Act.



An application may be made to a magistrate by:



a police officer, or



a person claiming to be the owner of the firearm or ammunition.



Upon hearing the application, the magistrate may order that the firearm or ammunition:



be forfeited to the Crown,



be returned to the person claiming ownership, or



be disposed of in any other manner determined by the magistrate.



These powers allow the court to determine an appropriate outcome depending on the legality of the possession, compliance with the Act, and the circumstances of the seizure.



Where firearms have been seized during police enforcement activity, this section ensures that their final disposition occurs through judicial oversight rather than administrative decision alone.



Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 149(2A) — Grounds for Forfeiture to the Crown



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 149(2A)]

\[CITATION: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 149(2A)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Forfeiture conditions for seized firearms]

\[CONCEPTS: firearm forfeiture grounds, unlawful firearm possession, unknown owner firearms, non-compliance with firearm law, disposal failure, Crown forfeiture]



Section 149(2A) of the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) sets out the circumstances in which a magistrate may order that a firearm or ammunition be forfeited to the Crown.



A forfeiture order may be made where the court is satisfied of one of the following conditions:



The owner is not authorised to possess the firearm or ammunition, and



The owner fails to provide an alternative lawful method of disposal within a reasonable time.



Alternatively, forfeiture may occur where:



the owner cannot be located after reasonable inquiry, or



the owner has failed to comply with the requirements of the Act relating to the firearm or ammunition.



These provisions ensure that firearms that are unlawfully possessed or whose lawful owner cannot be identified may be permanently removed from circulation through a court-ordered forfeiture to the Crown.



Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 149(2B) — Mandatory Forfeiture on Conviction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 149(2B)]

\[CITATION: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 149(2B)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: Mandatory forfeiture of firearms upon conviction]

\[CONCEPTS: firearm forfeiture conviction, mandatory forfeiture, firearm used in offence, weapons forfeiture law, firearm offences Tasmania]



Section 149(2B) of the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) provides that where a person is convicted of certain specified offences, the firearm used in relation to that offence must be forfeited to the Crown.



The provision applies to convictions for offences including:



Firearms Act offences under ss 111–114, and



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 14B(2A)(a).



Where such a conviction occurs, the firearm connected to the offence must be forfeited, removing any discretion from the court regarding whether forfeiture should occur.



This provision reflects a public safety policy that firearms used in serious firearm-related offences should not remain in private possession following conviction.



Circumstantial Evidence — Definition and Standard of Proof



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Circumstantial evidence and proof beyond reasonable doubt]

\[CONCEPTS: circumstantial evidence, inference of guilt, indirect evidence, reasonable hypotheses, proof beyond reasonable doubt, criminal inference]



Circumstantial evidence refers to evidence that proves or tends to prove a fact from which another fact may be inferred. Unlike direct evidence, circumstantial evidence relies on reasoning from surrounding circumstances rather than direct observation of the offence.



A conviction based on circumstantial evidence is permissible only where the evidence establishes guilt beyond reasonable doubt.



The governing principle is that the circumstantial evidence must exclude all reasonable hypotheses consistent with innocence. If a reasonable alternative explanation remains open on the evidence, the prosecution has not discharged the burden of proof.



Authority — Doney v R



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Doney v R \[1990] HCA 51; (1990) 171 CLR 207]

\[TOPIC: Standard for circumstantial proof of guilt]

\[CONCEPTS: circumstantial reasoning, reasonable hypothesis, inference of guilt, criminal standard of proof, exclusion of innocence hypotheses]



In Doney v R, the High Court confirmed that circumstantial evidence can establish guilt beyond reasonable doubt only where all other reasonable hypotheses consistent with innocence are excluded. The test requires that guilt be the only rational inference reasonably open on the evidence.



Lies as Consciousness of Guilt — Edwards Principle



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Lies as circumstantial evidence of guilt]

\[CONCEPTS: consciousness of guilt, false statements, Edwards direction, deliberate lie evidence, post-offence conduct]



Evidence that an accused has lied may sometimes be relied upon by the prosecution as circumstantial evidence indicating consciousness of guilt. This is commonly known as an Edwards lie.



Before a lie can be used as evidence of guilt, the tribunal of fact must be satisfied of several matters:



The accused deliberately told an untruth.



The lie relates to a material circumstance connected with the offence.



The lie was told because the accused knew the truth would implicate them in the offence.



A lie cannot be used as evidence of guilt if there is a reasonable innocent explanation for the false statement. Innocent explanations may include panic, confusion, embarrassment, or an attempt to conceal unrelated conduct.



Authority — R v Edwards



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Edwards \[1993] HCA 63; (1993) 178 CLR 193]

\[TOPIC: Lies as evidence of consciousness of guilt]

\[CONCEPTS: Edwards direction, deliberate lie, inference of guilt, post-offence conduct, criminal evidence]



In R v Edwards, the High Court established that lies may support an inference of guilt where the lie was told because the accused knew that telling the truth would implicate them in the offence. However, the jury need not first be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the accused is guilty before considering the lie; the lie forms part of the overall evidentiary assessment.



No Case to Answer — Legal Test



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Judicial test for no case submissions]

\[CONCEPTS: no case submission, sufficiency of evidence, judicial assessment, prosecution case closure, legal threshold]



A submission of no case to answer arises at the close of the prosecution case where the defence argues that the evidence is legally insufficient to justify a conviction.



Authority — May v O’Sullivan



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: May v O’Sullivan \[1955] HCA 38; (1955) 92 CLR 654]

\[TOPIC: Test for determining no case to answer]

\[CONCEPTS: sufficiency of evidence, legal capacity to convict, judicial test, prosecution evidence threshold]



In May v O’Sullivan, the High Court held that the relevant question is not whether the accused ought to be convicted, but whether a conviction could lawfully be entered on the evidence as it stands.



If the evidence, taken at its highest, is capable in law of supporting a conviction, the case must proceed to determination by the fact-finder.



Rule in Browne v Dunn



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Obligation to confront witnesses in cross-examination]

\[CONCEPTS: Browne v Dunn rule, procedural fairness, cross-examination duty, witness contradiction, trial fairness]



The rule in Browne v Dunn (1894) 6 R 67 is a principle of procedural fairness requiring that a witness be given an opportunity during cross-examination to address evidence that contradicts their testimony.



Where a party intends to challenge the credibility of a witness or rely on evidence inconsistent with the witness’s testimony, that matter must be put to the witness during cross-examination.



Failure to comply with the rule may lead to procedural remedies, including the recall of a witness under Evidence Act s 46, or adverse conclusions regarding the fairness of the trial.



Evidence Act — Exclusion of Unfairly Prejudicial Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 137]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Prejudicial Evidence Overview]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Exclusion of prejudicial evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, probative value balancing, jury misuse risk, evidence exclusion]



Section 137 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) requires courts in criminal proceedings to exclude prosecution evidence if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the accused.



Authority — Papakosmas v The Queen



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Papakosmas v The Queen (1999) 196 CLR 297]

\[TOPIC: Meaning of unfair prejudice under evidence law]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice definition, evidentiary balancing, jury misuse, probative value]



In Papakosmas v The Queen, the High Court clarified that unfair prejudice does not arise simply because evidence strengthens the prosecution case. Rather, prejudice becomes unfair where there is a real risk that the jury will misuse the evidence, for example by reasoning emotionally or relying on impermissible inferences.



Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 3 — Definition of Police Officer



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 3]

\[CITATION: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 3]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: Definition of police officer for the purposes of the Firearms Act]

\[CONCEPTS: police officer definition, Firearms Act interpretation, Tasmania Police, statutory definition, authorised officer, Police Service member, who counts as police, firearm law enforcement]



Section 3 of the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) defines the term “police officer” by reference to the Police Service Act 2003 (Tas). For the purposes of the Firearms Act, a police officer means a member of the Police Service established under s 4 of the Police Service Act 2003 (Tas).



This definition is important because a number of coercive and administrative powers under the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) are conferred specifically on a police officer, including seizure powers and applications relating to the disposal of firearms. The definition therefore determines who is legally authorised to exercise those statutory functions.



Because the definition is incorporated by reference to the Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) s 4, the scope of the term depends on membership of the established Police Service rather than a broader or informal understanding of policing functions. The provision supplies the statutory link needed to identify which officers can lawfully act under powers such as Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 88 and s 149.



Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) s 4 — Establishment of the Police Service



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Police Service Act 2003 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 4]

\[CITATION: Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) s 4]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: statutory definition]

\[TOPIC: Establishment and composition of the Police Service]

\[CONCEPTS: Police Service establishment, Tasmania Police ranks, police member definition, Commissioner, constable, special constable, trainee, police ranks Tasmania]



Section 4 of the Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) establishes the Police Service and specifies the ranks of which it consists. This provision is relevant to Tasmanian criminal practice because other statutes, including the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas), define a police officer by reference to membership of that Service.



The section provides that there is an established Police Service comprising the following ranks:



Commissioner



Deputy Commissioner



Assistant Commissioner



Commander



Inspector



Sergeant



Constable



Special Constable



Trainee



Junior Constable



Any other rank created under s 4(4)



The total number of police officers is to be determined by the Minister after considering any recommendation of the Commissioner. The section also permits creation or removal of ranks, with different mechanisms depending on whether the rank is below or at or above inspector.



For criminal law purposes, the significance of the section is mainly definitional. It identifies the body whose members may fall within statutory references to a police officer, including in the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas). That makes the section relevant when determining whether a person had lawful authority to seize firearms or make applications concerning forfeiture or disposal.



Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 129 — Firearms Amnesty



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 129]

\[CITATION: Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 129]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: Firearms amnesty limited to possession offences]

\[CONCEPTS: firearm amnesty, unlawful possession, surrender protection, possession only, firearms compliance, amnesty provision, hand in weapons, immunity for possession]



Section 129 of the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) provides for a firearms amnesty. On the source material, the doctrinal point is that the amnesty applies to possession only.



The practical significance of that limitation is that the amnesty is directed to encouraging the surrender or regularisation of firearms that are unlawfully possessed, without extending protection to broader categories of offending. It is therefore not a general immunity provision for all conduct connected with firearms.



The source does not set out the full statutory language of s 129, but the expressed principle is clear: where reliance is placed on the amnesty provision, attention must be given to whether the conduct in question is no more than possession. If the alleged offending extends beyond possession, the amnesty provision may not apply.



Because the source gives only a concise note rather than full statutory text, this chunk is confined to the supported proposition that the Firearms Act 1996 (Tas) s 129 amnesty is limited in operation to possession-related circumstances. \[REVIEW]



Circumstantial DNA Evidence — Assessment of Deposition and Context



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Evaluation of DNA evidence in a circumstantial case]

\[CONCEPTS: DNA evidence, circumstantial proof, forensic evidence, transfer of DNA, deposition of material, innocent explanation, context of sample, forensic inference]



Circumstantial DNA evidence must be assessed by considering where the DNA material was deposited, how it was deposited, and the context in which it was found. DNA evidence does not prove guilt automatically merely because the accused’s DNA is present; its probative value depends on the circumstances of transfer and discovery.



In a circumstantial case, the tribunal of fact must still determine whether the forensic evidence, viewed with the whole of the evidence, excludes reasonable hypotheses consistent with innocence. The presence of DNA may be powerful, but it must be evaluated carefully to avoid assuming that presence alone proves the manner or timing of involvement.



Authority — Roughan v Haines



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Roughan v Haines \[2019] TASMC 7]

\[TOPIC: Care required in assessing circumstantial DNA evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: DNA sample location, deposition mechanism, forensic caution, innocent transfer, circumstantial inference, no onus on accused]



In Roughan v Haines \[2019] TASMC 7, the court emphasised that DNA evidence must be approached with care in a circumstantial case. The judgment noted that consideration must be given to where DNA material was deposited and how it came to be deposited, with reference to Fitzgerald v The Queen \[2014] HCA 23 as an example of the need for caution in reasoning from DNA evidence. The case also reaffirmed that the defendant bears no onus to provide an innocent explanation.



Post-Offence Conduct as Part of a Circumstantial Case



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Use of post-offence conduct within circumstantial reasoning]

\[CONCEPTS: post-offence conduct, flight evidence, consciousness of guilt, circumstantial case, police chase, evasive conduct, combined force of evidence]



Post-offence conduct, including flight or evasive behaviour, may be used as part of the prosecution’s circumstantial case. Such evidence is not necessarily conclusive on its own, but it may have probative value when considered with the rest of the evidence.



The critical point is that post-offence conduct is ordinarily assessed as one strand within the total circumstantial case, not as a separate and self-sufficient basis for conviction. Even where there are alternative reasons for the conduct, the behaviour may still be relevant if its timing, character, or intensity supports an inference adverse to the accused.



Authority — Pickett v Tasmania



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Pickett v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 12]

\[TOPIC: Flight evidence as a cogent part of the circumstantial case]

\[CONCEPTS: flight from police, desperation, circumstantial weight, combined force, reaction to police, post-offence conduct]



In Pickett v Tasmania \[2022] TASCCA 12, the Court of Criminal Appeal held that evidence of the appellant’s flight, while not sufficient of itself to prove guilt, was a cogent piece of circumstantial evidence when considered with the other prosecution evidence. The court stressed that the strength of the circumstantial case depends on the combined force of all of the evidence.



Inference Must Be More Than Conjecture



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: Requirement that an inference be rational and evidence-based]

\[CONCEPTS: conjecture, reasonable inference, factual basis, criminal reasoning, rational conclusion, speculation, alternative hypothesis]



An inference of guilt in a criminal case must rest on more than mere conjecture. A conclusion is not reasonable simply because it is possible; it must be grounded in facts proved or accepted in evidence and arise logically from those facts.



This principle is especially important in circumstantial cases. Where the prosecution asks the tribunal of fact to draw an inference from surrounding circumstances, the inference must be one that is rationally available on the evidence. Speculative or imaginative possibilities do not meet that standard, but neither can guilt be inferred from a factual vacuum.



Authority — R v Baden-Clay



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Baden-Clay \[2016] HCA 35; 258 CLR 308]

\[TOPIC: Inference must be based on more than conjecture]

\[CONCEPTS: conjecture versus inference, rational reasoning, proved facts, circumstantial case, evidentiary foundation]



In R v Baden-Clay \[2016] HCA 35; 258 CLR 308, the High Court confirmed that for an inference to be reasonable it must rest on something more than mere conjecture. The case supports the doctrinal point that criminal inferences must be anchored in the evidence and cannot be founded on speculation.



Inference from Failure to Give Evidence — Weissensteiner Principle



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Adverse inferential use of an accused’s silence where facts are uniquely within knowledge]

\[CONCEPTS: failure to give evidence, accused silence, unique knowledge, circumstantial case, adverse inference, Weissensteiner principle, explanation absent]



In some circumstantial cases, an accused person’s failure to give evidence may strengthen an inference of guilt where the relevant facts or explanation would be peculiarly within the accused’s knowledge. The principle does not reverse the onus of proof and does not permit guilt to be inferred merely from silence. Rather, it allows the tribunal of fact to regard the absence of an expected explanation as making an otherwise available inference more secure.



The principle is confined to cases where:



The prosecution case is already capable of supporting the relevant inference.



The missing explanation concerns facts easily perceived to be within the accused’s knowledge.



It is reasonable to expect that, if an innocent explanation existed, it could come from the accused.



Authority — Weissensteiner v R



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Weissensteiner v R (1993) 178 CLR 217]

\[TOPIC: Silence may strengthen factual inference in a circumstantial case]

\[CONCEPTS: silence of accused, unique knowledge, inferential support, circumstantial guilt, missing explanation]



In Weissensteiner v R (1993) 178 CLR 217, the High Court held that where contradicatory facts or explanations could come only from the accused, and the accused does not give evidence, the jury may more safely draw the inference sought by the prosecution. The principle is a limited one and operates only in relation to facts peculiarly within the accused’s knowledge.



Weissensteiner Principle — Limitation to Inferences of Fact



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: Restriction on using silence to infer state of mind]

\[CONCEPTS: state of mind, inference of fact, accused silence, Weissensteiner limits, mens rea inference, mental element, circumstantial evidence]



The Weissensteiner principle is limited to the drawing of inferences of fact and does not extend to inferring an accused person’s state of mind merely from their failure to give evidence. The distinction matters because factual gaps may sometimes be uniquely explainable by the accused, whereas subjective intention, knowledge, or other mental elements cannot properly be inferred from silence alone under this doctrine.



Authority — B v Visser



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: B v Visser \[2000] TASSC 141]

\[TOPIC: Weissensteiner does not apply to inferring state of mind]

\[CONCEPTS: no inference of mens rea, silence limits, factual inference only, mental state restriction, accused silence]



In B v Visser \[2000] TASSC 141, the Supreme Court held that the Weissensteiner principle has no application in relation to drawing inferences as to an accused person’s state of mind, as distinct from factual inferences arising from circumstantial evidence. The case therefore limits the doctrine to objective factual matters.



No Case to Answer in Circumstantial Cases



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Test for no-case submissions where the prosecution relies on circumstantial evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: no case circumstantial, capable in law, exclude innocence hypotheses, prosecution at its highest, judicial function, reasonable mind]



In a case that depends on circumstantial evidence, a no-case submission succeeds only where the prosecution evidence, taken at its highest, is not capable in law of supporting a conviction. The judge does not decide which inference should be drawn and does not determine whether the prosecution case should ultimately be accepted. The question is whether a reasonable mind could reach a conclusion of guilt beyond reasonable doubt and exclude competing hypotheses consistent with innocence as not reasonably open.



Authority — Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993) \[1993] SASC 4152; (1993) 70 A Crim R 323]

\[TOPIC: Circumstantial no-case test]

\[CONCEPTS: no case submission, circumstantial capacity, reasonable mind, exclude innocence, legal sufficiency, prosecution inferences]



In Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993), King CJ stated that if a case depends on circumstantial evidence, there is a case to answer where that evidence, if accepted, is capable of producing in a reasonable mind a conclusion of guilt beyond reasonable doubt and is capable of causing a reasonable mind to exclude competing hypotheses as unreasonable. There is no case to answer only if the evidence is not capable in law of supporting a conviction.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 67 — Notice Requirement for Unavailable Witness Hearsay



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 67]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 67]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Notice requirements before relying on hearsay from an unavailable witness]

\[CONCEPTS: unavailable witness, hearsay notice, s 67 notice, Evidence Regulations 2022, procedural precondition, prior representation, admissibility procedure]



Before relying on hearsay evidence from an unavailable witness, notice must be given under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 67. The source material states that the notice must comply with the requirements of reg 4 of the Evidence Regulations 2022.



This requirement is procedural but significant. It ensures that the opposing party is informed of the intended reliance on the hearsay exception and has an opportunity to address admissibility issues. A failure to comply may affect whether the hearsay material can be received.



The source also notes that an argument about unavailability may be based on Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 18 where the witness is non-compellable.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 18 — Non-Compellable Witnesses and Unavailability



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 18]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 18]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Non-compellable witness as a basis for unavailability]

\[CONCEPTS: non-compellable witness, unavailable witness, hearsay exception, witness cannot be compelled, admissibility gateway, protected witness]



Section 18 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) is identified in the source as the basis for an unavailability argument where a witness is non-compellable. The doctrinal point is that unavailability is not confined to physical absence or incapacity; it may also arise where the law does not permit the witness to be compelled to testify.



Authority — Sanderson v Rabuntja



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Sanderson v Rabuntja \[2014] NTSC 13]

\[TOPIC: Non-compellability as a basis for witness unavailability]

\[CONCEPTS: non-compellable witness, unavailability, hearsay gateway, protected witness status, admissibility]



The source cites Sanderson v Rabuntja \[2014] NTSC 13 as authority relevant to the proposition that non-compellability may support an argument that a witness is unavailable for hearsay purposes.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(b) — Previous Representation Made Shortly After and Unlikely to Be Fabrication



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 65(2)(b)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(b)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Hearsay exception for unavailable witness where representation made shortly after and unlikely fabricated]

\[CONCEPTS: s 65(2)(b), shortly after, unlikely fabrication, previous representation, unavailable witness, hearsay exception, circumstances of making, reliability]



Section 65(2)(b) of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides a hearsay exception for a previous representation of an unavailable witness where the representation was made when or shortly after the asserted fact occurred and in circumstances that make it unlikely that the representation is a fabrication.



The source material emphasises that the court must identify the particular fact sought to be proved and then the particular representation relied upon to prove it. The admissibility assessment must then be undertaken representation by representation, rather than at a general level.



The enquiry focuses on the circumstances in which the representation was made, not simply on whether the asserted fact appears true. The source notes that same-day representations were admitted in the cited Tasmanian authority, while later statements made two days afterwards, including in a statutory declaration, were not admitted under this paragraph despite some features tending towards reliability.



Authority — Tasmania v Dolega



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Dolega \[2016] TASSC 65]

\[TOPIC: Circumstances of making and fabrication risk under s 65(2)(b)]

\[CONCEPTS: fabrication risk, credibility of representation, reliability circumstances, hearsay exception, objective assessment]



In Tasmania v Dolega \[2016] TASSC 65, the court is cited for the need to consider the circumstances in which the representation was made and the effect of those circumstances on the credibility or reliability of the representation, including whether those circumstances make fabrication unlikely.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(c) — Highly Probable Reliability



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 65(2)(c)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(c)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Hearsay exception requiring highly probable reliability]

\[CONCEPTS: s 65(2)(c), highly probable reliable, hearsay threshold, unavailable witness, stronger reliability test, previous representation]



Section 65(2)(c) of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) imposes a higher threshold than s 65(2)(b). The source material states that this paragraph is concerned with whether the representation was made in circumstances that make it highly probable that the representation is reliable.



The doctrinal point drawn from the source is that this test goes to reliability at large and sets a more demanding standard than the requirement in s 65(2)(b) that the circumstances make fabrication unlikely. On the source material, a later statement that failed under s 65(2)(b) also failed under s 65(2)(c), because the higher threshold of high probability of reliability was not met.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65 — Structured Admissibility Process



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 65]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65 — Representation-by-Representation Admissibility]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Representation-by-representation approach to hearsay admissibility]

\[CONCEPTS: hearsay procedure, identify fact to be proved, identify representation, representation by representation, admissibility process, unavailable witness]



The admissibility enquiry under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65 requires a structured process. The source material records that the court must first identify the particular fact sought to be proved, then identify the particular representation to be adduced as proof of that fact, and then assess the circumstances in which that representation was made to determine whether the statutory conditions are satisfied.



Authority — Sio v The Queen



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Sio v The Queen \[2016] HCA 32]

\[TOPIC: Method for analysing admissibility under s 65 hearsay exceptions]

\[CONCEPTS: particular fact, particular representation, step-by-step admissibility, hearsay methodology, representation-specific analysis]



In Sio v The Queen \[2016] HCA 32, the High Court explained that the admissibility process under s 65 requires identification of the relevant fact, the particular representation to prove it, and then consideration of the circumstances of that representation. The source material treats this as spelling out the required procedure for s 65 analysis.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65 — Meaning of “Circumstances”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 65]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65 — Meaning of Circumstances in Hearsay Assessment]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: interpretive principle]

\[TOPIC: Meaning of circumstances in evaluating hearsay reliability]

\[CONCEPTS: circumstances of making, objective circumstances, reliability of representation, not truth of fact, previous statements, surrounding conduct, hearsay assessment]



In the context of Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(b) and (c), the word “circumstances” refers to the circumstances in which the relevant representation was made. The focus is on the reliability of the representation as hearsay, not directly on whether the asserted fact is true.



The source material states that the enquiry concerns the objective circumstances of the making of the representation rather than the apparent honesty of the maker. It also notes that previous or subsequent statements or conduct of the maker may be taken into account to the extent they bear on the reliability of the making of the particular representation.



Authority — State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple \[2025] TASSC 6]

\[TOPIC: Interpretation of circumstances for s 65(2)(b) and (c)]

\[CONCEPTS: circumstances of representation, reliability focus, objective enquiry, prior and later conduct, unavailable complainant]



In State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple \[2025] TASSC 6, the court considered the admissibility of hearsay representations made by a complainant who had died before trial. The source records that the judgment treated the relevant “circumstances” as those bearing on the reliability of the making of the representation, and distinguished between representations made on the day of the events and later statements made two days afterwards.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Unfair Prejudice Distinguished from Mere Prejudice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 137]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Prejudicial vs Unfairly Prejudicial]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Difference between prejudicial evidence and unfairly prejudicial evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, prejudice versus unfairness, emotional reasoning, improper use by jury, evidence exclusion, probative value]



Section 137 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) is concerned with unfair prejudice, not prejudice in the ordinary sense. Most prosecution evidence that tends to prove guilt is prejudicial to an accused. The statute is directed to a narrower problem: the risk that evidence will be used by the jury in an improper or unfair way.



Authority — Neill-Fraser v Tasmania



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Neill-Fraser v Tasmania \[2012] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: Distinction between prejudice and unfair prejudice]

\[CONCEPTS: ordinary prejudice, unfair misuse, emotional basis, jury misuse, evidentiary fairness]



In Neill-Fraser v Tasmania \[2012] TASCCA 2, Crawford CJ explained that all evidence tending to convict is prejudicial in one sense, but unfair prejudice refers to the risk that the jury may use the evidence to decide the case on an improper, perhaps emotional, basis. That distinction assists in applying Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Examples of Alleged Unfair Prejudice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 137]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Unfair Prejudice: Unavailable Complainant Hearsay]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Alleged forms of unfair prejudice arising from unavailable complainant hearsay]

\[CONCEPTS: no cross-examination, forensic pressure, accused giving evidence, unassailed representation, tactical disadvantage, jury misuse, unfair prejudice examples]



The source material records several defence arguments said to amount to unfair prejudice under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 in a case involving hearsay representations by an unavailable complainant. The asserted unfairness included:



The representations would be unassailed by cross-examination.



Their admission would create forensic pressure on an accused to give evidence.



The jury might misuse one accused’s interview in another accused’s trial.



There would be no opportunity to cross-examine on matters such as drug use affecting reliability.



The accused had not been confronted in interview with the full detail of the complainant’s assertions.



Authority — State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple \[2025] TASSC 6]

\[TOPIC: Defence claims of unfair prejudice held manageable by jury directions]

\[CONCEPTS: unavailable complainant, hearsay prejudice, detailed jury directions, forensic disadvantage, self-defence interview issues]



In State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple \[2025] TASSC 6, the source records that all of those prejudice arguments were dismissed as amenable to proper and detailed directions to the jury. The doctrinal point is that asserted prejudice will not necessarily justify exclusion where the risk can be adequately managed by judicial direction.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 81-82, 86 — Admissions



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 81-82, 86] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 81-82, 86] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: admissions and hearsay exceptions] \[CONCEPTS: admission, hearsay exception, contextual representation, first-hand hearer, notebook interview, written acknowledgement, statement against interest, confession, what counts as an admission, police notebook]

An admission under the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) is treated in the source as a representation adverse to the person’s interest in the outcome of the proceeding. The source states that admissions are an exception to the hearsay rule under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 81, and that representations made around the same time may also be admitted where they provide context for the admission, such as the whole of a video statement rather than an isolated sentence.



The source also identifies a limiting rule under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 82. On that account, the hearsay exception is confined to first-hand hearers of the admission or the document in which the admission is contained. That reflects a control on how far secondary evidence of an admission may travel.



The source further notes a separate rule for oral admissions made to police in notebook form. Under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 86, such evidence is admissible if it has been written down and acknowledged by the defendant. The source also records that this mechanism is unavailable for “serious offences” as defined in the Act, described in the source as broadly covering indictable matters involving adults.



The practical proposition recorded in the source is that any acknowledgement of a relevant fact tending to establish guilt may amount to an admission, provided the representation is capable of rationally affecting assessment of a fact in issue.



Authority — Familiarity supporting admissions as proof of an element



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Bird v Adams, Criminal Law Review 1972, page 174] \[TOPIC: defendant familiarity giving evidentiary weight to an admission] \[CONCEPTS: admission as proof of element, familiarity with subject matter, dangerous drug admission, prima facie evidence, case to answer, accused knew what he was talking about]

Bird v Adams is used in the source for the proposition that an admission can support proof of an element of an offence where the defendant has sufficient familiarity with the subject matter of the admission. The example given is a defendant admitting that a substance in possession was LSD.



The source distinguishes between admissions on matters outside a defendant’s likely knowledge and admissions about circumstances of the defendant’s own conduct. It records that some admissions on legal or technical matters may be worthless if the defendant lacks the necessary knowledge. By contrast, an admission that a possessed substance was a dangerous drug, coupled with an admission of supplying it, was treated as at least prima facie evidence because the defendant had sufficient knowledge of the circumstances of his conduct.



The doctrinal value of the authority in the source is that a defendant’s own familiarity with the matter admitted may make the admission probative of an element, even without separate scientific proof at the no-case stage.



Authority — Familiarity with a drug making an admission probative



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Harris v Pandava, 24/75, Neasey J, 10/6/75] \[TOPIC: admission accepted where surrounding evidence shows knowledge and familiarity] \[CONCEPTS: cannabis admission, knowledge of drug, acceptance at face value, familiarity, reasonable inference, case to answer, drug identification]

Harris v Pandava is cited in the source as supporting the same doctrinal idea as Bird v Adams. The source states that where there is sufficient evidence of the defendant’s familiarity and knowledge of the drug in question, a court may accept an admission at face value.



The recorded passage treats the defendant’s statement that he had smoked cannabis in Hobart some months before the interview as admissible and probative because there was enough evidence of his knowledge of the drug to justify accepting that he knew what he was talking about. The authority is therefore used to reinforce a practical evidentiary rule: an admission about the nature of a drug may support proof of that fact when the accused’s own experience or familiarity makes the statement reliable enough to found a reasonable inference.



Authority — Admission as proof of age



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Recorder of Grimsby Ex P. Purser (1951) 2 All ER 889; 35 Cr App R 155] \[TOPIC: admission of age accepted without further proof] \[CONCEPTS: proof of age, defendant admission, magistrates, age element, no further proof required, age admitted, identity details, factual admission]

R v Recorder of Grimsby Ex P. Purser is cited in the source for the rule that magistrates may accept a defendant’s admission as to age where there is no reason to doubt its truth. The source records that no further proof of age is necessary in that circumstance.



The principle is narrow but practical. It treats age as a factual matter that may be proved by an admission if there is no apparent reason for scepticism. In the source, the authority is used as a simple illustration that not every formal fact requires independent documentary proof when the accused has admitted it and the surrounding circumstances do not call the admission into doubt.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90 — Unfairness and lack of awareness of suspicion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 90] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: exclusion of admissions for unfairness where accused misunderstood status] \[CONCEPTS: unfairness, accused not aware suspect, caution, voluntary conversation, onus on accused, misunderstanding situation, exclusion of admissions, police fairness, suspect notice]

The source states that it is not always necessary for police to tell a person that they are a suspect in relation to a specific offence before an admission may be used. It records, however, that the evidence may still be excluded under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90 if use of the statement would be unfair.



The unfairness analysis described in the source turns on whether the accused in fact misunderstood the situation and did not realise that he or she might be a suspect. The source emphasises that lawful police conduct and a voluntary conversation do not themselves create unfairness. Rather, unfairness arises only if the accused actually spoke in circumstances where, had the position been understood, silence would probably have been maintained.



The source also places the onus on the defendant. It states that an accused who seeks exclusion under s 90 must prove the factual foundation for unfairness. Where the argument depends on the accused’s own misunderstanding or belief, the best evidence will ordinarily be direct evidence from the accused on the voir dire rather than a circumstantial reconstruction of mental state.



Authority — Formal caution sufficient even if specific suspect notice is absent



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Tasmania v Thompson \[2022] TASSC 27] \[TOPIC: fairness does not require police to identify the interviewee as a suspect in every case] \[CONCEPTS: caution, suspect status, fairness not fixed categories, lawful search, voluntary statements, police interview, accused understanding, not always necessary to say suspect]

Tasmania v Thompson is used in the source for the proposition that fairness does not impose a fixed rule requiring police to tell an interviewee that he or she is a suspect before questioning. The source states that a formal caution may be enough, and that the fairness inquiry is fact-specific rather than categorical.



The recorded passage explains that prior remarks about informing a person they are a suspect were made in a context where there had been no caution at all. On the source’s reading of the case, the important point is that the administration of a caution can satisfy fairness even where the interviewee is not expressly told of a particular suspected offence. The authority therefore supports a flexible approach: the absence of a specific suspect label is not automatically unfair, although s 90 may still operate if the accused actually misunderstood the situation.



Authority — Onus on accused to prove unfairness under s 90



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Em v The Queen \[2007] HCA 46; 232 CLR 67] \[TOPIC: accused bears the onus of proving the factual basis for unfairness] \[CONCEPTS: voir dire, onus of proof, accused mental state, unfairness, circumstantial inference, direct evidence, misunderstanding, use against accused]

Em v The Queen is cited in the source for the proposition that the accused bears the onus of demonstrating unfairness for the purposes of Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90. The source uses the case to show that where unfairness depends on what the accused believed, proof by circumstantial inference may be inadequate if the accused does not give direct evidence.



The source stresses that if the argument is that the accused believed statements could not be used, or did not realise they were a suspect, the accused’s own evidence may be critical. Failure to give evidence on the voir dire increases difficulty in discharging the onus. The authority therefore supports both the allocation of onus and the evidentiary importance of direct proof of the accused’s understanding.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 36 — Examination without subpoena or other process



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 36] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 36] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: court power to require evidence from a person present in court] \[CONCEPTS: non-summonsed witness, witness in court, examination without subpoena, compulsory evidence, court power, unsummoned person, give evidence, present in courtroom]

The source states that under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 36 a court may order a person who is already in court to give evidence even if that person has not previously been served with a summons. The doctrinal point recorded is a straightforward procedural power: attendance under subpoena is not always a prerequisite to oral evidence if the person is physically present before the court.



The importance of the rule in practice is that it preserves the court’s control over the forensic process and prevents the accidental or strategic absence of prior process from defeating otherwise available evidence. The source does not elaborate limitations or discretionary controls, so the chunk is confined to the proposition expressly stated: presence in court can be enough to permit compulsory examination.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 170-171 — Business records by affidavit



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 170-171] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 170-171] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: business records proved by affidavit] \[CONCEPTS: business records, affidavit, record keeper, service on defence, not merely disclosed, documentary proof, affidavit certificate, hearing preparation, records admissibility]

The source states that business records may be proved by affidavit under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 170-171. It records that the prosecution may rely on an affidavit from a record keeper satisfying s 171(1)(a) to adduce evidence of business records.



A specific procedural condition is emphasised. The source states that the affidavit material must be served on the defence within a reasonable time prior to hearing; mere disclosure is not enough. That distinction matters because the source treats formal service as part of the gateway to admissibility.



The rule, as presented, therefore has two linked features: first, business records can be admitted through affidavit evidence of a qualified record keeper; second, the prosecution must comply with the service requirement in a timely way. The source does not expand on the detailed statutory criteria beyond s 171(1)(a), so the operative doctrinal content here is the availability of affidavit proof and the necessity of service.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 89 — Silence and improper credibility use



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 89] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 89] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: silence cannot be used to draw negative inferences against the defence] \[CONCEPTS: right to silence, no adverse inference, credibility attack, defence witness, silence as weapon, prejudicial questioning, complainant credibility, why would they lie, unfair inference]

The source states that silence cannot be used as a weapon to attack the later credibility of an account. It attributes that rule to Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 89 and extends the proposition to witnesses called in support of the defence as well as to the defendant.



The practical rule recorded is that a failure to speak earlier cannot later be turned into a negative inference about truthfulness. The source also includes a linked caution about complainant credibility. It notes that asking a defendant “why would they lie?” is prejudicial and should not be asked. The source accepts that complainant credibility can be challenged by other evidence and witnesses, but not by forcing the defendant into speculative commentary on another person’s motives.



Taken together, the source presents s 89 as protecting both the defendant and the defence case from unfair forensic use of silence, while also warning against a form of cross-examination that improperly invites the defendant to endorse the complainant’s honesty.



Illegally Obtained Evidence

Illegally obtained evidence — factors supporting admission



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: discretionary admission of illegally obtained evidence] \[CONCEPTS: illegally obtained evidence, probative value, importance of evidence, proof of charge, public harm, investigator mistake, misunderstanding of law, not deliberate breach, discretionary admission]

The source identifies a group of considerations supporting admission of illegally obtained evidence. The factors recorded are that the evidence has high probative value, is extremely important in the proceedings, and relates directly to proof of a charge. The source also notes a public protection dimension by referring to inherent potential for harm to an indefinite number of people.



A further theme in the source is the character of the contravention. The source says the case was not one in which the contraventions occurred during the course of an investigation in order to obtain evidence, and not one where investigators deliberately disregarded the law. Instead, the contravention is described as having occurred through misunderstanding while those involved were attempting to comply with legal requirements.



The doctrinal significance of the source material is that unlawfulness does not automatically require exclusion. Where the evidence is powerful and important, and where the breach arose from mistake rather than intentional or cynical misconduct, those matters may support admission.



Authority — Mistaken non-deliberate contravention supporting admission



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Tasmania v Melick \[2019] TASSC 19] \[TOPIC: factors favouring admission of illegally obtained evidence] \[CONCEPTS: Brett J, high probative value, importance in proceedings, proof of charge, public danger, misunderstanding, attempted compliance, not deliberate evidence-gathering]

Tasmania v Melick is cited in the source as authority for admitting unlawfully obtained evidence where the evidence is powerful, important, and the breach was not deliberate. The source attributes the reasoning to Brett J.



The recorded factors are that the evidence had high probative value, was extremely important, and related directly to proof of the charge. The source also emphasises that the misconduct was not part of a deliberate investigative strategy to obtain evidence unlawfully. Rather, it arose from a misunderstanding while officers were attempting to comply with the law. The authority is therefore used to support a balancing approach in which the seriousness and utility of the evidence, together with the non-deliberate nature of the breach, may justify admission.



Record of Interview and Mixed Statements

Record of interview fairness — oppressive or overzealous questioning



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 90] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: exclusion of admissions where record of interview questioning is unfair] \[CONCEPTS: record of interview, oppressive questioning, undue persistence, sceptical questioning, exclusion for unfairness, police disbelief, overzealous tactics, jury prejudice, admissions]

The source states that a record of interview may be excluded where the questioning is overzealous, oppressive, unduly persistent, or framed in a way that communicates police disbelief. The doctrinal basis identified is fairness under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90.



The source treats the problem as extending beyond the bare content of answers. A recorded interview may itself become unfair if the form of questioning lets the jury see the interviewer expressing ridicule, disbelief, or conclusions about guilt in a way that would not be permitted in oral testimony at trial. On the source’s account, a police interviewer should not be allowed to place inadmissible opinions before the fact-finder through tone and questioning style.



The rule is therefore not limited to physical oppression or denial of rights. It also extends to questioning techniques that erode fairness by wearing down resistance, cross-examining the suspect, or visually and audibly presenting official disbelief to the jury.



Authority — Overzealous interview tactics can justify exclusion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Tasmania v Purkiss \& Otto \[2019] TASSC 45] \[TOPIC: unfairness arising from disbelief and oppressive style in a recorded interview] \[CONCEPTS: overzealous interviewing, oppressive questioning, police disbelief, ridicule, fairness, jury seeing inadmissible opinions, interview exclusion, s 90]

Tasmania v Purkiss \& Otto is used in the source to show that admissions and confessions may be excluded where interview tactics are oppressive or improperly sceptical. The source highlights the unfairness of allowing a jury to see an inspector expressing disbelief and belief in the accused’s guilt during the interview.



The authority is presented as drawing on common law cases concerning undue persistence and oppressive questioning, while applying those ideas to Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90. The doctrinal lesson extracted in the source is that an interviewer cannot achieve through recorded questions what police would not be permitted to say directly as trial witnesses.



Authority — Improper cross-examination of a person in custody



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Amad \[1962] VR 545] \[TOPIC: impropriety of questioning in custody that tends to extort admissions] \[CONCEPTS: custody stress, extorted admissions, improper cross-examination, mental resistance, caution not enough, oppressive questioning, confession law, police interview]

R v Amad is cited in the source for the proposition that a person in custody is ordinarily under great stress and should not be subjected, even after caution, to cross-examination or questioning whose tendency is to extort admissions or overcome mental resistance.



The source uses the case as part of the fairness framework governing police interviews. The principle drawn from it is that caution alone does not legitimise all forms of questioning. Interrogation that operates like adversarial cross-examination may itself make the resulting admissions unfairly obtained.



Authority — Visible police ridicule in a recorded interview



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Pritchard \[1991] 1 VR 84] \[TOPIC: unfair prejudice where questioning conveys police ridicule or disbelief] \[CONCEPTS: videotaped interview, ridicule, derision, police disbelief, jury prejudice, form of questions, interview fairness, improper opinion]

R v Pritchard is cited in the source for the proposition that a videotaped interview may unfairly communicate police incredulity and ridicule to the jury. The source records that the harm lay in the viewer seeing, through the form of the questions, the interviewer’s disbelief in the accused’s answers.



The authority is used to support the broader principle that police must not smuggle inadmissible opinion evidence into trial through the style of questioning preserved on tape. On the source’s presentation, the unfairness arises because the jury receives the force of official disbelief in a way that would not be permitted through ordinary testimony.



Whole-of-interview rule where admissions are tendered



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 59, 81(2)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 59, 81(2)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: prosecution must tender the whole interview if relying on admissions] \[CONCEPTS: mixed statement, whole interview, good with the bad, hearsay exception, video interview, exculpatory material, prosecution fairness, tender entire ROI, defendant account]

The source states that a defendant’s video interview is hearsay because it contains prior representations adduced to prove facts asserted, but that if the interview contains an admission it may be admissible under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 81(2). The source then identifies a fairness obligation: once the prosecution relies on admissions in the interview, it must tender the whole interview, including exculpatory parts.



The doctrinal basis recorded is that the prosecution cannot isolate inculpatory answers and suppress the remainder. The source frames this as tendering “the good with the bad”. That principle matters especially where the interview is the only source of the defendant’s version. The source suggests that if there is no admission at all, the defendant may have difficulty relying on the interview because of hearsay, but once any admission appears, the whole recorded interview becomes arguable evidence.



The source therefore treats the record of interview as a double-edged evidentiary item: the prosecution may use admissions, but must accept that exculpatory assertions contained in the same interview also enter the evidentiary field.



Authority — Whole interview must be tendered if relying on admissions



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: SPINKS v L \[2020] TASMC 5] \[TOPIC: prosecution must tender the whole interview once admissions are relied upon] \[CONCEPTS: hearsay, s 59, s 81(2), whole interview, good with the bad, prosecution fairness, exculpatory statements, mixed statement]

SPINKS v L is cited in the source for the proposition that a recorded interview containing admissions is hearsay but is admissible under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 81(2), and that the prosecution must tender the whole interview rather than isolated admissions.



The source uses the case to articulate a fairness rule: once the Crown relies on confessional portions of a mixed statement, it must also put the exculpatory portions before the court. The authority is therefore central to the source’s treatment of the recorded interview as an indivisible evidentiary item once admissions are invoked.



Authority — “The good with the bad” in mixed statements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Soma \[2003] HCA 13; (2003) 212 CLR 299] \[TOPIC: fairness requires tender of the whole mixed statement] \[CONCEPTS: mixed statement, good with the bad, confessional parts, exculpatory parts, prosecution obligation, fairness, full context, admissions]

R v Soma is cited in the source through SPINKS v L for the proposition that fairness requires the prosecution to tender the whole interview — “the good with the bad” — when relying on admissions. The authority supports the indivisible treatment of a mixed statement containing both inculpatory and exculpatory material.



Its doctrinal role in the source is to reinforce that confessional value cannot be separated from exculpatory context where both are part of the same admissible statement.



Authority — Exculpatory ROI given little weight if not sworn or tested



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Police v Quinn \[2019] TASMC 4] \[TOPIC: exculpatory interview statements may receive little weight if unsworn and untested] \[CONCEPTS: weight, unsworn account, untested by cross-examination, defendant silence in court, no adverse inference, exculpatory statement, police preferred, little weight]

Police v Quinn is cited in the source to distinguish admissibility from weight.
