The source records that no adverse inference may be drawn from a defendant not testifying at trial, but that exculpatory statements made in an interview may nonetheless be given little weight where they conflict with sworn police evidence and have not been tested by cross-examination.



The authority therefore qualifies the benefit of the whole-of-interview rule. Exculpatory material may come into evidence, but its persuasive force remains a separate evaluative question.



Recorded interview must be played in full, not replaced by transcript



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: recorded interview cannot be substituted by transcript alone] \[CONCEPTS: play the video, transcript not evidence, full recording, recorded interview, mixed statement, error of law, whole interview, best evidence]

The source states that where a recorded interview is relied upon, it must be played in full. It says a transcript cannot be used as a substitute for the recording because the transcript is not itself evidence. Failure to play the interview is identified in the source as error.



The practical importance of the rule is that the fact-finder must receive the evidence in its original evidentiary form, including tone, sequence, and context. That aligns with the source’s broader insistence that interviews containing admissions and exculpatory assertions be treated as a whole rather than selectively reduced to text.



Authority — Transcript cannot replace the recording



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Baker v Barratt \[2019] TASSC 28] \[TOPIC: error to substitute transcript for the recorded interview] \[CONCEPTS: transcript not evidence, play full interview, recording required, evidentiary form, whole ROI, error on appeal, best evidence, video interview]

Baker v Barratt is cited in the source at \[28] for the proposition that part or all of a recorded interview cannot be substituted with a transcript because the transcript is not evidence. The source treats failure to play the actual interview as error.



The authority supports the proposition that a recorded interview must be received in its proper evidentiary form, preserving the whole content and its forensic context.



Authority — Mixed statements: admissions and exculpatory material both become evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v McCarrol \[2004] WASCA 131] \[TOPIC: prosecution tender of a mixed statement makes exculpatory material available as evidence] \[CONCEPTS: mixed statement, admissions, exculpatory material, jury consideration, unequal weight, whole statement, confessional qualities, prosecution tender]

R v McCarrol is cited in the source for the settled rule concerning mixed statements. Where the prosecution tenders a statement because of its confessional qualities, it must accept that the exculpatory material also becomes evidence for jury consideration.



The source also records an important qualification: admissions and exculpatory material need not be accorded equal weight. The authority therefore supports both the indivisible admission of the whole statement and the evaluative freedom of the fact-finder.



Authority — Exculpatory assertions may be relied upon by the defendant



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Jotheeswaran v Barnes \[2019] TASSC 42] \[TOPIC: exculpatory parts of a prosecution-relied interview may assist the defence] \[CONCEPTS: exculpatory assertions, favourable evidence, interview, statement relied on by prosecution, defendant benefit, mixed statement, admissions, defence use]

Jotheeswaran v Barnes is cited in the source for the proposition that when the prosecution relies on admissions in an interview or statement, the defendant may rely on exculpatory assertions in the same material as evidence favourable to the defence.



The authority is used to reinforce the double-edged nature of mixed statements: they do not become exclusively Crown evidence merely because the prosecution tendered them.



Authority — Early common law support for mixed statement doctrine



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Higgins (1829) 3 C \& P 603; 172 ER 565] \[TOPIC: exculpatory parts of an admitted statement travel with inculpatory parts] \[CONCEPTS: mixed statement doctrine, common law, whole statement, exculpatory material, inculpatory material, admissions, defence reliance, historical authority]

R v Higgins is cited in the source as an early common law authority for the principle that where a statement containing admissions is received, the exculpatory parts are also available for consideration. Its role in the source is confirmatory rather than elaborative.



Authority — Australian support for mixed statement doctrine



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Jack v Smail (1905) 2 CLR 684] \[TOPIC: whole statement principle in Australian authority] \[CONCEPTS: whole statement, exculpatory part, inculpatory part, defence use, admissions, common law, evidentiary balance, mixed statement]

Jack v Smail is cited in the source alongside R v Higgins as authority for the rule that exculpatory matter in a statement tendered for admissions may be relied on by the accused. The source uses it as part of the doctrinal lineage of the mixed statement principle.



Recent Complaint and Prior Inconsistent Statements

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66 — Recent complaint evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 66] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: first-hand complaint evidence and the distinction between spontaneous complaint and court-oriented statement] \[CONCEPTS: recent complaint, first complaint, spontaneous report, police at scene, neighbour complaint, formal statement, s 66(2), s 66(3), truth of facts asserted, hearsay exception]

The source states that recent complaint evidence may be admissible under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66(2) where a complainant tells another person what happened, provided the statutory preconditions are met. The source contrasts such spontaneous or scene-based reports with statements made for the purpose of indicating what will later be said in court, which are excluded by s 66(3).



The practical distinction recorded is between informal or immediate accounts, such as complaints to a neighbour or to police at the scene, and a formal police statement made with litigation in view. On the source’s approach, the former may come in as evidence of the truth of the facts asserted, while the latter may be excluded from that hearsay use if the court infers the complainant was outlining future court evidence.



The source also notes that third-party evidence of the complainant’s distress and account may be relevant because it tends to corroborate the complainant’s assertions. The rule, as presented, turns on purpose, context, and timing rather than any rigid “recent complaint” label.



Authority — Distinguishing spontaneous account from statement for court



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Marysej v McCullough \[2018] TASSC] \[TOPIC: complaint to neighbour or police at the scene may be admitted under s 66(2) if not made for court purposes] \[CONCEPTS: spontaneous complaint, police at scene, neighbour evidence, purpose of statement, s 66(2), s 66(3), truth of facts asserted, recent complaint] \[REVIEW]

Marysej v McCullough is cited in the source as an example of the distinction between a spontaneous complaint and a statement made for court purposes. The source records that evidence of the complainant’s account to another person, and to a police officer trying to find out what happened at the scene, could be used as evidence of the truth of the facts asserted.



The source treats the decisive issue as whether the complainant was explaining events as they unfolded, rather than outlining evidence for future proceedings. The neutral citation is incomplete in the source and is therefore marked for review.



Authority — Complaint evidence admissible for truth under s 66(2)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Papakosmas v The Queen \[1999] HCA 37; 196 CLR 297] \[TOPIC: prior complaint may be admitted for the truth of the facts asserted] \[CONCEPTS: complaint evidence, hearsay exception, truth of content, first-hand representation, complainant account, corroborative force, s 66, prior complaint]

Papakosmas v The Queen is cited in the source for the proposition that a complainant’s out-of-court account may be admitted as evidence of the truth of what was asserted under the statutory hearsay exception. The authority is used to support the admissibility of third-party evidence recounting the complainant’s description of the defendant’s conduct.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 60 — Prior inconsistent statements and hearsay use



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 60] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 60] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: prior inconsistent statement admitted for credit may also prove truth of content] \[CONCEPTS: prior inconsistent statement, credit use, hearsay use, s 60, truth of facts asserted, prosecution can adduce document, complainant inconsistency, statement becomes substantive evidence]

The source states that Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66(3) does not restrict prior inconsistent statements from being used for a different evidentiary pathway. It records that once a document is admissible as a prior inconsistent statement, the prosecution may adduce it not only for credit but also, by operation of Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 60, as evidence of the truth of the matters asserted.



The practical rule in the source is therefore that admissibility for one non-hearsay purpose can open the door to hearsay use. That substantially changes the evidentiary effect of the document. On the source’s account, the document is not confined to impeachment; it may become substantive proof of its contents.



Authority — Prior inconsistent statement can become proof of its contents



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Neill-Fraser v Tasmania (No 2) \[2019] TASSC 11] \[TOPIC: once admissible as a prior inconsistent statement, the document may be used for the truth of its contents via s 60] \[CONCEPTS: s 60, prior inconsistent statement, substantive evidence, truth of content, credit and hearsay, complainant statement, prosecution use, Brett J]

Neill-Fraser v Tasmania (No 2) is cited in the source for the proposition that once a prior representation is admissible as a prior inconsistent statement, it is also admissible by operation of Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 60 as proof of the truth of its content. The authority is used to support the source’s view that such material is not confined to credit purposes.



Tendency and Coincidence Evidence

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 98, 100, 101 — Coincidence evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 98, 100, 101] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 98, 100, 101] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: admissibility framework for coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, similarities, events, circumstances, significant probative value, notice requirement, prejudice balancing, improbability of coincidence, state of mind, particular act]

The source describes coincidence evidence as depending on evidence capable of establishing two or more events and capable of establishing similarities either in the events, in the circumstances of the events, or both. It then records a structured admissibility process under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 98, 100 and 101.



The steps recorded in the source are:



Identify the particular act or state of mind sought to be proved.



Identify the two or more events from which that act or state of mind is to be inferred.



Identify the similarities in the events or their circumstances said to make coincidence improbable.



Determine whether reasonable notice has been given, or whether dispensation should be granted under s 100(2).



Evaluate whether the evidence has significant probative value.



In a criminal case, determine whether the probative value substantially outweighs prejudicial effect under s 101(2).



The source therefore presents coincidence evidence as a disciplined inferential process, not a mere intuition that repeated allegations point to guilt.



Authority — Structured six-step approach to coincidence evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Tasmania v Harris \[2016] TASSC 47] \[TOPIC: adoption of a structured approach to coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, structured analysis, s 98, significant probative value, notice, prejudice balancing, similarities, improbability]

Tasmania v Harris is cited in the source as the Tasmanian authority adopting a structured approach to coincidence evidence. The case is used to introduce the framework taken from R v Gale; R v Duckworth and to emphasise that coincidence reasoning requires identified events, identified similarities, notice, significant probative value, and the statutory prejudice balancing.



Authority — Factual underpinnings and steps for coincidence evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Gale; R v Duckworth \[2012] NSWCCA 174; 217 A Crim R 487] \[TOPIC: detailed methodology for deciding coincidence evidence questions] \[CONCEPTS: two or more events, similarities, circumstances, particular act, state of mind, notice, significant probative value, step-by-step admissibility]

R v Gale; R v Duckworth is reproduced in the source as the principal formulation of the factual underpinnings and sequential steps for deciding whether evidence is admissible as coincidence evidence. Its doctrinal significance in the source is foundational: it supplies the full analytical template later applied in Tasmanian authority.



Authority — Balancing probative value and prejudicial effect



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v RN \[2005] NSWCCA 413] \[TOPIC: sixth step requires analysis of both probative value and prejudicial effect] \[CONCEPTS: balancing exercise, prejudice, probative value, coincidence evidence, unfair use, criminal proceeding, s 101(2), admissibility]

R v RN is cited in the source for the proposition that the final coincidence evidence step requires analysis both of the evidence’s probative value and of the prejudicial effect it may have, followed by a balancing of the two. The authority is used to sharpen the statutory comparison required by Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101(2).



Coincidence evidence — multiple complainants and rebutting concoction or mistake



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: coincidence reasoning that similar allegations by multiple complainants are unlikely to be coincidence] \[CONCEPTS: multiple complainants, concoction, mistake, sexual assault allegations, similar conduct, same context, same age group, improbability of coincidence, cross-admissibility, church connection]

The source identifies a specific use of coincidence evidence in cases involving multiple complainants. The proposition recorded is that, having regard to similarities in the events and the circumstances in which they are alleged to have occurred, it may be improbable that two complainants have independently concocted or mistakenly reported the same kind of conduct.



The source lists examples of similarities capable of supporting that inference, including the children’s ages, the defendant’s connection with them through the same church context, the sexual nature of each assault, the brief but deliberate application of force, the fact that each assault allegedly occurred during a game initiated by the defendant, and the fact that the allegations emerged within two days of each other.



The rule recorded is not that multiple complaints automatically prove one another. Rather, identified similarities may supply the significant probative force needed to rebut coincidence, mistake, or fabricated parallel accounts.



Authority — Similar allegations by two children may be too alike to be coincidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Biddle v Smith \[2012] TASMC 24] \[TOPIC: similarity between two complainants can make concoction or mistake improbable] \[CONCEPTS: multiple complainants, children, sexual assault, same game, same context, church connection, improbability of coincidence, significant probative value]

Biddle v Smith is cited in the source for the proposition that evidence from one complainant may have significant probative value in relation to another where the allegations share marked similarities. The authority is used to show how coincidence reasoning can operate to make it unlikely that two child complainants independently made up or were mistaken about similar assaults.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 97, 101, 135, 137 — Tendency evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: ss 97, 101, 135, 137] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 97, 101, 135, 137] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: tendency evidence as circumstantial reasoning and its admissibility requirements] \[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, circumstantial evidence, stepping stone reasoning, disposition, sexual interest, significant probative value, prejudice, unfair prejudice, cross-admissibility, state of mind]

The source describes tendency evidence as a species of circumstantial evidence. It records “stepping stone” reasoning:



On another occasion, the defendant acted in a particular way or had a particular state of mind.



From that, a tendency to act that way or hold that state of mind may be inferred.



Because of that tendency, it may be inferred that the defendant acted in conformity with it on the charged occasion.



The source then presents four admissibility steps:



Properly characterise the evidence as tendency evidence.



Determine whether it has significant probative value under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97(1)(b).



Determine whether its probative value outweighs prejudicial effect under s 101(2).



Consider the separate duty under s 137 to exclude if probative value is outweighed by danger of unfair prejudice, with ss 135 and 137 reflecting the same broad concern about misuse by the fact-finder.



The source emphasises that in a multiple complainant sexual offence case, linked features across allegations may provide the necessary probative force.



Authority — Tendency evidence as circumstantial “stepping stone” reasoning



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Police v FRS \[2020] TASMC 9] \[TOPIC: tendency evidence must be properly characterised and meet significant probative value and prejudice requirements] \[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, circumstantial evidence, stepping stone reasoning, significant probative value, sexual interest in nieces, cross-admissibility, s 97, s 101]

Police v FRS is cited in the source as the main articulation of tendency evidence. It is used to explain that tendency evidence is indirect evidence which supports an inference from prior acts or states of mind to conduct on the charged occasion. The source also extracts from the case the four-step admissibility analysis: proper characterisation, significant probative value, balancing under s 101(2), and consideration of unfair prejudice under s 137.



Authority — Threshold analysis for admissibility of tendency evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Pattison v Tasmania] \[TOPIC: four-step threshold analysis for tendency evidence] \[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, threshold requirements, proper characterisation, significant probative value, admissibility steps, linked complaints, disposition, sexual misconduct] \[REVIEW]

Pattison v Tasmania is referred to in the source as authority for the proposition that admissibility of tendency evidence involves four steps, beginning with proper characterisation of the evidence and moving through significant probative value and prejudice analysis. The source does not provide a full citation, so the authority is marked for review.



Authority — Linked features needed in multiple complainant sexual cases



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: The Queen v Bauer] \[TOPIC: tendency evidence usually requires some feature linking allegations across complainants] \[CONCEPTS: multiple complainants, linked features, sexual offences, significant probative value, probability reasoning, tendency evidence, common features, admissibility] \[REVIEW]

The Queen v Bauer is cited in the source for the proposition that in a multiple complainant sexual offence case, evidence of offending against one complainant will ordinarily be significantly probative of offending against another only if there is some feature linking the allegations together. The source does not provide a full citation, so the authority is marked for review.



Authority — Probative value requires capacity to prove the tendency



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Brown v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 4] \[TOPIC: assessment of tendency evidence asks how far it is capable of proving the tendency] \[CONCEPTS: probative value, prove the tendency, tendency reasoning, other evidence, DNA support, appellate guidance, sexual offence context, admissibility]

Brown v Tasmania is cited in the source for the proposition that assessment of the probative value of tendency evidence requires determining the extent to which the evidence is capable of proving the tendency. The source also notes that this may be evaluated together with other evidence, such as supporting DNA evidence.



Authority — High Court statement on probative value of tendency evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: McPhillamy v The Queen \[2018] HCA 52; 361 ALR 13] \[TOPIC: probative value assessment concerns the tendency the evidence is capable of proving] \[CONCEPTS: probative value, tendency evidence, capability to prove tendency, High Court, admissibility threshold, inferential force, evidence evaluation, tendency proof]

McPhillamy v The Queen is cited in the source through Brown v Tasmania for the proposition that the court assessing tendency evidence must determine the extent to which the evidence is capable of proving the alleged tendency. The authority provides the conceptual measure of “probative value” used in the source.



Authority — Subsequent conduct can still be tendency evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: RH v The Queen \[2014] NSWCCA 71; 241 A Crim R 1] \[TOPIC: subsequent events may still have strong probative value as tendency evidence] \[CONCEPTS: later conduct, future behaviour, tendency evidence, post-dating events, probative value not reduced, subsequent acts, admissibility, temporal sequence]

RH v The Queen is cited in the source, via Brown v Tasmania, for the proposition that the fact tendency evidence post-dates the charged conduct does not necessarily reduce its probative value. The source therefore treats later conduct as potentially admissible tendency material if it remains logically capable of proving the alleged tendency.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) — Tendency and Coincidence Evidence Framework



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) — Tendency/Coincidence: Statutory Framework]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: statutory exclusion and admission of tendency and coincidence evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, coincidence evidence, similar fact evidence, exclusionary rule, Uniform Evidence Law, admissibility test, circumstantial reasoning, probative value]



Part 6 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) establishes a statutory framework governing the admissibility of tendency and coincidence evidence in criminal proceedings. These forms of evidence are generally excluded because they risk unfair prejudice by inviting reasoning that an accused acted in accordance with a propensity or because multiple events appear similar.



Section 95 provides that evidence which could be used for tendency or coincidence reasoning is not admissible to prove those purposes unless it satisfies the statutory requirements contained in the Act. Sections 97 and 98 regulate the admission of tendency and coincidence evidence respectively, while s 101 imposes an additional balancing requirement in criminal proceedings.



The statutory regime therefore creates a rule of exclusion subject to defined exceptions. Evidence which suggests a person has a particular tendency, or that similar events occurred in circumstances suggesting a connection, will not be admissible unless the court is satisfied that the evidence possesses sufficient probative value and passes the statutory balancing test.



Importantly, the statutory regime only applies where the evidence is led for a tendency or coincidence purpose. If evidence is relevant for another purpose—such as identification, opportunity, motive, or part of the res gestae—the statutory exclusion in Part 6 does not apply. In those circumstances admissibility will instead be determined under the general exclusionary discretions contained in ss 135 and 137 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas).



The statutory framework therefore requires courts to determine both the purpose for which the evidence is led and whether the specific admissibility requirements under ss 97, 98 and 101 are satisfied.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 — Admissibility of Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 97]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 — Tendency Evidence: Admissibility Test]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: statutory admissibility test for tendency evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, significant probative value, admissibility threshold, character evidence, propensity reasoning, criminal trials, Uniform Evidence Law]



Section 97 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) governs the admissibility of tendency evidence. Tendency evidence refers to evidence that a person has a tendency to act in a particular way or to have a particular state of mind. The prosecution commonly relies on such evidence to invite an inference that, because a person has that tendency, they are more likely to have acted in that way on the occasion relevant to the charged offence.



Under s 97(1)(b), tendency evidence is admissible only if the court determines that the evidence, either by itself or having regard to other evidence in the proceeding, has significant probative value.



The requirement of significant probative value means that the evidence must do more than merely demonstrate relevance. It must be capable of materially advancing proof of a fact in issue. Courts therefore assess the evidential force of the proposed tendency evidence in the context of the case as a whole.



The evaluation involves determining whether the alleged tendency is sufficiently supported by the evidence and whether that tendency logically assists in proving an element of the offence or another fact in dispute.



Where the prosecution seeks to rely upon such evidence in criminal proceedings, s 101 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) imposes an additional safeguard requiring that the probative value of the evidence substantially outweigh any prejudicial effect it may have on the accused.



The combined operation of ss 97 and 101 means that tendency evidence is admissible only where its evidential significance is sufficiently strong to justify the risks associated with propensity reasoning.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 — Admissibility of Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 98]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 — Coincidence Evidence: Similar Events]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: coincidence reasoning and similar events]

\[CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, similar events, pattern evidence, identification reasoning, improbability of coincidence, similar fact reasoning]



Section 98 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) regulates the admissibility of coincidence evidence. Coincidence evidence does not concern a person’s tendency but instead focuses on the similarity between two or more events.



The prosecution may rely on coincidence reasoning where it contends that the similarities between events make it improbable that they occurred independently or by chance. If the events are sufficiently similar, a jury may infer that the same person was responsible for each event.



For coincidence evidence to be admissible, the court must determine that the evidence has significant probative value within the meaning of s 98(1)(b). In assessing whether that threshold is met, courts examine the similarities between the events and consider the capacity of those similarities to affect a fact in issue.



Relevant factors in assessing probative value include:



The degree of similarity between the events.



The number of similarities identified.



The timing of the similarities.



Whether the similarities, individually or collectively, affect a fact in issue.



The probative force of coincidence evidence often increases where the conduct is unusual or rare, because similarities in unusual conduct reduce the likelihood that the events occurred independently.



Coincidence reasoning may also be strengthened where other evidence connects the accused to multiple events. In such cases the combined effect of the evidence may make it improbable that anyone other than the accused committed the relevant acts.



Distinction Between Tendency and Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: distinction between tendency and coincidence evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency reasoning, coincidence reasoning, propensity evidence, similar fact evidence, person versus event distinction, circumstantial evidence]



A fundamental distinction exists between tendency evidence and coincidence evidence within the Uniform Evidence Law.



Tendency evidence concerns the conduct or mental state of a person. It is evidence led to establish that a person has a tendency to act in a particular way or possess a particular state of mind. The reasoning process invites the jury to infer that because the person has that tendency, they are more likely to have acted consistently with it in the circumstances of the charged offence.



Coincidence evidence concerns events rather than persons. It arises where the prosecution relies on the similarity between two or more events to argue that they are unlikely to have occurred independently. The inference sought is that the same individual was responsible for the events because the similarities make coincidence improbable.



Although conceptually distinct, the two forms of evidence frequently overlap in practice. Evidence of similar conduct may simultaneously demonstrate a tendency on the part of an accused and also support coincidence reasoning by revealing a pattern of events.



For this reason courts often analyse the evidence under both statutory provisions where appropriate. The key distinction remains the focus of the reasoning process: tendency reasoning focuses on a person’s propensity, whereas coincidence reasoning focuses on the improbability that similar events occurred independently.



Authority — Tendency Evidence Need Not Be Identical to Alleged Offending



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Brown v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 4]

\[TOPIC: similarity requirement in tendency reasoning]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, similarity requirement, linking features, circumstantial evidence, strands in a cable reasoning]



Brown v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 4 confirms that tendency evidence does not need to be identical or strikingly similar to the alleged offending in order to possess significant probative value.



The Court of Criminal Appeal explained that while striking similarity may strengthen probative value, the law does not require the conduct relied upon to closely mirror the charged offence. What is required is the presence of some feature or features linking the tendency evidence with the alleged offending.



In Brown, the prosecution alleged that the accused had a tendency to impose himself sexually upon women who were in vulnerable positions, particularly while asleep in bed. The defence argued that the prior conduct relied upon to establish the tendency was insufficiently similar to the charged offence.



The Court rejected that argument. It held that the crucial aspect of the reasoning was the accused’s tendency to impose himself sexually on women without seeking or obtaining consent. Although certain contextual factors differed between incidents, the central feature of sexual imposition provided the necessary link.



The Court emphasised that tendency evidence often operates as one strand within a broader circumstantial case. When considered together with other evidence—such as DNA evidence, evidence of flight, or false alibis—the tendency evidence strengthened the inference that the accused committed the charged offence.



The decision illustrates that tendency evidence functions as part of a cumulative evidential structure rather than as a standalone proof of guilt.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101 — Probative Value Must Substantially Outweigh Prejudice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 101]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101 — Balancing Probative Value vs Prejudice]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: balancing probative value against prejudicial effect]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, balancing test, criminal trials, probative value, jury misuse risk, evidentiary safeguards]



Section 101 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) imposes an additional admissibility requirement for tendency and coincidence evidence in criminal proceedings. Even if such evidence satisfies the “significant probative value” threshold in ss 97 or 98, it is not admissible unless the court concludes that its probative value substantially outweighs any prejudicial effect it may have on the accused.



The requirement that probative value “substantially outweigh” prejudice establishes a stringent safeguard against the risk that juries will misuse propensity evidence. The term “substantial” has been interpreted to mean large or weighty, indicating that the probative force of the evidence must significantly exceed its prejudicial potential.



Evidence is not considered prejudicial merely because it strengthens the prosecution case. Instead, prejudice arises where there is a risk that the jury will give the evidence greater weight than it deserves, reason unfairly from the evidence, or allow emotion rather than rational analysis to influence the verdict.



Courts therefore consider whether the evidence might encourage reasoning such as: if the accused committed similar acts previously, they must have committed the charged offence. That form of reasoning may be impermissible unless properly constrained by judicial directions.



The balancing exercise requires the court to weigh the evidential importance of the tendency or coincidence evidence against the risk that it may distort the jury’s evaluation of the evidence.



Non-Tendency Use of Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) — Admissibility for Non-Tendency Purposes]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of evidence for non-tendency purposes]

\[CONCEPTS: non-tendency evidence, alternative relevance, motive evidence, opportunity evidence, relationship evidence, res gestae]



The exclusionary provisions governing tendency and coincidence evidence apply only where the evidence is led for those purposes. If evidence is introduced for a different evidentiary purpose, the statutory restrictions in Part 6 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) do not apply.



Courts must therefore examine the purpose for which the evidence is tendered. This assessment ensures that evidence is not improperly admitted under the guise of a non-tendency purpose when in reality it is intended to support propensity reasoning.



Examples of legitimate non-tendency purposes include:



Evidence establishing opportunity to commit the offence.



Evidence relevant to identification of the accused.



Evidence demonstrating motive.



Evidence forming part of the res gestae.



Relationship evidence describing the nature of interactions between the accused and the complainant.



Where evidence is admitted for such purposes, the prosecution is not required to satisfy the admissibility thresholds contained in ss 97, 98 or 101. However, the evidence must still comply with the general exclusionary provisions contained in ss 135 and 137 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas).



If evidence admitted for a non-tendency purpose could potentially be misused by the jury as propensity evidence, the trial judge must direct the jury that it cannot rely upon the evidence for tendency or coincidence reasoning.



The court therefore bears responsibility for carefully analysing the true purpose of the evidence and controlling the manner in which the jury may use it.



Probative Value of Tendency Evidence — Method of Assessment



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: method for assessing probative value of tendency evidence] \[CONCEPTS: probative value, tendency evidence, evidence taken at its highest, assumed acceptance, favourable inference, fact in issue, rational effect on probability, credibility ignored, reliability ignored] 



block\_025



The probative value of tendency evidence is assessed by asking whether, taken at its highest, the evidence could rationally affect the probability of a fact in issue. In applying that test, the court must assume that the evidence will be accepted and that any inference reasonably open to the jury and favourable to the Crown will be drawn. The evidence is therefore evaluated on the footing most favourable to its admissibility, not by asking whether the trial judge personally finds it persuasive. 



block\_025



This approach means that questions of witness credibility or reliability are not part of the probative value assessment at the admissibility stage. The issue is not whether the witness is believable, but whether the evidence, if accepted, could logically and materially assist in proving a disputed fact. The source text states that the evidence “must be taken at its highest” and that credibility or reliability is irrelevant to that inquiry. Authorities cited for that proposition include Tasmania v L \[2013] TASSC 47, Tasmania v W (No 2) \[2012] TASSC 48, Tasmania v Finnegan (No 2) \[2012] TASSC 1, KMJ v Tasmania (2011) 218 A Crim R 87, DSR v R (2012) 215 A Crim R 349, R v Shamouil (2006) 66 NSWLR 112, R v Sood \[2007] NSWCCA 214, and R v Bauer \[2018] HCA 40. 



block\_025



This rule is important because it separates admissibility from ultimate fact-finding. The jury later decides whether the evidence should be accepted. At the admissibility stage, the court asks only whether the evidence is capable of having real logical force on an issue in dispute. 



block\_025



Authority — Significant Probative Value Requires More Than Mere Relevance



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: IMM v R \[2016] HCA 14] \[TOPIC: significant probative value in tendency reasoning] \[CONCEPTS: significant probative value, more than relevance, fact finding, influential evidence, disputed facts, admissibility threshold, tendency evidence] 



block\_025



IMM v R \[2016] HCA 14 is cited in the source for the proposition that tendency or coincidence evidence has significant probative value when it is potentially influential in fact-finding or potentially important in establishing one or more disputed facts. The concept therefore requires more than bare logical relevance. The evidence must matter in a practical evidentiary sense. 



block\_025



The source text places IMM v R alongside other authorities explaining that significant probative value does not require a “substantial degree of relevance”, but it does require that the evidence be important or of consequence in the case. The governing idea is whether the evidence could meaningfully assist the tribunal of fact on a real issue in dispute. 



block\_025



This authority supports the statutory threshold in Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 97 and 98 by explaining the quality of probative force required before tendency or coincidence reasoning may be left to a jury. The case is also used in the source for the proposition that the assessment must occur in the context of the other evidence and the facts in issue, rather than by viewing the evidence in isolation. 



block\_025



Authority — Two-Step Assessment of Tendency Probative Value



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Hughes v R \[2017] HCA 20] \[TOPIC: two-step assessment of tendency evidence] \[CONCEPTS: alleged tendency, support for tendency, support for offence elements, interrelated questions, significant probative value, contextual assessment, sexual offence tendency] 



block\_025



Hughes v R \[2017] HCA 20 is cited in the source for the proposition that assessing whether tendency evidence has significant probative value involves two interrelated but distinct inquiries. First, taking the evidence at its highest, the court asks whether the evidence supports the alleged tendency and, if so, to what extent. Secondly, the court asks to what extent that tendency, once supported, assists in proving an element of the charged offence or another disputed fact. 



block\_025



This structure is important because it prevents tendency reasoning from becoming overly abstract. It is not enough that the evidence suggests a general disposition. The identified tendency must also materially assist with a matter actually in issue in the prosecution. The source also uses Hughes v R for the propositions that tendency evidence need not be strikingly similar to the charged offending, and that the assessment of probative value must occur in the context of the whole evidentiary record. 



block\_025



The authority therefore supports a disciplined approach: identify the tendency precisely, assess the strength of the evidence supporting it, and then determine whether that tendency advances proof of the offence in a significant way. 



block\_025



Coincidence Evidence — Factors Relevant to Significant Probative Value



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 98] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: factors relevant to probative value in coincidence reasoning] \[CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, similarity, timing, number of similarities, fact in issue, identification, rare crime, pattern of events, improbability of chance] 



block\_025



Coincidence evidence under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 is assessed by examining whether the similarities between events give the evidence significant probative value. The source text identifies four relevant considerations drawn from Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528. Those considerations are:



The degree of similarity.



The number of similarities.



The timing of the similarities.



The capacity of each similarity, either individually or in combination with the others or with other evidence, to affect a fact in issue. 



block\_025



The source further explains that the more unusual or rare the conduct, the fewer similarities may be needed before the evidence acquires significant probative force. That is because rarity makes it harder to dismiss the events as coincidental. The assessment is not mechanical. Similarities are not counted in isolation. Their force depends on the character of the case, the issues in dispute, and the other evidence linking the accused to the events. 



block\_025



The source also states that, in many cases, significant probative value may derive not only from similarities between crimes but from other evidence that intrinsically connects the accused to each event. On that approach, the ultimate question is whether the evidence is cogent enough to be admitted as proof of identification or another disputed fact. 



block\_025



Authority — Holistic Similarity Analysis in Coincidence Reasoning



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528] \[TOPIC: holistic assessment of similarities in coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: coincidence reasoning, similarity analysis, rarity, whole of the evidence, not isolation, fact in issue, identification reasoning] 



block\_025



Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528 is cited in the source for two related propositions. First, coincidence reasoning requires an assessment of the degree, number, and timing of similarities and their capacity to affect a fact in issue. Secondly, those similarities should not be considered in isolation, but as a whole and in the context of the entire case. 



block\_025



The authority therefore supports a holistic method. Even where no single similarity is decisive, the combined effect of multiple features may render coincidence reasoning probative. Conversely, isolated points of resemblance may be weak if they are common or lacking in connection to a disputed issue. The source uses Sutton v R to reinforce that the force of similarity reasoning depends on the overall evidentiary context, especially where identity is disputed. 



block\_025



This principle is important in Tasmanian criminal practice because it prevents an overly compartmentalised analysis. Coincidence evidence is evaluated by reference to the total pattern disclosed by the evidence, not by dissecting each similarity as though it stood alone. 



block\_025



Concoction and Contamination



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 101(5)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101(5)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: treatment of collusion, concoction and contamination] \[CONCEPTS: concoction, contamination, collusion, suggestion, probative force, common law rule, statutory exclusion, witness coordination] 



block\_025



At common law, tendency and coincidence evidence could lose probative force if there was a real chance that the evidence had been concocted or contaminated. The source cites Hoch v R (1988) 165 CLR 292 for that proposition. The concern was that apparent similarity between accounts or incidents might not reflect true independent probative force, but instead arise from collusion, influence, or suggestion. 



block\_025



Under the statutory regime, however, the source states that the probability that tendency or coincidence evidence may be the result of collusion, concoction or suggestion is not to be taken into account when determining either its probative value or its prejudicial effect. That rule is attributed to Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101(5), with Bauer v R cited as authority. 



block\_025



The doctrinal consequence is that the statutory scheme departs from the former common law approach. Questions of possible concoction or contamination do not reduce the assessed probative value of the evidence for admissibility purposes under the Act. Those matters may still bear upon the way the evidence is challenged and evaluated at trial, but they do not form part of the statutory balancing exercise described in the source. 



block\_025



Authority — Common Law Loss of Probative Force Through Concoction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Hoch v R (1988) 165 CLR 292] \[TOPIC: concoction and contamination at common law] \[CONCEPTS: common law similar fact evidence, concoction, contamination, collusion, lost probative force, independent accounts, admissibility risk] 



block\_025



Hoch v R (1988) 165 CLR 292 is cited in the source for the proposition that, at common law, tendency and coincidence evidence loses probative force where there is a real chance that the evidence was concocted or contaminated. The underlying rationale is that the apparent force of similar accounts depends upon their independence. If the accounts may have been influenced by each other or jointly fabricated, the reasoning process loses reliability. 



block\_025



The source contrasts this common law position with the later statutory rule under s 101(5), which excludes consideration of collusion, concoction and suggestion when assessing probative value or prejudicial effect. The authority is therefore important as a statement of the prior common law approach and as a point of contrast with the Uniform Evidence Law. 



block\_025



Witness Similarity Evidence and Independent Accounts



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: legal doctrine] \[TOPIC: similar accounts from independent witnesses as probative evidence] \[CONCEPTS: independent witnesses, similar accounts, concoction, contamination, coincidence reasoning, tendency reasoning, credibility limits, probative force] 



block\_025



The source identifies a controversial doctrinal area concerning similarities in the accounts of two or more witnesses about the conduct of an accused. It states that, more controversially, such similarities may make it improbable that the witnesses are lying in the absence of concoction or contamination. Authorities cited in support include OPP v Boardman \[1975] AC 421, Hock v R (1988) 165 CLR 292, and WRC (2002) 130 A Crim R 89. 



block\_025



The source also records that in Tasmania v Y \[2007] TASSC 112 Crawford J treated those authorities as common law cases and held that if the evidence is relevant only to credibility, it is not admissible on that basis. Instead, his Honour admitted the evidence as tendency evidence, reasoning that the accused had a tendency to act in a similar manner and that the identical accounts of the complainants added to its probative value. 



block\_025



The doctrinal position emerging from the source is that similarity between independent accounts is not, by itself, a free-standing gateway to admissibility merely because it affects witness credibility. Rather, it may strengthen admissibility where it contributes to legitimate tendency or coincidence reasoning under the statutory regime. 



block\_025



Authority — Independent Witness Similarity Adds Probative Force



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: BBH v R \[2012] HCA 9] \[TOPIC: independent witness evidence and probative force] \[CONCEPTS: independent evidence, similar events, no collusion, coincidence, tendency, witness accounts, special probative force] 



block\_025



BBH v R \[2012] HCA 9 is cited in the source for the proposition that the probative force of evidence may arise in part from the similarity between an event observed by one witness and the acts of which a complainant complained, especially where the accounts are truly independent and there is no suggestion of collusion. The source quotes Crennan and Kiefel JJ as explaining that the coincidence of independent accounts gave the evidence its special probative force. 



block\_025



The source further notes that their Honours endorsed comments in WRC to the effect that, in cases of this kind, the related events need not display the same level of striking similarity required when the events themselves are the sole source of probative value. The doctrinal point is that independent matching accounts can augment the force of the reasoning once the statutory pathway is engaged. 



block\_025



This authority is used in the source to support the conclusion that the events still need to be sufficiently similar to engage the section, but that the fact of independent witnesses giving the same account can significantly increase the probative value of that similarity. 



block\_025



Jury Directions and Unfair Prejudice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 101] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: role of judicial directions in mitigating unfair prejudice] \[CONCEPTS: jury directions, unfair prejudice, limiting directions, tendency evidence, coincidence evidence, misuse risk, jury compliance, inculpatory evidence] 



block\_025



When assessing unfair prejudice under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101, the court must take into account the capacity of judicial directions to reduce the risk that the jury will misuse the evidence. The source states that juries are often required to follow complex legal directions and disregard inadmissible evidence, and that it must be assumed they will follow the trial judge’s directions. Authorities cited for that proposition are Gilbert v R (2000) 201 CLR 414 and R v PWD. 



block\_025



This principle matters because tendency and coincidence evidence is often highly inculpatory. The fact that evidence damages the accused does not itself amount to unfair prejudice. The relevant question is whether the jury may give it more weight than it deserves or use it in an irrational or impermissible way. Proper directions can reduce the risk of substitution reasoning, emotional misuse, or reliance on prior conduct as direct proof of guilt. 



block\_025



The source also cites Bauer for the proposition that prejudice caused by the inculpatory nature of admissible tendency evidence is not necessarily unfair prejudice. If the evidence goes to proof of a fact in issue and clear directions can ensure that the jury does not substitute the tendency evidence for proof of the charged acts, the evidence may still satisfy s 101. 



block\_025



Authority — Jury Directions Must Be Assumed Effective



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Gilbert v R (2000) 201 CLR 414] \[TOPIC: assumption that juries follow judicial directions] \[CONCEPTS: jury compliance, limiting directions, inadmissible evidence, prejudice control, trial directions, evidentiary safeguards] 



block\_025



Gilbert v R (2000) 201 CLR 414 is cited in the source for the proposition that courts must assume juries will follow directions from the trial judge. The authority is used in the context of assessing unfair prejudice arising from tendency or coincidence evidence. 



block\_025



The doctrinal effect of this principle is that the risk of unfair prejudice is not assessed on the pessimistic assumption that juries will disregard judicial instructions. Rather, the court proceeds on the basis that appropriate limiting directions can meaningfully constrain the use the jury makes of the evidence. This assists in determining whether potentially prejudicial evidence nevertheless remains admissible under s 101 because its risks can be properly managed at trial. 



block\_025



Unfair Prejudice — Recognised Forms of Jury Misuse



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 101] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: recognised forms of unfair prejudice in tendency and coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, jury misuse, emotional reasoning, propensity reasoning, prior guilt, overvaluation, impermissible inference, uncharged conduct] 



block\_025



The source explains that evidence is not unfairly prejudicial merely because it supports the Crown case. Unfair prejudice arises where the jury may give the evidence more weight than it deserves, use it in an unfair way, or allow emotion rather than logic to influence its reasoning. Authorities cited include Papakosmas v R 196 CLR 297 and Festa v R 208 CLR 593. 



block\_025



The source provides several examples of recognised prejudice. In R v Watkins \[2005] 153 A Crim R 434, the danger identified was that recognition of the accused’s prior guilt might divert the jury from proper consideration of the evidence on the actual issue in the charges before them. In R v GAG (2007) 178 A Crim R 408, the source describes the danger that the jury might reason no more rationally than that if the accused molested others, he must have done the same to the complainant. In Roach v R 242 CLR 610, the source identifies the risk that similar facts may be treated as establishing guilt where neither logic nor experience requires that conclusion. 



block\_025



The source also notes other forms of prejudice, including the risk that the jury may fail to appreciate that a person with a tendency may not have acted in accordance with it on the occasion in issue, may underestimate how many people share the alleged tendency, or may be distracted by the need for the accused to answer numerous allegations of uncharged conduct stretching over many years. 



block\_025



Cross-Admissibility of Tendency and Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: legal doctrine] \[TOPIC: overlap and cross-admissibility between tendency and coincidence reasoning] \[CONCEPTS: cross-admissibility, tendency evidence, coincidence evidence, overlap, significant probative value, same conduct, similar acts, dual reasoning] 



block\_025



The source states that some evidence may be admissible both as tendency evidence and as coincidence evidence. Although the distinction between the two forms of reasoning must be maintained because the statutory tests differ, the distinction may become less important where the same conduct is relied upon for both purposes. 



block\_025



In that setting, similarity remains relevant to both forms of reasoning. For coincidence evidence, similarities are expressly central because the issue is whether the events are too similar to be explained by chance. For tendency evidence, similarity is not mandated in the same way, but the source states that the degree of similarity between the conduct relied upon remains a significant factor in assessing the strength and probative value of the tendency evidence. 



block\_025



The doctrinal point is that the same body of evidence may satisfy both s 97 and s 98 if it supports an inference about a person’s tendency and also supports an inference that similar events are unlikely to have occurred coincidentally. In such cases, admissibility still depends on significant probative value and the statutory prejudice balance, but the practical distinction between the categories can narrow. 



block\_025



Authority — Distinction Less Stark Where Same Evidence Supports Both Routes



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Standage v Tasmania \[2012] TASSC 88] \[TOPIC: overlap between tendency and coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: cross-admissibility, significant probative value, similarities, killings, coincidence reasoning, tendency reasoning, overlap of categories] 



block\_025



Standage v Tasmania \[2012] TASSC 88 is cited in the source for the proposition that, while the distinction between tendency evidence and coincidence evidence must be kept in mind, the distinction may be less stark where the prosecution argues that the same conduct should be admitted for both purposes. Evans J explained, in the passage quoted in the source, that coincidence evidence centrally depends on the improbability that the similarities are explicable by coincidence, whereas tendency evidence does not contain the same statutory reference to similarities. Even so, similarity remains an important factor in the assessment of the strength of tendency evidence. 



block\_025



The authority therefore supports the proposition that one body of evidence may be cross-admissible under both routes if it has significant probative value for each. The source further states that this passage was approved by the Court of Criminal Appeal in Donohue v Tasmania \[2016] TASCCA 17. 



Relationship Evidence — Contextual Evidence Distinct from Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: relationship evidence admissibility]

\[CONCEPTS: relationship evidence, contextual evidence, background evidence, jury context, factual matrix, explain conduct, non-tendency reasoning]



Relationship evidence refers to evidence describing the nature of the relationship between the accused and another person where that evidence assists the fact-finder to understand the events surrounding an alleged offence. The evidence is admissible where it forms part of the factual matrix of the case and assists the court or jury to evaluate the probability of facts in issue.



Relationship evidence is not necessarily tendency evidence. It may be admitted for several non-tendency purposes including:



To place the alleged offending in its broader factual context.



To explain the conduct of the complainant or accused.



To explain why certain behaviour occurred or why a complaint was delayed.



To assist the jury to evaluate the plausibility of competing accounts of events.



For example, evidence of previous violence within a relationship may be admissible to explain why a complainant acquiesced in sexual intercourse or failed to complain immediately. In such circumstances, the evidence is not used to reason that the accused has a propensity to commit the offence. Instead it assists the jury to understand the complainant’s behaviour and the context in which the alleged conduct occurred.



Relationship evidence may also assist in evaluating the credibility of a witness where their behaviour might otherwise appear unusual or inconsistent with the alleged events. Evidence explaining that behaviour can make the account intelligible and more readily assessed by the fact-finder.



This category of evidence overlaps conceptually with background evidence and evidence forming part of a continuous narrative of events. 



block\_026



Authority — Relationship Evidence and the Pfennig Debate



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Gipp v R (1998) 194 CLR 106]

\[TOPIC: relationship evidence and propensity reasoning]

\[CONCEPTS: relationship evidence, propensity reasoning debate, Pfennig test, High Court authority, admissibility debate, contextual evidence]



Gipp v R (1998) 194 CLR 106 considered whether the strict admissibility test for propensity evidence articulated in Pfennig v R applied to relationship evidence.



In that case the High Court expressed differing views regarding the classification of relationship evidence. Gaudron J considered that the Pfennig test applied to relationship evidence. Callinan J agreed with that view. However, McHugh and Hayne JJ held that relationship evidence was not propensity evidence at all. Kirby J took the view that it might be tendency evidence but would nonetheless be admissible.



The decision illustrates the conceptual complexity of relationship evidence. Depending on the reasoning process relied upon by the party tendering the evidence, the same evidence may be characterised as:



tendency evidence,



background or contextual evidence, or



evidence of motive.



The classification depends primarily on the reasoning process by which the evidence is said to prove a fact in issue. If the reasoning process relies on an inference that the accused acted consistently with a prior tendency, the tendency rules will apply. If the evidence instead explains the factual context of the relationship, it may fall outside those rules.



The case therefore highlights that the admissibility of relationship evidence depends upon the purpose for which it is tendered rather than merely the content of the evidence itself.



Authority — Relationship Evidence Explaining Complainant Behaviour



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: P v Tasmania \[2009] TASSC 81]

\[TOPIC: relationship evidence explaining complainant conduct]

\[CONCEPTS: contextual evidence, explain behaviour, victim conduct, jury assessment, factual matrix, credibility context]



In P v Tasmania \[2009] TASSC 81 the Supreme Court recognised that relationship evidence can assist the fact-finder to evaluate the evidence concerning the alleged offence.



Relevant evidence under the Evidence Act is defined as evidence which, if accepted, could rationally affect the assessment of the probability of a fact in issue. Relationship evidence satisfies that test where it provides the factual context in which the alleged offence occurred.



Such evidence may assist the court in several ways:



Explaining why a complainant behaved in a manner that might otherwise appear consistent with consent.



Explaining why a complainant did not protest or complain immediately.



Explaining apparently unusual behaviour by a complainant or accused.



Assisting the jury to assess the plausibility of competing accounts.



For example, if evidence demonstrates a history of violence or coercion within a relationship, the complainant’s failure to resist or complain immediately may be understandable when viewed within that context.



Without such contextual evidence the complainant’s account might appear to describe an isolated incident that is difficult to accept. Relationship evidence therefore enables the jury to assess the alleged conduct in the broader context of the relationship.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Exclusion of Unfairly Prejudicial Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 137]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Balancing Test: Mandatory Exclusion]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: exclusion of unfairly prejudicial evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: probative value vs prejudice, mandatory exclusion, unfair prejudice, criminal trials, balancing test]



Section 137 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) requires a court in criminal proceedings to refuse to admit evidence adduced by the prosecution if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant.



The provision states:



In a criminal proceeding, the court must refuse to admit evidence adduced by the prosecutor if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant.



The assessment involves a comparison between:



the probative value of the evidence — the extent to which it could rationally affect the probability of a fact in issue; and



the risk of unfair prejudice to the accused.



The evaluation is mandatory rather than discretionary. If the danger of unfair prejudice outweighs the probative value of the evidence, the court must exclude it.



Importantly, probative value is assessed at its highest, meaning the court assumes that the evidence will be accepted by the jury.



The concept of prejudice refers specifically to unfair prejudice rather than the mere fact that the evidence strengthens the prosecution case. Evidence which increases the likelihood of conviction is not unfairly prejudicial simply because it is damaging to the accused.



The rule therefore protects against the risk that a jury may misuse evidence or give it improper weight.



Authority — Meaning of Unfair Prejudice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Neill-Fraser v Tasmania \[2012] TASCCA 2]

\[TOPIC: distinction between prejudice and unfair prejudice]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, evidentiary misuse, jury reasoning risk, probative value, prejudicial evidence]



In Neill-Fraser v Tasmania \[2012] TASCCA 2 Crawford CJ explained the meaning of unfair prejudice in the context of the exclusionary provisions of the Evidence Act.



All evidence that tends to prove the prosecution case will naturally be prejudicial to the accused. However, the legislation is concerned only with unfair prejudice.



Unfair prejudice arises where there is a real risk that the jury may use the evidence in an improper way. For example, the jury may:



decide the case on an emotional basis,



reason impermissibly about the accused’s character, or



give evidence more weight than it logically deserves.



The focus of the analysis is therefore on the potential misuse of the evidence rather than the fact that it is damaging to the accused.



This approach is consistent with the reasoning of the High Court in Papakosmas v The Queen (1999) 196 CLR 297, which emphasised that the relevant prejudice is the risk of unfair reasoning rather than the mere strengthening of the prosecution case.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 — Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 97]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 — Tendency Evidence: Definition]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: tendency evidence definition]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, propensity reasoning, particular conduct, state of mind, character evidence, inference of behaviour]



Section 97 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) governs the admissibility of tendency evidence. Tendency evidence is evidence that a person has a tendency to act in a particular way or to have a particular state of mind.



The evidence is typically relied upon to support an inference that because a person acted in a particular way on previous occasions, they acted in the same way on the occasion relevant to the proceedings.



For tendency evidence to be admissible the party seeking to adduce it must establish that the evidence has significant probative value. In criminal proceedings, additional restrictions apply where the evidence is adduced by the prosecution.



The reasoning process underlying tendency evidence involves an inferential step:



Evidence is adduced showing the person previously behaved in a particular way or possessed a particular state of mind.



The trier of fact is invited to infer that the person has a tendency to behave in that manner.



The trier of fact is then invited to infer that the person acted consistently with that tendency on the occasion in question.



The rule is concerned with controlling this reasoning process because of the risk that such evidence may lead to unfair prejudice if misused by the jury.



Authority — Meaning and Function of Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v PWD]

\[TOPIC: reasoning process underlying tendency evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: tendency reasoning, propensity inference, behavioural pattern evidence, state of mind inference, evidentiary reasoning]



In R v PWD the court explained the inferential reasoning that underlies the use of tendency evidence.



Evidence that a person has a tendency to act in a particular way does not prove a fact in issue by itself. Rather, it provides the foundation for an inference that the person acted consistently with that tendency on a particular occasion relevant to the proceedings.



The reasoning process operates as follows:



Evidence demonstrates that a person previously acted in a particular way or possessed a particular state of mind.



From this evidence the fact-finder infers that the person has a tendency to behave in that way.



The fact-finder then infers that the person behaved consistently with that tendency in the event that forms the subject of the charge.



The decision also confirms that tendency evidence is not limited to evidence showing a tendency to commit a particular offence. Evidence showing a tendency to behave in a particular manner — such as using violence in stressful domestic situations — may also qualify as tendency evidence.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 79 — Expert Opinion Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 79]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 79]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: admissibility of expert opinion]

\[CONCEPTS: expert opinion evidence, specialised knowledge, opinion exception, expert witness, forensic expertise]



Section 79 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides an exception to the general rule excluding opinion evidence. Under this provision, opinion evidence is admissible where the opinion is wholly or substantially based on specialised knowledge derived from a person’s training, study or experience.



The rule recognises that expert witnesses may assist the court by providing opinions in areas where specialised knowledge is required. This commonly occurs in areas such as forensic science, medicine, drug identification and financial analysis.



The expert must demonstrate that:



they possess specialised knowledge based on training, study or experience; and



the opinion expressed is based on that specialised knowledge.



Expert opinion evidence is often supported by reports explaining the reasoning process and the material relied upon by the expert. Such reports do not constitute hearsay where they are tendered as the basis of the expert’s opinion rather than to prove the truth of the underlying statements.



The role of the expert witness is therefore to assist the court in understanding matters beyond ordinary knowledge rather than to determine the ultimate issues in the case.



Authority — Underlying Facts and Weight of Expert Opinion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Langford v Tasmania \[2018] TASCCA 1]

\[TOPIC: effect of unproven assumptions on expert evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: expert evidence weight, admissibility vs weight, assumptions in expert opinion, probative value]



In Langford v Tasmania \[2018] TASCCA 1 the Court of Criminal Appeal considered whether the factual assumptions underlying expert opinion must be proved before the opinion becomes admissible.



The court held that proof of the underlying assumptions is not a precondition to admissibility under s 79 of the Evidence Act. If the opinion is relevant and based on specialised knowledge, it may be admitted even if the factual assumptions supporting the opinion are not independently proven.



However, the absence of proof of those underlying facts may affect the weight to be attributed to the opinion by the fact-finder. The distinction between admissibility and weight is therefore critical.



Evidence may be admissible if it satisfies the requirements of relevance and the statutory opinion rule, even if its persuasive force is weak. Questions regarding the reliability of the assumptions underlying the opinion are matters for the evaluation of the evidence rather than its admissibility.



Motive Evidence Distinguished from Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: motive evidence and its distinction from tendency evidence] \[CONCEPTS: motive evidence, tendency evidence, non-propensity reasoning, sexual interest motive, reason for offending, proof of motive, why offence happened]



Motive evidence refers to evidence tendered to show why an accused may have committed an offence. Motive evidence is not necessarily tendency evidence, because the reasoning process may focus on the accused’s reason for acting rather than an inference that the accused acted in conformity with a general disposition. 



block\_026



Where the alleged motive is theft, silencing a witness, or avoiding disclosure of another crime, the reasoning is ordinarily non-tendency reasoning. The evidence is used to show a concrete reason for the charged conduct. By contrast, where the alleged motive is sexual interest or indecent passion toward a class of persons, the evidence may be characterised as tendency evidence because it more readily invites reasoning from disposition to conduct. 



block\_026



Prior conduct directed toward a particular person may also be characterised as motive where it demonstrates attraction or sexual interest in that individual. The source material notes that the distinction between motive and tendency can be fine. In practice, especially after later High Court authority, that distinction may have limited practical significance where the evidence is admissible as tendency evidence in any event. 



block\_026



The key question is therefore not the label attached to the evidence, but the actual chain of reasoning relied upon to prove a fact in issue. If the reasoning depends on “this person acts this way, so probably acted this way again,” the tendency rules are engaged. If the reasoning is “this person had a reason to commit this offence,” the evidence may instead be motive evidence. 



block\_026



Authority — Motive Evidence May Overlap with Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Leonard v R (2006) 67 NSWLR 545] \[TOPIC: prior conduct as motive showing sexual interest] \[CONCEPTS: motive overlap, sexual interest evidence, prior conduct, attraction evidence, tendency overlap, evidentiary characterisation]



Leonard v R (2006) 67 NSWLR 545 is cited in the source as authority that prior conduct directed toward an individual may be characterised as motive where it shows that the accused was attracted to, or had a sexual interest in, that person. 



block\_026



The significance of the authority lies in its treatment of evidentiary characterisation. Evidence of prior conduct may support an inference of motive without necessarily being led to show a general tendency. At the same time, the source notes that the distinction between motive and tendency is a fine one, particularly where the prior conduct and the charged conduct are both said to reflect the same sexual interest. 



block\_026



The authority therefore supports the proposition that motive evidence should be analysed by reference to the reasoning process for which it is tendered. A court must identify whether the evidence is truly being used to prove a reason for the charged conduct, or whether it is in substance being used to prove a disposition and therefore falls within the tendency provisions. 



block\_026



Drug-Dealing Implements as Circumstantial Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: drug-dealing implements as non-tendency circumstantial evidence] \[CONCEPTS: drug implements, circumstantial evidence, scales and stash, intent to sell, knowledge of drugs, non-tendency reasoning, trafficking indicators]



Evidence of implements associated with drug dealing may be admissible as circumstantial evidence of knowledge, possession, or intention to sell drugs, without relying on tendency reasoning. The source gives examples such as a stash, scales, or firearms suggesting the carrying on of a drug business. 



block\_026



The reasoning process is not that the accused is the kind of person who deals drugs and therefore probably did so on the charged occasion. Instead, the reasoning is that possession of such implements is part of the surrounding circumstantial picture and may support an inference that the accused knew of the drugs or intended to traffic in them. 



block\_026
