The source states that this remains so even where the Crown is not putting its case on a Giretti count basis, and even where the implements cannot be directly linked to the precise drugs identified in the indictment. The evidence may still be logically probative of a fact in issue by conventional circumstantial reasoning rather than impermissible propensity reasoning. 



block\_026



This distinction matters because if the evidence is truly circumstantial and non-tendency in character, it is not excluded by the tendency rules merely because it is damaging to the accused. It remains subject, however, to general relevance and prejudice controls. 



block\_026



Authority — Drug Implements and Non-Tendency Reasoning



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Falzon \[2018] HCA 29] \[TOPIC: implements of drug business as circumstantial evidence] \[CONCEPTS: drug paraphernalia, knowledge inference, intent to sell, circumstantial proof, non-propensity evidence, High Court authority]



R v Falzon \[2018] HCA 29 is cited in the source for the proposition that evidence of implements suggesting a person carries on a drug business may be admissible as circumstantial evidence of knowledge of a drug or intention to sell a drug, without engaging tendency or propensity reasoning. 



block\_026



The significance of the authority is that the evidentiary force comes from the connection between the implements and the alleged drug activity, not from an inference that the accused has a bad character or criminal disposition. Thus, items such as scales, storage materials, or firearms may be probative of a charged drug offence by showing the practical apparatus of trafficking or commercial dealing. 



block\_026



The source notes that this reasoning may apply even if the Crown does not rely on a formal Giretti count and even if the implements are not directly tied to the exact indictment allegation. The authority therefore supports a broad understanding of circumstantial relevance in drug matters. 



block\_026



Res Gestae or Evidence Forming Part of a Relevant Transaction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: evidence forming part of a connected transaction] \[CONCEPTS: res gestae, transaction evidence, connected events, background evidence, explain what happened, continuous narrative, contextual admissibility]



Evidence of acts forming an integral part of a chain of events may be admissible as evidence of a relevant transaction, sometimes described as res gestae or transactional evidence. The rule applies where omission of the evidence would leave the remaining evidence unintelligible or not easily understood. 



block\_026



The source states that such evidence is not led for a tendency purpose. Instead, it is led to explain what happened, set the scene, and provide a coherent account of the events surrounding the alleged offence. The evidence may include acts that are themselves discreditable or prejudicial, but are still admissible because they are part of the relevant narrative. 



block\_026



Although such evidence will often be close in time to the charged offence, temporal proximity is not essential. The critical question is whether the acts are sufficiently connected to make the later conduct comprehensible and probative. This category may overlap with relationship evidence and motive evidence, but the operative reasoning remains contextual rather than propensity-based. 



block\_026



Under the Uniform Evidence Act framework, the evidence remains relevant because it could rationally affect the assessment of the probability of a fact in issue. It is not excluded by ss 97 or 98 if the evidence is used for a non-tendency purpose. Even where admitted on that basis, the evidence remains subject to s 137 where adduced by the prosecution in a criminal proceeding. 



block\_026



Authority — Transactional Evidence Remains Admissible Under the Evidence Act



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Adam (1999) 106 A Crim R 510] \[TOPIC: non-tendency transactional evidence under uniform evidence legislation] \[CONCEPTS: transaction evidence, non-tendency purpose, contextual relevance, chain of events, admissible background evidence]



R v Adam (1999) 106 A Crim R 510 is cited in the source as authority that evidence forming part of a connected series of events remains admissible under the Uniform Evidence Act where it is tendered for a non-tendency purpose. 



block\_026



The authority supports the proposition that relevant background evidence explaining the sequence of events is not excluded by ss 97 and 98 merely because it reveals other discreditable conduct. The decisive question is whether the evidence is used to prove a tendency, or instead to explain the circumstances of the charged event. 



block\_026



The source also pairs this authority with Samadi v R and related cases to reinforce that connected-events evidence may be necessary to render the prosecution case intelligible. The admissibility of such evidence depends on its explanatory and contextual role, not on any inference of criminal propensity. 



block\_026



Authority — Background Evidence May Set the Scene for the Charged Incident



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v FDP (2008) 74 NSWLR 645] \[TOPIC: background evidence explaining conduct before the charged event] \[CONCEPTS: set the scene, background evidence, connected events, explain later conduct, contextual narrative, relationship evidence]



R v FDP (2008) 74 NSWLR 645 is cited in the source for the principle that evidence of earlier incidents may be admissible as background or context evidence explaining the conduct of the parties before and during the charged event. 



block\_026



The passage extracted in the source shows that the earlier conduct and the making of an ADVO explained why the parties acted and spoke as they did on the later date, including why direct contact could not occur and why certain arrangements existed. The evidence therefore “set the scene” for the incident giving rise to the charges. 



block\_026



The authority demonstrates that connected-events evidence may remain relevant even where it concerns different dates and distinct conduct. What matters is whether the earlier evidence is capable of making the later events intelligible and rationally affecting the assessment of facts in issue. 



block\_026



Onus of Proof for Evidentiary Facts and Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: standard of proof for evidentiary facts and tendency material] \[CONCEPTS: onus of proof, evidentiary facts, indispensable link, beyond reasonable doubt, tendency evidence, circumstantial proof]



As a general rule, a jury does not need to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt about every individual piece of evidence led at trial. The source states that this is only required where the particular piece of evidence is an indispensable link in the chain of reasoning proving guilt. 



block\_026



The source further records that, at common law, a jury had to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt in respect of tendency or propensity evidence. However, not every evidentiary detail going to prove the tendency itself had to be established to that standard. Under the Uniform Evidence Act, such a direction should not ordinarily be given unless there is a significant possibility that the jury may treat the uncharged acts as an indispensable link in reasoning toward guilt. 



block\_026



Accordingly, the modern approach distinguishes between:



ordinary evidentiary facts,



indispensable links in the proof of guilt, and



special cases where jury misuse of tendency material creates a need for a stronger direction. 



block\_026



The practical question is whether the evidence is being used as one item in a larger circumstantial matrix, or whether the jury may effectively treat it as necessary proof of guilt. That distinction determines whether a beyond reasonable doubt direction is required. 



block\_026



Authority — Indispensable Link and Proof Beyond Reasonable Doubt



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Shepherd v R (1990) 170 CLR 573] \[TOPIC: indispensable evidentiary links require proof beyond reasonable doubt] \[CONCEPTS: indispensable link, circumstantial case, proof standard, evidentiary fact, guilt reasoning, High Court authority]



Shepherd v R (1990) 170 CLR 573 is cited in the source for the principle that a jury does not ordinarily have to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt about each item of evidence, unless that item is an indispensable link in the chain of reasoning to guilt. 



block\_026



The authority is important in circumstantial cases because it limits the circumstances in which a trial judge must direct the jury that a particular subsidiary fact must itself be proven beyond reasonable doubt. The requirement arises only where the fact is essential to the inferential path to conviction. 



block\_026



The source uses this authority to contrast ordinary evidentiary facts with tendency material and with cases where an item of evidence may be wrongly treated by the jury as determinative. The principle therefore informs both jury directions and the assessment of how particular evidence functions within the prosecution case. 



block\_026



Notice Requirements for Tendency Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: notice requirements for tendency and coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: tendency notice, reasonable notice, identify tendency, facts in issue, procedural fairness, admissibility notice, coincidence notice]



A party seeking to adduce tendency or coincidence evidence must give reasonable notice identifying the evidence and the purpose for which it is to be used. The source states that it may be sufficient in some circumstances to refer to Crown papers where the tendency use of the material is readily identifiable, but adequacy depends on whether the notice enables the opposing party to meet the case and enables the court to assess probative value properly. 



block\_026



A proper tendency notice should explicitly identify:



the fact or facts in issue to which the evidence is said to relate; and



the tendency sought to be proved. 



block\_026



A coincidence notice should identify the related events, the person whose conduct or state of mind is relied upon, and whether the evidence is said to prove a particular act or a state of mind. The source emphasises that admissibility is judged against the tendency articulated in the notice. It is not for the court to devise some alternative tendency that might make the evidence admissible. 



block\_026



A failure to provide adequate notice may make the evidence inadmissible or result in a miscarriage of justice. A notice framed at too high a level of generality may amount to little more than a relevance assertion and may fail to articulate a legally significant tendency with sufficient precision. 



block\_026



Authority — Purpose of a Tendency Notice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Martin v R \[2002] NSWCCA 337] \[TOPIC: function and specificity of tendency notice] \[CONCEPTS: notice function, specific conduct, circumstances of conduct, probative value assessment, procedural fairness, tendency particulars]



Martin v R \[2002] NSWCCA 337 is cited in the source for the proposition that the purpose of a tendency notice is, first, to ensure attention is directed to specific conduct and its circumstances, and second, to enable the affected party to meet the tendency evidence. 



block\_026



The authority links notice directly to admissibility. Without knowledge of the specific conduct and circumstances relied upon, neither the opposing party nor the court can properly evaluate the probative value of the evidence. The case therefore supports a substantive, not merely technical, understanding of the notice requirement. 



block\_026



This authority also reinforces that vague notice is insufficient. The notice must identify with enough precision what conduct is relied upon and how it is said to bear upon the charged facts. That precision is essential both to fairness and to the judicial evaluation of admissibility. 



block\_026



Tendency and Coincidence Evidence Adduced by the Defence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: defence use of tendency and coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: defence tendency evidence, third party guilt, reasonable possibility, innocence hypothesis, s 97 and s 98, s 135 discretion]



An accused may seek to adduce tendency or coincidence evidence to support a hypothesis that another person committed the offence, or to raise a reasonable possibility consistent with innocence. The source notes that this commonly occurs, though not exclusively, in cases involving co-accused or alternative-perpetrator theories. 



block\_026



Such evidence must still comply with ss 97 or 98 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas), including the requirement of significant probative value. However, the additional prosecution-specific restriction in s 101 does not apply because the evidence is not adduced by the Crown. The discretionary exclusion provisions, especially s 135, may still apply. 



block\_026



In the defence context, the evidentiary question is whether the material shows a reasonable possibility that somebody else may have committed the offence, or in a circumstantial case raises a hypothesis consistent with innocence. The evidence must do more than merely leave that possibility notionally open. It must support the hypothesis in a logically probative way. 



block\_026



Where the accused relies on similar-fact style evidence as to identity, there must be a sufficiently close connection between the fact in issue and the other conduct relied upon. Mere similarity is not enough. The defence must identify a real and rational basis for the inference sought. 



block\_026



Authority — Similar Fact Evidence Led by the Accused on Identity



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Cheney v R \[1991] FCA 37] \[TOPIC: defence similar fact evidence and identity] \[CONCEPTS: identity evidence, alternative offender, sufficient similarity, defence evidence, real possibility, similar fact reasoning]



Cheney v R \[1991] FCA 37 is cited in the source as authority that where identity is in issue and an accused seeks to rely on similar-fact evidence, the evidence must demonstrate a sufficient degree of similarity between the charged crime and the other event to give rise to the real possibility that the same person was responsible for both. 



block\_026



The source quotes the principle that a mere similarity in the way a task was performed on one occasion and another will generally be insufficient. There must be a close or immediate connection such that, in common human experience, the fact in issue is likely to have occurred if the similar fact occurred. 



block\_026



In this way, the authority imposes a genuine logical threshold on defence use of similar-fact evidence. The evidence must do more than invite speculation. It must provide a rational basis for concluding that the alternative offender may have been the person responsible for the charged offence. 



block\_026



Reasonable Hypothesis Consistent with Innocence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: legal doctrine] \[TOPIC: requirement that innocence hypothesis have evidentiary support] \[CONCEPTS: reasonable hypothesis, consistent with innocence, not fanciful, evidentiary support, circumstantial case, alternative inference]



Where an accused relies on a hypothesis consistent with innocence, the hypothesis must possess some degree of acceptability or credibility. The source states that it cannot be fanciful, incredible, untenable, remote, tenuous, irrational, absurd, or ridiculous. It must be supported by evidence rather than merely being left theoretically open. 



block\_026



The doctrine requires more than conjecture. A reasonable hypothesis must arise from the totality of the evidence and be one that is genuinely open on the facts. In a circumstantial case, the trier of fact must consider all the circumstances together rather than in a piecemeal way. 



block\_026



This principle is especially important when defence evidence is led to implicate another person or to suggest an innocent explanation for suspicious circumstances. The question is not whether innocence can be imagined in the abstract, but whether the evidence supports an inference consistent with innocence that is reasonably available. 



block\_026



Authority — Reasonable Inference Must Rest on More Than Mere Conjecture



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: R v Baden Clay (2016) 258 CLR 308; \[2016] HCA 35] \[TOPIC: innocence inference in circumstantial cases] \[CONCEPTS: mere conjecture, reasonable inference, circumstantial evidence, totality of circumstances, guilt vs innocence inference]



R v Baden Clay (2016) 258 CLR 308; \[2016] HCA 35 is cited in the source for the proposition that a reasonable inference must rest on something more than mere conjecture, and that the bare possibility of innocence does not prevent conviction where guilt is the only reasonable inference open on the whole of the evidence. 



block\_026



The authority emphasises that in a circumstantial case all of the circumstances established by the evidence must be considered and weighed together in deciding whether an inference consistent with innocence is reasonably open. The evidence is not to be fragmented into isolated items. 



block\_026



This authority therefore supports the source’s broader proposition that a defence hypothesis must be grounded in evidence and rational evaluation. A merely speculative or abstract possibility is insufficient to qualify as a reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence. 



block\_026



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 — Coincidence Rule



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 98] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: coincidence evidence and related events] \[CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, related events, improbability of coincidence, similar circumstances, significant probative value, pattern reasoning]



Section 98 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) regulates coincidence evidence. Coincidence evidence involves reasoning that because two or more events occurred in similar circumstances, it is improbable that those events occurred coincidentally, and that improbability tends to prove a fact in issue such as whether a person did a particular act or had a particular state of mind. 



block\_026



The source states that related events may be relied upon if they are substantially or relevantly similar, and the evidence is admissible only if it has significant probative value. Similarity is therefore an important source of probative force, but the statutory question is whether the evidence is capable to a significant degree of rationally affecting the assessment of a fact in issue. 



block\_026



The coincidence rule differs from tendency reasoning because it focuses on the improbability of repeated similar events occurring by chance, rather than on a disposition to act in a particular way. Nonetheless, the rule is subject to the same legislative concern that evidence of this kind may be powerful and prejudicial, especially in criminal proceedings. 



block\_026



Where coincidence reasoning is not available because the event is not a “related event,” the source notes that the evidence may still need to be assessed under s 137 if adduced by the prosecution. 



block\_026



Authority — Questions for Admissibility of Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: CGL v DPP (Vic) \[2010]] \[TOPIC: structured test for coincidence evidence admissibility] \[CONCEPTS: coincidence checklist, improbability test, related events, significant probative value, prejudicial effect, admissibility framework]



CGL v DPP (Vic) \[2010] is cited in the source for a structured sequence of questions to determine the admissibility of coincidence evidence. Those questions are:



Are the similarities in the specified events or circumstances such that it is improbable that they occurred coincidentally?



If so, would the evidence tend to prove that the accused did the specified act or had the specified state of mind?



If so, does the evidence have significant probative value, either alone or in light of other prosecution evidence?



If so, does the probative value substantially outweigh any prejudicial effect on the accused? 



block\_026



The authority is useful because it separates the logical question of coincidence from the statutory question of significant probative value and the further criminal balancing of probative force against prejudice. It therefore provides a practical analytical sequence for admissibility disputes involving similar events. 



block\_026



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 95 — Tendency and Coincidence Rules Still Apply



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 95] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 95] \[TYPE: interpretive principle] \[TOPIC: evidence admitted for another purpose may still require compliance with tendency or coincidence rules] \[CONCEPTS: section 95, other purpose evidence, no automatic use, tendency compliance, coincidence compliance, statutory interpretation]



Section 95 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) makes clear that, unlike some other exclusionary rules such as hearsay or character, evidence admitted for another purpose cannot automatically be used for tendency or coincidence reasoning without compliance with the specific statutory provisions governing those uses. 



block\_026



The source stresses that it is not sufficient simply to assert that evidence is relevant for some purpose other than tendency. To avoid the tendency provisions, the tendering party must demonstrate a chain of reasoning to a fact in issue that does not depend on an inference from tendency to conforming conduct. If the evidence is in truth being used for tendency or coincidence reasoning, the statutory requirements must still be satisfied. 



block\_026



This provision therefore prevents parties from circumventing the safeguards in ss 97 and 98 by first tendering the evidence on some other basis and then inviting the tribunal of fact to use it impermissibly. 



block\_026



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101 — Further Restrictions on Prosecution Tendency and Coincidence Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 101] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: further restrictions on prosecution tendency and coincidence evidence] \[CONCEPTS: prosecution evidence, substantial outweighing, prejudicial effect, criminal restrictions, defendant opens the door, stricter admissibility]



Section 101 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) imposes additional restrictions where tendency or coincidence evidence is adduced by the prosecution in a criminal proceeding. The source states that in such cases the probative value of the evidence must substantially outweigh its prejudicial effect. 



block\_026



This is a stricter test than the threshold requirement of significant probative value. It reflects the recognition that prosecution tendency and coincidence evidence can be especially powerful and may create a substantial risk of unfair prejudice if misused by the jury. 



block\_026



The source also notes that certain subsections are inapplicable if the defendant has opened the door, and that reasonable notice must be given under the surrounding provisions. The practical effect is that prosecution use of such evidence is subject to layered statutory controls: notice, significant probative value, and in criminal cases the requirement that probative value substantially outweigh prejudicial effect. 



block\_026



Lay Opinion Evidence Under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 78



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 78] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 78] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: lay opinion as descriptive evidence] \[CONCEPTS: lay opinion, witness perception, descriptive shorthand, what witness saw, what witness heard, ordinary observation]



Section 78 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) creates an exception allowing a witness to give lay opinion evidence where the opinion is necessary to obtain an adequate account or understanding of the witness’s perception of a matter or event. The source summarises this as allowing a witness to give opinion representations to provide better descriptive weight to what the witness saw, heard, or otherwise perceived. 



block\_026



This evidence is not direct evidence of the ultimate fact merely because it contains a conclusion or impression. Its admissibility lies in the practical reality that some observations are best communicated in an evaluative way, such as a person appearing drunk, angry, frightened, fast-moving, or distressed. 



block\_026



The rule is therefore concerned with the witness’s firsthand perception. It does not permit speculative opinion, but it does allow ordinary descriptive judgments that help the court understand the substance of what was perceived. 



block\_026



Expert Qualification for Cannabis Identification



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: laying foundation for police expert opinion on cannabis identification] \[CONCEPTS: expert qualification, cannabis identification, police expertise, training and experience, specialised knowledge, voir dire foundation, drug identification]



Where a police witness is called to identify cannabis or another controlled plant or substance by opinion evidence, the foundation for admissibility under s 79 should establish specialised knowledge derived from training, study, or experience. The source provides a practical checklist for qualifying such a witness. 



block\_026



Relevant foundation matters include:



length of service,



years in uniform and investigative roles,



specialist drug or CIB secondments,



specialist courses, seminars, and academy training,



work with forensic services,



operational experience seizing and identifying controlled plants, and



whether prior presumptive tests or analyses have confirmed the witness’s identifications. 



block\_026



Once the witness’s specialised knowledge is established, the witness may be asked whether, based on training, study, and experience, the exhibit can be identified as cannabis. The source also indicates the forensic sequence of confirming the exhibit, confirming examination, and then eliciting the opinion. 



block\_026



This chunk concerns the practical admissibility foundation for expert opinion, not the ultimate correctness of the opinion. 



block\_026



Police Expert Evidence About Drug Practices and Communications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: police expert evidence concerning drug trade practices] \[CONCEPTS: drug jargon, trafficking practices, specialist police opinion, coded communication, common dealing items, drug market evidence]



The source states that police may give evidence that, through numerous investigations into drug-related offences, they have become familiar with forms of communication used by people involved in that activity. This is a form of opinion evidence grounded in experience, typically directed to matters beyond ordinary knowledge such as drug jargon, dealing practices, common implements, and network structures. 



block\_026



The same logic may support police opinion evidence about matters such as:



cutting agents used to dilute methylamphetamine,



common methods of using the drug,



market value and price fluctuations,



items commonly possessed by drug dealers, and



use of multiple phones, encrypted applications, or dedicated encrypted devices. 



block\_026



The admissibility question is whether the evidence is truly based on specialised knowledge derived from training or experience, and whether it assists the court in understanding facts in issue. Such evidence is not admitted to prove general criminal disposition, but to explain practices, language, and circumstances relevant to drug offending. 



block\_026



Authority — Expert Reports and Drug Trade Context



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Jackson v The State of Western Australia \[2025] WASCA 33] \[TOPIC: approval of expert-style evidence on drug trade practices] \[CONCEPTS: expert reports, drug value evidence, cutting agents, dealing indicators, coded communications, appellate approval]



Jackson v The State of Western Australia \[2025] WASCA 33 is cited in the source as approving a range of expert or specialised evidence about drug practices. The source lists examples including evidence that methylamphetamine can be diluted with cutting agents such as MSM, the ways in which it is commonly used, its market value, items commonly possessed by drug dealers, use of multiple mobile phones and encrypted devices, and the structure of drug dealing networks. 



block\_026



The authority is relevant because it supports the admissibility of specialised contextual evidence that helps the court interpret physical exhibits, communications, and surrounding circumstances in drug prosecutions. The evidence is not tendered as proof of prior representation simply because it is contained in a report; rather, it may be admissible as opinion evidence where the statutory conditions are satisfied. 



Procedure for Identifying Documents Over Video Link



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: video-link proof of report identification details] \[CONCEPTS: video link evidence, identify report, witness document reference, sample identification, barcode details, remote evidence procedure]



Where a witness gives evidence by video link and needs to identify a report or analytical document, the source suggests a practical sequence. First, establish the witness’s identity and qualifications. Then confirm that the witness generated the report and has a copy available. Next, seek leave for the witness to refer to the document for the limited purpose of describing identification details such as sample identifiers, barcodes, or document identifiers. 



block\_026



After those identifying matters are placed on the record, confirm that the relevant material was provided by police, that the sample was analysed, and what result the analysis produced. The procedure is directed to authenticating and identifying the document and its subject matter while preserving proper proof of the analysis. 



block\_026



This is a practical evidentiary sequence rather than a distinct statutory rule, but it is directed to orderly proof of expert or forensic material in remote hearing conditions. 



block\_026



Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 43 — Witness Refusing to Answer



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 43] \[CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 43] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: witness refusing examination, oath, or answers] \[CONCEPTS: witness refusal, refusal to answer, refusal to take oath, summary proceedings, committal to gaol, coercive witness powers]



Section 43 of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas) provides a coercive mechanism where a witness appearing before justices refuses to participate properly in the examination process without sufficient excuse. The section applies where the witness:



refuses to be examined on oath concerning the matters,



refuses to take an oath, or



having taken an oath, refuses to answer questions then put concerning the matter. 



block\_026



If those conditions are met and no sufficient excuse is offered, a justice present and having jurisdiction may issue a warrant committing the person to gaol for up to 7 days, unless in the meantime the person consents to be examined and answer concerning the matter. 



block\_026



The provision therefore serves both a coercive and remedial purpose. It enforces compliance with the witness’s obligation to participate in judicial examination while allowing release if the witness relents and agrees to answer. 



block\_026



Credibility Assessment of a Defence-Favourable Witness



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: reduced weight where witness becomes mere echo of counsel] \[CONCEPTS: credibility, leading questions, partisan witness, echo of counsel, weight of evidence, defence-favourable witness]



Where a witness is plainly partisan and entirely favourable to one side, answers elicited through heavily leading cross-examination may attract little or no weight. The source uses Police v Endlay to illustrate that although leading questions in cross-examination are generally permissible, there are circumstances in which the resulting answers are so suggestive that the witness effectively becomes “a mere echo of counsel.” 



block\_026



The principle is not that such questioning is automatically inadmissible. Rather, the point is evaluative: the fact-finder may place no meaningful weight on answers that appear to be counsel’s formulation repeated by a partisan witness rather than the witness’s own reliable recollection. 



block\_026



This doctrine is relevant to submissions on credit and reliability. It supports an argument that the form of questioning, combined with the witness’s evident alignment with a party, undermines the independent probative value of the answers given. 



block\_026



Authority — Partisan Witness as a Mere Echo of Counsel



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Police v Endlay \[2020] TASMC 13 (9 January 2020)] \[TOPIC: little weight to leading answers from defence-favourable complainant] \[CONCEPTS: partisan complainant, leading cross-examination, reliability, little weight, credibility assessment, echo of counsel]



Police v Endlay \[2020] TASMC 13 is cited in the source for the proposition that a court may place no weight on answers given in response to leading cross-examination where the witness is plainly partisan and effectively functioning as a defence witness. 



block\_026



The source quotes the judicial observation that the witness in that case functioned as “a mere echo of counsel.” The importance of the authority is not to prohibit leading questions in cross-examination, but to demonstrate that the court may discount the resulting evidence where its reliability is undermined by the way in which it was elicited and by the witness’s partiality. 



block\_026



The authority supports credibility submissions directed to the weight, rather than the technical admissibility, of such evidence. 



block\_026



Prompt Complaint Conduct as Supporting Credibility



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: post-event conduct as bearing on credibility] \[CONCEPTS: prompt complaint, post-assault conduct, credibility indicators, immediate reporting, consistency with assault, witness reliability]



A complainant’s conduct immediately after an alleged assault may be relied upon as a matter supporting credibility where that conduct is consistent with the making of the complaint alleged. The source refers to conduct such as immediately contacting a friend, father, grandmother, and going promptly to the police station as behaviour consistent with having been assaulted. 



block\_026



The evidentiary significance lies in the consistency between the post-event behaviour and the account given in court. Such conduct may support an inference that the complainant’s version was drawn from actual experience rather than invention, especially when coupled with evidence that the account was delivered confidently, chronologically, and with minor detail, and was free from internal inconsistency. 



block\_026



This is not a rule that prompt complaint automatically proves truthfulness. Rather, it is a credibility consideration available to the fact-finder in evaluating whether the surrounding conduct aligns with the alleged event. 



block\_026



Authority — Prompt Complaint Conduct Relevant to Credibility



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Kilby v The Queen \[1973] HCA 30; (1973) 129 CLR 460] \[TOPIC: immediate complaint conduct as credibility indicator] \[CONCEPTS: prompt complaint, credibility support, consistency of conduct, assault complaint, complainant reliability, High Court authority]



Kilby v The Queen \[1973] HCA 30; (1973) 129 CLR 460 is cited in the source as authority supporting the use of immediate post-event complaint conduct as a matter bearing on credibility. 



block\_026



The authority is used in the source to support the submission that a complainant’s immediate communications with others and prompt attendance at a police station may be consistent with having been assaulted as alleged. The evidentiary point is one of consistency between conduct and account, not a standalone rule of proof. 



block\_026



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106 — Rebuttal Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 106] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: rebuttal evidence after denial or failure to admit in cross-examination] \[CONCEPTS: rebuttal evidence, prior inconsistent statement, cross-examination foundation, credibility attack, contradiction evidence, section 106]



Section 106 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) permits rebuttal evidence in certain circumstances after a witness has been confronted in cross-examination and has denied, failed to admit, or not remembered the relevant matter. The source discusses s 106 in the context of proof of prior inconsistent statements and related credibility issues. 



block\_026



The source’s example indicates that where a witness is asked about making an earlier statement and responds that they cannot remember making it, the necessary foundation may be laid for another witness to prove that the statement was in fact made. Once that rebuttal evidence is admitted through s 106, s 60 may enable the evidence to be used as proof of the facts asserted. 



block\_026



The rule therefore operates as a mechanism for contradiction and credibility rebuttal, but may in some circumstances also have substantive evidentiary consequences. Its operation depends on compliance with the necessary confrontation requirements, including those addressed by s 43. 



block\_026



Authority — Rebuttal of Witness Denial Through Evidence of Coaching



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Australian Automotive Repairers Association v Insurance Australia Ltd (2006) FCAFC 33] \[TOPIC: rebuttal evidence showing witness coaching] \[CONCEPTS: witness coaching, rebuttal evidence, direct contradiction, cross-examination rebuttal, credibility challenge, section 106 context]



Australian Automotive Repairers Association v Insurance Australia Ltd (2006) FCAFC 33 is cited in the source as an example of evidence sought to be tendered to rebut a witness’s evidence by calling direct evidence from people who had seen the witness being coached before trial about how to answer questions in cross-examination. 



block\_026



The authority is relevant as an illustration of rebuttal material directed to credibility and reliability. It shows the type of direct contradictory evidence that may be used to undermine a witness’s account where the proper foundation has been laid. 



Authority — Diary-Assisted Recollection, Prior Representation, and Rebuttal Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Neill-Fraser v Tasmania (No 2) \[2019] TASSC 11 (21 March 2019)] \[TOPIC: witness recollection refreshed from diary and proof of prior statement] \[CONCEPTS: diary memory, prior representation, second-hand hearsay, personal knowledge, section 48, section 106]



Neill-Fraser v Tasmania (No 2) \[2019] TASSC 11 is cited in the source as authority for the proposition that where a witness had earlier provided dates to police after consulting her own diary, but at the hearing could no longer remember the dates and the diary could not be found, the evidence was still admissible in circumstances grounded in personal knowledge and statutory exception. 



block\_026



The source records that the witness was permitted to give oral evidence about the facts in the diary because it could not be found, and that the statement to police was not treated as second-hand hearsay because the representation depended on her own contemporaneous notes rather than on another person’s representation. The court accepted that the matter remained sufficiently fresh in memory in the circumstances. 



block\_026



The source further states that once the witness was confronted with the prior statement and responded that she could not remember making it, s 106 was enlivened. Evidence could then be tendered through the police officer that the statement had been made and that the dates given were those stated by the witness. Because the evidence came in through s 106, s 60 permitted its use as proof of the fact. 



block\_026



Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 34 — Prosecution Witness Need Not Disclose Source



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 34] \[CITATION: Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) s 34] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: non-disclosure of source information by prosecution witness] \[CONCEPTS: informer protection, source non-disclosure, prosecution witness, drug proceedings, confidential information, statutory immunity]



Section 34 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas) provides that a witness for the prosecution in proceedings under that Act is not obliged to disclose:



the fact that the witness received information,



the nature of the information received, or



the name of the person who gave the witness the information. 



block\_026



The provision protects confidential sources and the integrity of drug investigations by removing any obligation on a prosecution witness to reveal informer-related material falling within those categories. It is a statutory non-disclosure rule specifically tied to proceedings under the Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 (Tas). 



block\_026



Its practical effect is to preserve anonymity and confidentiality even where the witness is otherwise being examined about investigative steps or background matters. The section does not create a general immunity from answering all questions, but protects the specified source-information categories. 



block\_026



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 26 — Court Control Over Questioning of Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 26] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 26] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: court control over questioning and use of documents] \[CONCEPTS: court control, questioning witnesses, fair hearing, document use, order of questioning, witness management]



Section 26 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) gives the court broad power to make any orders it considers just in relation to the way witnesses are questioned, the production and use of documents and things in connection with questioning, the order in which parties may question a witness, and the presence and behaviour of any person in connection with the questioning of witnesses. 



block\_026



The source identifies this provision as a tool that may be used to curtail defence questioning where the manner or extent of questioning is unjust, unfair, or inefficient. The section is therefore not merely administrative. It provides active procedural control designed to ensure fairness and efficient hearing management. 



block\_026



Its breadth means the court may intervene not only in relation to improper questioning, but also in relation to associated documentary use and courtroom management issues connected to witness examination. 



block\_026



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 192 — Factors Relevant to Leave, Permission, or Direction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 192] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 192] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: statutory factors governing leave, permission, or direction] \[CONCEPTS: section 192, leave factors, fairness to witness, fairness to party, hearing length, importance of evidence, judicial terms]



Section 192 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides that where the Act allows a court to give leave, permission, or a direction, that leave, permission, or direction may be given on any terms the court thinks fit. The section then identifies matters to which the court is to have regard, including:



the extent to which granting the leave or direction would add unduly to or shorten the length of the hearing,



the extent to which it would be unfair to a party or to a witness,



the importance of the evidence,



the nature of the proceeding, and



the availability of adjournment or some other order or direction. 



block\_026



The source emphasises the relevance of paras (a) and (b) in controlling unfair or unduly prolonged questioning. The provision therefore supplies an explicit statutory framework for discretionary case management decisions made under other parts of the Act. 



block\_026



Authority — Section 26 Powers Informed by Section 192 Factors



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: ASIC v Rich (2006) 201 FLR 206] \[TOPIC: exercise of court control powers by reference to s 192 factors] \[CONCEPTS: section 26, section 192, witness questioning control, leave factors, fair and efficient hearing, case management]



ASIC v Rich (2006) 201 FLR 206 is cited in the source for the proposition that when exercising the power under s 26 to control witness questioning, the court should consider the factors listed in s 192 of the Evidence Act. 



block\_026



The authority is significant because it connects the broad control power in s 26 with the structured evaluative criteria in s 192. This means that intervention in questioning should not be ad hoc; it should be guided by fairness, efficiency, the importance of the evidence, and the nature of the proceeding. 



block\_026



This authority supports the use of s 26 as a principled procedural safeguard rather than merely an unconfined discretion. 



block\_026



Example Tendency Notice



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[CASE: Police v FRS \[2020] TASMC 9 (25 May 2020)] \[TOPIC: example formulation of a tendency notice] \[CONCEPTS: tendency particulars, notice drafting, sexual interest tendency, non-consensual touching, overnight stays, bed touching, alone with complainant]



The source includes an example tendency notice drawn from Police v FRS \[2020] TASMC 9. The notice alleged tendencies that the accused:



had a sexual interest in and or engaged in sexual contact with his nieces aged about 16 years,



imposed himself sexually on his nieces without seeking or obtaining consent,



sexually touched his nieces under their clothing,



sexually touched his nieces when they stayed overnight,



sexually touched his nieces when they were in bed, and



sexually touched his nieces when they were alone. 



block\_026



The example is significant because it shows a tendency notice framed with factual particularity rather than at a high level of abstraction. It identifies the alleged tendency, the class of complainants, and the recurring circumstances said to give the evidence significant probative force. That level of specificity reflects the source’s broader point that adequate notice must identify both the tendency and the way it bears on facts in issue. 



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 — Cross-Examination of Unfavourable Witnesses



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 38]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 — Cross-Examination of Unfavourable Witness]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: cross-examination of unfavourable witness]

\[CONCEPTS: hostile witness, unfavourable witness, prior inconsistent statement, prosecution cross-examining own witness, credibility challenge, contradictory testimony, witness recanting, inconsistent evidence]



Section 38 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) permits a party to cross-examine its own witness if the court grants leave and the witness is considered unfavourable. The provision recognises that witnesses sometimes depart from earlier accounts or provide evidence damaging to the calling party.



Leave may be granted where the witness:



Gives evidence that is unfavourable to the party calling the witness.



Gives evidence inconsistent with a prior statement made by the witness.



Appears unwilling to make a genuine attempt to give evidence about matters within their knowledge.



An application for leave is usually made once the inconsistency or hostility becomes apparent during examination-in-chief. The court commonly requires that the witness be excused while the application is argued so the issue can be determined without influencing the witness’s further testimony.



If leave is granted, the party calling the witness may cross-examine the witness about:



The prior inconsistent statement.



Matters relevant to the witness’s credibility.



The reasons why the witness has changed their account.



In practice, the court will require the applicant to identify precisely which parts of a prior statement are inconsistent with the witness’s oral evidence. The scope of cross-examination will generally be limited to those specific inconsistencies unless broader leave is granted.



The provision is frequently used in criminal prosecutions where a complainant or key witness recants or alters their evidence at trial.



Credit Examination Under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38(3)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 38(3)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38(3)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: cross-examination as to witness credibility]

\[CONCEPTS: credibility challenge, motive to lie, bias, witness reliability, reconciliation motive, intimidation concerns, credibility attack, inconsistent testimony]



Section 38(3) of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) allows a party granted leave to cross-examine its own witness not only about prior inconsistent statements but also about matters affecting the witness’s credibility.



Where a witness has provided testimony inconsistent with a prior statement, the court may permit questioning directed toward the reasons for the inconsistency. These credibility inquiries may include matters such as:



Whether the witness has reconciled with the accused and seeks to protect them.



Whether the witness has been subject to pressure, intimidation, or influence.



Whether the witness has a motive to minimise or deny prior allegations.



Whether the witness has fabricated or altered their account.



The purpose of such questioning is not merely to demonstrate inconsistency but to explore why the witness has departed from their earlier account. Establishing a plausible reason for the inconsistency may assist the fact-finder in assessing which account is more reliable.



Courts typically require counsel to articulate the basis for expanding cross-examination into matters of credit. The questioning must remain relevant to the credibility of the witness and must not become an impermissible attack unrelated to the issue of reliability.



Notice and Procedural Requirements Under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38(6)



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 38(6)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38(6)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: notice requirement for unfavourable witness applications]

\[CONCEPTS: notice to opposing counsel, surprise witness evidence, fairness to defence, procedural fairness, application timing, evidentiary fairness, trial management]



Section 38(6) of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) requires the court to consider whether adequate notice has been given before granting leave to cross-examine a witness as unfavourable.



Notice may arise in two ways:



Prior notice to the opposing party that the applicant intends to seek leave under s 38.



Notification given during the hearing when an unexpected inconsistency emerges.



Where a party becomes aware during examination-in-chief that a witness has departed significantly from a prior statement, counsel may signal to the court that an application under s 38 is likely to be made. Such notification assists the court in determining that the application was made at the earliest reasonable opportunity.



The purpose of the notice requirement is to prevent procedural unfairness and ensure that opposing parties are not taken by surprise by a sudden change in the mode of questioning.



Courts generally adopt a pragmatic approach where the inconsistency arises unexpectedly during testimony. If the applicant demonstrates that the inconsistency could not reasonably have been anticipated, the lack of prior notice will rarely prevent the granting of leave.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 192 — Discretion to Grant Leave, Permission, or Direction



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 192]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 192]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: discretionary leave factors]

\[CONCEPTS: judicial discretion, leave of court, procedural fairness, hearing length, trial efficiency, evidentiary rulings, balancing fairness, evidentiary management]



Section 192 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) sets out general considerations that guide the court when deciding whether to grant leave, permission, or direction under the Act.



Subsection (2) provides that, without limiting the matters the court may consider, the court must take into account the following factors:



The extent to which granting leave would add unduly to, or shorten, the length of the hearing.



The extent to which granting leave would be unfair to a party or to a witness.



The importance of the evidence in relation to which the leave is sought.



The nature of the proceeding.



The power of the court to adjourn the hearing or otherwise deal with the evidence.



These considerations frequently arise in applications under Evidence Act provisions such as s 38 or s 32 where the court must determine whether a procedural step should be permitted.



The overarching purpose of the provision is to ensure that evidentiary rulings balance procedural efficiency with fairness to the parties and witnesses. Courts will generally favour allowing relevant evidence to be tested where doing so does not create significant prejudice or delay.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 43 — Proof of Prior Inconsistent Statements



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 43]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 43]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: prior inconsistent statements]

\[CONCEPTS: prior inconsistent statement, contradiction rule, confronting witness, proof of statement, credibility impeachment, statement verification, witness confrontation]



Section 43 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) regulates the use of prior inconsistent statements to contradict a witness’s evidence.



Before extrinsic evidence of a prior inconsistent statement can be admitted, the examining party must put the substance of the inconsistency to the witness. This generally involves:



Drawing the witness’s attention to the circumstances in which the earlier statement was made.



Identifying the relevant portion of the statement said to be inconsistent.



Giving the witness an opportunity to admit, deny, or explain the inconsistency.



If the witness denies making the statement or fails to acknowledge the inconsistency, the party may then call other evidence to prove that the statement was made.



In practice this commonly occurs by calling the police officer who recorded the original statement. Once the inconsistency is established, the prior statement may be admitted as evidence relevant to credibility and, depending on other evidentiary provisions, may also become admissible as evidence of the truth of its contents.



Authority — Aslett v The Queen



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Aslett v The Queen]

\[TOPIC: treatment of prior inconsistent statements]

\[CONCEPTS: prior inconsistent statement, proof of statement, contradiction evidence, witness credibility, statement tendering, impeachment of witness]



Aslett v The Queen is authority concerning the evidentiary use of prior inconsistent statements and the circumstances in which such statements may be tendered when a witness denies having made them.



The case confirms that where a witness denies making a prior statement, the party seeking to rely upon the statement may prove the making of the statement through another witness who was present when it was made or recorded.



Typically this involves calling the police officer who took the statement. That officer may give evidence regarding:



The circumstances in which the statement was taken.



Whether the witness read or adopted the statement.



Whether the statement was signed or otherwise verified.



Once the court is satisfied that the statement was made, the document may be admitted subject to the requirements of the Evidence Act. The case illustrates the procedural importance of establishing the authenticity of a prior statement before relying upon it to contradict a witness’s testimony.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 32 — Refreshing a Witness’s Memory



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 32]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 32]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: refreshing memory with contemporaneous documents]

\[CONCEPTS: refreshing memory, contemporaneous note, witness recollection, document verification, memory revival, recorded recollection, courtroom memory aid]



Section 32 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) allows a witness, with leave of the court, to use a document to revive their memory about a fact or opinion while giving evidence.



The provision recognises that witnesses may have difficulty recalling events that occurred some time earlier and allows reliance on contemporaneous records to assist recollection.



Under s 32(2), the court must consider:



Whether the witness will be able to recall the fact or opinion adequately without using the document.



Whether the document was made or verified by the witness when the events were fresh in the witness’s memory.



If those conditions are satisfied, the court may grant leave for the witness to consult the document.



Section 32(3) further provides that, once a witness has used a document to refresh their memory, the witness may read aloud the relevant portion of the document with leave of the court.



The provision is commonly used where a witness made a contemporaneous statement or note shortly after an event and later experiences genuine memory loss at trial.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66(3) — Representations Made in Contemplation of Litigation



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 66(3)]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66(3)]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: hearsay exclusion for litigation-motivated statements]

\[CONCEPTS: hearsay rule, prior representation, contemplation of litigation, witness purpose, evidentiary motive, litigation preparation]



Section 66(3) of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) limits the admissibility of certain hearsay representations where the representation was made for the purpose of indicating the evidence the person would give in an Australian proceeding.



The subsection reflects the concern that statements created specifically for litigation may lack the reliability ordinarily associated with spontaneous or contemporaneous statements.



A key issue when applying s 66(3) is determining the purpose of the person who made the representation. The exclusion only applies where the maker intended the statement to be used as evidence in a proceeding.



Statements made for other purposes—such as explaining events to police officers immediately after an incident—may not fall within the exclusion if the primary purpose was not preparation for litigation.



Authority — Saunders \[2004] TASSC 95



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Saunders \[2004] TASSC 95]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of Evidence Act s 66(3)]

\[CONCEPTS: hearsay exception, litigation purpose, representation maker’s intention, evidentiary purpose, admissibility test]



Saunders \[2004] TASSC 95 considered the interpretation of Evidence Act provisions governing hearsay representations made in contemplation of legal proceedings.



The Supreme Court of Tasmania held that the relevant inquiry under s 66(3) concerns the purpose of the person who made the representation rather than the purpose of the person who received it. The question is whether the maker intended the representation to indicate what evidence they would later give in a court proceeding.



This distinction is significant where a statement is made to a police officer shortly after an incident. A person describing events to police may be motivated by the immediate circumstances—such as reporting an offence or explaining events—rather than by a conscious intention to prepare evidence for litigation.



Accordingly, statements made in such circumstances will not necessarily fall within the exclusion in s 66(3).



Authority — Maher v Maynard \[1991] TASSC 8



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Maher v Maynard \[1991] TASSC 8]

\[TOPIC: reopening the prosecution case]

\[CONCEPTS: reopening case, prosecutorial discretion, missing evidence, trial fairness, procedural discretion]



Maher v Maynard \[1991] TASSC 8 addresses the circumstances in which a prosecution may seek leave to reopen its case after failing to adduce necessary evidence.



The case confirms that reopening is a discretionary power exercised cautiously by the court. Leave will generally only be granted where the omission arose from circumstances that could not reasonably have been foreseen by the prosecution during the presentation of its case.



Courts consider several factors when determining whether to permit reopening, including:



Whether the omission was inadvertent or tactical.



The significance of the evidence sought to be introduced.



Whether reopening would cause unfair prejudice to the accused.



Whether the interests of justice favour admitting the additional evidence.



The authority illustrates that the prosecution cannot ordinarily reopen its case merely to remedy an oversight that should have been addressed during the presentation of the evidence.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106 — Counter-Credibility Evidence After Denial of Prior Statement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 106]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106 — Proof of Prior Inconsistent Statement]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: counter-credibility evidence to prove prior inconsistent statement]

\[CONCEPTS: counter-credibility evidence, prior inconsistent statement, proving statement through another witness, impeachment of witness, contradiction evidence, police officer evidence, denial of statement, credibility attack]



Section 106 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) permits a party, in defined circumstances, to adduce evidence relevant only to a witness’s credibility after the witness has been cross-examined about a matter affecting credibility. In the context of a prior inconsistent statement, the provision is used after the witness has been confronted under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 43 and denies making the earlier representation or does not accept the inconsistency.



The practical operation described in the source is that, once the witness has been asked about the statement and the circumstances of making it, the prosecution may call another witness, usually the police officer who took the statement, to prove that the prior statement was in fact made. That evidence is admissible as counter-credibility evidence because it goes to whether the witness is truthful or reliable in denying the earlier account.



The officer’s evidence will commonly address:



When and where the statement was made.



The witness’s demeanour, clarity, and apparent state at the time.



Whether questions were asked and answered.



Whether the witness made spontaneous representations.



Whether the contents were recorded accurately and adopted or signed by the witness.



In this use, s 106 operates as the gateway for evidence contradicting the witness’s denial and proving the making of the prior inconsistent statement. 



block\_027



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 60 — Credibility Evidence Becoming Proof of the Facts Asserted



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 60]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 60]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: non-hearsay use expanding to hearsay use]

\[CONCEPTS: hearsay exception, prior representation, truth of contents, credibility evidence, prior inconsistent statement, non-hearsay purpose, proof of assault, statement used for truth]



Section 60 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides that evidence admitted for a non-hearsay purpose may also be used as evidence of the truth of the facts asserted in the representation. In the context of a prior inconsistent statement, this provision is significant because a statement first admitted to attack credibility can become evidence that the events described in the statement actually occurred.



The source material treats this as the final step in the prior inconsistent statement sequence. Once the witness has been confronted under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 43 and the prior statement is proved, often through another witness under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 106, the statement is not confined to showing that the witness is inconsistent. By operation of s 60, it may also be used substantively as evidence of the representation itself.



This matters where the witness:



Adopts the prior account as true.



Accepts making the statement but says the events did not happen.



Says they cannot now remember what happened.



Denies making the statement but the statement is later proved.



The source notes that, even where the statement first appears relevant only to credibility, once admitted it may become evidence of the truth of the allegation, such as that an assault occurred. The prosecution use described depends on this section to convert a credibility contradiction into substantive proof.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Exclusion of Unfairly Prejudicial Prosecution Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 137]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 — Memory Loss Witness]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: exclusion of prejudicial prosecution evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, probative value, prosecution evidence, exclusion discretion, memory loss witness, inability to test evidence, criminal proceeding, evidentiary balance]



Section 137 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) requires the court in a criminal proceeding to refuse to admit prosecution evidence if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant.



In the source material, s 137 is identified as a live objection where the prosecution seeks to rely on a prior representation after a witness claims memory loss or is unable to engage meaningfully with the facts asserted. The concern is that the statement may carry apparent persuasive force while the defence has limited practical capacity to challenge the underlying events through cross-examination of the maker.



The specific risk identified is that a claimed lack of memory may prevent detailed testing of the truth of the prior representation. That circumstance may reduce the real probative value of the statement, because the trier of fact cannot confidently treat the lack of challenge as confirming reliability. At the same time, there may be unfair prejudice if the statement is used as substantive evidence despite the accused being unable to test its details effectively.



The section therefore requires a balancing exercise:



Assess the actual probative value of the representation in light of the witness’s present inability or refusal to engage with it.



Assess the danger that the statement will be given disproportionate weight.



Exclude the evidence if unfair prejudice outweighs probative value.



The source treats s 137 as a necessary limiting principle on the use of prior representations in criminal prosecutions.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 42 — Leading Questions Where Witness Is Effectively Aligned With the Opposing Party



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 42]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 42]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: limiting leading questions to partisan witness]

\[CONCEPTS: leading questions, partisan witness, witness aligned with defence, examination control, witness echoing counsel, hostile witness dynamics, unfair prompting, witness management]



Section 42 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) regulates the use of leading questions in examination-in-chief and re-examination, and may become relevant where a witness called by one party is in substance aligned with the opposing party.



The source material identifies a tactical use of s 42 where a witness is plainly “in the defence camp” and the prosecution expects to need Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38. The concern expressed is that ordinary cross-examination by defence, using heavily leading questions, may produce little reliable factual material because the witness is merely affirming propositions put by counsel rather than giving an independent account.



In that setting, an application under s 42 may be used to ask the court to control the form of questioning and reduce the extent to which the witness simply adopts counsel’s version. The rationale is that, if the witness is effectively partisan, leading questions may have little evidentiary value and may distort the assessment of what the witness actually knows or remembers.



The source frames this as a practical trial-management measure where the witness is not functioning as a neutral source of evidence. The object is to ensure that the fact-finder receives genuine testimony rather than answers shaped entirely by counsel’s propositions.



Authority — J v Hamilton \[2007] TASSC 105



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: J v Hamilton \[2007] TASSC 105]

\[TOPIC: prior inconsistent material adduced through police officer before witness evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: prior inconsistent statement, police officer called first, statement for identification, pre-emptive proof, witness expected to be unfavourable, hearsay sequencing, trial practice]



J v Hamilton \[2007] TASSC 105 is referred to in the source as an example of trial practice in which a police officer was called before the witness in order to place an interview or prior account before the court, anticipating that the witness would later prove unfavourable.



The source notes that this occurred at first instance, was consented to by the defence, and was not a live ground of appeal. For that reason, the case is not treated as binding authority establishing a general rule of admissibility. Its significance is narrower: it demonstrates that, in practice, courts have permitted a police officer to be called first so that the prior account can be marked for identification and procedural groundwork laid before the witness gives inconsistent evidence.



The doctrinal value of the reference is therefore procedural rather than substantive. It supports the proposition that, where a witness is expected to depart from an earlier account, the prosecution may seek to structure the evidence in a way that avoids later proof difficulties, provided the ordinary evidentiary rules are still satisfied.



The source emphasises that the material remains only marked for identification unless and until an admissible pathway is established. The statement does not become evidence merely because it is tendered for a preliminary purpose.



Authority — Police v Endlay \[2020] TASMC 13



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Police v Endlay \[2020] TASMC 13]

\[TOPIC: limited weight of answers from partisan witness under leading cross-examination]

\[CONCEPTS: partisan witness, leading questions, witness as mere echo of counsel, credit and reliability, defence-aligned witness, reduced weight, cross-examination limits]



Police v Endlay \[2020] TASMC 13 supports the principle that answers given by a witness who is plainly aligned with one party may attract little or no weight where the questioning is entirely leading.



The passage quoted in the source records that the court placed no weight on answers given by the complainant during cross-examination because the questions were wholly leading and the witness was a partisan witness entirely favourable to the defence case. The court described the witness, adopting a recognised evidence text, as functioning as “a mere echo of counsel”.



The principle supported by the case is not that leading questions are impermissible in cross-examination. Rather, it is that the weight and reliability of the answers may be significantly diminished where:



The witness is obviously partisan.



The witness is effectively acting as a witness for the opposing party.



The answers are produced entirely through suggestive questioning.



The court cannot be confident the answers reflect independent recollection.



This authority supports applications to control questioning or to resist giving substantial weight to answers extracted from a witness who has ceased to function as an independent source of evidence.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 — Procedural Sequence for Prior Inconsistent Statement Applications



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 38]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 — Practical Workflow for PIS Application]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: practical workflow for unfavourable witness application]

\[CONCEPTS: prosecution workflow, hostile witness process, prior inconsistent statement sequence, witness recall, mark for identification, voir dire, application for leave, tactical examination]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 applications involving a prior inconsistent statement follow a practical sequence designed to preserve admissibility and fairness. The source material sets out a repeated workflow for handling a witness who departs from an earlier account.



The sequence is:



Exhaust examination-in-chief and note all departures, omissions, or signs that the witness is becoming unfavourable.



Signal to the court, when the significant inconsistency emerges, that an application under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 is likely. This assists with notice and fairness.



Ask that the witness be excused so the application can be argued in the witness’s absence.



Identify the basis for the application under s 38, including the precise inconsistency and, if relevant, the need to cross-examine on credit under s 38(3).



Hand up the prior statement for the purposes of the application and take the court directly to the inconsistent paragraphs.



Address discretionary factors under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 192 and procedural notice matters under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38(6).



If leave is granted, recall the witness, prove the circumstances of the making of the statement, and confront the witness with the inconsistency in compliance with Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 43.



If necessary, call the police officer or other witness to prove the prior statement and tender it through the available evidentiary pathway.



The source treats this sequence as the standard method for converting an inconsistent prior account into admissible evidence while preserving procedural fairness.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 and s 43 — Voir Dire Procedure Where Witness Denies Making Prior Statement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: ss 38, 43]

\[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) ss 38, 43]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: proof of denied prior statement through voir dire]

\[CONCEPTS: voir dire, denial of statement, police proof witness, balance of probabilities, statement authenticity, witness adoption, prior representation proof, marked for identification]



Where a witness denies making a prior statement, the source material describes a voir dire process to prove the existence and authenticity of the statement before it is used as part of the evidentiary case.



The source indicates that only a balance of probabilities standard is required for the court to be satisfied, at the interlocutory stage, that the prior inconsistent statement was made for the purpose of advancing the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 38 application. If the defence challenges the existence or authenticity of the statement, the prosecution may call the police officer who took it on the voir dire.



The officer’s evidence should cover:



When and where the statement was taken.



The witness’s emotional state, demeanour, and speech clarity.



Whether questions were asked and answered.



Whether any representations were spontaneous.



Whether the document accurately recorded what was said.



Whether the witness read, adopted, signed, or dated the statement.



The statement should be marked for identification pending a ruling. If the court is satisfied that the witness made the statement, the prosecution may proceed to confront the witness with it and, if necessary, tender it through the officer’s evidence. The source treats this as the appropriate method where the witness denies ever making the out-of-court representation.



Privilege Against Self-Incrimination — Statutory Framework



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: statutory privilege against self-incrimination in court proceedings]

\[CONCEPTS: privilege against self-incrimination, witness protection, compelled testimony, Evidence Act s128, immunity certificates, refusal to answer questions, incriminating evidence]
