Privilege against self-incrimination under uniform evidence legislation operates through statutory procedures allowing a witness to object to giving evidence that may incriminate them. The privilege applies where a witness objects to giving particular evidence or evidence on a particular matter because the evidence may tend to prove that the witness has committed an offence or is liable to a civil penalty. 



block\_028



The provision creates a structured mechanism that balances two competing objectives:



Protecting individuals from being compelled to incriminate themselves; and



Allowing courts to receive relevant evidence where appropriate.



The statutory scheme establishes a process whereby the court must assess whether the witness has reasonable grounds for the objection. If such grounds exist, the witness is informed that they are not required to give the evidence unless ordered by the court. The court may nevertheless require the evidence to be given where the interests of justice require it, provided statutory safeguards are satisfied.



A key feature of the statutory regime is the availability of a certificate protecting the witness from the future use of the evidence. Where a witness gives evidence after invoking the privilege, the court may issue a certificate granting both use immunity and derivative use immunity in later proceedings.



The statutory framework replaces the common law privilege within its sphere of operation. Where a witness objects to giving evidence in court on self-incrimination grounds, the statutory procedure governs the process and determines whether the evidence may ultimately be compelled.



Evidence Act — Objection Procedure for Self-Incriminating Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: procedure when witness objects to giving self-incriminating evidence]

\[CONCEPTS: objection to testimony, incriminating evidence objection, witness rights in court, privilege claim process, court warning obligations, certificate procedure]



When a witness objects to giving evidence on the ground that it may tend to incriminate them, the court must follow a defined statutory procedure designed to safeguard the privilege while allowing judicial assessment of the objection. 



block\_028



The process generally involves the following steps:



The witness raises an objection to giving particular evidence or evidence on a particular matter on the basis that it may tend to prove the witness committed an offence or is liable to a civil penalty.



The court must determine whether reasonable grounds exist for the objection.



If reasonable grounds are established, the court must inform the witness that:



they are not required to give the evidence unless required by the court;



a certificate will be granted if they give the evidence either voluntarily or after being compelled; and



the certificate protects the witness against the use of that evidence in future proceedings.



The procedure ensures that a witness is properly informed of the consequences of asserting or waiving the privilege. Courts frequently determine privilege issues outside the presence of a jury where necessary to avoid prejudicial inference arising from the claim.



This structured process ensures that witnesses receive legal protection while preserving the ability of courts to obtain relevant evidence where justice requires it.



Evidence Act — “Reasonable Grounds for Objection”



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: requirement for reasonable grounds when claiming privilege]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds test, risk of prosecution, judicial assessment of privilege, incrimination risk evaluation, witness burden of proof, balance of probabilities]



The statutory privilege against self-incrimination only applies where the witness has reasonable grounds for objecting to giving the evidence. The court must determine whether such grounds exist before the privilege can operate. 



block\_028



A witness’s subjective belief that answering may incriminate them is not sufficient on its own. The court must be able to identify objective grounds supporting the claim. This determination is made judicially and may require evidence demonstrating the existence of a real risk of incrimination.



Relevant considerations may include:



Whether the evidence could expose the witness to criminal prosecution.



Whether the evidence may form a link in a chain of evidence leading to prosecution.



Whether any limitation period or legal barrier makes prosecution impossible.



Whether the answer could realistically lead to investigation or prosecution.



The burden of establishing reasonable grounds rests on the witness claiming the privilege. Under uniform evidence legislation, the applicable standard of proof for this determination is the balance of probabilities.



The concept of reasonable grounds reflects the balance between protecting witnesses from self-incrimination and preventing the privilege from being invoked in circumstances where no realistic risk of prosecution exists.



Authority — R v Bikic \[2001] NSWCCA 537



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v Bikic \[2001] NSWCCA 537]

\[TOPIC: interpretation of reasonable grounds for privilege claims]

\[CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds privilege test, risk of prosecution requirement, judicial assessment of incrimination risk, witness objection standard, privilege against self-incrimination case law]



R v Bikic clarified the meaning of “reasonable grounds for objection” in the context of privilege against self-incrimination. 



block\_028



The court emphasised that the statutory wording refers to reasonable grounds for objecting to giving evidence, rather than reasonable grounds for concluding that the evidence will actually prove the commission of an offence.



The decision confirms that:



The witness need not demonstrate that the evidence would definitely establish criminal liability.



It is sufficient if the evidence could realistically place the witness in legal jeopardy.



Courts must examine whether answering the question could expose the witness to prosecution or contribute to an investigative chain leading to prosecution.



The judgment reinforces the principle that privilege protects against real risks of criminal liability rather than speculative or remote possibilities.



Fraud — Concept of Deception



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: deception as a core element of fraud offences]

\[CONCEPTS: deception definition, misleading conduct, fraudulent misrepresentation, dishonest trick, misleading another person, deceptive conduct in fraud]



Deception is a fundamental concept in fraud-related offences and refers to conduct intended to mislead another person in order to obtain a benefit or cause loss. 



block\_028



In general terms, deception occurs where a person says or does something deliberately designed to mislead another person. The deception must be intentional or reckless in the sense that the offender was aware that their conduct may mislead but proceeded regardless.



Examples of deceptive conduct include:



Misrepresenting one’s intentions in order to obtain money or goods.



Causing a computer system to respond in a way the person is not authorised to cause, such as entering another person’s password to access their account.



The concept of deception in fraud offences captures both verbal misrepresentations and technological manipulation designed to produce misleading outcomes.



The mental element associated with deception may involve either deliberate intent to mislead or recklessness regarding whether the conduct is deceptive.



Fraud — Dishonesty



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: dishonesty as an element of fraud]

\[CONCEPTS: dishonesty test, standards of ordinary people, criminal dishonesty, subjective knowledge of dishonesty, fraudulent intent, community standards]



Dishonesty is a central element of fraud offences and is determined according to the standards of ordinary people combined with the accused’s awareness of those standards. 



block\_028



The determination of dishonesty involves two aspects:



Whether the conduct would be considered dishonest according to the standards of ordinary members of the community; and



Whether the accused knew that the conduct would be regarded as dishonest by those standards.



This dual evaluation ensures that criminal liability is not imposed solely on the basis of unusual personal moral standards held by an accused person. Instead, dishonesty reflects both community expectations and the accused’s awareness of those expectations.



The assessment of dishonesty is ultimately a question of fact for the tribunal of fact, whether a jury or a magistrate.



Criminal Code — Evidence Relating to Intent to Deceive or Defraud



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s255A]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s255A]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: proof of intent to deceive or defraud]

\[CONCEPTS: fraud intent evidence, proving dishonest intention, intent to defraud rule, deception offences evidence, no need to identify victim]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s255A provides an evidentiary rule governing proof of intent in offences involving deception or fraud. 



block\_028



The provision states that in the trial of an offence under the relevant chapter where an intent to deceive or defraud is an element, it is not necessary to prove that the accused intended to deceive or defraud any particular person.



This rule recognises that fraudulent schemes often involve indeterminate victims or operate in ways that make identifying a specific intended victim difficult. The prosecution therefore does not need to establish that the accused targeted a particular individual.



Instead, the prosecution must prove that the accused acted with the general intent to deceive or defraud someone.



The provision simplifies the evidentiary burden in fraud prosecutions by allowing proof of a general fraudulent intention rather than requiring identification of a specific victim.



Authority — Murphy v R (1987) Tas R 178



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Murphy v R \[1987] TASSC 15; (1987) Tas R 178]

\[TOPIC: meaning of financial advantage in fraud offences]

\[CONCEPTS: financial advantage definition, services money property benefit, fraud advantage interpretation, benefit obtained dishonestly]



Murphy v R confirmed that the concept of a “financial advantage” in fraud offences includes services, money, or property. 



block\_028



The decision recognises that fraudulent conduct may produce a wide range of benefits and should not be restricted to direct monetary gain. A financial advantage may therefore include the acquisition of goods, services, or property obtained through deceptive conduct.



This broad interpretation ensures that fraud provisions capture the full spectrum of benefits that may be obtained through dishonest conduct.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128 — Judicial Obligations After Reasonable Grounds Are Established



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 128(3)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(3)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: court obligations once reasonable grounds for self-incrimination objection are found] \[CONCEPTS: self-incrimination warning, court must inform witness, privilege procedure, certificate advice, compelled evidence warning, witness protection, s 128(3), judicial duty]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(3) requires the court to give specified information to a witness once it determines that there are reasonable grounds for an objection based on self-incrimination. 



block\_028



Once reasonable grounds are established, the court must inform the witness:



that the witness need not give the evidence unless required to do so by the court;



that a certificate will be given if the witness gives the evidence willingly without being required to do so, or gives it after being required to do so; and



of the effect of the certificate.



This stage is central to the statutory scheme because it converts the privilege from a general objection into a structured judicial process. The witness is not left to guess the consequences of answering or refusing. Instead, the court must explain both the immediate protection against compulsory disclosure and the future protection that a certificate may provide.



The source also notes that where privilege issues arise before a jury, prudence may require the application and any warning process to occur in the absence of the jury to avoid unfair prejudice or inappropriate inferences. That procedural caution supports the protective purpose of the section.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128 — Judicial Power to Compel Evidence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 128(4)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: court power to require self-incriminating evidence despite objection] \[CONCEPTS: compelled testimony, judicial discretion, interests of justice, foreign law limit, self-incrimination exception, mandatory certificate, forcing witness to answer, s 128(4)]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4) allows a court to require a witness to give self-incriminating evidence despite a valid objection, but only where the statutory conditions are satisfied.



The power is discretionary and operates as a limited statutory abrogation of the privilege against self-incrimination. Two conditions must both be met:



the evidence must not tend to prove that the witness has committed an offence, or is liable to a civil penalty, under a law of a foreign country; and



the court must be satisfied that the interests of justice require the witness to give the evidence.



If the court compels the witness to answer under s 128(4), the statutory protection does not disappear. The scheme instead requires the grant of a certificate, and that certificate carries the immunity described elsewhere in s 128.



This provision shows that the privilege is not absolute in court proceedings governed by the Act. The statute preserves the protection against later use of the evidence, but permits compulsion where the evidence is sufficiently important and the risk cannot be left unresolved consistently with the interests of justice.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4)(a) — Foreign Offence Limitation on Compulsion



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 128(4)(a)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4)(a)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: court cannot compel evidence that may incriminate under foreign law] \[CONCEPTS: foreign offence risk, foreign civil penalty, no guaranteed overseas immunity, limits on compulsion, overseas prosecution risk, s 128 foreign law, certificate not binding abroad]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4)(a) prevents a court from compelling evidence where the evidence may tend to prove that the witness has committed an offence, or is liable to a civil penalty, under a foreign law. 



block\_028



This limitation reflects the practical boundary of Australian judicial power. An Australian court may issue a certificate protecting the witness against later use of the evidence in the jurisdictions covered by the legislation, but it cannot guarantee that a foreign court or foreign regulator will recognise that protection.



Because the certificate may have no effect overseas, the statute does not permit an Australian court to override a legitimate claim to privilege where foreign criminal or civil exposure remains live. The point is protective rather than technical: compulsion is withheld because the court cannot fully secure the witness from the consequences of answering.



The source cites R v Lodhi as an example of judicial consideration of this issue, where the court was not satisfied that the proposed evidence did not tend to prove an offence under foreign law. The rule therefore creates a hard limit on the otherwise broad judicial discretion to require answers in the interests of justice.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4)(b) — “Interests of Justice” Test



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 128(4)(b)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4)(b)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: interests of justice requirement for compelling self-incriminating evidence] \[CONCEPTS: interests of justice, compelling witness, balancing exercise, importance of evidence, fairness to parties, reliability concerns, prosecution risk, s 128(4)(b)]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(4)(b) requires the court to be satisfied that the interests of justice require the witness to give the evidence before compulsion may occur. 



block\_028



This is not a mere relevance test. The source indicates that relevance alone is insufficient because the statutory scheme intrudes on a common law protection against self-incrimination. The court must therefore undertake a broader evaluative judgment about whether justice positively requires the evidence to be given.



Relevant factors identified in the source include:



the importance of the evidence in the proceeding;



the likelihood that the evidence will be unreliable even if a certificate is granted;



whether the proceeding is criminal and, if so, whether the party seeking the evidence is the prosecution or the defence;



the nature of the offence, cause of action, defence, or alleged liability;



the likelihood of later prosecution or penalty proceedings;



any unfairness created by compelling the evidence; and



the means available to limit publication or prejudice.



The source also notes that this question may appropriately be assessed topic by topic rather than globally across an entire body of evidence.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128 — Effect of a Certificate



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 128(7), s 128(9)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(7), s 128(9)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: use immunity and derivative use immunity under self-incrimination certificate] \[CONCEPTS: certificate immunity, derivative use immunity, use immunity, later proceedings, retrial exception, false evidence exception, protected testimony, s 128 certificate effect]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128 gives a witness who receives a certificate both use immunity and derivative use immunity in later proceedings within the statutory reach of the legislation. 



block\_028



The protection extends to:



the evidence given by the witness and covered by the certificate; and



information, documents, or things obtained directly or indirectly because the witness gave that evidence.



The immunity is broad, but it is not absolute. The source identifies two key statutory exceptions. First, the protection does not apply in criminal proceedings about the falsity of the evidence, such as perjury-related use. Second, the protection does not prevent use in a retrial of the accused for the same offence, or in a trial for an offence arising from the same facts, reflecting the statutory response to Cornwell v R.



The source also notes that the certificate regime was expanded so that protection extends beyond ordinary courts to certain bodies authorised to hear, receive, and examine evidence. The practical effect is that the certificate is designed to give real future protection, not merely symbolic reassurance at the time the witness answers.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(8) — Challenge to Validity of a Certificate



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 128(8)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(8)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: certificate remains effective despite challenge to validity] \[CONCEPTS: certificate validity, challenge to certificate, continuing immunity, witness certainty, evidentiary ruling distinction, self-incrimination protection, s 128(8)]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(8) provides that a certificate remains effective regardless of the outcome of any challenge to its validity. 



block\_028



This rule addresses a practical and policy problem identified in the source material. A witness who gives self-incriminating evidence under the statutory procedure must be able to rely on the promised protection in future proceedings. If the protection could later disappear because the certificate was challenged, lost, or shown to have been issued irregularly, the statutory assurance would be undermined.



The provision therefore treats a certificate differently from an ordinary evidentiary ruling. Its function is not merely procedural management within the current hearing. Its function is to secure reliable future immunity so that the witness can answer under the statutory process without fear that the protection will later collapse.



The source links this rule to concerns raised in Cornwell v R, including the absurdity of denying protection merely because copies of the certificate were lost. Section 128(8) is therefore directed to certainty, continuity, and preservation of the protective purpose of the privilege regime.



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(10) — Exception for Evidence of an Accused on Facts in Issue



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 128(10)] \[CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(10)] \[TYPE: evidentiary rule] \[TOPIC: accused cannot claim s 128 for evidence of act or state of mind in issue] \[CONCEPTS: accused evidence exception, facts in issue, state of mind in issue, act in issue, privilege limitation, criminal defendant testimony, voir dire exception, s 128(10)]



Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 128(10) excludes the statutory privilege where, in criminal proceedings, an accused gives evidence about an act or state of mind that is itself a fact in issue.



The rule means that an accused cannot use s 128 to obtain certificate protection for testimony that directly addresses the central matters the tribunal must decide, such as whether the accused performed the act alleged or possessed the relevant mental state.



The source explains that the exclusion is not confined to direct admissions. It also extends to evidence of surrounding facts from which the doing of the act or the existence of the state of mind may be inferred.



The source further notes an important qualification. Section 189(6) preserves the privilege in a voir dire. Because a preliminary question under s 189 concerns whether evidence should be admitted, the exclusion in s 128(10) does not apply there. An accused may therefore still rely on the privilege when giving evidence on a voir dire.



The subsection marks a deliberate boundary: an accused cannot selectively advance their trial case on matters in issue while also claiming the special protection of the certificate regime.



Authority — Cornwell v R (2007) 231 CLR 260; \[2007] HCA 12



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Cornwell v R (2007) 231 CLR 260; \[2007] HCA 12] \[TOPIC: scope of certificate protection and accused evidence exception] \[CONCEPTS: retrial exception, proceeding meaning, certificate validity, accused facts in issue, self-incrimination certificate, admissions at retrial, High Court authority]



Cornwell v R is authority for several limits and structural features of the s 128 certificate regime.



The source identifies three principles drawn from the case.



First, the High Court held that a retrial was not a “proceeding” to which the earlier version of s 128 applied, with the result that admissions made under certificate protection at the first trial could be tendered at the retrial. The later enactment of s 128(9) preserved that practical result.



Second, the case supports the proposition that the exception for evidence of an accused concerning an act or state of mind in issue extends beyond direct evidence. It also covers evidence of facts from which the act or state of mind may be inferred.



Third, the source records the Court’s concern about situations where a party wishes to give evidence for forensic advantage but seeks certificate protection at the same time. That concern informed later debate about whether such a person truly “objects” to giving the evidence within the meaning of s 128(1).



The case is therefore a leading authority on the boundaries of certificate protection rather than on the existence of the privilege in general.



Authority — DPP v Peters (2019) 59 VR 203



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: DPP v Peters (2019) 59 VR 203] \[TOPIC: statutory privilege confined to court testimony and past offending] \[CONCEPTS: court evidence only, testimonial disclosure, common law displaced, past offence only, perjury excluded, privilege scope, witness in court]



DPP v Peters confirms that the statutory privilege under s 128 is confined to witnesses giving or about to give evidence in court, and that within that field the statute displaces the common law. 



block\_028



The source identifies two main principles. First, s 128 applies to testimonial disclosure at a hearing rather than to every context in which self-incrimination concerns may arise. Outside that setting, the common law privilege may still operate.



Second, the decision establishes that s 128 is directed to evidence tending to prove past offending. It does not apply to a situation where the witness would commit an offence only by giving the evidence itself, such as by answering untruthfully and thereby committing perjury.



The case therefore narrows the scope of the statutory privilege in two ways: by forum and by subject matter. It is about court testimony, not all compulsory processes, and it protects against exposure for past offences or civil penalties, not future wrongdoing constituted by the act of testifying.



Authority — Song v Ying (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; \[2010] NSWCA 237



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Song v Ying (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; \[2010] NSWCA 237] \[TOPIC: no s 128 certificate for party answering own lawyer in examination-in-chief or re-examination] \[CONCEPTS: no compulsion, own lawyer questions, examination-in-chief, re-examination, party witness, certificate unavailable, objecting to evidence, forensic choice]



Song v Ying is authority for the proposition that s 128 does not apply where a party gives evidence in examination-in-chief or re-examination in response to questions from that party’s own lawyer. 



block\_028



The reasoning identified in the source is that such evidence is not compelled. A party who wishes to give evidence, but only with the protection of a certificate, is not truly objecting to giving the evidence in the statutory sense because there is no element of compulsion or potential compulsion making the objection apt.



The source also notes a broader proposition from the case: a compellable witness may object under s 128 at any stage where compulsion exists, including examination-in-chief and re-examination. The critical distinction is not the formal stage of evidence, but whether the witness is being compelled to answer.



The case is important because it draws a line between protective privilege and strategic forensic choice. The certificate regime exists to answer compulsion, not to provide immunity for evidence a party voluntarily elects to lead through their own advocate.



Authority — Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v Commonwealth (Ruling No 5) \[2018] VSC 338



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v Commonwealth (Ruling No 5) \[2018] VSC 338] \[TOPIC: objective assessment of reasonable grounds and real prospect of prosecution] \[CONCEPTS: objective privilege test, reasonable grounds, real prospect of prosecution, limitations periods, trail of investigation, not mere apprehension, judicial evidence assessment]



Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v Commonwealth (Ruling No 5) confirms that a witness’s personal apprehension of incrimination is insufficient by itself to establish reasonable grounds for objection under s 128. 



block\_028



The source records that the court must be able to see objective reasonable grounds for the objection, and that this may require evidence. The question is whether there is a real prospect of criminal prosecution if the evidence is given, not whether the witness merely feels exposed.



Relevant matters include whether the answer could directly incriminate the witness, whether it could create a trail of investigation leading to prosecution, and whether practical barriers such as limitation periods make prosecution unrealistic.



The case therefore reinforces that the statutory privilege is protective but disciplined. It is not triggered by speculative fear. It is triggered by a demonstrable forensic risk that answering may place the witness in real legal jeopardy.



Authority — Ross v Internet Wines (2004) 60 NSWLR 436; \[2004] NSWCA 195



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Ross v Internet Wines (2004) 60 NSWLR 436; \[2004] NSWCA 195] \[TOPIC: limits on applying s 128 to affidavits and pre-trial procedure] \[CONCEPTS: affidavit privilege, pre-trial stage, compelled affidavit, no artificial procedure, common law privilege, deponent objection, documentary evidence]



Ross v Internet Wines is authority on the relationship between s 128 and affidavit or pre-trial processes. 



block\_028



The source uses the case for two propositions. First, where a witness is compelled to make an affidavit, later authority accepted that the witness may raise a self-incrimination objection without first giving oral evidence. Second, s 128 does not apply generally at a pre-trial stage by force of s 131A, and a court should not invent an artificial procedure to transform a person into a witness or deponent merely to trigger the statutory regime.



The decision therefore preserves the distinction between the statutory privilege, which is tied to evidence-giving in court, and the common law privilege, which continues to govern outside that field unless legislation provides otherwise.



The case is important for document-heavy proceedings because it warns against distorting procedure in order either to evade or to manufacture the operation of s 128.



Authority — Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex (1993) 178 CLR 477; \[1993] HCA 74



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex (1993) 178 CLR 477; \[1993] HCA 74] \[TOPIC: corporations cannot claim privilege but officers may claim it personally] \[CONCEPTS: corporate privilege denied, officer personal privilege, body corporate, self-incrimination, representative capacity, personal capacity, company officer evidence]



Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex supports the distinction between a corporation and an individual officer for privilege purposes. 



block\_028



The source states that while a body corporate may not claim the privilege against self-incrimination, an individual officer may claim the privilege on their own behalf when speaking in a personal capacity rather than purely as a representative of the corporation.



The principle matters in fraud, regulatory, and corporate offending contexts where the same factual event may expose both a company and an officer to different forms of legal liability. The inability of the corporation to invoke the privilege does not strip the human officer of the personal protection of the privilege when the evidence may incriminate that individual.



The decision therefore reinforces that the privilege is personal in character even where the surrounding litigation involves corporate structures or corporate documents.



Authority — Versace v Monte \[2001] FCA 1572



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Versace v Monte \[2001] FCA 1572] \[TOPIC: no adverse inference from claiming privilege against self-incrimination] \[CONCEPTS: no adverse inference, privilege claim, tribunal approach, silence not evidence of guilt, witness protection, self-incrimination claim, evidentiary fairness]



Versace v Monte supports the principle that no adverse inference should be drawn merely from the fact that a witness claims privilege against self-incrimination under s 128. 



block\_028



The source aligns the statutory position with the common law approach. A claim of privilege is an invocation of a legal protection, not an admission of wrongdoing. Accordingly, the mere fact that the privilege is claimed should not be treated as probative of guilt, liability, or dishonesty.



The principle protects the utility of the privilege itself. If tribunals could infer guilt from the assertion of the privilege, the statutory protection would be undermined because a witness would face forensic punishment merely for relying on a lawful safeguard.



The source separately notes a more qualified issue in civil proceedings concerning failures to call evidence and possible Jones v Dunkel consequences, but the primary rule supported by Versace v Monte is that the claim of privilege itself does not justify an adverse inference.



Authority — Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd (2015) 49 VR 402; \[2015] VSCA 137



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd (2015) 49 VR 402; \[2015] VSCA 137] \[TOPIC: privilege does not justify refusal to enter witness box and may not explain failure to call evidence in civil case] \[CONCEPTS: must enter witness box, hearing only, testimonial disclosure, civil proceedings, Jones v Dunkel, privilege limitation, refusal to testify]



Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd supports two propositions about the operation of s 128. 



block\_028



First, the source records that a witness cannot refuse to enter the witness box on the basis of the privilege. The section operates when the witness is giving evidence and objects to particular evidence or evidence on a particular matter. It does not create a right to avoid being sworn or called altogether.



Second, the source notes that in civil proceedings the assertion of s 128 may not necessarily provide an adequate explanation for a failure to call evidence, so that the reasoning associated with Jones v Dunkel may still arise depending on the circumstances.



The case therefore shows the privilege is targeted rather than absolute. It protects against compelled incriminating answers, but it does not entitle a witness to avoid the courtroom process entirely, and it does not automatically remove every forensic consequence of not adducing evidence in civil litigation.



Public Interest Immunity — Primacy of Protection of Police Informers



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: legal doctrine] \[TOPIC: public interest immunity protecting confidentiality of informers] \[CONCEPTS: informer privilege, public interest immunity, confidential police source, disclosure immunity, law enforcement confidentiality, source protection, police informer identity, recruitment of informers]



Public interest immunity strongly protects the confidentiality of police informers, and the source treats that protection as primary in disclosure disputes involving informer identity or source confidentiality.



The rationale is functional as well as doctrinal. If confidentiality promised to informers can later be broken through disclosure processes, future sources may refuse to assist police. The source quotes the proposition that public knowledge of such a breach would undermine the capacity of police to recruit informers in future.



This doctrine operates as a substantive limit on disclosure rather than as a mere discretionary preference. The protection is directed to preserving the administration of justice by safeguarding the ongoing availability of confidential human sources.



In the context of Tasmanian criminal law research, the doctrinal point is that informer confidentiality is treated as a serious public interest capable of defeating disclosure claims unless an established exception or countervailing principle applies.



Authority — Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi \[2024] HCA 34



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi \[2024] HCA 34] \[TOPIC: High Court reaffirmation of primacy of informer confidentiality] \[CONCEPTS: informer privilege, High Court, confidential source, public interest immunity, police disclosure, source expectations of confidentiality, law enforcement effectiveness]



Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi is cited in the source as recent High Court authority reaffirming the primacy of protecting police informers through public interest immunity.



The source states that the case reiterates the principles expressed in AB (a pseudonym) v CD (a pseudonym). The key proposition extracted is that public knowledge that a source’s expectation of confidentiality had been breached would undermine the ability of police to recruit informers in the future.



The significance of the case lies in its reinforcement of the systemic rationale for informer protection. The doctrine is not only about fairness to a particular source. It is about preserving the capacity of law enforcement agencies to gather information from confidential human sources over time.



As used in the source, the case supports a high level of protection for informer confidentiality in disclosure disputes and related criminal process questions.



Fraud — DPP Charging Approach to Computer-Related Fraud Under $20,000



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: procedural rule] \[TOPIC: prosecutorial charging approach for lower-value computer-related fraud] \[CONCEPTS: DPP charging advice, summary versus indictment, computer fraud charging, under 20000 fraud, prosecutorial discretion, course of conduct, co-accused consistency, lower court adequacy]



The source records prosecutorial guidance that computer-related fraud involving less than $20,000 should ordinarily be charged summarily unless aggravating features justify indictment.



The stated approach is analogous to the charging treatment of stealing under s 72 of the Justices Act 1959 (Tas). A summary charge is said to be preferable where the amount involved is below $20,000 unless one or more of the following applies:



the conduct forms part of a course of conduct or a series of crimes that are indictable;



the offender’s circumstances or the conduct are such that the penalty available in the lower court would be inadequate; or



a co-accused has been dealt with on indictment, such that consistency between co-offenders is important absent compelling reasons otherwise.



This chunk records charging guidance rather than a statutory rule of liability. Its relevance is procedural and practical: it informs forum selection and prosecutorial discretion in Tasmanian fraud matters involving computer-related conduct.



Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth) s 480.4 — Dishonestly Obtaining or Dealing in Personal Financial Information



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth)] \[SECTION: s 480.4] \[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth) s 480.4] \[TYPE: offence] \[TOPIC: Commonwealth offence involving dishonest acquisition or dealing with personal financial information] \[CONCEPTS: personal financial information, passwords, credit card data, bank account details, identity fraud, Commonwealth fraud offence, dishonest obtaining, financial institution]



Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth) s 480.4 is identified in the source as a Commonwealth offence used where deceit is employed to obtain or deal with personal financial information. 



block\_028



The source describes the provision as applying in cases where an offender uses deceit to elicit information that can then be used to access accounts or obtain loans. Examples given include credit card numbers, verbal passwords, and account numbers. The source distinguishes this category from more general personal information such as a driver licence number.



The source also states that the offence must involve a bank, lending institution, or corporation. On the material provided, that proposition appears as source commentary rather than quoted statutory text, so it should be treated as a research note requiring verification against the legislation itself. \[REVIEW]



The doctrinal utility of the provision in this research block is its role in identity-fraud and financial-information prosecutions where the dishonest conduct centres on acquiring access credentials or financial account data rather than immediately obtaining property.



Authority — JOD v R \[2009] NSWCCA 205



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: JOD v R \[2009] NSWCCA 205] \[TOPIC: parity in sentencing for identity fraud offending within organised scheme] \[CONCEPTS: identity fraud, sentencing parity, co-offender comparison, organised syndicate, financial institution fraud, higher objective criminality, repeated offending, sentence appeal]



JOD v R is used in the source as authority on sentencing parity in identity-fraud offending involving financial institutions and organised criminal activity. 



block\_028



The source describes the case as involving an offender in an organised syndicate that assumed the identities of legitimate persons and entities in order to defraud financial institutions. The offending included false documents, opening bank accounts in false names, withdrawing money from genuine accounts, and making fraudulent claims involving credit cards and loans.



The principle drawn from the case is that formal differences in charges do not automatically make offenders true comparators for parity purposes. The court accepted that factual and forensic differences may justify different sentences. The source notes that the applicant’s higher objective criminality, greater role in the syndicate, and repeated offending over a lengthy period meant there was no disproportion in the sentence compared with others.



The case is therefore relevant where identity-fraud sentencing raises parity arguments among offenders with overlapping but not identical participation.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 252A — Dishonestly Acquiring a Financial Advantage



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 252A] \[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 252A] \[TYPE: offence] \[TOPIC: dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage] \[CONCEPTS: financial advantage, deception offence, dishonest gain, property services money, alternative to false pretences, temporary or permanent benefit, price swapping, fraudulent sale]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 252A concerns dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage by deceptive conduct.



The source frames the offence as applicable where a person deceives another in order to obtain a financial advantage for themselves or for someone else, or to cause a financial disadvantage to another. The advantage or disadvantage may be permanent or temporary.



Examples given in the source include:



switching price stickers so that a higher-priced item is purchased for less;



obtaining prepaid mobile phone credit from a staff member using a stolen credit card; and



advertising goods for sale online, receiving the buyer’s funds, and then failing to supply the goods.



The source also notes that a false representation about an intention to pay later, while not amounting to a false pretence, may still constitute a deception for the purposes of s 252A. That observation identifies the section as a useful alternative charge where the conduct is deceptive but does not comfortably fit the older language of false pretences.



Authority — Tasmania v Clark \[2018] TASSC 64



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[TYPE: case authority] \[CASE: Tasmania v Clark \[2018] TASSC 64] \[TOPIC: elements and definition of dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage] \[CONCEPTS: s 252A elements, financial advantage, deception, dishonesty, Tasmanian Supreme Court, fraud charge analysis, definition of advantage]



Tasmania v Clark is identified in the source as authority on the elements and definition of dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 252A. 



block\_028



The source does not set out the reasoning in detail, but it specifically identifies the case as relevant to the constituent elements of the offence and the meaning of dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage. On that basis, the case should be treated as a Tasmanian authority to consult when particularising or analysing a charge under s 252A. \[REVIEW]



The doctrinal significance of the case within this research block is that it anchors the offence in local authority rather than relying only on research notes or analogies to fraud concepts from other jurisdictions. Where a charge under s 252A is under consideration, this case is the cited local authority for element analysis.



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 250-4 — Obtaining Goods by False Pretences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 250-4] \[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 250-4] \[TYPE: offence] \[TOPIC: obtaining goods by false pretences] \[CONCEPTS: false pretences, obtaining goods, face-to-face deception, dishonest representation, staff interaction, supplier fraud, stolen credit card purchase, pretended authority]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 250-4 is identified in the source as the offence used where goods are obtained by false pretences through a deceptive representation made to another person.



The source examples indicate that the offence is apt where the fraudulent transaction involves direct interaction with a human decision-maker. Examples include:



using a stolen credit card to purchase goods from a business where the transaction occurs with a staff member; and



pretending to be an employee of a business in order to obtain goods from that business’s supplier.



These examples distinguish the offence from computer-related fraud by emphasising deception mediated through a person rather than an automated system. The doctrinal point in the source is therefore classificatory: where the dishonest acquisition depends on a false representation to a person who then parts with the goods, obtaining goods by false pretences may be the better fit.



The source does not provide the full statutory elements, so further verification against the Code would be required before charge finalisation. \[REVIEW]



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 43A — Computer-Related Fraud



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] \[ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)] \[SECTION: s 43A] \[CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 43A] \[TYPE: offence] \[TOPIC: computer-related fraud using automated systems rather than direct human interaction] \[CONCEPTS: computer fraud, automated transaction, self-service checkout, ATM withdrawal, online card use, automated phone payment, no human interaction, stolen card]



Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 43A is identified in the source as the provision dealing with computer-related fraud where the dishonest transaction occurs through an automated system rather than through direct misrepresentation to a person.



The source gives several examples:



purchasing items with a stolen credit card at a self-service checkout with no staff interaction;



using a stolen ATM card to withdraw cash from an ATM;



using a stolen credit card to pay a bill through an automated telephone payment system; and



using a stolen credit card to purchase items online without communication with an actual person.



The charging distinction drawn in the source is practical. Where the fraudulent mechanism depends on electronic or automated response rather than a human being deceived into handing over goods or money, s 43A may be the more accurate offence selection.



The source presents this as a charging-differences note rather than a full statutory analysis, so the precise elements should be checked against the legislation before formal use. \[REVIEW]



Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 249–250 — False Pretence Offences



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 249–250]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: offence]

\[TOPIC: Obtaining goods by false pretence]

\[CONCEPTS: false pretence offence, obtaining goods by deception, fraudulent representation, inducing delivery of goods, dishonest misrepresentation, deception offences, reliance by victim, property obtained by deceit]



The offences created by Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 249–250 criminalise obtaining goods or inducing delivery of goods through false representations of fact. The Crown must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the accused made a false representation of an existing fact, and that this representation induced the victim to deliver goods or property.



The representation may be made by words, conduct, or other communicative behaviour, but it must be a statement of present or past fact. A mere promise of future conduct is insufficient unless the promise itself is false at the time it is made.



The prosecution must prove three central elements:



A false representation of fact was made by the accused.



The accused obtained goods or induced delivery of goods.



The delivery occurred because the victim relied upon the false representation.



Reliance is critical. The offence is not established if the victim would have provided the goods regardless of the representation. For example, if goods were given indiscriminately without regard to identity or representations, the causal link between representation and delivery may be absent.



Accordingly, the prosecution must prove that the false pretence operated on the mind of the person delivering the goods, thereby inducing the transaction.



Evidentiary Requirement — Proof of Inducement



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Proof that false pretence induced delivery of goods]

\[CONCEPTS: inducement element, victim reliance, proof of deception, causation in fraud offences, evidence of reliance, direct testimony of victim, did the victim rely on the lie]



A key evidentiary requirement in offences of false pretence is proof that the false representation actually induced the victim to deliver goods or property.



The ordinary and preferred method of establishing inducement is through direct evidence from the person who was induced. The victim should testify that the false representation influenced their decision to provide the goods.



The rationale is that inducement concerns the state of mind of the recipient of the representation, which is best proven through their testimony.



However, direct evidence is not strictly mandatory in every case. Courts have recognised that inducement may sometimes be inferred from surrounding circumstances where:



the false representation was made; and



the surrounding facts indicate that the representation was the only plausible reason the victim acted.



Even so, courts have emphasised that reliance on inference should be approached cautiously, and direct evidence remains the ordinary method of proof.



Authority — Laverty (1970) 54 Cr App R 495



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Laverty (1970) 54 Cr App R 495]

\[TOPIC: Proof of inducement in false pretence offences]

\[CONCEPTS: inducement requirement, reliance evidence, proof of deception, victim testimony requirement, evidentiary standard in fraud, inference of inducement]



The decision in Laverty (1970) 54 Cr App R 495 confirms that in false pretence offences the prosecution must prove that the false representation acted upon the mind of the person induced.



The court emphasised that:



it is axiomatic that the prosecution must establish inducement; and



the ordinary method of proving this element is through direct evidence from the person who received the false representation.



The case recognised that inducement may sometimes be inferred from circumstances, relying on earlier authority such as Sullivan (1945) 30 Cr App R 132, but warned against extending that principle unnecessarily.



The court stressed that the proper and usual method remains proof through the testimony of the person who relied on the representation.



Authority — Gordon Daniel McLeod v R \[1996] TASSC 10



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Gordon Daniel McLeod v R \[1996] TASSC 10]

\[TOPIC: Inducement requirement in obtaining goods by false pretence]

\[CONCEPTS: inducement element, deception offence proof, obtaining goods by misrepresentation, causal link in fraud, victim reliance]



In Gordon Daniel McLeod v R \[1996] TASSC 10, the Supreme Court of Tasmania confirmed that conviction for obtaining goods by false pretence requires proof that the accused obtained goods through a false representation of fact which induced the victim to deliver them.



The Court held that the prosecution must establish that:



the accused made a false representation of fact, and



the representation caused the victim or company to deliver goods.



The evidence in that case was held insufficient, as the prosecution failed to demonstrate that the representation operated as an inducing factor.



The Court compared the evidence with Clemesha v R (1978) WAR 193, where the use of a false name was recognised as a false representation but was found not to have influenced the victim’s decision to supply goods.



The case illustrates that proof of the representation alone is insufficient. The prosecution must establish the causal connection between the representation and the transfer of property.



Compensation Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: legal doctrine]

\[TOPIC: Criminal compensation orders]

\[CONCEPTS: compensation orders, restitution in criminal cases, victim compensation, summary civil remedy, property damage compensation, sentencing compensation orders]



Compensation orders in criminal proceedings provide a summary and inexpensive mechanism for victims to obtain compensation for personal injury or property damage resulting from criminal conduct.



These orders operate as a supplement to the sentencing process, allowing victims to recover damages without commencing separate civil proceedings.



The jurisdiction is intended for straightforward claims and is not designed to resolve disputes requiring extensive investigation or complex factual determination.



Where the claim raises significant factual controversy or requires detailed inquiry, courts should decline to determine the issue summarily and leave the matter to civil proceedings.



Authority — Lusted v Doornbusch \[2011] TASMC 28



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Lusted v Doornbusch \[2011] TASMC 28]

\[TOPIC: Scope and purpose of compensation orders]

\[CONCEPTS: victim compensation orders, summary compensation jurisdiction, sentencing compensation, civil damages alternative, limits of criminal compensation]



In Lusted v Doornbusch \[2011] TASMC 28, the Magistrates Court explained the purpose and limits of compensation orders within criminal proceedings.



The Court held that the legislation provides a convenient and summary method for compensating victims for personal injury or property damage resulting from an offence. It is intended to avoid the need for separate civil proceedings in simple cases.



However, the jurisdiction is not suitable for complex disputes requiring extensive evidence or investigation. If a genuine issue exists requiring detailed inquiry, the court should decline to make an order and leave the matter to civil litigation.



The Court also emphasised that compensation orders operate as an amount “on account” of damages that might ultimately be recovered in civil proceedings.



Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 68 — Power to Make Compensation Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 68]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Power of court to order compensation]

\[CONCEPTS: sentencing compensation power, restitution order, sentencing evidence, property damage compensation, criminal court compensation jurisdiction]



Section 68 of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) empowers courts to make compensation orders where the facts supporting the claim sufficiently appear from material before the court.



The court may rely on:



evidence given at trial,



material presented during sentencing, or



admissions made by or on behalf of a party.



The provision does not authorise a separate trial-style hearing to determine compensation claims. Instead, the court may determine compensation based on available materials presented during criminal proceedings.



Importantly, the court is not bound by the strict rules of evidence when determining compensation claims relating to loss, destruction, or damage to property. It may inform itself in any manner it considers appropriate.



This flexibility reflects the summary nature of the jurisdiction, which aims to provide an efficient mechanism for compensating victims without the complexity of civil litigation.



Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 94 — Requirement for an Actual Sentence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 94]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: Requirement that a sentencing order impose a sentence]

\[CONCEPTS: sentencing order validity, requirement for sentence, dismissal as sentence, sentencing jurisdiction, lawful sentencing disposition, sentencing formalities]



Section 94 of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) requires that when a court deals with an offender following a plea or finding of guilt, it must impose an actual sentence recognised by the Act. A sentencing order must therefore correspond to one of the dispositions authorised by the legislation.



A dismissal of the charge following a plea of guilty or finding of guilt under s 7 of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) constitutes a sentence for the purposes of the Act. Such a dismissal is therefore a valid sentencing disposition.



However, a dismissal following a “TNE” (taken into account but not proceeded with) or other informal resolution does not constitute a sentence under the Act. Where no authorised sentencing disposition is imposed, the order may fail to comply with the statutory requirement that a sentence be imposed.



The provision ensures that sentencing outcomes are formally recorded and legally valid, preventing courts from disposing of criminal matters without applying a recognised sentencing order.



Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) — Evidence of Facts for Sentencing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 81]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: evidentiary rule]

\[TOPIC: Proof of facts relied upon for sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: sentencing evidence rules, facts for prosecutor, reading of facts, informal proof of sentencing facts, sentencing procedure, evidentiary flexibility in sentencing]



Facts relied upon by the prosecution in support of a charge at the sentencing stage must be supported by evidence placed before the court. However, the evidentiary rules governing sentencing are more flexible than those governing trial.



Evidence supporting the prosecution’s account of the offending may be provided:



Formally, through admissible evidence tendered according to the usual rules; or



Informally, through procedures permitted under s 81 of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) and s 4(2) of the Evidence Act.



In practice, the most common method in Tasmania is for the prosecutor to read the facts for the prosecutor (FFP) during sentencing. This narrative summary of the offending is presented to the court and forms the factual basis upon which the sentencing judge or magistrate determines the appropriate penalty.



Although the rules of evidence are relaxed at sentencing, the factual basis of the sentence must still be supported by reliable material before the court.



Authority — Parker v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 16



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Parker v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 16 (8 October 2019)]

\[TOPIC: Informal proof of sentencing facts]

\[CONCEPTS: sentencing facts procedure, facts for prosecutor practice, sentencing evidence flexibility, sentencing submissions, informal proof]



In Parker v Tasmania \[2019] TASCCA 16, the Court of Criminal Appeal confirmed that the common practice in Tasmania is for the prosecution to read the facts for the prosecutor to establish the factual basis of the offending for sentencing purposes.



The case recognises that sentencing courts may receive evidence informally, consistent with the statutory framework allowing relaxed evidentiary rules at the sentencing stage.



Sentencing Principles

Common Law Sentencing Principles — Proportionality



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: Proportionality in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: proportionality principle, sentence must match offence seriousness, objective gravity, fairness in punishment, criminal culpability, sentencing proportionality]



The principle of proportionality requires that the sentence imposed on an offender be proportionate to the objective seriousness of the offence.



The seriousness of an offence is assessed by reference to factors including:



the nature of the conduct,



the harm caused or risked,



the offender’s level of culpability, and



the surrounding circumstances of the offending.



The principle ensures that punishment remains commensurate with the criminality involved, preventing courts from imposing sentences that exceed what the offence itself justifies.



Authority — Veen v The Queen (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Veen v The Queen (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465]

\[TOPIC: proportionality principle in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: proportionality doctrine, limits on punishment, objective seriousness, sentencing fairness, punishment must fit the crime]



In Veen v The Queen (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465, the High Court confirmed that proportionality is a fundamental principle of sentencing. A sentence must not exceed what is justified by the seriousness of the offence, even where considerations such as protection of the community are present.



Common Law Sentencing Principles — Totality



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: Totality principle in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: totality principle, aggregate sentencing, multiple offences sentencing, overall criminality, crushing sentence avoidance]



The totality principle applies when an offender is sentenced for multiple offences. It requires the sentencing court to ensure that the overall sentence imposed is just and appropriate when viewed as a whole.



The principle serves two complementary purposes:



To prevent the accumulation of individual sentences from producing an overall sentence that is crushing or disproportionate.



To ensure the total penalty adequately reflects the overall criminality of the offending.



Courts may adjust the structure of sentences—such as by making some sentences concurrent—to achieve a proportionate aggregate outcome.



Authority — R v KM \[2004] NSWCCA 65



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: R v KM \[2004] NSWCCA 65]

\[TOPIC: operation of the totality principle]

\[CONCEPTS: sentencing multiple offences, aggregate sentencing review, avoiding crushing sentences, proportional aggregate penalty]



The decision in R v KM \[2004] NSWCCA 65 explains that when imposing sentences for multiple offences, courts must ensure that the combined effect of all sentences reflects the total criminality involved and does not produce an unjust overall penalty.



Youth as a Sentencing Consideration



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: Youth and rehabilitation in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: youth mitigation, rehabilitation priority, young offender sentencing, reduced moral culpability, reform prospects]



Youth is recognised as a significant sentencing consideration because younger offenders are generally regarded as having greater prospects of rehabilitation.



Where an offender is young, courts may give greater weight to:



rehabilitation,



opportunities for reform, and



avoidance of exposure to the negative influences of custodial environments.



Authority — Garcie v Lusted \[2014] TASSC 27



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Garcie v Lusted \[2014] TASSC 27]

\[TOPIC: rehabilitation emphasis for youthful offenders]

\[CONCEPTS: youth mitigation, rehabilitation focus, sentencing young offenders, reduced deterrence emphasis]



In Garcie v Lusted \[2014] TASSC 27, the Supreme Court emphasised that youthful offenders should ordinarily be given every reasonable opportunity to reform, and that considerations of rehabilitation may outweigh punishment and deterrence in appropriate cases.



Delay as a Mitigating Factor



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: delay between offending and sentence]

\[CONCEPTS: delay mitigation, sentencing delay, anxiety awaiting sentence, rehabilitation during delay, mitigating circumstances]



Delay between the commission of an offence and the imposition of sentence may operate as a mitigating factor in appropriate circumstances.



Two principal rationales justify mitigation based on delay:



Anxiety and stress experienced by the offender during a prolonged period awaiting resolution of the charges, which may amount to a form of punishment.



Evidence that the offender has demonstrated rehabilitation during the period of delay.



Authority — Palmer v Tasmania \[2024] TASCCA 6



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Palmer v Tasmania \[2024] TASCCA 6]

\[TOPIC: sentencing delay as mitigation]

\[CONCEPTS: delay mitigation principles, anxiety awaiting trial, rehabilitation during delay, unnecessary delay]



In Palmer v Tasmania \[2024] TASCCA 6, the Court of Criminal Appeal confirmed that delay may mitigate sentence where:



the delay is unnecessary or excessive, and



the delay is not attributable to the offender.



If the delay was caused by the offender—for example by absconding or avoiding prosecution—the mitigating effect will be reduced or eliminated.



Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 11C — Voluntary Intoxication and Sentencing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 11C]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: effect of voluntary intoxication on sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: voluntary intoxication sentencing, intoxication mitigation limits, alcohol and sentencing, self-induced intoxication, criminal responsibility intoxication]



Section 11C of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) provides that self-induced intoxication is not to be treated as a mitigating factor in sentencing.



The provision reflects the principle that offenders should not ordinarily receive a reduced sentence simply because they voluntarily consumed alcohol or drugs prior to committing an offence.



Authority — Brown v Jones \[2021] TASSC 58



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Brown v Jones \[2021] TASSC 58 (22 November 2021)]

\[TOPIC: intoxication rarely mitigates sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: intoxication sentencing effect, alcohol and culpability, intoxication aggravation, violent offending intoxication]



In Brown v Jones \[2021] TASSC 58, the Supreme Court confirmed that intoxication will rarely operate as mitigation. It may sometimes explain offending behaviour and therefore relate to remorse or prospects of rehabilitation, but it does not ordinarily reduce culpability.



In some cases, intoxication may even operate as an aggravating factor, particularly where intoxication makes violent conduct more frightening for the victim.



Sentencing Principle — Use of Crime Prevalence in Sentencing



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: relevance of crime prevalence statistics in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: deterrence, prevalence of crime, sentencing statistics, police crime data, general deterrence, sentencing context, crime trends, statistical evidence]



Deterrence in sentencing may take into account the prevalence of a particular category of offending, but courts must exercise caution when relying on statistical claims about crime trends.



Courts may receive submissions that a particular type of offence has become prevalent within the community and that stronger sentencing is therefore required to reinforce general deterrence. The rationale is that a rise in offending may require sentencing responses that emphasise denunciation and deterrence in order to discourage further offending.



However, courts must treat statistical evidence about crime prevalence with caution. Police data relating to the “commission” of offences may involve subjective classification decisions or changes in reporting practices. These factors may distort the apparent prevalence of particular offences.



The sentencing court must therefore ensure that statistical material does not displace the court’s own evaluation of the seriousness of the offence before it. Sentencing remains an individualized process that must be based on the objective gravity of the offending and the circumstances of the offender.



Evidence about crime prevalence may assist the court in understanding the broader social context of offending, but it cannot override the fundamental sentencing task of determining an appropriate penalty for the specific offence.



Authority — Limits on Reliance on Crime Statistics



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania \[2010] TASCCA 1]

\[TOPIC: limits on use of crime prevalence statistics in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: sentencing statistics caution, deterrence, crime prevalence evidence, judicial evaluation, sentencing discretion, police statistics reliability]



The decision in Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania \[2010] TASCCA 1 confirms that courts may consider statistical information about crime prevalence but must avoid allowing such material to overshadow the court’s own evaluation of the seriousness of the offence.



Wright J emphasised that while statistical material may assist sentencing courts, caution must be exercised in relying on police figures relating to the commission of offences. Such data may involve subjective judgment and may not accurately reflect true crime prevalence.



The court stated that statistical evidence must not “overshadow or displace” the sentencing court’s independent assessment of the gravity of the offending conduct in light of the known facts and circumstances of the case.



The authority therefore establishes an interpretive principle for sentencing: contextual information about crime trends may be relevant to deterrence, but sentencing remains grounded in the specific facts of the individual case.



Sentencing Principle — Youth as a Sentencing Consideration



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: role of youth in sentencing]

\[CONCEPTS: youthful offenders, mitigation by age, rehabilitation, deterrence vs youth, seriousness of offence, sentencing balance, criminal maturity]



Youth is a recognised mitigating factor in sentencing because young offenders are generally considered to have greater prospects for rehabilitation and less mature judgment.



Courts frequently place significant weight on rehabilitation when sentencing youthful offenders, particularly those under the age of 18. Youth may reduce the weight given to punitive considerations such as retribution and general deterrence.



However, youth does not automatically displace other sentencing objectives. The seriousness of the offence, the degree of planning involved, and the offender’s demonstrated disregard for the law may justify greater emphasis on deterrence and denunciation.



Where the offending behaviour displays characteristics of adult criminal conduct—such as premeditation, violence, or sustained offending—the mitigating effect of youth may be reduced.



Accordingly, sentencing courts must balance youth and rehabilitation against the gravity of the offending and the need to protect the community.



Authority — Youth Does Not Automatically Override Deterrence



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania \[2010] TASCCA 1]

\[TOPIC: limits of youth mitigation in serious offending]

\[CONCEPTS: youthful offender sentencing, serious violence, premeditation, deterrence weight, denunciation, rehabilitation balance]



In Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania \[2010] TASCCA 1, the Court of Criminal Appeal held that youth does not automatically outweigh other sentencing considerations such as deterrence and denunciation.



The court observed that although the offender was still technically a youthful offender, the offences involved premeditation and detailed planning and included serious violent conduct.



These circumstances indicated behaviour consistent with adult criminal conduct. As a result, the sentencing court was entitled to give substantial weight to deterrence and denunciation rather than allowing youth and rehabilitation to dominate the sentencing exercise.



The decision illustrates that youth is a relevant sentencing factor but must be weighed against the nature and seriousness of the offending.



Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)

Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AC — Home Detention Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 42AC]

\[CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AC — Power to Impose Home Detention Order]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: power to impose home detention order]

\[CONCEPTS: home detention order, electronic monitoring sentence, alternative to imprisonment, sentencing option, custodial alternative, residential detention]



Section 42AC of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) allows a court to impose a home detention order as a sentencing option.



A court may impose a home detention order where it convicts an offender and considers that the offender would otherwise have been sentenced to imprisonment, including a suspended sentence. Home detention is therefore designed as an alternative to custodial imprisonment.



A home detention order is not merely a substitute sentence for imprisonment. It is an independent sentencing option under the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas).



The order requires the offender to reside at specified premises and comply with conditions designed to restrict their liberty and facilitate supervision.



Before making such an order the court must consider whether the offender and the proposed residence are suitable for home detention and must ensure that statutory preconditions have been satisfied.



Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AF — Duration of Home Detention Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 42AF]

\[CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AF]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: sentencing principle]

\[TOPIC: maximum duration of home detention order]

\[CONCEPTS: home detention duration, maximum sentence length, electronic monitoring duration, custodial alternative duration]



Section 42AF of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) establishes that a home detention order may operate for a maximum period of 18 months.



Where multiple home detention orders are imposed, the combined operational period of those orders must not exceed 18 months.



This statutory limit reflects the legislative intention that home detention operate as a structured but limited alternative to imprisonment rather than as a long-term custodial substitute.



Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AD — Core Conditions of Home Detention Orders



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)]

\[SECTION: s 42AD]

\[CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AD]
\[CATEGORY: doctrine]

\[TYPE: procedural rule]

\[TOPIC: mandatory conditions of home detention orders]

\[CONCEPTS: electronic monitoring condition, residence requirement, police entry power, monitoring compliance, probation supervision]



Section 42AD of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) specifies core conditions that apply to every home detention order.



These include conditions requiring that the offender:



Not commit any offence punishable by imprisonment.



Reside at the specified premises during the order.



Remain at those premises during specified times unless authorised to leave.



Permit police or probation officers to enter the premises.



Permit searches and monitoring necessary to enforce compliance.



Electronic monitoring is ordinarily a mandatory condition of a home detention order unless the court determines that there are suitable reasons not to impose such monitoring.



Authority — Meaning of Bodily Harm



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: McCallum \[1969] Tas SR 73]

\[TOPIC: definition of bodily harm]

\[CONCEPTS: bodily harm definition, injury to health, interference with comfort, criminal injury threshold, harm element]



The case of McCallum \[1969] Tas SR 73 established the authoritative definition of “bodily harm” in Tasmanian criminal law.



The court held that bodily harm includes any hurt or injury that interferes with a person’s health or comfort. The injury need not be permanent but must be more than transient or trifling.



This definition has been consistently applied in Tasmanian criminal law when interpreting offences that include bodily harm as an element.



Authority — Psychological Harm as Bodily Harm



\[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law]

\[TYPE: case authority]

\[CASE: Tasmania v Joseph \[2017] TASSC 23]

\[TOPIC: psychological injury as bodily harm]

\[CONCEPTS: bodily harm psychological injury, psychiatric injury, robbery trauma, victim harm interpretation]



In Tasmania v Joseph \[2017] TASSC 23, the Supreme Court confirmed that bodily harm may include psychological or psychiatric injury.



The court reasoned that psychological injury inflicted through threats or intimidation can constitute bodily harm because it interferes with a person’s health or comfort in the same way as physical injury.



This interpretation aligns with the definition in McCallum and recognises that psychological trauma inflicted during violent crime can produce serious and lasting harm to victims.
